title: Edges Are All You Need: Robust Gait Recognition via Label-Free Structure
summary: Gait recognition is a non-intrusive biometric technique for security applications, yet existing studies are dominated by silhouette- and parsing-based representations. Silhouettes are sparse and miss internal structural details, limiting discriminability. Parsing enriches silhouettes with part-level structures, but relies heavily on upstream human parsers (e.g., label granularity and boundary precision), leading to unstable performance across datasets and sometimes even inferior results to silhouettes. We revisit gait representations from a structural perspective and describe a design space defined by edge density and supervision form: silhouettes use sparse boundary edges with weak single-label supervision, while parsing uses denser cues with strong semantic priors. In this space, we identify an underexplored paradigm: dense part-level structure without explicit semantic labels, and introduce SKETCH as a new visual modality for gait recognition. Sketch extracts high-frequency structural cues (e.g., limb articulations and self-occlusion contours) directly from RGB images via edge-based detectors in a label-free manner. We further show that label-guided parsing and label-free sketch are semantically decoupled and structurally complementary. Based on this, we propose SKETCHGAIT, a hierarchically disentangled multi-modal framework with two independent streams for modality-specific learning and a lightweight early-stage fusion branch to capture structural complementarity. Extensive experiments on SUSTech1K and CCPG validate the proposed modality and framework: SketchGait achieves 92.9% Rank-1 on SUSTech1K and 93.1% mean Rank-1 on CCPG.

AI.tldr: Proposes SKETCH, a dense, label-free structural representation for gait, and SKETCHGAIT, a dual-stream framework that fuses sketch and parsing cues to exploit structural complementarity, achieving ~93% Rank-1 on SUSTech1K and CCPG.
AI.motivation: Silhouette-based gait features are sparse and miss internal structural details, while parsing-based methods depend on upstream human parsers (label granularity, boundary accuracy), causing instability across datasets. The authors seek a robust, dense structural cue set without reliance on explicit semantic labels.
AI.method: (1) Reframe gait representations via a design space of edge density vs. supervision strength. (2) Introduce SKETCH: extract high-frequency structural cues (limb articulations, self-occlusion contours) directly from RGB using edge-based detectors in a label-free manner. (3) Demonstrate that parsing (label-guided) and sketch (label-free) are semantically decoupled yet structurally complementary. (4) Propose SKETCHGAIT: a hierarchically disentangled multi-modal framework with two independent streams for modality-specific learning and a lightweight early-stage fusion branch to capture cross-modality structural complementarity.
AI.result: On SUSTech1K, SKETCHGAIT attains 92.9% Rank-1 accuracy; on CCPG, it achieves 93.1% mean Rank-1, indicating strong performance and validating the new modality and fusion framework.
AI.conclusion: Dense, label-free structural cues from edge-based sketching provide a stable, discriminative alternative to silhouettes and a complementary signal to parsing. A disentangled dual-stream with early fusion leverages this complementarity to yield state-of-the-art gait recognition across datasets.

title: Thinking with Spatial Code for Physical-World Video Reasoning
summary: We introduce Thinking with Spatial Code, a framework that transforms RGB video into explicit, temporally coherent 3D representations for physical-world visual question answering. We highlight the empirical finding that our proposed spatial encoder can parse videos into structured spatial code with explicit 3D oriented bounding boxes and semantic labels, enabling large language models (LLMs) to reason directly over explicit spatial variables. Specifically, we propose the spatial encoder that encodes image and geometric features by unifying 6D object parsing and tracking backbones with geometric prediction, and we further finetuning LLMs with reinforcement learning using a spatial rubric reward that encourages perspective-aware, geometrically grounded inference. As a result, our model outperforms proprietary vision-language models on VSI-Bench, setting a new state-of-the-art. Code is available at https://github.com/Beckschen/spatialcode.

AI.tldr: They propose a video-to-3D “spatial code” pipeline that parses RGB videos into explicit, temporally coherent 3D object states (oriented boxes + labels) and trains an LLM with a geometry-aware RL reward to reason over these variables, achieving SOTA on a physical-world VQA benchmark (VSI-Bench).
AI.motivation: Current vision-language models struggle with perspective-aware, geometrically grounded reasoning from raw RGB because implicit features lack explicit 3D structure and temporal coherence. The authors aim to expose explicit spatial variables so LLMs can reason about physical scenes more reliably.
AI.method: 1) A spatial encoder that unifies 6D object parsing and multi-object tracking backbones with geometric prediction to convert video frames into structured 3D representations (oriented bounding boxes + semantics) that are temporally consistent. 2) Finetune LLMs via reinforcement learning using a spatial rubric reward that favors perspective-aware, geometry-grounded answers over mere pattern matching.
AI.result: The approach surpasses proprietary vision-language models on VSI-Bench and sets a new state of the art for physical-world visual QA; empirical evidence shows the encoder yields coherent spatial code enabling improved reasoning.
AI.conclusion: Making 3D structure explicit and aligning LLM training with geometry-aware rewards significantly improves physical reasoning in VQA. The released code suggests the method is practical and reproducible, and offers a promising direction for integrating explicit spatial representations with language reasoning.

title: From Decoupled to Coupled: Robustness Verification for Learning-based Keypoint Detection with Joint Specifications
comment: 21 pages, 4 figures, 9 tables. arXiv admin note: text overlap with arXiv:2408.00117
summary: Keypoint detection underpins many vision tasks, including pose estimation, viewpoint recovery, and 3D reconstruction, yet modern neural models remain vulnerable to small input perturbations. Despite its importance, formal robustness verification for keypoint detectors is largely unexplored due to high-dimensional inputs and continuous coordinate outputs. We propose the first coupled robustness verification framework for heatmap-based keypoint detectors that bounds the joint deviation across all keypoints, capturing their interdependencies and downstream task requirements. Unlike prior decoupled, classification-style approaches that verify each keypoint independently and yield conservative guarantees, our method verifies collective behavior. We formulate verification as a falsification problem using a mixed-integer linear program (MILP) that combines reachable heatmap sets with a polytope encoding joint deviation constraints. Infeasibility certifies robustness, while feasibility provides counterexamples, and we prove the method is sound: if it certifies the model as robust, then the keypoint detection model is guaranteed to be robust. Experiments show that our coupled approach achieves high verified rates and remains effective under strict error thresholds where decoupled methods fail.

AI.tldr: Introduces the first sound, coupled robustness verification framework for heatmap-based keypoint detectors using an MILP that jointly bounds deviations across all keypoints, yielding higher verified robustness than prior decoupled methods—especially under strict error thresholds.
AI.motivation: Keypoint detectors are critical for tasks like pose estimation and 3D reconstruction but are fragile to small input perturbations. Existing verification methods struggle due to high-dimensional images, continuous coordinate outputs, and interdependent keypoints; decoupled, classification-style verifiers are overly conservative and misaligned with downstream needs.
AI.method: Formulate verification as a falsification MILP that couples all keypoints: combine reachable sets of heatmaps from the network with a polytope that encodes joint deviation constraints across keypoints. If the MILP is infeasible, robustness is certified; if feasible, it returns a counterexample. The approach is proven sound for heatmap-based detectors.
AI.result: Empirically, the coupled MILP achieves higher verified robustness rates than decoupled baselines and continues to verify under tight error thresholds where decoupled methods fail; when robustness cannot be certified, it provides concrete adversarial examples.
AI.conclusion: Coupled, sound MILP-based verification aligns with downstream requirements by capturing keypoint interdependencies, delivering stronger and more practical robustness guarantees than prior decoupled approaches for heatmap-based keypoint detectors.

title: DreamCAD: Scaling Multi-modal CAD Generation using Differentiable Parametric Surfaces
summary: Computer-Aided Design (CAD) relies on structured and editable geometric representations, yet existing generative methods are constrained by small annotated datasets with explicit design histories or boundary representation (BRep) labels. Meanwhile, millions of unannotated 3D meshes remain untapped, limiting progress in scalable CAD generation. To address this, we propose DreamCAD, a multi-modal generative framework that directly produces editable BReps from point-level supervision, without CAD-specific annotations. DreamCAD represents each BRep as a set of parametric patches (e.g., Bézier surfaces) and uses a differentiable tessellation method to generate meshes. This enables large-scale training on 3D datasets while reconstructing connected and editable surfaces. Furthermore, we introduce CADCap-1M, the largest CAD captioning dataset to date, with 1M+ descriptions generated using GPT-5 for advancing text-to-CAD research. DreamCAD achieves state-of-the-art performance on ABC and Objaverse benchmarks across text, image, and point modalities, improving geometric fidelity and surpassing 75% user preference. Code and dataset will be publicly available.

AI.tldr: DreamCAD is a multi-modal framework that generates editable BRep CAD models directly from point-level supervision by representing shapes as parametric patches and using differentiable tessellation, enabling large-scale training on mesh datasets; it also releases CADCap-1M for text-to-CAD, and achieves SOTA on ABC and Objaverse with >75% user preference.
AI.motivation: Annotated CAD datasets with explicit design histories or BRep labels are scarce, restricting current CAD generative models, while vast unannotated 3D meshes remain unused. The goal is scalable, high-fidelity CAD generation (including text-to-CAD) without relying on CAD-specific labels.
AI.method: Represent each BRep as a set of parametric surface patches (e.g., Bézier). Use a differentiable tessellation pipeline to render meshes from these patches, enabling supervision from points/meshes. Train a multi-modal generator (text, image, point inputs) to predict BReps directly. Additionally, provide CADCap-1M, a 1M+ synthetic caption dataset to support text-to-CAD training.
AI.result: State-of-the-art performance on ABC and Objaverse benchmarks across text, image, and point modalities; improved geometric fidelity and >75% user preference in evaluations. Code and dataset to be released.
AI.conclusion: Point-level supervision plus differentiable tessellation allows learning editable BRep generation at scale without CAD-specific annotations. The approach improves fidelity and user preference and supplies a large caption dataset, potentially accelerating text-to-CAD research.

title: Verify as You Go: An LLM-Powered Browser Extension for Fake News Detection
summary: The rampant spread of fake news in the digital age poses serious risks to public trust and democratic institutions, underscoring the need for effective, transparent, and user-centered detection tools. Existing browser extensions often fall short due to opaque model behavior, limited explanatory support, and a lack of meaningful user engagement. This paper introduces Aletheia, a novel browser extension that leverages Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) and Large Language Models (LLMs) to detect fake news and provide evidence-based explanations. Aletheia further includes two interactive components: a Discussion Hub that enables user dialogue around flagged content and a Stay Informed feature that surfaces recent fact-checks. Through extensive experiments, we show that Aletheia outperforms state-of-the-art baselines in detection performance. Complementing this empirical evaluation, a complementary user study with 250 participants confirms the system's usability and perceived effectiveness, highlighting its potential as a transparent tool for combating online fake news.

AI.tldr: Aletheia is a browser extension that uses RAG-powered LLMs to detect fake news, provide evidence-based explanations, and engage users via discussion and fact-check feeds; it outperforms baselines and is perceived as usable/effective by 250 users.
AI.motivation: Fake news undermines public trust and democratic processes. Existing browser extensions are limited by opaque models, weak explanations, and little user engagement, motivating a transparent, user-centered detection tool.
AI.method: Design a browser extension (Aletheia) that combines Retrieval-Augmented Generation with LLMs to classify and explain news veracity using retrieved evidence. Add two interactive features: a Discussion Hub for user dialogue on flagged content and a Stay Informed feed surfacing recent fact-checks. Evaluate detection performance against state-of-the-art baselines and run a 250-participant user study on usability and perceived effectiveness.
AI.result: Empirically, Aletheia outperforms state-of-the-art baselines in detection accuracy (specific metrics not provided in abstract). The user study reports high usability and perceived effectiveness, indicating users find the explanations and features helpful.
AI.conclusion: Aletheia is a promising, transparent, and user-centered tool for combating online fake news, coupling evidence-backed explanations with engagement features and demonstrating superior performance and positive user reception.

title: RoboLayout: Differentiable 3D Scene Generation for Embodied Agents
summary: Recent advances in vision language models (VLMs) have shown strong potential for spatial reasoning and 3D scene layout generation from open-ended language instructions. However, generating layouts that are not only semantically coherent but also feasible for interaction by embodied agents remains challenging, particularly in physically constrained indoor environments. In this paper, RoboLayout is introduced as an extension of LayoutVLM that augments the original framework with agent-aware reasoning and improved optimization stability. RoboLayout integrates explicit reachability constraints into a differentiable layout optimization process, enabling the generation of layouts that are navigable and actionable by embodied agents. Importantly, the agent abstraction is not limited to a specific robot platform and can represent diverse entities with distinct physical capabilities, such as service robots, warehouse robots, humans of different age groups, or animals, allowing environment design to be tailored to the intended agent. In addition, a local refinement stage is proposed that selectively reoptimizes problematic object placements while keeping the remainder of the scene fixed, improving convergence efficiency without increasing global optimization iterations. Overall, RoboLayout preserves the strong semantic alignment and physical plausibility of LayoutVLM while enhancing applicability to agent-centric indoor scene generation, as demonstrated by experimental results across diverse scene configurations.

AI.tldr: RoboLayout extends LayoutVLM with agent-aware reachability constraints and a local refinement stage to generate semantically aligned, physically plausible, and navigable indoor layouts from language instructions.
AI.motivation: While VLMs can produce 3D scene layouts from open-ended language, they often ignore embodied-agent feasibility (navigation, manipulation) and struggle with optimization stability in constrained indoor spaces. There is a need to tailor environments to diverse agents (robots, humans, animals) with different physical capabilities.
AI.method: Integrate explicit agent reachability constraints into a differentiable layout optimization pipeline; use a platform-agnostic agent abstraction to encode varying physical capabilities; add a local refinement stage that selectively reoptimizes problematic object placements while keeping the rest of the scene fixed to improve convergence without extra global iterations.
AI.result: Generated layouts are more navigable and actionable for embodied agents while preserving LayoutVLM’s strong semantic alignment and physical plausibility; optimization becomes more stable and efficient; demonstrated across diverse indoor scene configurations.
AI.conclusion: RoboLayout makes language-driven indoor scene generation agent-centric and platform-agnostic, improving practical usability by producing layouts that are feasible for interaction across different agents without sacrificing semantic or physical quality.

title: Adversarial Batch Representation Augmentation for Batch Correction in High-Content Cellular Screening
summary: High-Content Screening routinely generates massive volumes of cell painting images for phenotypic profiling. However, technical variations across experimental executions inevitably induce biological batch (bio-batch) effects. These cause covariate shifts and degrade the generalization of deep learning models on unseen data. Existing batch correction methods typically rely on additional prior knowledge (e.g., treatment or cell culture information) or struggle to generalize to unseen bio-batches. In this work, we frame bio-batch mitigation as a Domain Generalization (DG) problem and propose Adversarial Batch Representation Augmentation (ABRA). ABRA explicitly models batch-wise statistical fluctuations by parameterizing feature statistics as structured uncertainties. Through a min-max optimization framework, it actively synthesizes worst-case bio-batch perturbations in the representation space, guided by a strict angular geometric margin to preserve fine-grained class discriminability. To prevent representation collapse during this adversarial exploration, we introduce a synergistic distribution alignment objective. Extensive evaluations on the large-scale RxRx1 and RxRx1-WILDS benchmarks demonstrate that ABRA establishes a new state-of-the-art for siRNA perturbation classification.

AI.tldr: ABRA is a domain-generalization method for cell painting that adversarially perturbs batch-related feature statistics with an angular margin and a distribution-alignment objective, achieving state-of-the-art siRNA classification on RxRx1 and RxRx1-WILDS.
AI.motivation: High-content screening images suffer from biological batch effects that cause covariate shift and hurt generalization. Prior batch-correction approaches often need extra metadata (e.g., treatments, culture conditions) and still fail on unseen batches. A metadata-free, generalizable solution is needed.
AI.method: Frame bio-batch mitigation as domain generalization. Parameterize batch-wise feature statistics as structured uncertainties and use a min-max optimization to synthesize worst-case batch perturbations directly in representation space. Enforce a strict angular geometric margin to preserve fine-grained class separability, and couple this with a distribution-alignment objective to avoid representation collapse.
AI.result: On large-scale RxRx1 and RxRx1-WILDS benchmarks, ABRA outperforms prior methods, setting a new state of the art for siRNA perturbation classification under batch shift.
AI.conclusion: Adversarially augmenting batch statistics with geometric constraints and alignment learns batch-robust, discriminative representations without relying on prior metadata, improving generalization to unseen bio-batches.

title: Attention Meets Reachability: Structural Equivalence and Efficiency in Grammar-Constrained LLM Decoding
summary: We study grammar-constrained decoding (GCD) as a coupling between an autoregressive next-token distribution and a reachability oracle over a pushdown system compiled from a context-free grammar (CFG). We prove an oracle invariance theorem: language-equivalent grammars induce identical admissible next-token sets for every prefix, hence identical logit masks, yet can yield provably different compiled state spaces and online ambiguity costs. We give exact control-state blowup counts for the canonical $a^n b^n$ language under redundant nonterminal delegation, and introduce a left-to-right structural ambiguity cost (SAC) measuring incremental packed-parse-forest growth per token. For two equivalent grammars over all finite strings, SAC is $O(1)$ per token under right-recursion but $Θ(t^2)$ per token and $Θ(n^3)$ cumulatively under concatenation. We establish engine-independent lower bounds: any sound, retrieval-efficient, parse-preserving online masking engine must incur $Ω(t^2)$ work per token on a specific constant-size CFG family, unconditionally within this model. We define decoding-cost equivalence classes of grammars and prove existence of minimal-SAC representatives within bounded rewrite families. Finally, we characterize the true conditional sampler via a Doob $h$-transform and derive sharp one-step KL and total-variation distortion bounds for hard-masked decoding in terms of survival-probability spread among admissible next tokens. We integrate these results with Transformer and Mixture-of-Experts architectures, derive latency envelopes in terms of vocabulary size, active state sets, and beam width, and connect SAC to instrumentation-based predictive performance models and automated grammar optimization.

AI.tldr: The paper formalizes grammar-constrained decoding (GCD) as coupling a language model’s next-token distribution with a CFG-based reachability oracle, proves invariance of admissible masks across language-equivalent grammars, quantifies structural ambiguity and state blowups, establishes engine-independent lower bounds, and links these to latency envelopes and grammar optimization in modern architectures.
AI.motivation: Although different CFGs can define the same language, their compiled pushdown systems can impose very different online decoding costs. Practitioners lack principled metrics and lower bounds to choose/optimize grammars and engines, and need distortion guarantees when hard-masking alters the conditional distribution. The work seeks unified theory and practical guidance for cost, accuracy, and latency in GCD.
AI.method: Model GCD as an autoregressive sampler coupled to a pushdown reachability oracle. Prove an oracle invariance theorem for admissible token sets. Analyze control-state blowups (e.g., a^n b^n with redundant delegation). Define a left-to-right Structural Ambiguity Cost (SAC) measuring incremental packed-parse-forest growth. Derive per-token and cumulative SAC under right-recursion vs concatenation. Prove engine-independent Ω(t^2) per-token lower bounds for any sound, retrieval-efficient, parse-preserving online masking engine on a constant-size CFG family. Define decoding-cost equivalence classes and show minimal-SAC representatives exist within bounded rewrite families. Use Doob h-transform to characterize the true conditional sampler and bound one-step KL/TV distortion from hard masks via survival-probability spread. Integrate with Transformers/MoE to derive latency envelopes in terms of vocabulary, active states, and beam, and connect SAC to instrumentation-based performance models and automated grammar optimization.
AI.result:
- Oracle invariance: language-equivalent CFGs yield identical admissible next-token masks for any prefix, despite differing compiled state spaces and ambiguity costs.
- Exact counts of control-state blowup for a^n b^n under redundant nonterminal delegation.
- SAC behavior: O(1) per token under right recursion; Θ(t^2) per token and Θ(n^3) cumulative under concatenation for language-equivalent grammars.
- Engine-independent lower bound: any sound, retrieval-efficient, parse-preserving online masking engine must incur Ω(t^2) work per token on a constructed constant-size CFG family.
- Existence of minimal-SAC grammar representatives in bounded rewrite classes.
- Doob h-transform characterization of the conditional sampler; sharp one-step KL/TV distortion bounds for hard-masked decoding via survival-probability spread among admissible tokens.
- Latency envelopes for Transformers/MoE tying runtime to vocabulary size, active state sets, and beam width; linkage of SAC to empirical performance models and automated grammar tuning.
AI.conclusion: Equivalent grammars can be operationally very different: while they mask the same tokens, their online decoding costs can vary from constant to quadratic per token. These costs are not eliminable by better engines due to lower bounds. SAC provides a practical metric to choose or rewrite grammars (favor right recursion) and guide engine design. The Doob h-transform and distortion bounds clarify when hard masking remains faithful. The theory supports latency prediction and automated optimization in modern LLM stacks.

title: Real-Time AI Service Economy: A Framework for Agentic Computing Across the Continuum
summary: Real-time AI services increasingly operate across the device-edge-cloud continuum, where autonomous AI agents generate latency-sensitive workloads, orchestrate multi-stage processing pipelines, and compete for shared resources under policy and governance constraints. This article shows that the structure of service-dependency graphs, modelled as DAGs whose nodes represent compute stages and whose edges encode execution ordering, is a primary determinant of whether decentralised, price-based resource allocation can work reliably at scale. When dependency graphs are hierarchical (tree or series-parallel), prices converge to stable equilibria, optimal allocations can be computed efficiently, and under appropriate mechanism design (with quasilinear utilities and discrete slice items), agents have no incentive to misreport their valuations within each decision epoch. When dependencies are more complex, with cross-cutting ties between pipeline stages, prices oscillate, allocation quality degrades, and the system becomes difficult to manage. To bridge this gap, we propose a hybrid management architecture in which cross-domain integrators encapsulate complex sub-graphs into resource slices that present a simpler, well-structured interface to the rest of the market. A systematic ablation study across six experiments (1,620 runs, 10 seeds each) confirms that (i) dependency-graph topology is a first-order determinant of price stability and scalability,(ii) the hybrid architecture reduces price volatility by up to 70-75% without sacrificing throughput, (iii) governance constraints create quantifiable efficiency-compliance trade-offs that depend jointly on topology and load, and (iv) under truthful bidding the decentralised market matches a centralised value-optimal baseline, confirming that decentralised coordination can replicate centralised allocation quality.

AI.tldr: Topology of AI service dependency DAGs largely determines whether decentralized, price-based resource allocation is stable and efficient; hierarchical DAGs enable convergence, optimality, and (under assumptions) truthfulness, while cross-cutting dependencies destabilize prices. A hybrid architecture that encapsulates complex subgraphs into slices restores stability (70–75% volatility reduction) without throughput loss and can match centralized optimal allocation under truthful bidding, with measurable governance trade-offs.
AI.motivation: Real-time AI workloads span device–edge–cloud and must be orchestrated under latency and governance constraints. Practitioners want scalable, decentralized resource allocation (markets/prices) but observe instability and inefficiency at scale. The paper seeks to identify the structural conditions (in dependency graphs) under which price-based mechanisms work and how to make them work when they otherwise would not.
AI.method: Model services as DAGs of compute stages; analyze price dynamics and equilibria for different topologies (hierarchical vs cross-cutting). Introduce a hybrid architecture where cross-domain integrators encapsulate complex subgraphs into resource slices exposing simpler interfaces. Employ mechanism design assumptions (quasilinear utilities, discrete slice items) to reason about incentives. Validate via a systematic ablation study across six experiments (1,620 runs, 10 seeds) measuring price stability, throughput, efficiency–compliance trade-offs, and parity with a centralized value-optimal baseline.
AI.result:
- Hierarchical (tree/series–parallel) DAGs: prices converge to stable equilibria; optimal allocations computable efficiently; no incentive to misreport within epochs under stated assumptions.
- Complex cross-cutting dependencies: price oscillations and degraded allocation quality.
- Hybrid integrator-based slicing: reduces price volatility by 70–75% without throughput loss.
- Governance constraints impose measurable efficiency–compliance trade-offs dependent on topology and load.
- Under truthful bidding, decentralized market matches centralized value-optimal allocation quality.
AI.conclusion: Dependency-graph topology is a first-order driver of the feasibility of decentralized price-based coordination. Structuring or encapsulating complex subgraphs enables scalable, stable markets that can replicate centralized optimal allocations while navigating governance constraints; thus, topology-aware architecture and mechanism design are key to reliable real-time AI service management.

title: Post Fusion Bird's Eye View Feature Stabilization for Robust Multimodal 3D Detection
summary:
Camera-LiDAR fusion is widely used in autonomous driving to enable accurate 3D object detection. However, bird's-eye view (BEV) fusion detectors can degrade significantly under domain shift and sensor failures, limiting reliability in real-world deployment. Existing robustness approaches often require modifying the fusion architecture or retraining specialized models, making them difficult to integrate into already deployed systems.
  We propose a Post Fusion Stabilizer (PFS), a lightweight module that operates on intermediate BEV representations of existing detectors and produces a refined feature map for the original detection head. The design stabilizes feature statistics under domain shift, suppresses spatial regions affected by sensor degradation, and adaptively restores weakened cues through residual correction. Designed as a near-identity transformation, PFS preserves performance while improving robustness under diverse camera and LiDAR corruptions. Evaluations on the nuScenes benchmark demonstrate that PFS achieves state-of-the-art results in several failure modes, notably improving camera dropout robustness by +1.2% and low-light performance by +4.4% mAP while maintaining a lightweight footprint of only 3.3 M parameters.

AI.tldr: PFS is a lightweight, plug-in module that refines intermediate BEV features in camera–LiDAR detectors via near-identity residual corrections, stabilizing statistics and suppressing corrupted regions to improve robustness under domain shift and sensor failures, yielding SOTA robustness gains on nuScenes with only 3.3M params.
AI.motivation: BEV fusion detectors suffer large performance drops under domain shifts (e.g., lighting) and sensor degradations/failures (e.g., camera dropout). Existing robustness methods often require modifying fusion architectures or retraining specialized models, making them hard to integrate in deployed systems. A backward-compatible, low-overhead solution that strengthens robustness without reengineering the detector is needed.
AI.method: Introduce a Post Fusion Stabilizer (PFS) that operates after modality fusion on the intermediate BEV feature map. It outputs a refined BEV map for the original detection head by (1) stabilizing feature statistics under shift, (2) suppressing spatial regions affected by sensor degradation, and (3) adaptively restoring weakened cues via residual (near-identity) correction, preserving nominal performance while improving robustness.
AI.result: On nuScenes, PFS achieves state-of-the-art robustness in multiple failure modes, notably +1.2% mAP under camera dropout and +4.4% mAP in low-light conditions, while adding only 3.3M parameters and leaving the base detector architecture intact.
AI.conclusion: PFS is a practical, drop-in refinement module for deployed BEV fusion detectors that enhances robustness to domain shift and sensor corruptions with minimal compute/parameter overhead and without sacrificing clean-condition performance.

title: NOTAI.AI: Explainable Detection of Machine-Generated Text via Curvature and Feature Attribution
summary: We present NOTAI.AI, an explainable framework for machine-generated text detection that extends Fast-DetectGPT by integrating curvature-based signals with neural and stylometric features in a supervised setting. The system combines 17 interpretable features, including Conditional Probability Curvature, ModernBERT detector score, readability metrics, and stylometric cues, within a gradient-boosted tree (XGBoost) meta-classifier to determine whether a text is human- or AI-generated. Furthermore, NOTAI.AI applies Shapley Additive Explanations (SHAP) to provide both local and global feature-level attribution. These attributions are further translated into structured natural-language rationales through an LLM-based explanation layer, which enables user-facing interpretability. The system is deployed as an interactive web application that supports real-time analysis, visual feature inspection, and structured evidence presentation. A web interface allows users to input text and inspect how neural and statistical signals influence the final decision. The source code and demo video are publicly available to support reproducibility.

AI.tldr: NOTAI.AI is an explainable detector of machine‑generated text that extends Fast‑DetectGPT by fusing curvature‑based, neural, readability, and stylometric features into an XGBoost classifier, with SHAP attributions and LLM‑generated rationales, delivered via an interactive web app.
AI.motivation: Improve reliability and transparency in AI‑text detection: prior detectors can be black‑box and/or single‑signal (e.g., only likelihood/curvature). The authors aim to provide an interpretable, supervised, multi‑feature system that users can inspect and trust.
AI.method: Construct 17 interpretable features (e.g., Conditional Probability Curvature from Fast‑DetectGPT, ModernBERT detector score, readability indices, stylometric cues). Train a gradient‑boosted tree (XGBoost) meta‑classifier to classify human vs AI text. Use SHAP for local/global feature attribution and an LLM layer to convert attributions into structured natural‑language rationales. Deploy as a real‑time interactive web application for analysis and visualization. Code and demo released.
AI.result: A working framework and web app that provide real‑time detection with per‑feature explanations and user‑facing rationales; public code/demo for reproducibility. The abstract does not report quantitative benchmarks or accuracy metrics.
AI.conclusion: A supervised, interpretable ensemble that combines curvature‑based, neural, and stylometric signals—augmented by SHAP and LLM rationales—can deliver user‑friendly, transparent machine‑text detection; the system is available as an interactive, reproducible tool, though empirical performance evidence is not detailed in the abstract.

title: Reasoning Models Struggle to Control their Chains of Thought
summary: Chain-of-thought (CoT) monitoring is a promising tool for detecting misbehaviors and understanding the motivations of modern reasoning models. However, if models can control what they verbalize in their CoT, it could undermine CoT monitorability. To measure this undesirable capability -- CoT controllability -- we introduce the CoT-Control evaluation suite, which includes tasks that require models to solve problems while adhering to CoT instructions, e.g., reasoning about a genetics question without using the word 'chromosome'. We show that reasoning models possess significantly lower CoT controllability than output controllability; for instance, Claude Sonnet 4.5 can control its CoT only 2.7% of the time but 61.9% when controlling its final output. We also find that CoT controllability is higher for larger models and decreases with more RL training, test-time compute, and increased problem difficulty. CoT controllability failures extend even to situations in which models are given incentives (as opposed to direct requests) to evade CoT monitors, although models exhibit slightly higher controllability when they are told they are being monitored. Similarly, eliciting controllability by adversarially optimizing prompts does not meaningfully increase controllability. Our results leave us cautiously optimistic that CoT controllability is currently unlikely to be a failure mode of CoT monitorability. However, the mechanism behind low controllability is not well understood. Given its importance for maintaining CoT monitorability, we recommend that frontier labs track CoT controllability in future models.

AI.tldr: They introduce a benchmark showing that current reasoning models can rarely control what they write in their chain-of-thought (CoT), even though they can control final answers much more easily.
AI.motivation: If models can strategically edit or hide their reasoning, CoT monitoring becomes unreliable for detecting misbehavior and understanding motivations. The paper asks: can models obey instructions that constrain their internal/explained reasoning while still solving tasks?
AI.method: Build CoT-Control, an evaluation suite where tasks must be solved while following explicit CoT constraints (e.g., avoid specific words or reasoning routes). Measure success rates across model sizes, RL training levels, test-time compute, and task difficulty; compare to controlling final outputs; test incentives to evade monitoring and adversarial prompt optimization.
AI.result: Models exhibit very low CoT controllability versus output controllability (e.g., Claude Sonnet 4.5: 2.7% CoT vs 61.9% output). CoT controllability increases with model size but decreases with more RL fine-tuning, more test-time compute, and harder problems. Incentive-based evasion and adversarial prompt search do not substantially raise controllability; being told that monitoring occurs yields slight improvements.
AI.conclusion: Today’s models are unlikely to subvert CoT monitoring by precisely controlling their reasoning text, though the underlying cause of low controllability remains unclear. The authors recommend tracking CoT controllability in future models to preserve CoT-based safety tooling.

title: Rethinking Concept Bottleneck Models: From Pitfalls to Solutions
summary: Concept Bottleneck Models (CBMs) ground predictions in human-understandable concepts but face fundamental limitations: the absence of a metric to pre-evaluate concept relevance, the "linearity problem" causing recent CBMs to bypass the concept bottleneck entirely, an accuracy gap compared to opaque models, and finally the lack of systematic study on the impact of different visual backbones and VLMs. We introduce CBM-Suite, a methodological framework to systematically addresses these challenges. First, we propose an entropy-based metric to quantify the intrinsic suitability of a concept set for a given dataset. Second, we resolve the linearity problem by inserting a non-linear layer between concept activations and the classifier, which ensures that model accuracy faithfully reflects concept relevance. Third, we narrow the accuracy gap by leveraging a distillation loss guided by a linear teacher probe. Finally, we provide comprehensive analyses on how different vision encoders, vision-language models, and concept sets interact to influence accuracy and interpretability in CBMs. Extensive evaluations show that CBM-Suite yields more accurate models and provides insights for improving concept-based interpretability.

AI.tldr: CBM-Suite tackles key shortcomings of Concept Bottleneck Models by introducing an entropy-based metric to pre-assess concept-set suitability, adding a non-linear head to prevent linear bypass of the bottleneck, using distillation from a linear teacher to narrow the accuracy gap to opaque models, and systematically studying how vision encoders, VLMs, and concept sets affect accuracy and interpretability—yielding more accurate, more faithful CBMs.
AI.motivation: CBMs promise human-understandable predictions but suffer from: no principled way to judge if a concept set is adequate for a dataset; a linearity issue that lets models sidestep the bottleneck; an accuracy deficit versus black-box models; and little understanding of how backbone choice and VLMs interact with concept sets.
AI.method: CBM-Suite proposes: (1) an entropy-based suitability metric for concept sets relative to a dataset; (2) inserting a non-linear layer between concept activations and the classifier to ensure performance tracks concept relevance rather than linear shortcutting; (3) a distillation loss guided by a linear teacher probe to improve accuracy while maintaining concept alignment; and (4) comprehensive comparative analyses across vision encoders, VLMs, and concept sets.
AI.result: Across extensive evaluations, CBM-Suite produces more accurate CBMs, mitigates the linear bypass problem, shows that the entropy metric predicts concept-set adequacy, and reveals interactions between encoder choice, VLM grounding, and concept sets that jointly shape accuracy and interpretability.
AI.conclusion: A practical framework and training recipe for more reliable concept-based interpretability: pre-evaluate concepts, train with a non-linear head, leverage linear-teacher distillation, and choose backbones/VLMs informed by the provided analyses.

title: Safer Reasoning Traces: Measuring and Mitigating Chain-of-Thought Leakage in LLMs
summary: Chain-of-Thought (CoT) prompting improves LLM reasoning but can increase privacy risk by resurfacing personally identifiable information (PII) from the prompt into reasoning traces and outputs, even under policies that instruct the model not to restate PII. We study such direct, inference-time PII leakage using a model-agnostic framework that (i) defines leakage as risk-weighted, token-level events across 11 PII types, (ii) traces leakage curves as a function of the allowed CoT budget, and (iii) compares open- and closed-source model families on a structured PII dataset with a hierarchical risk taxonomy. We find that CoT consistently elevates leakage, especially for high-risk categories, and that leakage is strongly family- and budget-dependent. Increasing the reasoning budget can either amplify or attenuate leakage depending on the base model. We then benchmark lightweight inference-time gatekeepers: a rule-based detector, a TF-IDF + logistic regression classifier, a GLiNER-based NER model, and an LLM-as-judge, using risk-weighted F1, Macro-F1, and recall. No single method dominates across models or budgets, motivating hybrid, style-adaptive gatekeeping policies that balance utility and risk under a common, reproducible protocol.

AI.tldr: Chain-of-Thought prompting increases inference-time PII leakage in LLMs. The authors define a risk-weighted, token-level leakage metric across 11 PII types, map leakage as a function of CoT budget, compare open/closed models, and benchmark lightweight gatekeepers. Leakage rises—especially for high-risk PII—and depends strongly on model family and CoT length; no single detector works best, motivating hybrid, adaptive gatekeeping policies.
AI.motivation: While CoT improves reasoning, it can inadvertently restate sensitive user information in intermediate traces and outputs, even when instructed not to. The field lacks a standardized, model-agnostic way to quantify and compare this inference-time PII leakage and to evaluate practical mitigations.
AI.method: Propose a model-agnostic framework that: (i) defines leakage as risk-weighted token-level events over 11 PII categories with a hierarchical risk taxonomy; (ii) traces leakage curves versus allowed CoT (reasoning) budget; (iii) evaluates multiple model families (open and closed) on a structured PII dataset; and (iv) benchmarks inference-time gatekeepers—rule-based, TF-IDF + logistic regression, a GLiNER-based NER, and an LLM-as-judge—using risk-weighted F1, Macro-F1, and recall.
AI.result: CoT consistently elevates PII leakage, particularly for high-risk categories. Leakage magnitude varies by model family and the allocated reasoning budget; increasing the budget can either increase or decrease leakage depending on the base model. Among gatekeepers, no single method dominates across models or budgets under risk-weighted F1, Macro-F1, and recall.
AI.conclusion: PII leakage due to CoT is real, model- and budget-dependent, and not solved by a single detector. Practitioners should adopt hybrid, style-adaptive, budget-aware gatekeeping policies within a reproducible protocol to balance reasoning utility and privacy risk.

title: Evolving Medical Imaging Agents via Experience-driven Self-skill Discovery
summary: Clinical image interpretation is inherently multi-step and tool-centric: clinicians iteratively combine visual evidence with patient context, quantify findings, and refine their decisions through a sequence of specialized procedures. While LLM-based agents promise to orchestrate such heterogeneous medical tools, existing systems treat tool sets and invocation strategies as static after deployment. This design is brittle under real-world domain shifts, across tasks, and evolving diagnostic requirements, where predefined tool chains frequently degrade and demand costly manual re-design. We propose MACRO, a self-evolving, experience-augmented medical agent that shifts from static tool composition to experience-driven tool discovery. From verified execution trajectories, the agent autonomously identifies recurring effective multi-step tool sequences, synthesizes them into reusable composite tools, and registers these as new high-level primitives that continuously expand its behavioral repertoire. A lightweight image-feature memory grounds tool selection in a visual-clinical context, while a GRPO-like training loop reinforces reliable invocation of discovered composites, enabling closed-loop self-improvement with minimal supervision. Extensive experiments across diverse medical imaging datasets and tasks demonstrate that autonomous composite tool discovery consistently improves multi-step orchestration accuracy and cross-domain generalization over strong baselines and recent state-of-the-art agentic methods, bridging the gap between brittle static tool use and adaptive, context-aware clinical AI assistance. Code will be available upon acceptance.

AI.tldr: MACRO is a self-evolving, experience-augmented medical agent that mines verified execution trajectories to discover recurring multi-step tool chains, composes them into reusable “composite tools,” and reinforces their use with a GRPO-like loop, improving multi-step orchestration accuracy and cross-domain generalization in clinical imaging tasks.
AI.motivation: Static, predefined tool chains in LLM-based medical agents are brittle under domain shift, varied tasks, and evolving diagnostic needs, requiring costly manual redesign. The goal is to create an agent that adaptively discovers and reuses effective procedures from experience to remain robust and efficient in real-world clinical settings.
AI.method: From verified trajectories, the agent identifies frequent successful multi-step tool sequences, synthesizes them into higher-level composite tools, and registers them as new primitives. A lightweight image-feature memory conditions tool selection on visual-clinical context. A GRPO-like training loop reinforces reliable invocation, enabling closed-loop self-improvement with minimal supervision.
AI.result: Across diverse medical imaging datasets and tasks, autonomous composite tool discovery yields consistent gains in multi-step orchestration accuracy and cross-domain generalization over strong baselines and recent state-of-the-art agentic methods.
AI.conclusion: Experience-driven composite tool discovery, coupled with contextual memory and reinforcement-style finetuning, transitions medical agents from brittle static pipelines to adaptive, context-aware clinical assistants. MACRO narrows the gap toward robust, evolvable tool orchestration; code to be released upon acceptance.

title: Making Reconstruction FID Predictive of Diffusion Generation FID
summary: It is well known that the reconstruction FID (rFID) of a VAE is poorly correlated with the generation FID (gFID) of a latent diffusion model. We propose interpolated FID (iFID), a simple variant of rFID that exhibits a strong correlation with gFID. Specifically, for each element in the dataset, we retrieve its nearest neighbor (NN) in the latent space and interpolate their latent representations. We then decode the interpolated latent and compute the FID between the decoded samples and the original dataset. Additionally, we refine the claim that rFID correlates poorly with gFID, by showing that rFID correlates with sample quality in the diffusion refinement phase, whereas iFID correlates with sample quality in the diffusion navigation phase. Furthermore, we provide an explanation for why iFID correlates well with gFID, and why reconstruction metrics are negatively correlated with gFID, by connecting to results in the diffusion generalization and hallucination. Empirically, iFID is the first metric to demonstrate a strong correlation with diffusion gFID, achieving Pearson linear and Spearman rank correlations approximately 0.85. The source code is provided in https://github.com/tongdaxu/Making-rFID-Predictive-of-Diffusion-gFID.

AI.tldr: Introduces interpolated FID (iFID)—FID between real data and decodes of latent-space interpolations with nearest neighbors—which tracks diffusion models’ generation FID (gFID) strongly (~0.85 Pearson/Spearman), unlike standard reconstruction FID (rFID). It disentangles phases of diffusion (navigation vs refinement) to explain why rFID and iFID correlate with different aspects of sample quality; connects to generalization/hallucination theory; code released.
AI.motivation: rFID, a common VAE/autoencoder diagnostic, fails to predict the generation quality (gFID) of latent diffusion models that rely on these autoencoders. Practitioners need a simple, early, and inexpensive proxy for gFID to select, train, and debug VAEs/LDMS. A clearer understanding of which autoencoder properties matter for diffusion (manifold navigation vs local refinement) is also lacking.
AI.method: Define iFID: for each dataset sample x, encode z=E(x); find its nearest neighbor z′ in the latent space among encodings of the dataset; interpolate z_t=(1−t)z + t z′ (fixed t, e.g., 0.5); decode y=D(z_t). Compute FID between the set {y} and the original dataset, yielding iFID. Evaluate correlations of iFID and rFID with gFID across models/training and across diffusion phases. Provide theoretical/empirical rationale linking iFID’s behavior to diffusion generalization and hallucination phenomena, and analyze why reconstruction metrics can be negatively correlated with gFID.
AI.result: Empirically, iFID shows strong linear and rank correlations with gFID (≈0.85 Pearson and Spearman), outperforming rFID. rFID aligns with quality changes in the diffusion refinement phase, while iFID aligns with quality in the navigation phase. Provides explanatory account for iFID’s positive correlation with gFID and for reconstruction metrics’ negative correlation with gFID. Public code is available.
AI.conclusion: iFID is a simple, first-of-its-kind proxy that reliably predicts diffusion generation quality from autoencoder behavior by probing interpolative manifold coverage rather than exact reconstruction. It clarifies which autoencoder properties matter for diffusion (navigation vs refinement) and cautions against relying on reconstruction metrics when optimizing for gFID. iFID can guide model selection and training for latent diffusion systems.

title: The Fragility Of Moral Judgment In Large Language Models
summary:
People increasingly use large language models (LLMs) for everyday moral and interpersonal guidance, yet these systems cannot interrogate missing context and judge dilemmas as presented. We introduce a perturbation framework for testing the stability and manipulability of LLM moral judgments while holding the underlying moral conflict constant. Using 2,939 dilemmas from r/AmItheAsshole (January-March 2025), we generate three families of content perturbations: surface edits (lexical/structural noise), point-of-view shifts (voice and stance neutralization), and persuasion cues (self-positioning, social proof, pattern admissions, victim framing). We also vary the evaluation protocol (output ordering, instruction placement, and unstructured prompting). We evaluated all variants with four models (GPT-4.1, Claude 3.7 Sonnet, DeepSeek V3, Qwen2.5-72B) (N=129,156 judgments).
  Surface perturbations produce low flip rates (7.5%), largely within the self-consistency noise floor (4-13%), whereas point-of-view shifts induce substantially higher instability (24.3%). A large subset of dilemmas (37.9%) is robust to surface noise yet flips under perspective changes, indicating that models condition on narrative voice as a pragmatic cue. Instability concentrates in morally ambiguous cases; scenarios where no party is assigned blame are most susceptible. Persuasion perturbations yield systematic directional shifts. Protocol choices dominate all other factors: agreement between structured protocols is only 67.6% (kappa=0.55), and only 35.7% of model-scenario units match across all three protocols. These results show that LLM moral judgments are co-produced by narrative form and task scaffolding, raising reproducibility and equity concerns when outcomes depend on presentation skill rather than moral substance.

AI.tldr: LLM moral judgments are highly sensitive to narrative perspective and protocol design—even when the underlying moral conflict is unchanged—while simple surface edits matter little; this raises serious reproducibility and fairness risks for advice-giving systems.
AI.motivation: People turn to LLMs for moral and interpersonal advice, but current systems accept problems as phrased and may be manipulable by wording, stance, or prompting. The paper seeks a controlled way to assess stability and susceptibility to manipulation while holding the core dilemma constant.
AI.method: Build a perturbation framework over 2,939 r/AmItheAsshole dilemmas (Jan–Mar 2025). Generate three perturbation families: (1) surface edits (lexical/structural noise), (2) point-of-view shifts (voice and stance neutralization), (3) persuasion cues (self-positioning, social proof, pattern admissions, victim framing). Vary evaluation protocols (output ordering, instruction placement, unstructured prompting). Evaluate four LLMs (GPT-4.1, Claude 3.7 Sonnet, DeepSeek V3, Qwen2.5-72B) across all variants, yielding 129,156 judgments. Measure flip rates and cross-protocol agreement.
AI.result: Surface perturbations yield low flip rates (7.5%), comparable to a self-consistency noise floor (4–13%). Point-of-view shifts cause much higher instability (24.3%). 37.9% of dilemmas remain stable to surface noise yet flip under perspective changes, indicating conditioning on narrative voice. Instability concentrates in morally ambiguous cases (e.g., NAH). Persuasion cues drive systematic directional shifts. Protocol design dominates: agreement between structured protocols is 67.6% (κ=0.55), and only 35.7% of model–scenario units match across all three protocols.
AI.conclusion: LLM moral judgments are co-produced by narrative form and task scaffolding, not solely by moral content. This undermines reproducibility and equity, as outcomes hinge on presentation skill. Standardized protocols, perspective-neutral prompting, and robustness checks are needed before deploying LLMs for moral or interpersonal guidance.

title: The World Won't Stay Still: Programmable Evolution for Agent Benchmarks
summary: LLM-powered agents fulfill user requests by interacting with environments, querying data, and invoking tools in a multi-turn process. Yet, most existing benchmarks assume static environments with fixed schemas and toolsets, neglecting the evolutionary nature of the real world and agents' robustness to environmental changes. In this paper, we study a crucial problem: how to evolve the agent environment in a scalable and controllable way, thereby better evaluating agents' adaptability to real-world dynamics. We propose ProEvolve, a graph-based framework that makes environment evolution programmable. At its core, a typed relational graph provides a unified, explicit representation of the environment: data, tools, and schema. Under this formalism, adding, removing, or modifying capabilities are expressed as graph transformations that coherently propagate updates across tools, schemas, and data access. Building on this, ProEvolve can (1) program the evolutionary dynamics as graph transformations to generate environments automatically, and (2) instantiate task sandboxes via subgraph sampling and programming. We validate ProEvolve by evolving a single environment into 200 environments and 3,000 task sandboxes, and benchmark representative agents accordingly.

AI.tldr: ProEvolve is a graph-based framework that makes agent environment evolution programmable, enabling automatic generation of dynamic environments and task sandboxes to test LLM agents’ adaptability beyond static benchmarks.
AI.motivation: Current LLM-agent benchmarks largely assume fixed schemas and toolsets, ignoring that real-world data, tools, and APIs change over time. This gap makes it hard to evaluate agents’ robustness and adaptability to evolving environments.
AI.method: Model the environment (data, tools, schemas) as a typed relational graph. Evolutionary changes—adding/removing/modifying capabilities—are formalized as graph transformations that propagate coherently across tools, schemas, and data access. Use this to (1) program evolutionary dynamics to auto-generate evolved environments and (2) sample/program subgraphs to instantiate task sandboxes.
AI.result: From a single seed environment, the authors evolved 200 distinct environments and 3,000 task sandboxes and used them to benchmark representative agents. The abstract highlights scalability and feasibility; detailed performance metrics are not provided.
AI.conclusion: Programmable environment evolution via graph transformations offers a scalable, controlled way to evaluate agent adaptability to real-world dynamics. ProEvolve operationalizes dynamic benchmarking and could reveal robustness gaps that static tests miss.

title: When Rubrics Fail: Error Enumeration as Reward in Reference-Free RL Post-Training for Virtual Try-On
summary: Reinforcement learning with verifiable rewards (RLVR) and Rubrics as Rewards (RaR) have driven strong gains in domains with clear correctness signals and even in subjective domains by synthesizing evaluation criteria from ideal reference answers. But many real-world tasks admit multiple valid outputs and lack the single ideal answer that rubric generation depends on. We identify this reference-free setting as a gap in current post-training methods and propose Implicit Error Counting (IEC) to fill it. Instead of checking what a response gets right against a rubric, IEC enumerates what it gets wrong, applying severity-weighted scores across task-relevant axes and converting them into calibrated per-aspect rewards. We show that naïve explicit enumeration is too noisy for stable optimization, and that two design choices: implicit score emission and group calibration are necessary to make error counting a reliable reward. As a case study, we validate IEC on virtual try-on (VTO), a domain that is simultaneously too constrained for holistic scoring and too permissive for rubric-based evaluation: subtle garment errors are unacceptable, yet many output variations are correct. We introduce Cascaded Error Counting (CEC) as an evaluation metric, which tracks human preferences well (60% top-1 vs. 30% others), and curate Mismatch-DressCode (MDressBench), a benchmark with maximal attribute mismatch to stress-test reward designs. On MDressBench, IEC outperforms RaR across all metrics (CEC: 5.31 vs. 5.60 on flat references; 5.20 vs. 5.53 on non-flat). On VITON-HD and DressCode, IEC matches or surpasses six baselines on 6 of 8 perceptual metrics. These results suggest that when ideal answers are unavailable, counting errors provide a stronger signal than constructing rubrics.

AI.tldr: Introduce Implicit Error Counting (IEC), a reference-free reward for RL that weights and calibrates detected errors per aspect; validated in virtual try-on with a new CEC metric and MDressBench, IEC outperforms rubric-based RaR and strong baselines when no single ideal answer exists.
AI.motivation: Existing post-training methods like RLVR and RaR rely on either clear correctness signals or rubrics distilled from ideal references. Many real tasks have multiple valid outputs and lack a unique ground-truth answer (e.g., virtual try-on), making rubric construction brittle and holistic scoring too coarse. A robust, reference-free reward is needed.
AI.method: Propose IEC: (1) enumerate what a response gets wrong along task-relevant axes; (2) assign severity-weighted, per-aspect scores; (3) aggregate into calibrated rewards using two key stabilizers—implicit score emission (avoid noisy explicit enumeration) and group calibration. Introduce Cascaded Error Counting (CEC) as an evaluation metric aligned with human preferences and curate MDressBench to stress-test reward designs in VTO with maximal attribute mismatch. Train/evaluate on VITON-HD and DressCode.
AI.result: CEC aligns with human preferences (60% top-1 vs ~30% for others). On MDressBench, IEC beats RaR on all metrics (CEC: 5.31 vs 5.60 on flat refs; 5.20 vs 5.53 on non-flat). On VITON-HD and DressCode, IEC matches or exceeds six baselines on 6/8 perceptual metrics.
AI.conclusion: When ideal references are absent and outputs are many-to-one valid, counting and calibrating errors yields a more reliable optimization signal than rubric synthesis. IEC’s implicit scoring and group calibration are necessary for stability, and the approach generalizes across VTO benchmarks.

title: FreeTxt-Vi: A Benchmarked Vietnamese-English Toolkit for Segmentation, Sentiment, and Summarisation
summary: FreeTxt-Vi is a free and open source web based toolkit for creating and analysing bilingual Vietnamese English text collections. Positioned at the intersection of corpus linguistics and natural language processing NLP it enables users to build explore and interpret free text data without requiring programming expertise. The system combines corpus analysis features such as concordancing keyword analysis word relation exploration and interactive visualisation with transformer based NLP components for sentiment analysis and summarisation. A key contribution of this work is the design of a unified bilingual NLP pipeline that integrates a hybrid VnCoreNLP and Byte Pair Encoding BPE segmentation strategy a fine tuned TabularisAI sentiment classifier and a fine tuned Qwen2.5 model for abstractive summarisation. Unlike existing text analysis platforms FreeTxt Vi is evaluated as a set of language processing components. We conduct a three part evaluation covering segmentation sentiment analysis and summarisation and show that our approach achieves competitive or superior performance compared to widely used baselines in both Vietnamese and English. By reducing technical barriers to multilingual text analysis FreeTxt Vi supports reproducible research and promotes the development of language resources for Vietnamese a widely spoken but underrepresented language in NLP. The toolkit is applicable to domains including education digital humanities cultural heritage and the social sciences where qualitative text data are common but often difficult to process at scale.

AI.tldr: FreeTxt‑Vi is an open‑source web toolkit for building and analyzing bilingual Vietnamese–English corpora, combining classic corpus‑linguistics tools with transformer‑based NLP (sentiment and abstractive summarization) via a unified bilingual pipeline, and it performs competitively or better than common baselines.
AI.motivation: Lower the technical barrier for multilingual (VN–EN) free‑text analysis, support reproducible research, and address the underrepresentation of Vietnamese in NLP by providing an integrated, easy‑to‑use platform that bridges corpus linguistics and modern NLP. Also, evaluate components rigorously rather than only the end system.
AI.method: Design a web toolkit that merges corpus analysis (concordancing, keywording, word‑relation exploration, interactive visualizations) with a unified bilingual NLP pipeline: a hybrid segmentation strategy combining VnCoreNLP and Byte Pair Encoding (BPE), a fine‑tuned TabularisAI sentiment classifier, and a fine‑tuned Qwen2.5 model for abstractive summarization. Conduct a three‑part evaluation on segmentation, sentiment, and summarization against widely used baselines in Vietnamese and English.
AI.result: Across segmentation, sentiment classification, and summarization, the proposed components achieve competitive or superior performance to standard baselines in both Vietnamese and English.
AI.conclusion: FreeTxt‑Vi makes multilingual corpus analysis accessible without programming, advances Vietnamese NLP resource development, and supports reproducible research. It is broadly applicable to domains like education, digital humanities, cultural heritage, and the social sciences for scaling qualitative text analysis.

title: DeepFact: Co-Evolving Benchmarks and Agents for Deep Research Factuality
summary: Search-augmented LLM agents can produce deep research reports (DRRs), but verifying claim-level factuality remains challenging. Existing fact-checkers are primarily designed for general-domain, factoid-style atomic claims, and there is no benchmark to test whether such verifiers transfer to DRRs. Yet building such a benchmark is itself difficult. We first show that static expert-labeled benchmarks are brittle in this setting: in a controlled study with PhD-level specialists, unassisted experts achieve only 60.8% accuracy on a hidden micro-gold set of verifiable claims. We propose Evolving Benchmarking via Audit-then-Score (AtS), where benchmark labels and rationales are explicitly revisable: when a verifier disagrees with the current benchmark, it must submit evidence; an auditor adjudicates the dispute; and accepted revisions update the benchmark before models are scored. Across four AtS rounds, expert micro-gold accuracy rises to 90.9%, indicating experts are substantially more reliable as auditors than as one-shot labelers. We instantiate AtS as DeepFact-Bench, a versioned DRR factuality benchmark with auditable rationales, and DeepFact-Eval, a document-level verification agent (with a grouped lite variant) that outperforms existing verifiers on DeepFact-Bench and transfers well to external factuality datasets.

AI.tldr: They introduce an evolving, audit-driven benchmarking framework for verifying factual claims in deep research reports (DRRs). Static expert labels are unreliable (60.8% accuracy), but iterative audit-and-revision lifts accuracy to 90.9%. They release DeepFact-Bench (a versioned, auditable DRR factuality benchmark) and DeepFact-Eval (a document-level verifier) that outperform prior methods and transfer well.
AI.motivation: Search-augmented LLMs can write long, complex DRRs, but existing fact-checkers target simple, atomic claims and lack suitable benchmarks for DRR-scale verification. Moreover, building a reliable static benchmark is hard because even domain experts are inconsistent when labeling in one pass.
AI.method: Propose Audit-then-Score (AtS): a dynamic benchmark process where labels and rationales are revisable. When a verifier disputes a label, it must provide evidence; an auditor adjudicates; accepted changes update the benchmark before scoring. Instantiate AtS as DeepFact-Bench (versioned benchmark with auditable rationales) and build DeepFact-Eval, a document-level verification agent with a grouped lite variant.
AI.result: In a controlled study, unassisted PhD-level experts achieve only 60.8% accuracy on a hidden micro-gold set. After four AtS rounds, expert micro-gold accuracy increases to 90.9%. DeepFact-Eval outperforms existing verifiers on DeepFact-Bench and shows strong transfer to external factuality datasets.
AI.conclusion: Static expert-labeled benchmarks for complex DRR factuality are brittle. An evolving, audit-based process substantially improves label reliability and enables fairer evaluation. The released DeepFact-Bench and DeepFact-Eval advance claim-level verification for DRRs and generalize beyond the benchmark.

title: Keeping the Evidence Chain: Semantic Evidence Allocation for Training-Free Token Pruning in Video Temporal Grounding
summary: Video Temporal Grounding (VTG) localizes the temporal boundaries of a query-relevant moment in long, untrimmed videos, making video-language-model (VLM) pipelines prohibitively expensive. While recent training-free visual token pruning has shown success in video question answering, naively applying these objectives to VTG often causes drastic degradation, as VTG crucially depends on boundary-sensitive evidence and cross-frame reasoning chains. We therefore identify two VTG-specific pruning principles: Evidence Retention (ER), which keeps query-critical patches especially around event boundaries, and Connectivity Strength (CS), which preserves token-level cross-frame connectivity for long-range evidence aggregation. Building on these insights, we propose SemVID, a training-free pruning framework that constructs a compact yet coherent token subset with complementary semantic roles. SemVID first allocates per-frame token budgets by balancing query relevance and inter-frame variation to avoid over-pruned segments, and then selects three types of tokens: object tokens for diverse query-critical evidence, motion tokens to capture meaningful transitions and serve as cross-frame relays, and a small set of context tokens for scene continuity. Extensive experiments on VTG benchmarks show that SemVID achieves a strong accuracy-efficiency trade-off, retaining up to 95.4% mIoU with only 12.5% visual tokens and delivering up to a 5.8x prefill speedup, consistently outperforming prior methods under the same budgets.

AI.tldr: SemVID is a training‑free visual token pruning framework for Video Temporal Grounding that preserves boundary evidence and cross‑frame connectivity by selecting complementary object, motion, and context tokens under per‑frame budgets; it keeps ~95.4% mIoU with only 12.5% tokens and delivers up to 5.8× prefill speedups, outperforming prior pruning under equal budgets.
AI.motivation: Running video-language models on long, untrimmed videos is computationally expensive. Naive training-free pruning that works for VQA fails for VTG because grounding needs boundary-sensitive cues and long-range cross-frame reasoning. The paper seeks a pruning strategy tailored to VTG that retains critical evidence and temporal connectivity while cutting cost.
AI.method: Derive two VTG-specific pruning principles: Evidence Retention (keep query-critical patches, especially near event boundaries) and Connectivity Strength (maintain token-level cross-frame links for long-range aggregation). SemVID then (1) allocates per-frame token budgets by balancing query relevance with inter-frame variation to avoid over-pruning segments; (2) selects three complementary token types—object tokens for diverse query-relevant evidence, motion tokens to capture transitions and act as cross-frame relays, and a small set of context tokens for scene continuity. Entirely training-free.
AI.result: On VTG benchmarks, SemVID achieves a superior accuracy–efficiency trade-off: up to 95.4% mIoU with just 12.5% of visual tokens and up to a 5.8× prefill speedup, consistently surpassing prior pruning methods under the same token budgets.
AI.conclusion: Task-aware, training-free pruning that preserves evidence near temporal boundaries and maintains cross-frame connectivity enables compact yet coherent token subsets for VTG, substantially reducing compute without large accuracy loss. The results highlight the value of semantic role–aware token selection for video–language grounding.

title: Towards Robust Retrieval-Augmented Generation Based on Knowledge Graph: A Comparative Analysis
comment: The paper is 6 pages long and includes 5 figures and 3 tables illustrating the experimental framework and results. It is submitted to the IEEE International Conference on Systems, Man, and Cybernetics (SMC 2025) and studies improving the robustness of Retrieval-Augmented Generation systems using knowledge graph based GraphRAG approaches
summary: Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) was introduced to enhance the capabilities of Large Language Models (LLMs) beyond their encoded prior knowledge. This is achieved by providing LLMs with an external source of knowledge, which helps reduce factual hallucinations and enables access to new information not available during pretraining. However, inconsistent retrieved information can negatively affect LLM responses. The Retrieval-Augmented Generation Benchmark (RGB) was introduced to evaluate the robustness of RAG systems under such conditions. In this work, we use the RGB corpus to evaluate LLMs in four scenarios: noise robustness, information integration, negative rejection, and counterfactual robustness. We perform a comparative analysis between the RGB RAG baseline and GraphRAG, a knowledge graph based retrieval system. We test three GraphRAG customizations to improve robustness. Results show improvements over the RGB baseline and provide insights for designing more reliable RAG systems for real world scenarios.

AI.tldr: They evaluate RAG robustness with the RGB benchmark, comparing a standard RAG baseline to a knowledge‑graph–based GraphRAG with three customizations, and find improved robustness across noisy, conflicting, and counterfactual settings, yielding practical design insights.
AI.motivation: RAG extends LLMs with external knowledge to reduce hallucinations and access fresher information, but inconsistent or misleading retrieved content can degrade answers. There is a need to systematically evaluate and improve robustness of RAG systems under such adverse retrieval conditions.
AI.method: Use the RGB corpus/benchmark to test LLMs under four robustness scenarios: noise robustness, information integration, negative rejection (refusing irrelevant info), and counterfactual robustness. Compare the RGB RAG baseline against GraphRAG (knowledge‑graph retrieval) and evaluate three customizations to GraphRAG intended to boost robustness.
AI.result: GraphRAG variants show improvements over the RGB baseline on the RGB tasks; specific metrics are not reported in the abstract, but gains are claimed across the evaluated scenarios along with qualitative insights for design.
AI.conclusion: Knowledge‑graph–augmented retrieval with targeted customizations can make RAG systems more robust to inconsistent evidence, offering guidance for building more reliable, real‑world RAG pipelines.

title: An Interactive Multi-Agent System for Evaluation of New Product Concepts
comment: 46 pages, 3 figures + This paper proposes an LLM-based multi-agent system (MAS) for automated evaluation of new product concepts, incorporating retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) and cross-functional virtual agents to assess technical and market feasibility
summary: Product concept evaluation is a critical stage that determines strategic resource allocation and project success in enterprises. However, traditional expert-led approaches face limitations such as subjective bias and high time and cost requirements. To support this process, this study proposes an automated approach utilizing a large language model (LLM)-based multi-agent system (MAS). Through a systematic analysis of previous research on product development and team collaboration, this study established two primary evaluation dimensions, namely technical feasibility and market feasibility. The proposed system consists of a team of eight virtual agents representing specialized domains such as R&D and marketing. These agents use retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) and real-time search tools to gather objective evidence and validate concepts through structured deliberations based on the established criteria. The agents were further fine-tuned using professional product review data to enhance their judgment accuracy. A case study involving professional display monitor concepts demonstrated that the system's evaluation rankings were consistent with those of senior industry experts. These results confirm the usability of the proposed multi-agent-based evaluation approach for supporting product development decisions.

AI.tldr: They automate product concept evaluation with an LLM-based multi‑agent system that assesses technical and market feasibility, retrieves evidence, and reaches rankings that match expert judgments in a case study.
AI.motivation: Expert-led concept reviews are slow, costly, and subject to bias, yet they critically shape resource allocation and project success. A more objective, scalable, and timely evaluation method is needed.
AI.method: Design an LLM-based multi-agent system with eight domain-specialized agents (e.g., R&D, marketing). Agents use retrieval-augmented generation and real-time search to collect evidence and deliberate via structured criteria across two dimensions: technical feasibility and market feasibility. The agents are further fine-tuned on professional product review data to improve judgment accuracy.
AI.result: In a case study on professional display monitor concepts, the system’s evaluation rankings aligned with those of senior industry experts, indicating comparable assessment quality.
AI.conclusion: An LLM-driven multi-agent approach is practical for assisting product development decisions, offering consistent, evidence-grounded evaluations that can mitigate bias and reduce time/cost versus traditional expert-only reviews.

title: OWL: A Novel Approach to Machine Perception During Motion
