summary: We introduce a perception-related function, OWL, designed to address the complex challenges of 3D perception during motion. It derives its values directly from two fundamental visual motion cues, with one set of cue values per point per time instant. During motion, two visual motion cues relative to a fixation point emerge: 1) perceived local visual looming of points near the fixation point, and 2) perceived rotation of the rigid object relative to the fixation point. It also expresses the relation between two well-known physical quantities, the relative instantaneous directional range and directional translation in 3D between the camera and any visible 3D point, without explicitly requiring their measurement or prior knowledge of their individual values. OWL offers a unified, analytical time-based approach that enhances and simplifies key perception capabilities, including scaled 3D mapping and camera heading. Simulations demonstrate that OWL achieves geometric constancy of 3D objects over time and enables scaled 3D scene reconstruction from visual motion cues alone. By leveraging direct measurements from raw visual motion image sequences, OWL values can be obtained without prior knowledge of stationary environments, moving objects, or camera motion. This approach employs minimalistic, pixel-based, parallel computations, providing an alternative real-time representation for 3D points in relative motion. OWL bridges the gap between theoretical concepts and practical applications in robotics and autonomous navigation and may unlock new possibilities for real-time decision-making and interaction, potentially serving as a building block for next-generation autonomous systems. This paper offers an alternative perspective on machine perception, with implications that may extend to natural perception and contribute to a better understanding of behavioral psychology and neural functionality.

AI.tldr: OWL is a time-based, per-pixel function computed from two visual motion cues—local looming and rotation around a fixation point—that enables scaled 3D reconstruction and camera heading estimation directly from raw image sequences, without explicitly estimating depth or ego-motion parameters.
AI.motivation: Robust, real-time 3D perception during motion is hard with classical monocular approaches, which often require explicit estimation of depth and full ego-motion, scene stationarity assumptions, and heavy optimization. The paper seeks a simpler, biologically inspired, analytically grounded alternative that works in dynamic settings and runs in real time.
AI.method: Define a perception function (OWL) derived per point and per time instant from two observable visual motion cues relative to a chosen fixation point: (1) perceived local looming (radial expansion) near the fixation and (2) perceived rotation of the rigid object about that fixation. OWL analytically encodes the relation between two physical quantities—the relative instantaneous directional range and directional translation—without needing their separate measurement. Implementation relies on minimalistic, pixel-parallel computations on raw motion image sequences.
AI.result: Simulations indicate OWL maintains geometric constancy of 3D objects over time, recovers scaled 3D scene structure, and yields camera heading from motion cues alone, without prior knowledge of scene stationarity, object motion, or camera motion. The computations are lightweight and real-time capable.
AI.conclusion: OWL provides a unified, time-domain representation of 3D points in relative motion that bridges theoretical vision concepts and practical robotics/autonomy needs. It may serve as a building block for real-time navigation and also offers hypotheses relevant to natural perception and neural processing.

title: Cultural Perspectives and Expectations for Generative AI: A Global Survey Approach
summary: There is a lack of empirical evidence about global attitudes around whether and how GenAI should represent cultures. This paper assesses understandings and beliefs about culture as it relates to GenAI from a large-scale global survey. We gathered data about what culture means to different groups, and about how GenAI should approach the representation of cultural artifacts, concepts, or values. We distill working definitions of culture directly from these communities to build an understanding of its conceptual complexities and how they relate to representations in Generative AI. We survey from across parts of Europe, North and South America, Asia, and Africa. We conclude with a set of recommendations for Culture and GenAI development. These include participatory approaches, prioritizing specific cultural dimensions beyond geography, such as religion and tradition, and a sensitivity framework for addressing cultural ``redlines''.

AI.tldr: Global, large-scale survey on how GenAI should represent cultures; distills community-defined meanings of culture and offers design recommendations: participatory methods, focus on dimensions beyond geography (e.g., religion, tradition), and a sensitivity framework for cultural redlines.
AI.motivation: There is little empirical evidence on worldwide attitudes about cultural representation in Generative AI. Clarifying what different communities mean by “culture” and how they want it represented is necessary to guide responsible GenAI design and deployment.
AI.method: A large-scale cross-regional survey (Europe, North and South America, Asia, Africa) collected perceptions of what culture means to diverse groups and views on how GenAI ought to represent cultural artifacts, concepts, and values. The authors distilled working, community-derived definitions of culture and analyzed their relation to GenAI representations.
AI.result: Community-informed definitions of culture and insights into its conceptual complexity were produced, along with mapped attitudes on GenAI’s role in representing culture. The study yields actionable guidance: adopt participatory approaches, prioritize cultural dimensions beyond geography (e.g., religion, tradition), and implement a sensitivity framework to handle cultural "redlines."
AI.conclusion: Effective cultural representation in GenAI requires engaging affected communities, moving beyond simplistic geographic proxies to salient cultural dimensions, and instituting guardrails for redline topics. These findings provide practical recommendations to inform Culture-and-GenAI development efforts.

title: Agentic LLM Planning via Step-Wise PDDL Simulation: An Empirical Characterisation
summary: Task planning, the problem of sequencing actions to reach a goal from an initial state, is a core capability requirement for autonomous robotic systems. Whether large language models (LLMs) can serve as viable planners alongside classical symbolic methods remains an open question. We present PyPDDLEngine, an open-source Planning Domain Definition Language (PDDL) simulation engine that exposes planning operations as LLM tool calls through a Model Context Protocol (MCP) interface. Rather than committing to a complete action sequence upfront, the LLM acts as an interactive search policy that selects one action at a time, observes each resulting state, and can reset and retry. We evaluate four approaches on 102 International Planning Competition (IPC) Blocksworld instances under a uniform 180-second budget: Fast Downward lama-first and seq-sat-lama-2011 as classical baselines, direct LLM planning (Claude Haiku 4.5), and agentic LLM planning via PyPDDLEngine. Fast Downward achieves 85.3% success. The direct and agentic LLM approaches achieve 63.7% and 66.7%, respectively, a consistent but modest three-percentage-point advantage for the agentic approach at $5.7\times$ higher token cost per solution. Across most co-solved difficulty blocks, both LLM approaches produce shorter plans than seq-sat-lama-2011 despite its iterative quality improvement, a result consistent with training-data recall rather than generalisable planning. These results suggest that agentic gains depend on the nature of environmental feedback. Coding agents benefit from externally grounded signals such as compiler errors and test failures, whereas PDDL step feedback is self-assessed, leaving the agent to evaluate its own progress without external verification.

AI.tldr: They introduce PyPDDLEngine, which lets an LLM interactively plan in PDDL by choosing one action at a time with state feedback. On Blocksworld, this “agentic” LLM is slightly better than direct LLM planning but still trails a classical planner, and its gains are costly and fragile without externally grounded feedback.
AI.motivation: Assess whether LLMs can be viable task planners compared to classical symbolic planners, and whether making LLMs agentic with interactive environment feedback improves planning quality and reliability.
AI.method: Build PyPDDLEngine, an MCP-exposed PDDL simulator enabling LLM tool calls for stepwise planning with observe–act–reset. Compare four approaches on 102 IPC Blocksworld tasks with a 180s cap: Fast Downward (lama-first, seq-sat-lama-2011), direct LLM planning (Claude Haiku 4.5), and agentic LLM using PyPDDLEngine. Measure success rate, plan length, and token cost.
AI.result: Fast Downward achieves 85.3% success. Direct LLM reaches 63.7%, agentic LLM 66.7%—a modest +3% over direct but at 5.7× higher token cost per solution. In co-solved difficulty ranges, LLM plans are often shorter than seq-sat-lama-2011’s, indicative of pattern recall rather than robust planning generalization.
AI.conclusion: LLMs can plan to a degree but lag strong classical planners; making them agentic via PDDL step feedback yields only small improvements and high costs. Agentic gains rely on externally grounded signals (e.g., compilers/tests in coding). In PDDL, feedback is self-assessed, limiting reliability and suggesting the need for richer external verification or hybrid methods.

title: MultiHaystack: Benchmarking Multimodal Retrieval and Reasoning over 40K Images, Videos, and Documents
summary: Multimodal large language models (MLLMs) achieve strong performance on benchmarks that evaluate text, image, or video understanding separately. However, these settings do not assess a critical real-world requirement, which involves retrieving relevant evidence from large, heterogeneous multimodal corpora prior to reasoning. Most existing benchmarks restrict retrieval to small, single-modality candidate sets, substantially simplifying the search space and overstating end-to-end reliability. To address this gap, we introduce MultiHaystack, the first benchmark designed to evaluate both retrieval and reasoning under large-scale, cross-modal conditions. MultiHaystack comprises over 46,000 multimodal retrieval candidates across documents, images, and videos, along with 747 open yet verifiable questions. Each question is grounded in a unique validated evidence item within the retrieval pool, requiring evidence localization across modalities and fine-grained reasoning. In our study, we find that models perform competitively when provided with the corresponding evidence, but their performance drops sharply when required to retrieve that evidence from the full corpus. Additionally, even the strongest retriever, E5-V, achieves only 40.8% Recall@1, while state-of-the-art MLLMs such as GPT-5 experience a significant drop in reasoning accuracy from 80.86% when provided with the corresponding evidence to 51.4% under top-5 retrieval. These results indicate that multimodal retrieval over heterogeneous pools remains a primary bottleneck for MLLMs, positioning MultiHaystack as a valuable testbed that highlights underlying limitations obscured by small-scale evaluations and promotes retrieval-centric advances in multimodal systems.

AI.tldr: MultiHaystack is a new large-scale benchmark that jointly tests multimodal retrieval and reasoning across text, images, and videos, revealing that retrieval over heterogeneous corpora is the main bottleneck for current MLLMs.
AI.motivation: Existing evaluations often assume the correct evidence is provided or restrict retrieval to small, single-modality sets, overstating end-to-end reliability. Real-world use requires finding relevant evidence across large, mixed-modality corpora before reasoning.
AI.method: Construct a benchmark with 46k+ multimodal candidates (documents, images, videos) and 747 open but verifiable questions. Each question maps to a single validated evidence item. Evaluate SOTA retrievers and MLLMs in two settings: (1) evidence given, and (2) evidence must be retrieved from the full heterogeneous pool (e.g., top-k). Metrics include Recall@1 for retrieval and reasoning accuracy with/without retrieved evidence.
AI.result: Models perform well when given the gold evidence but degrade sharply when required to retrieve it. Best retriever (E5-V) attains only 40.8% Recall@1. A leading MLLM (e.g., GPT-5) drops from 80.86% accuracy with gold evidence to 51.4% with top-5 retrieved evidence.
AI.conclusion: End-to-end performance of MLLMs is constrained primarily by multimodal retrieval over heterogeneous corpora. MultiHaystack exposes limitations masked by small-scale or single-modality benchmarks and provides a testbed to drive retrieval-centric improvements in multimodal systems.

title: Structured Multidimensional Representation Learning for Large Language Models
comment: 25 pages, 6 figures. Preprint of a journal submission
summary: Transformer architectures achieve state-of-the-art performance across a wide range of pattern recognition and natural language processing tasks, but their scaling is accompanied by substantial parameter growth and redundancy in the embedding dimension. In this work, we introduce a structured spectral factorization of the embedding space based on the L-product for third-order tensors. By reshaping token representations into spectral tensor slices and performing attention and feed-forward operations in the transform domain, we obtain a Tensor Transformer architecture that decomposes the encoder into p independent spectral sub-transformers while preserving standard Transformer semantics. We prove that the proposed L-Transformer is spectrally equivalent to p parallel Transformers operating on reduceddimensional embeddings, which yields approximately 1/p reduction (up to lower-order terms such as biases and normalization parameters) in encoder parameters under fixed total embedding size. When instantiated with a real-valued Discrete Cosine Transform (DCT), the method remains fully differentiable and compatible with existing training pipelines. Beyond compression, the spectral decomposition introduces an inductive bias over embedding frequencies, enabling slice-dependent frequency scaling that improves generalization. Experiments on IMDB and AG~News show that the proposed model can substantially reduce encoder parameters (up to 75\% for p=4) while maintaining competitive accuracy. On IMDB, the tensorized encoder matches or improves upon the standard baseline under compression, whereas on AG~News at moderate width we observe a small accuracy decrease in exchange for a 4 times encoder reduction; at BERT-base width (d=768), performance returns to parity.

AI.tldr: They restructure Transformer encoders via a spectral tensor factorization (using an L-product/DCT) that splits the embedding into p frequency slices, runs p smaller independent sub-transformers, and cuts encoder parameters by about 1/p while preserving accuracy on text benchmarks.
AI.motivation: Standard Transformers scale parameter counts with embedding width and exhibit redundancy across embedding dimensions; there is a need to compress encoders without sacrificing accuracy and to introduce inductive biases that improve generalization.
AI.method: Reshape token embeddings into third-order tensors and apply a structured spectral factorization via the L-product; perform self-attention and feed-forward layers in the transform (e.g., DCT) domain. This yields p independent spectral sub-transformers operating on reduced-dimensional slices. They prove spectral equivalence to p parallel Transformers and maintain differentiability and compatibility with standard training.
AI.result: On IMDB and AG News, the approach reduces encoder parameters substantially (up to ~75% with p=4). IMDB shows matched or improved accuracy under compression; AG News shows a small accuracy drop at moderate width for 4× reduction, with parity restored at BERT-base width (d=768).
AI.conclusion: Spectral tensorization provides an effective, drop-in compression for Transformer encoders, delivering large parameter savings with minimal or no accuracy loss and adding a useful frequency-based inductive bias.

title: Aggregative Semantics for Quantitative Bipolar Argumentation Frameworks
summary: Formal argumentation is being used increasingly in artificial intelligence as an effective and understandable way to model potentially conflicting pieces of information, called arguments, and identify so-called acceptable arguments depending on a chosen semantics. This paper deals with the specific context of Quantitative Bipolar Argumentation Frameworks (QBAF), where arguments have intrinsic weights and can attack or support each other. In this context, we introduce a novel family of gradual semantics, called aggregative semantics. In order to deal with situations in which attackers and supporters do not play a symmetric role, and in contrast to modular semantics, we propose to aggregate attackers and supporters separately. This leads to a three-stage computation, which consists in computing a global weight for attackers and another for supporters, before aggregating these two values with the intrinsic weight of the argument. We discuss the properties that the three aggregation functions should satisfy depending on the context, as well as their relationships with the classical principles for gradual semantics. This discussion is supported by various simple examples, as well as a final example on which five hundred aggregative semantics are tested and compared, illustrating the range of possible behaviours with aggregative semantics. Decomposing the computation into three distinct and interpretable steps leads to a more parametrisable computation: it keeps the bipolarity one step further than what is done in the literature, and it leads to more understandable gradual semantics.

AI.tldr: Introduces a new family of gradual semantics for Quantitative Bipolar Argumentation Frameworks that aggregates attackers and supporters separately, then combines them with an argument’s intrinsic weight in a three-stage process; formalizes desired properties, relates them to classical principles, and empirically explores 500 instantiations to show flexibility and interpretability.
AI.motivation: Existing QBAF approaches often treat attack and support symmetrically and/or collapse bipolarity too early via modular aggregation, reducing expressiveness and interpretability. Many AI applications need asymmetric, context-sensitive treatment of attacks vs. supports and transparent, parameterizable scoring of arguments.
AI.method: Define “aggregative semantics” with three functions: (1) aggregate all attackers into a global attacker weight; (2) aggregate all supporters into a global supporter weight; (3) combine these two with the argument’s intrinsic weight. Specify axioms/properties these functions should meet under different contexts and analyze compatibility with classical gradual-semantics principles. Validate via simple examples and a comparative test of 500 concrete instantiations.
AI.result: Demonstrates that separate aggregation of attackers and supporters yields a broad range of behaviors; maps which property combinations satisfy or violate classical principles; on a final example, 500 instantiations display diverse, interpretable outcomes that expose trade-offs among design choices.
AI.conclusion: Aggregative semantics preserve bipolar structure longer than modular approaches, enabling more parameterizable and understandable scoring in QBAFs. They generalize prior methods and provide a flexible toolkit to tailor reasoning to domain needs while clarifying the implications of aggregation choices.

title: Interpretable Perception and Reasoning for Audiovisual Geolocation
summary: While recent advances in Multimodal Large Language Models (MLLMs) have improved image-based localization, precise global geolocation remains a formidable challenge due to the inherent ambiguity of visual landscapes and the largely untapped potential of auditory cues. In this paper, we introduce Audiovisual Geolocation, a framework designed to resolve geographic ambiguity through interpretable perception and reasoning. We present AVG, a high-quality global-scale video benchmark for geolocation, comprising 20,000 curated clips across 1,000 distinct locations. To address the complexity of audiovisual geolocation, we propose a three-stage framework: (1) a Perception stage that utilizes a mixture-autoregressive sparse autoencoder to decompose noisy audio into semantically grounded "acoustic atoms"; (2) a Multimodal Reasoning stage that employs an MLLM finetuned via Group Relative Policy Optimization (GRPO) to synthesize these atoms with visual features; and (3) a Precision Prediction stage using Riemannian Flow Matching on the $S^2$ manifold. Our experiments demonstrate that our framework significantly outperforms unimodal baselines. These results entail that interpretable perception of the soundscape provides a critical, orthogonal signal that, when coupled with multimodal reasoning, enables high-precision global localization.

AI.tldr: Introduces a three-stage, interpretable audiovisual framework and a 20k-clip, 1k-location benchmark (AVG) that significantly improves global geolocation by combining learned “acoustic atoms,” MLLM reasoning, and manifold-aware prediction.
AI.motivation: Visual-only geolocation remains ambiguous at global scale, and audio—an informative, complementary cue—has been underutilized. The work seeks to reduce geographic ambiguity with interpretable audio perception fused with visual context and to provide a standardized benchmark.
AI.method: (1) Perception: a mixture-autoregressive sparse autoencoder decomposes noisy audio into semantically meaningful “acoustic atoms.” (2) Multimodal Reasoning: an MLLM, finetuned via Group Relative Policy Optimization (GRPO), fuses these atoms with visual features. (3) Precision Prediction: Riemannian Flow Matching on the S^2 sphere yields precise latitude–longitude estimates. Also releases AVG, a curated global-scale audiovisual dataset (20,000 clips, 1,000 locations).
AI.result: On the AVG benchmark, the proposed pipeline outperforms unimodal (vision-only or audio-only) baselines, achieving higher precision in global localization and offering interpretable audio factors that aid reasoning.
AI.conclusion: Interpretable audio perception provides an orthogonal signal that, when combined with MLLM-based multimodal reasoning and geometry-aware prediction, enables more precise global geolocation; the AVG benchmark facilitates standardized evaluation.

title: Let's Talk, Not Type: An Oral-First Multi-Agent Architecture for Guaraní
summary: Although artificial intelligence (AI) and Human-Computer Interaction (HCI) systems are often presented as universal solutions, their design remains predominantly text-first, underserving primarily oral languages and indigenous communities. This position paper uses Guaraní, an official and widely spoken language of Paraguay, as a case study to argue that language support in AI remains insufficient unless it aligns with lived oral practices. We propose an alternative to the standard "text-to-speech" pipeline, proposing instead an oral-first multi-agent architecture. By decoupling Guaraní natural language understanding from dedicated agents for conversation state and community-led governance, we demonstrate a technical framework that respects indigenous data sovereignty and diglossia. Our work moves beyond mere recognition to focus on turn-taking, repair, and shared context as the primary locus of interaction. We conclude that for AI to be truly culturally grounded, it must shift from adapting oral languages to text-centric systems to treating spoken conversation as a first-class design requirement, ensuring digital ecosystems empower rather than overlook diverse linguistic practices.

AI.tldr: Position paper arguing that AI/HCI must be designed oral-first, not text-first, using Guaraní as a case study; proposes a multi-agent, speech-centered architecture with community governance to respect indigenous data sovereignty and diglossia, focusing on conversational dynamics (turn-taking, repair, shared context) rather than mere recognition.
AI.motivation: Current AI/HCI pipelines are text-centric and underserve primarily oral languages and indigenous communities. Guaraní illustrates how text-first assumptions clash with lived oral practices, diglossia, and sovereignty concerns. The paper seeks culturally grounded AI that aligns with oral conversational norms and community control of data and systems.
AI.method: Proposes an oral-first multi-agent architecture as an alternative to standard text-to-speech pipelines. It decouples Guaraní NLU from agents that manage conversation state and from a community-led governance agent, emphasizing mechanisms for turn-taking, repair, and shared context. The framework is designed to respect indigenous data sovereignty and sociolinguistic realities (e.g., diglossia).
AI.result: Conceptual and architectural framework rather than empirical results. Demonstrates how to structure agents and interaction flows to prioritize spoken conversation and community governance; argues feasibility and benefits over text-first designs.
AI.conclusion: For culturally grounded AI, spoken conversation must be a first-class requirement. Systems should move beyond recognition accuracy to social-interactional competencies and embed community governance to empower diverse linguistic practices instead of forcing adaptation to text-centric paradigms.

title: Offline Materials Optimization with CliqueFlowmer
summary: Recent advances in deep learning inspired neural network-based approaches to computational materials discovery (CMD). A plethora of problems in this field involve finding materials that optimize a target property. Nevertheless, the increasingly popular generative modeling methods are ineffective at boldly exploring attractive regions of the materials space due to their maximum likelihood training. In this work, we offer an alternative CMD technique based on offline model-based optimization (MBO) that fuses direct optimization of a target material property into generation. To that end, we introduce a domain-specific model, dubbed CliqueFlowmer, that incorporates recent advances of clique-based MBO into transformer and flow generation. We validate CliqueFlowmer's optimization abilities and show that materials it produces strongly outperform those provided by generative baselines. To enable employment of CliqueFlowmer in specialized materials optimization problems and support interdisciplinary research, we open-source our code at https://github.com/znowu/CliqueFlowmer.

AI.tldr: CliqueFlowmer combines transformer and flow-based generation with clique-driven offline model-based optimization to directly target material properties during generation, outperforming standard MLE-trained generative baselines; code is open-sourced.
AI.motivation: Many CMD tasks require finding materials that maximize a target property, but MLE-trained generative models tend to stay close to the data distribution and under-explore high-performing regions, limiting property optimization.
AI.method: An offline model-based optimization approach tailored to materials design: CliqueFlowmer integrates recent clique-based MBO advances into transformer and flow generative modeling so samples are guided toward property-optimized regions while using only offline data.
AI.result: On materials optimization benchmarks, CliqueFlowmer’s generated candidates achieve substantially better target-property values than those from popular generative baselines.
AI.conclusion: Fusing clique-based MBO with generative models enables more effective property-driven search in materials space; CliqueFlowmer provides a practical, open-source tool for specialized CMD optimization tasks.

title: Any to Full: Prompting Depth Anything for Depth Completion in One Stage
summary: Accurate, dense depth estimation is crucial for robotic perception, but commodity sensors often yield sparse or incomplete measurements due to hardware limitations. Existing RGBD-fused depth completion methods learn priors jointly conditioned on training RGB distribution and specific depth patterns, limiting domain generalization and robustness to various depth patterns. Recent efforts leverage monocular depth estimation (MDE) models to introduce domain-general geometric priors, but current two-stage integration strategies relying on explicit relative-to-metric alignment incur additional computation and introduce structured distortions. To this end, we present Any2Full, a one-stage, domain-general, and pattern-agnostic framework that reformulates completion as a scale-prompting adaptation of a pretrained MDE model. To address varying depth sparsity levels and irregular spatial distributions, we design a Scale-Aware Prompt Encoder. It distills scale cues from sparse inputs into unified scale prompts, guiding the MDE model toward globally scale-consistent predictions while preserving its geometric priors. Extensive experiments demonstrate that Any2Full achieves superior robustness and efficiency. It outperforms OMNI-DC by 32.2\% in average AbsREL and delivers a 1.4$\times$ speedup over PriorDA with the same MDE backbone, establishing a new paradigm for universal depth completion. Codes and checkpoints are available at https://github.com/zhiyuandaily/Any2Full.

AI.tldr: Any2Full adapts a pretrained monocular depth estimator with scale prompts distilled from sparse depth, enabling single-stage, domain-general, pattern-agnostic depth completion that is both more accurate and faster than prior art.
AI.motivation: RGBD-fused depth completion models entangle learned priors with specific RGB domains and sensor-specific depth sparsity patterns, hurting cross-domain robustness. Two-stage uses of monocular depth estimators require explicit relative-to-metric alignment, adding computation and introducing structured artifacts.
AI.method: Reformulate depth completion as scale-prompting of a pretrained monocular depth estimation (MDE) model. A Scale-Aware Prompt Encoder encodes sparsity level and irregular spatial distribution of the sparse inputs into unified scale prompts that steer the MDE toward globally scale-consistent dense predictions while preserving its learned geometric priors. This yields a one-stage, alignment-free pipeline.
AI.result: Across benchmarks, Any2Full shows higher robustness and efficiency: 32.2% average AbsREL improvement over OMNI-DC and 1.4× speedup over PriorDA with the same MDE backbone. Code and checkpoints are released.
AI.conclusion: A single-stage, domain-general, and pattern-agnostic depth completion paradigm that avoids explicit alignment, reduces distortions, improves accuracy and speed, and generalizes across varying sparsity and sensor patterns.

title: CodeScout: Contextual Problem Statement Enhancement for Software Agents
summary: Current AI-powered code assistance tools often struggle with poorly-defined problem statements that lack sufficient task context and requirements specification. Recent analysis of software engineering agents reveals that failures on such underspecified requests are highly correlated with longer trajectories involving either over-exploration or repeated attempts at applying the same fix without proper evolution or testing, leading to suboptimal outcomes across software development tasks. We introduce CodeScout, a contextual query refinement approach that systematically converts underspecified user requests into comprehensive, actionable problem statements through lightweight pre-exploration of the target codebase. Our key innovation is demonstrating that structured analysis before task execution can supplement existing agentic capabilities without requiring any modifications to their underlying scaffolds. CodeScout performs targeted context scoping, conducts multi-perspective analysis examining potential fixes and exploration opportunities, then synthesizes these insights into enhanced problem statements with reproduction steps, expected behaviors, and targeted exploration hints. This pre-exploration directly addresses the identified failure patterns by reducing non-converging agent trajectories while clarifying user intent in natural language space. We evaluate CodeScout using state-of-the-art agentic scaffolds and language models on SWEBench-Verified, demonstrating a 20\% improvement in resolution rates with up to 27 additional issues resolved compared to the default baseline method. Our results suggest that systematic query refinement through contextual analysis represents a promising direction for enhancing AI code assistance capabilities.

AI.tldr: CodeScout performs lightweight pre-exploration of a target codebase to turn vague user requests into detailed, testable problem statements, which shortens wandering agent trajectories and improves bug-fix success by ~20% on SWEBench-Verified—without modifying existing agent scaffolds.
AI.motivation: AI code assistants often fail on underspecified tasks, leading to long, non-converging trajectories marked by over-exploration or repetitive, untested fixes. There is a need to clarify user intent and supply missing task context before execution.
AI.method: Contextual query refinement via structured, lightweight pre-analysis: (1) targeted context scoping of the relevant code, (2) multi-perspective analysis of plausible fixes and exploration avenues, and (3) synthesis of enhanced problem statements including reproduction steps, expected behavior, and focused exploration hints. Designed to slot into existing agentic workflows without scaffold changes.
AI.result: On SWEBench-Verified with state-of-the-art scaffolds and LLMs, CodeScout yields a 20% improvement in resolution rates, resolving up to 27 additional issues versus the baseline, and reduces non-convergent trajectories.
AI.conclusion: Systematic, pre-execution contextual analysis to refine queries is an effective, scaffold-agnostic way to mitigate underspecification in AI code assistance, improving reliability and outcomes across software development tasks.

title: Conversational Demand Response: Bidirectional Aggregator-Prosumer Coordination through Agentic AI
comment: 6 pages, 2 figures. Code available at: https://github.com/RedaElMakroum/cdr
summary: Residential demand response depends on sustained prosumer participation, yet existing coordination is either fully automated, or limited to one-way dispatch signals and price alerts that offer little possibility for informed decision-making. This paper introduces Conversational Demand Response (CDR), a coordination mechanism where aggregators and prosumers interact through bidirectional natural language, enabled through agentic AI. A two-tier multi-agent architecture is developed in which an aggregator agent dispatches flexibility requests and a prosumer Home Energy Management System (HEMS) assesses deliverability and cost-benefit by calling an optimization-based tool. CDR also enables prosumer-initiated upstream communication, where changes in preferences can reach the aggregator directly. Proof-of-concept evaluation shows that interactions complete in under 12 seconds. The architecture illustrates how agentic AI can bridge the aggregator-prosumer coordination gap, providing the scalability of automated DR while preserving the transparency, explainability, and user agency necessary for sustained prosumer participation. All system components, including agent prompts, orchestration logic, and simulation interfaces, are released as open source to enable reproducibility and further development.

AI.tldr: Introduces Conversational Demand Response (CDR), a bidirectional, natural-language, agent-based coordination between aggregators and residential prosumers that keeps DR scalable while improving transparency, explainability, and user agency; proof-of-concept runs interactions in <12s and is released open-source.
AI.motivation: Residential DR needs sustained prosumer participation, but current approaches are either fully automated (opaque) or rely on one-way signals/price alerts that limit informed, user-centric decisions. There is a gap between aggregator coordination needs and prosumer preferences/constraints.
AI.method: Proposes CDR: a two-tier multi-agent system. An aggregator agent communicates flexibility requests in natural language; a prosumer-side HEMS agent evaluates deliverability and cost-benefit by invoking an optimization tool, and responds in natural language. The system also supports prosumer-initiated preference updates upstream. Implemented with agentic AI, prompts/orchestration, and simulation interfaces, all open-sourced.
AI.result: Proof-of-concept evaluation shows end-to-end conversational interactions complete in under 12 seconds, demonstrating feasibility of timely coordination. The architecture operationalizes bidirectional communication and optimization-backed decisions.
AI.conclusion: Agentic AI-enabled CDR can bridge the aggregator–prosumer gap by combining the scalability of automated DR with the transparency, explainability, and user agency necessary for long-term engagement. Open-sourcing components supports reproducibility and future research/deployment.

title: Unlocking ImageNet's Multi-Object Nature: Automated Large-Scale Multilabel Annotation
summary: The original ImageNet benchmark enforces a single-label assumption, despite many images depicting multiple objects. This leads to label noise and limits the richness of the learning signal. Multi-label annotations more accurately reflect real-world visual scenes, where multiple objects co-occur and contribute to semantic understanding, enabling models to learn richer and more robust representations. While prior efforts (e.g., ReaL, ImageNetv2) have improved the validation set, there has not yet been a scalable, high-quality multi-label annotation for the training set. To this end, we present an automated pipeline to convert the ImageNet training set into a multi-label dataset, without human annotations. Using self-supervised Vision Transformers, we perform unsupervised object discovery, select regions aligned with original labels to train a lightweight classifier, and apply it to all regions to generate coherent multi-label annotations across the dataset. Our labels show strong alignment with human judgment in qualitative evaluations and consistently improve performance across quantitative benchmarks. Compared to traditional single-label scheme, models trained with our multi-label supervision achieve consistently better in-domain accuracy across architectures (up to +2.0 top-1 accuracy on ReaL and +1.5 on ImageNet-V2) and exhibit stronger transferability to downstream tasks (up to +4.2 and +2.3 mAP on COCO and VOC, respectively). These results underscore the importance of accurate multi-label annotations for enhancing both classification performance and representation learning. Project code and the generated multi-label annotations are available at https://github.com/jchen175/MultiLabel-ImageNet.

AI.tldr: They convert ImageNet-1k training data into a scalable multi-label dataset using an automated pipeline: self-supervised ViTs discover object regions, regions aligned to the original label train a lightweight classifier, which then assigns coherent multi-labels to all regions/images—yielding better accuracy and transfer without human annotation.
AI.motivation: ImageNet’s single-label assumption introduces label noise because many images contain multiple objects. Multi-label supervision better matches real scenes, potentially improving representation learning. Prior work refined validation (ReaL, ImageNetv2) but lacked a scalable, high-quality, multi-label training set.
AI.method: Use self-supervised Vision Transformers to perform unsupervised object discovery, select regions consistent with the original class to create supervision, train a lightweight region-level classifier, and apply it to all discovered regions to produce per-image multi-label annotations across the training set—entirely automated, no manual labels beyond original ImageNet classes.
AI.result: Qualitatively, labels align with human judgment. Quantitatively, training with these multi-labels improves in-domain accuracy across architectures: up to +2.0 top-1 on ReaL and +1.5 on ImageNet-V2. It also improves transfer: up to +4.2 mAP on COCO and +2.3 mAP on VOC. Code and annotations are released.
AI.conclusion: Accurate, scalable multi-label annotations for ImageNet training enhance both classification accuracy and representation quality, underscoring the value of multi-label supervision for generalization and downstream transfer.

title: NERdME: a Named Entity Recognition Dataset for Indexing Research Artifacts in Code Repositories
summary: Existing scholarly information extraction (SIE) datasets focus on scientific papers and overlook implementation-level details in code repositories. README files describe datasets, source code, and other implementation-level artifacts, however, their free-form Markdown offers little semantic structure, making automatic information extraction difficult. To address this gap, NERdME is introduced: 200 manually annotated README files with over 10,000 labeled spans and 10 entity types. Baseline results using large language models and fine-tuned transformers show clear differences between paperlevel and implementation-level entities, indicating the value of extending SIE benchmarks with entity types available in README files. A downstream entity-linking experiment was conducted to demonstrate that entities derived from READMEs can support artifact discovery and metadata integration.

AI.tldr: NERdME introduces a manually annotated NER dataset of 200 GitHub README files (10k+ spans, 10 entity types) to capture implementation-level scholarly information. Baselines with LLMs and fine-tuned transformers plus a downstream entity-linking task show README-derived entities are distinct from paper-centric ones and can aid artifact discovery and metadata integration.
AI.motivation: Most scholarly IE benchmarks target scientific papers, overlooking implementation details (datasets, code, tools) that live in repositories. README files are rich but unstructured Markdown, making automatic extraction difficult. A structured, annotated resource is needed to model and evaluate extraction of implementation-level entities.
AI.method: Construct and manually annotate a corpus of 200 README files with 10 entity types and 10k+ spans. Train/evaluate baselines using large language models and fine-tuned transformer NER systems to compare performance and characterize differences from paper-level entities. Conduct a downstream entity-linking experiment to external resources to test utility for artifact discovery and metadata integration.
AI.result: Baseline evaluations reveal clear differences between paper-level and implementation-level entities, underscoring the need for README-specific categories. The entity-linking experiment shows entities extracted from READMEs can be linked to external artifacts to support discovery and metadata integration (no quantitative metrics provided in the abstract).
AI.conclusion: README-derived entities add value to SIE benchmarks, and NERdME provides a needed resource to model implementation-level information. Entities extracted from READMEs can improve artifact discovery and metadata workflows; extending SIE to this space is warranted.

title: Artificial Intelligence for Climate Adaptation: Reinforcement Learning for Climate Change-Resilient Transport
summary: Climate change is expected to intensify rainfall and, consequently, pluvial flooding, leading to increased disruptions in urban transportation systems over the coming decades. Designing effective adaptation strategies is challenging due to the long-term, sequential nature of infrastructure investments, deep climate uncertainty, and the complex interactions between flooding, infrastructure, and mobility impacts. In this work, we propose a novel decision-support framework using reinforcement learning (RL) for long-term flood adaptation planning. Formulated as an integrated assessment model (IAM), the framework combines rainfall projection and flood modeling, transport simulation, and quantification of direct and indirect impacts on infrastructure and mobility. Our RL-based approach learns adaptive strategies that balance investment and maintenance costs against avoided impacts. We evaluate the framework through a case study of Copenhagen's inner city over the 2024-2100 period, testing multiple adaptation options, and different belief and realized climate scenarios. Results show that the framework outperforms traditional optimization approaches by discovering coordinated spatial and temporal adaptation pathways and learning trade-offs between impact reduction and adaptation investment, yielding more resilient strategies. Overall, our results showcase the potential of reinforcement learning as a flexible decision-support tool for adaptive infrastructure planning under climate uncertainty.

AI.tldr: An integrated reinforcement-learning decision-support framework learns long-term, adaptive flood adaptation strategies for urban transport under climate uncertainty, outperforming traditional optimization in a Copenhagen 2024–2100 case study by coordinating spatial-temporal investments and balancing costs against avoided impacts.
AI.motivation: Intensifying rainfall will worsen pluvial flooding and disrupt urban transport. Planning adaptations is difficult due to sequential, long-lived infrastructure decisions, deep climate uncertainty, and complex couplings between flooding, infrastructure, and mobility impacts.
AI.method: Formulate an integrated assessment model that links rainfall projections and flood modeling with transport simulation and impact quantification. Use reinforcement learning to select and time adaptation and maintenance actions, learning policies that trade off investment costs and avoided damages. Evaluate multiple adaptation options across belief and realized climate scenarios in Copenhagen’s inner city from 2024 to 2100, and benchmark against traditional optimization approaches.
AI.result: The RL-based framework discovers coordinated spatial and temporal adaptation pathways, captures trade-offs between impact reduction and investment, and achieves more resilient outcomes than traditional optimization across tested climate scenarios.
AI.conclusion: Reinforcement learning offers a flexible, effective decision-support tool for adaptive infrastructure planning under climate uncertainty, enabling strategies that better balance costs and resilience than conventional optimization.

title: From Phase Grounding to Intelligent Surgical Narratives
summary: Video surgery timelines are an important part of tool-assisted surgeries, as they allow surgeons to quickly focus on key parts of the procedure. Current methods involve the surgeon filling out a post-operation (OP) report, which is often vague, or manually annotating the surgical videos, which is highly time-consuming. Our proposed method sits between these two extremes: we aim to automatically create a surgical timeline and narrative directly from the surgical video. To achieve this, we employ a CLIP-based multi-modal framework that aligns surgical video frames with textual gesture descriptions. Specifically, we use the CLIP visual encoder to extract representations from surgical video frames and the text encoder to embed the corresponding gesture sentences into a shared embedding space. We then fine-tune the model to improve the alignment between video gestures and textual tokens. Once trained, the model predicts gestures and phases for video frames, enabling the construction of a structured surgical timeline. This approach leverages pretrained multi-modal representations to bridge visual gestures and textual narratives, reducing the need for manual video review and annotation by surgeons.

AI.tldr: Fine-tuned CLIP aligns surgical video frames with gesture text to automatically predict per-frame gestures/phases and assemble a structured surgical timeline and narrative, reducing manual review.
AI.motivation: Post-op reports are often vague and manual video annotation is time-consuming; surgeons need quick, structured access to key procedural moments without exhaustive labeling.
AI.method: Use CLIP’s visual encoder on surgical frames and text encoder on gesture sentences to embed both in a shared space; fine-tune to tighten video–text alignment; then infer gestures and phases per frame and aggregate them into a timeline and narrative.
AI.result: After training, the model outputs frame-level gesture and phase predictions that can be organized into a coherent surgical timeline, decreasing reliance on manual annotations. The abstract reports feasibility rather than quantitative gains.
AI.conclusion: Leveraging pretrained multimodal representations can bridge visual gestures and textual descriptions to automatically generate surgical timelines/narratives, offering a practical middle ground between sparse reports and labor-intensive manual annotation.

title: PVminerLLM: Structured Extraction of Patient Voice from Patient-Generated Text using Large Language Models
summary:
Motivation: Patient-generated text contains critical information about patients' lived experiences, social circumstances, and engagement in care, including factors that strongly influence adherence, care coordination, and health equity. However, these patient voice signals are rarely available in structured form, limiting their use in patient-centered outcomes research and clinical quality improvement. Reliable extraction of such information is therefore essential for understanding and addressing non-clinical drivers of health outcomes at scale.
  Results: We introduce PVminer, a benchmark for structured extraction of patient voice, and propose PVminerLLM, a supervised fine-tuned large language model tailored to this task. Across multiple datasets and model sizes, PVminerLLM substantially outperforms prompt-based baselines, achieving up to 83.82% F1 for Code prediction, 80.74% F1 for Sub-code prediction, and 87.03% F1 for evidence Span extraction. Notably, strong performance is achieved even with smaller models, demonstrating that reliable patient voice extraction is feasible without extreme model scale. These results enable scalable analysis of social and experiential signals embedded in patient-generated text.
  Availability and Implementation: Code, evaluation scripts, and trained LLMs will be released publicly. Annotated datasets will be made available upon request for research use.
  Keywords: Large Language Models, Supervised Fine-Tuning, Medical Annotation, Patient-Generated Text, Clinical NLP

AI.tldr: PVminer introduces a benchmark and a supervised fine-tuned LLM (PVminerLLM) to extract structured “patient voice” (codes, sub-codes, evidence spans) from patient-generated text, outperforming prompt-based methods with F1 up to ~84% (codes), ~81% (sub-codes), and ~87% (spans), with strong results even from smaller models.
AI.motivation: Patient-generated text captures lived experiences, social context, and care engagement that drive adherence, coordination, and equity, but this information is unstructured and hard to use at scale. Structured extraction enables patient-centered outcomes research and quality improvement.
AI.method: Define a benchmark (PVminer) for structured patient voice extraction with tasks for code, sub-code, and evidence span identification. Train and evaluate PVminerLLM via supervised fine-tuning across multiple datasets and model sizes, and compare against prompt-based LLM baselines using F1-based metrics.
AI.result: PVminerLLM significantly outperforms prompt-based approaches, achieving up to 83.82% F1 on Code prediction, 80.74% F1 on Sub-code prediction, and 87.03% F1 on evidence Span extraction. Performance remains strong with smaller models, indicating feasibility without extreme scale.
AI.conclusion: Reliable, scalable extraction of patient voice from patient-generated text is feasible using supervised fine-tuned LLMs. The released code/models (with datasets available upon request) can catalyze research and quality improvement by unlocking social and experiential signals relevant to health outcomes.

title: The EpisTwin: A Knowledge Graph-Grounded Neuro-Symbolic Architecture for Personal AI
summary: Personal Artificial Intelligence is currently hindered by the fragmentation of user data across isolated silos. While Retrieval-Augmented Generation offers a partial remedy, its reliance on unstructured vector similarity fails to capture the latent semantic topology and temporal dependencies essential for holistic sensemaking. We introduce EpisTwin, a neuro-symbolic framework that grounds generative reasoning in a verifiable, user-centric Personal Knowledge Graph. EpisTwin leverages Multimodal Language Models to lift heterogeneous, cross-application data into semantic triples. At inference, EpisTwin enables complex reasoning over the personal semantic graph via an agentic coordinator that combines Graph Retrieval-Augmented Generation with Online Deep Visual Refinement, dynamically re-grounding symbolic entities in their raw visual context. We also introduce PersonalQA-71-100, a synthetic benchmark designed to simulate a realistic user's digital footprint and evaluate EpisTwin performance. Our framework demonstrates robust results across a suite of state-of-the-art judge models, offering a promising direction for trustworthy Personal AI.

AI.tldr: EpisTwin is a neuro‑symbolic Personal AI that builds a user‑centric Personal Knowledge Graph from multimodal data and performs Graph‑RAG with online visual re‑grounding; it introduces a synthetic PersonalQA benchmark and reports strong performance judged by SOTA models.
AI.motivation: Personal AI is limited by fragmented, siloed user data and by vanilla RAG’s reliance on unstructured vector similarity, which misses semantic structure and temporal relations needed for holistic, verifiable reasoning.
AI.method: Use Multimodal Language Models to lift heterogeneous, cross‑app artifacts into semantic triples, forming a verifiable Personal Knowledge Graph. At inference, an agentic coordinator executes complex reasoning via Graph Retrieval‑Augmented Generation combined with Online Deep Visual Refinement, dynamically re‑grounding graph entities in their raw visual context.
AI.result: On the new synthetic benchmark PersonalQA‑71‑100, EpisTwin achieves robust results according to multiple state‑of‑the‑art judge models (no specific metrics provided in the abstract).
AI.conclusion: Grounding generative reasoning in a personal, verifiable knowledge graph and complementing it with on‑the‑fly visual re‑grounding offers a promising, more trustworthy path for Personal AI beyond standard vector‑based RAG.

title: Full Dynamic Range Sky-Modelling For Image Based Lighting
summary: Accurate environment maps are a key component to modelling real-world outdoor scenes. They enable captivating visual arts, immersive virtual reality and a wide range of scientific and engineering applications. To alleviate the burden of physical-capture, physically-simulation and volumetric rendering, sky-models have been proposed as fast, flexible, and cost-saving alternatives. In recent years, sky-models have been extended through deep learning to be more comprehensive and inclusive of cloud formations, but recent work has demonstrated these models fall short in faithfully recreating accurate and photorealistic natural skies. Particularly at higher resolutions, DNN sky-models struggle to accurately model the 14EV+ class-imbalanced solar region, resulting in poor visual quality and scenes illuminated with skewed light transmission, shadows and tones. In this work, we propose Icarus, an all-weather sky-model capable of learning the exposure range of Full Dynamic Range (FDR) physically captured outdoor imagery. Our model allows conditional generation of environment maps with intuitive user-positioning of solar and cloud formations, and extends on current state-of-the-art to enable user-controlled texturing of atmospheric formations. Through our evaluation, we demonstrate Icarus is interchangeable with FDR physically captured outdoor imagery or parametric sky-models, and illuminates scenes with unprecedented accuracy, photorealism, lighting directionality (shadows), and tones in Image Based Lightning (IBL).

AI.tldr: Icarus is an all‑weather, controllable deep sky model that learns full dynamic range from physically captured outdoor imagery to generate environment maps with accurate sun/sky radiance, cloud structure, and user‑guided placement/texturing, achieving photorealistic, directionally correct lighting for IBL on par with captured FDR or parametric skies.
AI.motivation: Existing sky/environment-map solutions either require costly capture/physics-based rendering or use DNN sky models that fail to reproduce high‑dynamic‑range solar regions (14+ EV), degrading realism and lighting fidelity (shadows, tones), especially at higher resolutions. A fast, flexible, accurate, and controllable alternative is needed.
AI.method: Propose Icarus, an all‑weather deep generative sky model trained to learn the exposure range of Full Dynamic Range (FDR) outdoor imagery. It supports conditional generation with intuitive user control of sun and cloud positions and extends state‑of‑the‑art by allowing user‑controlled texturing of atmospheric formations, explicitly addressing HDR/class‑imbalance around the solar region.
AI.result: Empirically, Icarus can substitute for FDR captured skies or parametric sky models, producing environment maps that illuminate scenes with higher accuracy, photorealism, correct lighting directionality (shadows), and faithful tonal rendition in Image‑Based Lighting (IBL) scenarios.
AI.conclusion: Icarus overcomes key shortcomings of prior DNN sky models in HDR solar modeling and control, offering a practical, interchangeable, and photorealistic sky/environment mapping solution that reduces reliance on physical capture while improving IBL quality.

title: Tutor Move Taxonomy: A Theory-Aligned Framework for Analyzing Instructional Moves in Tutoring
summary: Understanding what makes tutoring effective requires methods for systematically analyzing tutors' instructional actions during learning interactions. This paper presents a tutor move taxonomy designed to support large-scale analysis of tutoring dialogue within the National Tutoring Observatory. The taxonomy provides a structured annotation framework for labeling tutors' instructional moves during one-on-one tutoring sessions. We developed the taxonomy through a hybrid deductive-inductive process. First, we synthesized research from cognitive science, the learning sciences, classroom discourse analysis, and intelligent tutoring systems to construct a preliminary framework of tutoring moves. We then refined the taxonomy through iterative coding of authentic tutoring transcripts conducted by expert annotators with extensive instructional and qualitative research experience. The resulting taxonomy organizes tutoring behaviors into four categories: tutoring support, learning support, social-emotional and motivational support, and logistical support. Learning support moves are further organized along a spectrum of student engagement, distinguishing between moves that elicit student reasoning and those that provide direct explanation or answers. By defining tutoring dialogue in terms of discrete instructional actions, the taxonomy enables scalable annotation using AI, computational modeling of tutoring strategies, and empirical analysis of how tutoring behaviors relate to learning outcomes.

AI.tldr: They introduce a rigorously developed taxonomy of tutor moves—spanning instructional, learning, socio-emotional/motivational, and logistical supports—with learning moves arranged by how much they elicit student reasoning versus provide direct explanation, enabling scalable annotation, AI modeling, and outcome analysis.
AI.motivation: To understand what makes tutoring effective, researchers need a systematic, scalable way to characterize tutors’ instructional actions across many sessions, particularly for the National Tutoring Observatory.
AI.method: A hybrid deductive–inductive approach: synthesize theory and prior research from cognitive science, learning sciences, classroom discourse, and intelligent tutoring systems to draft a framework; iteratively code authentic one-on-one tutoring transcripts with expert annotators to refine it.
AI.result: A structured annotation framework (tutor move taxonomy) with four categories: tutoring support, learning support, social-emotional/motivational support, and logistical support. Learning support is organized along a spectrum from eliciting student reasoning to providing direct explanations/answers. The taxonomy is suitable for large-scale labeling and downstream computational analyses.
AI.conclusion: Defining tutoring dialogue as discrete instructional actions makes large-scale, AI-assisted annotation feasible and supports computational modeling and empirical studies linking tutoring strategies to learning outcomes.

title: SAHOO: Safeguarded Alignment for High-Order Optimization Objectives in Recursive Self-Improvement
comment: Published at ICLR 2026 Workshop on AI with Recursive Self-Improvement. 20 pages, 5 figures
summary: Recursive self-improvement is moving from theory to practice: modern systems can critique, revise, and evaluate their own outputs, yet iterative self-modification risks subtle alignment drift. We introduce SAHOO, a practical framework to monitor and control drift through three safeguards: (i) the Goal Drift Index (GDI), a learned multi-signal detector combining semantic, lexical, structural, and distributional measures; (ii) constraint preservation checks that enforce safety-critical invariants such as syntactic correctness and non-hallucination; and (iii) regression-risk quantification to flag improvement cycles that undo prior gains. Across 189 tasks in code generation, mathematical reasoning, and truthfulness, SAHOO produces substantial quality gains, including 18.3 percent improvement in code tasks and 16.8 percent in reasoning, while preserving constraints in two domains and maintaining low violations in truthfulness. Thresholds are calibrated on a small validation set of 18 tasks across three cycles. We further map the capability-alignment frontier, showing efficient early improvement cycles but rising alignment costs later and exposing domain-specific tensions such as fluency versus factuality. SAHOO therefore makes alignment preservation during recursive self-improvement measurable, deployable, and systematically validated at scale.

AI.tldr: SAHOO is a practical framework that monitors and controls alignment drift during recursive self-improvement using a learned Goal Drift Index, constraint checks, and regression-risk quantification, yielding sizable quality gains while preserving safety constraints and revealing trade-offs that grow across improvement cycles.
AI.motivation: Modern models can iteratively critique and modify their own outputs, but such recursive self-improvement risks subtle goal/behavior drift and performance regressions. There is a need for measurable, deployable safeguards that preserve alignment and safety while allowing capability gains.
AI.method: SAHOO employs three safeguards: (1) Goal Drift Index (GDI), a learned multi-signal detector combining semantic, lexical, structural, and distributional cues to spot drift; (2) constraint-preservation checks to enforce safety-critical invariants (e.g., syntactic correctness, non-hallucination); and (3) regression-risk quantification to flag improvement cycles that undo prior gains. Thresholds are calibrated on a small validation set (18 tasks across three cycles). The system is evaluated on 189 tasks spanning code generation, mathematical reasoning, and truthfulness, and it maps the capability–alignment frontier over improvement cycles.
AI.result: Across 189 tasks, SAHOO delivers substantial gains: +18.3% in code generation and +16.8% in mathematical reasoning. It preserves constraints in two domains and shows low violation rates in truthfulness. Analysis of improvement cycles shows efficient early gains but increasing alignment costs later and highlights domain tensions such as fluency versus factuality.
AI.conclusion: SAHOO makes alignment preservation during recursive self-improvement measurable and deployable at scale, securing capability improvements while maintaining safety constraints and clarifying trade-offs that intensify over successive cycles. Future work should broaden domains, strengthen truthfulness safeguards, and test robustness of threshold calibration beyond the small validation set.

title: Layer-wise Instance Binding for Regional and Occlusion Control in Text-to-Image Diffusion Transformers
summary: Region-instructed layout control in text-to-image generation is highly practical, yet existing methods suffer from limitations: (i) training-based approaches inherit data bias and often degrade image quality, and (ii) current techniques struggle with occlusion order, limiting real-world usability. To address these issues, we propose LayerBind. By modeling regional generation as distinct layers and binding them during the generation, our method enables precise regional and occlusion controllability. Our motivation stems from the observation that spatial layout and occlusion are established at a very early denoising stage, suggesting that rearranging the early latent structure is sufficient to modify the final output. Building on this, we structure the scheme into two phases: instance initialization and subsequent semantic nursing. (1) First, leveraging the contextual sharing mechanism in multimodal joint attention, Layer-wise Instance Initialization creates per-instance branches that attend to their own regions while anchoring to the shared background. At a designated early step, these branches are fused according to the layer order to form a unified latent with a pre-established layout. (2) Then, Layer-wise Semantic Nursing reinforces regional details and maintains the occlusion order via a layer-wise attention enhancement. Specifically, a sequential layered attention path operates alongside the standard global path, with updates composited under a layer-transparency scheduler. LayerBind is training-free and plug-and-play, serving as a regional and occlusion controller across Diffusion Transformers. Beyond generation, it natively supports editable workflows, allowing for flexible modifications like changing instances or rearranging visible orders. Both qualitative and quantitative results demonstrate LayerBind's effectiveness, highlighting its strong potential for creative applications.

AI.tldr: LayerBind is a training-free, plug-and-play controller for Diffusion Transformers that treats regions as compositing layers and binds them early in denoising, enabling precise regional placement and explicit occlusion ordering without sacrificing image quality, while supporting easy edits (e.g., reordering, replacing instances).
AI.motivation: Existing region-controlled text-to-image methods either require training—bringing data bias and image quality degradation—or cannot robustly enforce occlusion order, limiting practical use in real scenes and creative workflows.
AI.method: Model regional generation as layered components. Phase 1: Layer-wise Instance Initialization creates per-instance attention branches that focus on their specified regions while sharing a common background; at an early diffusion step, branches are fused according to desired layer (depth) order to set the layout/occlusions. Phase 2: Layer-wise Semantic Nursing reinforces per-region details and preserves occlusion via a sequential layered attention path that runs alongside the standard global attention; updates are blended using a layer-transparency scheduler. The approach is training-free and plug-and-play across Diffusion Transformers.
AI.result: Qualitative and quantitative experiments show accurate regional control, stable occlusion handling, and maintained image quality; the method naturally supports editable generation (e.g., changing instances or reordering visibility).
AI.conclusion: Early-stage layer binding plus layer-wise attention refinement enables practical, high-quality regional and occlusion control in text-to-image generation, offering an editable, training-free controller with strong potential for creative applications across Diffusion Transformers.

title: RouteGoT: Node-Adaptive Routing for Cost-Efficient Graph of Thoughts Reasoning
summary: Large Language Models (LLMs) excel at multi-step reasoning, yet increasing the structural complexity of inference does not consistently improve system-level returns. Methods such as Tree of Thoughts (ToT), Graph of Thoughts (GoT), and Adaptive Graph of Thoughts (AGoT) can boost accuracy on some benchmarks, but often introduce substantial overhead in token consumption and latency, and their gains can be unstable across task distributions-sometimes underperforming simpler Chain-of-Thought (CoT) or direct input-output prompting (IO). We attribute this inefficiency to stage-wise and node-wise heterogeneity inside GoT-style reasoning pipelines: high-quality planning and final synthesis are globally coupled and typically benefit from strong models, whereas many intermediate subtasks are localized and can be solved accurately by lighter models with far fewer tokens. Motivated by these observations, we propose RouteGoT, a budget-controllable, node-adaptive routing framework for graph-structured reasoning. RouteGoT performs in-graph routing by prioritizing strong models for planning and synthesis, while dynamically allocating lightweight models and cost-effective strategies to leaf subtasks based on predicted difficulty. It further integrates explicit budget constraints into a global inference scheduler to control graph expansion under a user-specified token budget, enabling predictable performance-cost trade-offs. Experiments across reasoning, retrieval, and multi-hop QA benchmarks show that RouteGoT matching or improving accuracy while substantially reducing token usage; specifically, it achieves an average 8.1 percentage points accuracy improvement and 79.1\% output token reduction compared to AGoT. Furthermore, RouteGoT outperforms existing routing baselines by maintaining a superior cost-accuracy trade-off, demonstrating improved robustness under varying budget targets and tasks.

AI.tldr: RouteGoT is a node-adaptive, budget-aware routing framework for graph-structured LLM reasoning that assigns strong models to global planning/synthesis and cheaper models to easier leaf subtasks, delivering better accuracy with far fewer tokens under explicit budget control.
AI.motivation: Complex reasoning methods like ToT/GoT/AGoT often consume many tokens and are slow, with gains that vary across tasks. The authors observe heterogeneity within reasoning graphs: planning and final synthesis are globally coupled and need strong models, while many intermediate/leaf subtasks are local and solvable by lightweight models. Exploiting this mismatch could cut costs without hurting accuracy.
AI.method: Introduce RouteGoT, which performs in-graph, node-level routing based on predicted difficulty: strong models are prioritized for planning and synthesis; lightweight models and cheaper strategies are allocated to leaf/local nodes. A global inference scheduler enforces user-specified token budgets, controlling graph expansion to achieve predictable performance–cost trade-offs.
AI.result: Across reasoning, retrieval, and multi-hop QA benchmarks, RouteGoT matches or improves accuracy while greatly reducing tokens. Relative to AGoT, it yields an average +8.1 percentage-point accuracy improvement and a 79.1% reduction in output tokens. It also surpasses routing baselines by offering a superior cost–accuracy trade-off and better robustness under varying budgets and tasks.
AI.conclusion: Budget-controllable, node-adaptive routing within graph-structured reasoning effectively exploits task heterogeneity, enabling strong performance where it matters and cheap processing elsewhere. This leads to consistent accuracy gains with major token savings and robust behavior across tasks and budget settings.

title: Talk Freely, Execute Strictly: Schema-Gated Agentic AI for Flexible and Reproducible Scientific Workflows
summary:
Large language models (LLMs) can now translate a researcher's plain-language goal into executable computation, yet scientific workflows demand determinism, provenance, and governance that are difficult to guarantee when an LLM decides what runs. Semi-structured interviews with 18 experts across 10 industrial R&D stakeholders surface 2 competing requirements--deterministic, constrained execution and conversational flexibility without workflow rigidity--together with boundary properties (human-in-the-loop control and transparency) that any resolution must satisfy. We propose schema-gated orchestration as the resolving principle: the schema becomes a mandatory execution boundary at the composed-workflow level, so that nothing runs unless the complete action--including cross-step dependencies--validates against a machine-checkable specification.
  We operationalize the 2 requirements as execution determinism (ED) and conversational flexibility (CF), and use these axes to review 20 systems spanning 5 architectural groups along a validation-scope spectrum. Scores are assigned via a multi-model protocol--15 independent sessions across 3 LLM families--yielding substantial-to-near-perfect inter-model agreement (Krippendorff a=0.80 for ED and a=0.98 for CF), demonstrating that multi-model LLM scoring can serve as a reusable alternative to human expert panels for architectural assessment.
  The resulting landscape reveals an empirical Pareto front--no reviewed system achieves both high flexibility and high determinism--but a convergence zone emerges between the generative and workflow-centric extremes. We argue that a schema-gated architecture, separating conversational from execution authority, is positioned to decouple this trade-off, and distill 3 operational principles--clarification-before-execution, constrained plan-act orchestration, and tool-to-workflow-level gating--to guide adoption.

AI.tldr: The paper argues that LLM-driven scientific workflows must balance execution determinism with conversational flexibility and proposes schema-gated orchestration to safely achieve both, supported by an evaluation of 20 systems and a multi-model LLM scoring protocol.
AI.motivation: Scientific R&D requires determinism, provenance, and governance, which are hard to guarantee when LLMs autonomously choose what to execute. Practitioners want both constrained, reliable execution and flexible, conversational interaction, with human oversight and transparency preserved.
AI.method: (1) Semi-structured interviews with 18 experts across 10 industrial R&D organizations to elicit requirements and boundary properties. (2) Formalize two evaluation axes: execution determinism (ED) and conversational flexibility (CF). (3) Review 20 systems across 5 architectural groups along a validation-scope spectrum. (4) Score systems using a multi-model protocol (15 independent sessions across 3 LLM families) to assess ED and CF and measure inter-model agreement.
AI.result: Multi-model scoring shows substantial-to-near-perfect agreement (Krippendorff's alpha = 0.80 for ED, 0.98 for CF), suggesting LLM ensembles can substitute for human expert panels in architectural assessment. The landscape exhibits a Pareto front—no system simultaneously achieves high ED and high CF—though a convergence zone appears between fully generative and workflow-centric designs.
AI.conclusion: Separating conversational from execution authority via schema-gated orchestration—where full, cross-step actions must validate against a machine-checkable schema before running—can decouple flexibility from determinism. The paper distills three adoption principles: clarification-before-execution, constrained plan–act orchestration, and gating from tool to workflow level; and positions multi-model LLM scoring as a reusable evaluation method.

title: Visual Words Meet BM25: Sparse Auto-Encoder Visual Word Scoring for Image Retrieval
summary: Dense image retrieval is accurate but offers limited interpretability and attribution, and it can be compute-intensive at scale. We present \textbf{BM25-V}, which applies Okapi BM25 scoring to sparse visual-word activations from a Sparse Auto-Encoder (SAE) on Vision Transformer patch features. Across a large gallery, visual-word document frequencies are highly imbalanced and follow a Zipfian-like distribution, making BM25's inverse document frequency (IDF) weighting well suited for suppressing ubiquitous, low-information words and emphasizing rare, discriminative ones. BM25-V retrieves high-recall candidates via sparse inverted-index operations and serves as an efficient first-stage retriever for dense reranking. Across seven benchmarks, BM25-V achieves Recall@200 $\geq$ 0.993, enabling a two-stage pipeline that reranks only $K{=}200$ candidates per query and recovers near-dense accuracy within $0.2$\% on average. An SAE trained once on ImageNet-1K transfers zero-shot to seven fine-grained benchmarks without fine-tuning, and BM25-V retrieval decisions are attributable to specific visual words with quantified IDF contributions.

AI.tldr: BM25-V applies classic BM25 scoring to sparse “visual words” induced by a sparse autoencoder over ViT patch features, yielding an interpretable, scalable first-stage image retriever that reaches ≥0.993 Recall@200 across seven benchmarks and enables dense reranking of only 200 candidates with ~0.2% average accuracy loss.
AI.motivation: Dense image retrieval is accurate but compute-heavy at scale and provides limited interpretability/attribution. The authors seek a high-recall, efficient, and explainable first-stage retriever that can feed a lightweight reranker while preserving near-dense accuracy.
AI.method: Train a Sparse Auto-Encoder (SAE) on Vision Transformer patch features to produce sparse visual-word activations. Build an inverted index over these words and rank images using Okapi BM25 with IDF derived from gallery-wide visual-word frequencies (which are Zipfian). Use BM25-V to fetch high-recall candidates, then apply a dense reranker to the top K=200. The SAE is trained once on ImageNet-1K and used zero-shot on multiple fine-grained benchmarks.
AI.result: Across seven benchmarks, BM25-V achieves Recall@200 ≥ 0.993. A two-stage pipeline that reranks only the top 200 candidates recovers near-dense performance within 0.2% on average.
