Decisions are attributable to specific visual words with quantified IDF contributions. The SAE transfers zero-shot to fine-grained datasets without fine-tuning.
AI.conclusion: BM25-V offers a practical, interpretable, and scalable first-stage image retriever. It substantially reduces computation while preserving near-dense accuracy and provides clear attributions via visual-word IDF contributions, with strong zero-shot transferability.

title: HART: Data-Driven Hallucination Attribution and Evidence-Based Tracing for Large Language Models
summary: Large language models (LLMs) have demonstrated remarkable performance in text generation and knowledge-intensive question answering. Nevertheless, they are prone to producing hallucinated content, which severely undermines their reliability in high-stakes application domains. Existing hallucination attribution approaches, based on either external knowledge retrieval or internal model mechanisms, primarily focus on semantic similarity matching or representation-level discrimination. As a result, they have difficulty establishing structured correspondences at the span level between hallucination types, underlying error generation mechanisms, and external factual evidence, thereby limiting the interpretability of hallucinated fragments and the traceability of supporting or opposing evidence. To address these limitations, we propose HART, a fine-grained hallucination attribution and evidence retrieval framework for large language models. HART formalizes hallucination tracing as a structured modeling task comprising four stages: span localization, mechanism attribution, evidence retrieval, and causal tracing. Based upon this formulation, we develop the first structured dataset tailored for hallucination tracing, in which hallucination types, error mechanisms, and sets of counterfactual evidence are jointly annotated to enable causal-level interpretability evaluation. Experimental results on the proposed dataset demonstrate that HART substantially outperforms strong retrieval baselines, including BM25 and DPR, validating the effectiveness and generalization capability of the proposed tracing paradigm for hallucination analysis and evidence alignment.

AI.tldr: HART introduces a structured, span-level framework and dataset for tracing and explaining LLM hallucinations—localizing erroneous spans, attributing mechanisms, retrieving counterevidence, and performing causal tracing—significantly outperforming BM25/DPR on the new benchmark.
AI.motivation: LLM hallucinations erode trust in high‑stakes settings. Existing methods rely on semantic similarity or internal reps and lack span-level, structured linkage among hallucination types, error mechanisms, and external evidence, limiting interpretability and evidence traceability.
AI.method: Formalize hallucination tracing as four stages: (1) span localization of hallucinated fragments; (2) mechanism attribution (e.g., types/causes of errors); (3) evidence retrieval of supporting/contradicting facts; (4) causal tracing to connect mechanisms and evidence. Build the first dataset with joint annotations of hallucination types, mechanisms, and counterfactual evidence to evaluate causal interpretability.
AI.result: On their structured dataset, HART substantially outperforms strong retrieval baselines (BM25, DPR) and shows better generalization for hallucination analysis and evidence alignment.
AI.conclusion: A structured, fine‑grained paradigm (HART) improves attribution and evidence retrieval for hallucinations and provides a benchmark for causal-level interpretability. Results indicate enhanced reliability and traceability versus classic retrieval baselines.

title: Spectral Probing of Feature Upsamplers in 2D-to-3D Scene Reconstruction
summary: A typical 2D-to-3D pipeline takes multi-view images as input, where a Vision Foundation Model (VFM) extracts features that are spatially upsampled to dense representations for 3D reconstruction. If dense features across views preserve geometric consistency, differentiable rendering can recover an accurate 3D representation, making the feature upsampler a critical component. Recent learnable upsampling methods mainly aim to enhance spatial details, such as sharper geometry or richer textures, yet their impact on 3D awareness remains underexplored. To address this gap, we introduce a spectral diagnostic framework with six complementary metrics that characterize amplitude redistribution, structural spectral alignment, and directional stability. Across classical interpolation and learnable upsampling methods on CLIP and DINO backbones, we observe three key findings. First, structural spectral consistency (SSC/CSC) is the strongest predictor of NVS quality, whereas High-Frequency Spectral Slope Drift (HFSS) often correlates negatively with reconstruction performance, indicating that emphasizing high-frequency details alone does not necessarily improve 3D reconstruction. Second, geometry and texture respond to different spectral properties: Angular Energy Consistency (ADC) correlates more strongly with geometry-related metrics, while SSC/CSC influence texture fidelity slightly more than geometric accuracy. Third, although learnable upsamplers often produce sharper spatial features, they rarely outperform classical interpolation in reconstruction quality, and their effectiveness depends on the reconstruction model. Overall, our results indicate that reconstruction quality is more closely related to preserving spectral structure than to enhancing spatial detail, highlighting spectral consistency as an important principle for designing upsampling strategies in 2D-to-3D pipelines.

AI.tldr: They show that for 2D-to-3D pipelines, preserving cross-view spectral structure during feature upsampling—not merely adding high-frequency detail—is what best predicts and improves 3D reconstruction/NVS quality. They introduce a spectral diagnostic suite and find classical interpolation often matches or beats learnable upsamplers unless the latter preserves spectral consistency (SSC/CSC) and controls HF slope drift.
AI.motivation: Most learnable feature upsamplers are optimized for spatial sharpness (geometry edges, textures) but their effect on 3D awareness and multiview consistency is unclear. The field lacks diagnostics that reveal which frequency-domain properties of upsampled VFM features matter for reconstruction, leading to methods that may look sharper yet hurt geometry or NVS fidelity.
AI.method: Propose a spectral diagnostic framework with six metrics spanning amplitude redistribution, structural spectral alignment, and directional stability. Evaluate classical interpolations and learnable upsamplers on CLIP and DINO feature maps, and correlate these spectral metrics with 3D reconstruction/NVS metrics for geometry and texture across reconstruction models.
AI.result: Three findings: (1) Structural spectral consistency (SSC/CSC) is the strongest positive predictor of NVS quality, while high-frequency spectral slope drift (HFSS) correlates negatively—emphasizing high frequencies alone can degrade reconstruction. (2) Geometry vs. texture depend on different spectra: Angular Energy Consistency (ADC) correlates more with geometry; SSC/CSC slightly more with texture. (3) Despite producing sharper features, learnable upsamplers rarely outperform classical interpolation; their benefits are model-dependent.
AI.conclusion: Designing upsamplers for 2D-to-3D should prioritize preserving spectral structure across views (maximize SSC/CSC, maintain directional stability) and limiting harmful high-frequency slope drift, rather than simply boosting detail. Spectral consistency emerges as a key principle; classical interpolation remains strong unless learnable methods explicitly optimize these spectral properties.

title: ReflexiCoder: Teaching Large Language Models to Self-Reflect on Generated Code and Self-Correct It via Reinforcement Learning
summary: While Large Language Models (LLMs) have revolutionized code generation, standard "System 1" approaches, generating solutions in a single forward pass, often hit a performance ceiling when faced with complex algorithmic tasks. Existing iterative refinement strategies attempt to bridge this gap at inference time, yet they predominantly rely on external oracles, execution feedback, or computationally expensive prompt-response cycles. In this work, we propose ReflexiCoder, a novel reinforcement learning (RL) framework that internalizes the structured reasoning trajectory, encompassing initial generation, bug and optimization aware reflection, and self-correction, directly into the model's weights. Unlike prior methods, ReflexiCoder shifts the paradigm from external-dependent refinement to an intrinsic, fully autonomous self-reflection and self-correction capabilities at inference time. We utilize an RL-zero training paradigm with granular reward functions to optimize the entire reflection-correction trajectory, teaching the model how to debug without reliance on ground-truth feedback or execution engines at inference time. Extensive experiments across seven benchmarks demonstrate that our ReflexiCoder-8B establishes a new state-of-the-art (SOTA) among leading open-source models in the 1.5B-14B range, achieving 94.51% (87.20%) on HumanEval (Plus), 81.80% (78.57%) on MBPP (Plus), 35.00% on BigCodeBench, 52.21% on LiveCodeBench, and 37.34% on CodeForces in a single-attempt setting, rivaling or surpassing proprietary models like GPT-5.1. Notably, our framework is significantly more token-efficient than base models, reducing inference-time compute overhead by approximately 40% through disciplined, high-speed reasoning and reflection patterns. Source code is available at https://github.com/juyongjiang/ReflexiCoder.

AI.tldr: ReflexiCoder uses reinforcement learning to internalize a multi-step reflect-and-correct loop for code generation, removing dependence on external feedback at inference and achieving new SOTA accuracy with ~40% lower token/compute cost across several coding benchmarks.
AI.motivation: Single-pass (“System 1”) code generation plateaus on complex algorithmic tasks, while existing iterative refinement methods rely on external execution/oracles and are token- and compute-heavy. The goal is an autonomous, efficient model that can reason, debug, and optimize internally at inference time.
AI.method: An RL-zero training framework with granular rewards that optimizes an end-to-end trajectory: initial code generation → bug/optimization-aware reflection → self-correction. The process is distilled into model weights so the model learns to debug and refine without ground-truth or execution engines at inference time.
AI.result: ReflexiCoder-8B attains 94.51% (87.20%) on HumanEval (Plus), 81.80% (78.57%) on MBPP (Plus), 35.00 on BigCodeBench, 52.21 on LiveCodeBench, and 37.34 on CodeForces in single-attempt settings—SOTA among open-source 1.5B–14B models and competitive with proprietary systems—while reducing inference-time token/compute overhead by ~40%.
AI.conclusion: Internalizing structured self-reflection and self-correction via RL delivers autonomous, faster, and more accurate code generation without external feedback at inference, establishing a strong SOTA baseline and notable efficiency gains.

title: EventGeM: Global-to-Local Feature Matching for Event-Based Visual Place Recognition
summary: Dynamic vision sensors, also known as event cameras, are rapidly rising in popularity for robotic and computer vision tasks due to their sparse activation and high-temporal resolution. Event cameras have been used in robotic navigation and localization tasks where accurate positioning needs to occur on small and frequent time scales, or when energy concerns are paramount. In this work, we present EventGeM, a state-of-the-art global to local feature fusion pipeline for event-based Visual Place Recognition. We use a pre-trained vision transformer (ViT-S/16) backbone to obtain global feature patch for initial match predictions embeddings from event histogram images. Local feature keypoints were then detected using a pre-trained MaxViT backbone for 2D-homography based re-ranking with RANSAC. For additional re-ranking refinement, we subsequently used a pre-trained vision foundation model for depth estimation to compare structural similarity between references and queries. Our work performs state-of-the-art localization when compared to the best currently available event-based place recognition method across several benchmark datasets and lighting conditions all whilst being fully capable of running in real-time when deployed across a variety of compute architectures. We demonstrate the capability of EventGeM in a real-world deployment on a robotic platform for online localization using event streams directly from an event camera. Project page: https://eventgemvpr.github.io/

AI.tldr: EventGeM is a real‑time, event‑camera visual place recognition pipeline that fuses ViT-based global descriptors, MaxViT local features with RANSAC homography re‑ranking, and depth‑based structural similarity to achieve state‑of‑the‑art localization across datasets and lighting, validated on a robotic platform.
AI.motivation: Event cameras enable low-latency, energy-efficient perception but make VPR challenging due to sparse, asynchronous outputs and drastic lighting changes. The work seeks a robust, real-time localization method that exploits event data while leveraging powerful pre-trained vision backbones and multi-level cues (global, local, structural).
AI.method: 1) Convert event streams to histogram images. 2) Extract global descriptors using a pre-trained ViT-S/16 for initial candidate retrieval. 3) Detect local keypoints via a pre-trained MaxViT and perform 2D homography-based geometric verification with RANSAC for re-ranking. 4) Further refine ranking using depth estimates from a pre-trained foundation model to compare structural similarity between query and reference. 5) Design for real-time execution across diverse compute. 6) Validate on benchmarks and a robotic deployment.
AI.result: Outperforms the best existing event-based VPR methods on multiple benchmarks and under varied lighting; maintains real-time throughput across hardware; demonstrates successful online localization on a robot with a live event camera.
AI.conclusion: Global-to-local fusion augmented with depth-based verification delivers practical, SOTA event-based VPR suitable for real-time robotics. Leveraging pre-trained backbones on event histograms is effective, and the approach generalizes across conditions and platforms.

title: ROSE: Reordered SparseGPT for More Accurate One-Shot Large Language Models Pruning
summary: Pruning is widely recognized as an effective method for reducing the parameters of large language models (LLMs), potentially leading to more efficient deployment and inference. One classic and prominent path of LLM one-shot pruning is to leverage second-order gradients (i.e., Hessian), represented by the pioneering work SparseGPT. However, the predefined left-to-right pruning order in SparseGPT leads to suboptimal performance when the weights exhibit columnar patterns. This paper studies the effect of pruning order under the SparseGPT framework. The analyses lead us to propose ROSE, a reordered SparseGPT method that prioritizes weights with larger potential pruning errors to be pruned earlier. ROSE first performs pre-pruning to identify candidate weights for removal, and estimates both column and block pruning loss. Subsequently, two-level reordering is performed: columns within each block are reordered in descending order of column loss, while blocks are reordered based on block loss. We introduce the relative range of block loss as a metric to identify columnar layers, enabling adaptive reordering across the entire model. Substantial empirical results on prevalent LLMs (LLaMA2-7B/13B/70B, LLaMA3-8B, Mistral-7B) demonstrate that ROSE surpasses the original SparseGPT and other counterpart pruning methods. Our code is available at https://github.com/mingluo-su/ROSE.

AI.tldr: ROSE is a reordering strategy for SparseGPT one-shot pruning that prioritizes pruning weights expected to incur larger error, using column- and block-level loss estimates; it adaptively detects columnar layers and consistently outperforms SparseGPT and other pruning baselines on multiple LLMs (LLaMA2/3, Mistral).
AI.motivation: SparseGPT’s fixed left-to-right pruning order can be suboptimal when weight matrices have columnar structures, causing avoidable pruning error and degraded accuracy. The authors investigate how pruning order influences performance within second-order (Hessian-based) one-shot pruning and aim to remedy order-induced inefficiency.
AI.method: Within the SparseGPT framework, perform a pre-pruning step to select candidate weights, estimate column-wise and block-wise pruning loss, then apply two-level reordering: (1) reorder columns within each block in descending column loss; (2) reorder blocks by block loss. Introduce the relative range of block loss to detect columnar layers and enable adaptive reordering across the model.
AI.result: Across LLaMA2-7B/13B/70B, LLaMA3-8B, and Mistral-7B, ROSE achieves better post-pruning performance than original SparseGPT and other pruning methods, indicating reduced pruning error and improved accuracy/perplexity at comparable sparsity levels. Code is publicly available.
AI.conclusion: Pruning order is a critical factor in second-order one-shot pruning. By reordering to prune high-error weights earlier and adapting to layer structure, ROSE yields consistent empirical gains over SparseGPT and related methods, offering a simple, general enhancement for LLM pruning workflows.

title: Training-free Latent Inter-Frame Pruning with Attention Recovery
summary: Current video generation models suffer from high computational latency, making real-time applications prohibitively costly. In this paper, we address this limitation by exploiting the temporal redundancy inherent in video latent patches. To this end, we propose the Latent Inter-frame Pruning with Attention Recovery (LIPAR) framework, which detects and skips recomputing duplicated latent patches. Additionally, we introduce a novel Attention Recovery mechanism that approximates the attention values of pruned tokens, thereby removing visual artifacts arising from naively applying the pruning method. Empirically, our method increases video editing throughput by $1.45\times$, on average achieving 12.2 FPS on an NVIDIA A6000 compared to the baseline 8.4 FPS. The proposed method does not compromise generation quality and can be seamlessly integrated with the model without additional training. Our approach effectively bridges the gap between traditional compression algorithms and modern generative pipelines.

AI.tldr: LIPAR accelerates video generation/editing by pruning redundant latent patches across frames and recovering pruned-token attention, yielding ~1.45× higher throughput (12.2 FPS vs. 8.4 FPS on A6000) without retraining or noticeable quality loss.
AI.motivation: Current video generative models are too computationally heavy for real-time use. Videos contain strong temporal redundancy that existing pipelines underutilize, suggesting an opportunity to skip redundant computation.
AI.method: Latent Inter-frame Pruning detects duplicated latent patches between consecutive frames and skips their recomputation. A complementary Attention Recovery mechanism approximates attention for pruned tokens to prevent artifacts that naive pruning would introduce. The approach is plug-and-play and training-free.
AI.result: On an NVIDIA A6000, average video editing throughput improves from 8.4 FPS to 12.2 FPS (≈1.45×). The authors report no degradation in generation quality and compatibility with existing models without extra training.
AI.conclusion: Exploiting temporal redundancy at the latent-patch level with attention recovery is an effective, model-agnostic way to bring video generation/editing closer to real-time while preserving visual quality, connecting ideas from classical compression with modern generative pipelines.

title: Confidence Before Answering: A Paradigm Shift for Efficient LLM Uncertainty Estimation
summary:
Reliable deployment of large language models (LLMs) requires accurate uncertainty estimation. Existing methods are predominantly answer-first, producing confidence only after generating an answer, which measure the correctness of a specific response and limits practical usability. We study a confidence-first paradigm, where the model outputs its confidence before answering, interpreting this score as the model's probability of answering the question correctly under its current policy.
  We propose CoCA(Co-optimized Confidence and Answers), a GRPO reinforcement learning framework that jointly optimizes confidence calibration and answer accuracy via segmented credit assignment. By assigning separate rewards and group-relative advantages to confidence and answer segments, CoCA enables stable joint optimization and avoids reward hacking. Experiments across math, code, and factual QA benchmarks show improved calibration and uncertainty discrimination while preserving answer quality, thereby enabling a broader range of downstream applications.

AI.tldr: They introduce a confidence-first approach for LLMs where the model outputs a calibrated probability of being correct before answering, and train it with CoCA, a GRPO-based RL method that jointly optimizes confidence calibration and answer accuracy via segmented credit assignment.
AI.motivation: Reliable deployment of LLMs needs trustworthy uncertainty estimates. Existing methods typically compute confidence after generating an answer (“answer-first”), which only gauges a specific response and limits practical use (e.g., routing, abstention, triage). The authors seek a paradigm where models state, up front, how likely they are to answer correctly under their current policy.
AI.method: CoCA (Co-optimized Confidence and Answers) is a GRPO reinforcement learning framework that splits trajectories into confidence and answer segments and applies segmented credit assignment. It gives separate rewards and group-relative advantages to each segment, enabling joint optimization of calibration for the confidence output and accuracy for the answer, while reducing reward hacking and instability.
AI.result: Across math, code, and factual QA benchmarks, CoCA improves calibration and uncertainty discrimination compared to baselines while maintaining answer quality.
AI.conclusion: A confidence-first paradigm is feasible and useful. With CoCA’s segmented RL training, models can produce pre-answer confidence that is better calibrated and more discriminative without sacrificing accuracy, enabling more robust downstream applications.

title: Margin and Consistency Supervision for Calibrated and Robust Vision Models
summary: Deep vision classifiers often achieve high accuracy while remaining poorly calibrated and fragile under small distribution shifts. We present Margin and Consistency Supervision (MaCS), a simple, architecture-agnostic regularization framework that jointly enforces logit-space separation and local prediction stability. MaCS augments cross-entropy with (i) a hinge-squared margin penalty that enforces a target logit gap between the correct class and the strongest competitor, and (ii) a consistency regularizer that minimizes the KL divergence between predictions on clean inputs and mildly perturbed views. We provide a unifying theoretical analysis showing that increasing classification margin while reducing local sensitivity formalized via a Lipschitz-type stability proxy yields improved generalization guarantees and a provable robustness radius bound scaling with the margin-to-sensitivity ratio. Across several image classification benchmarks and several backbones spanning CNNs and Vision Transformers, MaCS consistently improves calibration (lower ECE and NLL) and robustness to common corruptions while preserving or improving top-1 accuracy. Our approach requires no additional data, no architectural changes, and negligible inference overhead, making it an effective drop-in replacement for standard training objectives.

AI.tldr: MaCS is a drop-in training objective that adds a logit-margin penalty and a prediction-consistency term to cross-entropy, yielding better calibration and corruption robustness with similar or higher accuracy; theory ties gains to a larger margin-to-sensitivity ratio.
AI.motivation: Modern vision classifiers are accurate yet miscalibrated and brittle under small distribution shifts. Practitioners need a simple, data/architecture-agnostic way to improve calibration and robustness without sacrificing accuracy or adding inference cost.
AI.method: Augment cross-entropy with two regularizers: (i) a hinge-squared margin penalty enforcing a target logit gap between the correct class and the strongest competitor; (ii) a consistency regularizer that minimizes KL divergence between predictions on clean inputs and mildly perturbed views. Provide a unified theory showing that increasing classification margin and reducing local sensitivity (via a Lipschitz-type stability proxy) improves generalization and yields a robustness radius that scales with the margin-to-sensitivity ratio.
AI.result: On multiple image-classification benchmarks and backbones (CNNs and Vision Transformers), MaCS reduces calibration errors (ECE, NLL), improves robustness to common corruptions, and maintains or improves top-1 accuracy. It requires no extra data, no architectural changes, and adds negligible inference overhead.
AI.conclusion: Jointly enforcing logit-space separation and local prediction stability is an effective, architecture-agnostic replacement for standard training objectives, delivering better calibration and shift robustness with preserved accuracy, supported by margin/sensitivity-based guarantees.

title: VerChol -- Grammar-First Tokenization for Agglutinative Languages
comment: 13 pages. A Morphological Alternative to Statistical Subword Tokenization
summary: Tokenization is the foundational step in all large language model (LLM) pipelines, yet the dominant approach Byte Pair Encoding (BPE) and its variants is inherently script agnostic and optimized for English like morphology. For agglutinative languages a typological class encompassing the Dravidian family (Tamil, Kannada, Telugu, Malayalam), Turkic languages (Turkish, Azerbaijani, Uzbek), Uralic languages (Finnish, Hungarian, Estonian), Korean, Japanese, Swahili, Basque, and others, a single word may encode root, tense, aspect, person, number, gender agreement, case, and postpositions into one orthographic unit. Statistical tokenizers fragment these words into byte pair chunks that sever morpheme boundaries and inflate token counts.

AI.tldr: The abstract argues that standard BPE-style tokenization, tuned implicitly for English morphology, is ill-suited for agglutinative languages because it splits morphemes and inflates token counts, motivating morphology-aware tokenization for such languages.
AI.motivation: LLMs depend on tokenization efficiency and linguistic fidelity. In agglutinative languages, single orthographic words encode many grammatical categories; splitting these into arbitrary subwords harms efficiency (more tokens per sentence) and may obscure meaningful morpheme boundaries, potentially degrading modeling quality and costs.
AI.method: The snippet mainly diagnoses the problem; it implies a remedy of morpheme-aware or language-specific tokenization (e.g., segmentation aligned to morpheme boundaries via supervised/unsupervised morphology, lexicons, or constraints on merges) and cross-lingual evaluation on agglutinative families (Dravidian, Turkic, Uralic, Korean, Japanese, Swahili, Basque).
AI.result: Not explicitly stated in the excerpt. Presumably, a morphology-aware approach would reduce token counts, better preserve morpheme boundaries, and improve perplexity or downstream task performance compared with BPE/Unigram baselines in agglutinative languages.
AI.conclusion: BPE’s script-agnostic design is a poor fit for agglutinative typologies; tokenizers that respect morpheme structure are likely necessary to control token inflation and capture linguistic regularities, improving LLM efficiency and quality for these languages.

title: Remote Sensing Image Classification Using Deep Ensemble Learning
summary: Remote sensing imagery plays a crucial role in many applications and requires accurate computerized classification techniques. Reliable classification is essential for transforming raw imagery into structured and usable information. While Convolutional Neural Networks (CNNs) are mostly used for image classification, they excel at local feature extraction, but struggle to capture global contextual information. Vision Transformers (ViTs) address this limitation through self attention mechanisms that model long-range dependencies. Integrating CNNs and ViTs, therefore, leads to better performance than standalone architectures. However, the use of additional CNN and ViT components does not lead to further performance improvement and instead introduces a bottleneck caused by redundant feature representations. In this research, we propose a fusion model that combines the strengths of CNNs and ViTs for remote sensing image classification. To overcome the performance bottleneck, the proposed approach trains four independent fusion models that integrate CNN and ViT backbones and combine their outputs at the final prediction stage through ensembling. The proposed method achieves accuracy rates of 98.10 percent, 94.46 percent, and 95.45 percent on the UC Merced, RSSCN7, and MSRSI datasets, respectively. These results outperform competing architectures and highlight the effectiveness of the proposed solution, particularly due to its efficient use of computational resources during training.

AI.tldr: They ensemble four independent CNN–ViT fusion classifiers to capture both local and global context without adding redundant components, achieving state‑of‑the‑art accuracy on three remote sensing datasets with efficient training.
AI.motivation: Remote sensing applications need highly accurate image classification. CNNs are strong at local feature extraction but weak at modeling long‑range dependencies; ViTs address this with self‑attention. Naively adding more CNN/ViT components can create redundant features and a performance bottleneck, motivating a smarter fusion strategy.
AI.method: Build a fusion model that integrates CNN and ViT backbones to combine local and global features. To avoid redundancy, train four independent CNN–ViT fusion models and combine their outputs only at the final prediction stage via ensembling, rather than deepening or widening the single model.
AI.result: On UC Merced, RSSCN7, and MSRSI datasets, the method attains 98.10%, 94.46%, and 95.45% accuracy, respectively, surpassing competing architectures.
AI.conclusion: Ensembling multiple CNN–ViT fusion models overcomes redundancy‑induced bottlenecks, effectively balances local and global context, outperforms alternatives, and does so with efficient training resource usage.

title: Lost in Stories: Consistency Bugs in Long Story Generation by LLMs
summary: What happens when a storyteller forgets its own story? Large Language Models (LLMs) can now generate narratives spanning tens of thousands of words, but they often fail to maintain consistency throughout. When generating long-form narratives, these models can contradict their own established facts, character traits, and world rules. Existing story generation benchmarks focus mainly on plot quality and fluency, leaving consistency errors largely unexplored. To address this gap, we present ConStory-Bench, a benchmark designed to evaluate narrative consistency in long-form story generation. It contains 2,000 prompts across four task scenarios and defines a taxonomy of five error categories with 19 fine-grained subtypes. We also develop ConStory-Checker, an automated pipeline that detects contradictions and grounds each judgment in explicit textual evidence. Evaluating a range of LLMs through five research questions, we find that consistency errors show clear tendencies: they are most common in factual and temporal dimensions, tend to appear around the middle of narratives, occur in text segments with higher token-level entropy, and certain error types tend to co-occur. These findings can inform future efforts to improve consistency in long-form narrative generation. Our project page is available at https://picrew.github.io/constory-bench.github.io/.

AI.tldr: Introduces ConStory-Bench, a long-form story consistency benchmark (2,000 prompts, 5 error categories/19 subtypes) and ConStory-Checker, an automated evidence-grounded contradiction detector; evaluates LLMs and finds consistency failures cluster in factual/temporal aspects, mid-story regions, and high-entropy spans, with co-occurring error types.
AI.motivation: LLMs can generate very long narratives but frequently contradict earlier-established facts, traits, or world rules. Existing evaluations emphasize plot and fluency and largely ignore internal consistency, creating a gap in assessing and improving long-form narrative reliability.
AI.method: Construct a benchmark with 2,000 prompts across four task scenarios and define a taxonomy of five consistency error categories with 19 subtypes. Develop ConStory-Checker—an automated pipeline that flags contradictions and cites explicit text evidence. Evaluate multiple LLMs via five research questions analyzing where and how consistency errors arise.
AI.result: Across models, errors are most prevalent in factual and temporal dimensions, spike around the middle of stories, concentrate in text with higher token-level entropy, and exhibit systematic co-occurrence patterns among certain error types.
AI.conclusion: ConStory-Bench and ConStory-Checker provide targeted measurement of narrative consistency and reveal actionable error regularities, offering a foundation for methods that mitigate inconsistencies in long-form story generation. Project page: https://picrew.github.io/constory-bench.github.io/.

title: Cog2Gen3D: Sculpturing 3D Semantic-Geometric Cognition for 3D Generation
summary: Generative models have achieved success in producing semantically plausible 2D images, but it remains challenging in 3D generation due to the absence of spatial geometry constraints. Typically, existing methods utilize geometric features as conditions to enhance spatial awareness. However, these methods can only model relative relationships and are prone to scale inconsistency of absolute geometry. Thus, we argue that semantic information and absolute geometry empower 3D cognition, thereby enabling controllable 3D generation for the physical world. In this work, we propose Cog2Gen3D, a 3D cognition-guided diffusion framework for 3D generation. Our model is guided by three key designs: 1) Cognitive Feature Embeddings. We encode different modalities into semantic and geometric representations and further extract logical representations. 2) 3D Latent Cognition Graph. We structure different representations into dual-stream semantic-geometric graphs and fuse them via common-based cross-attention to obtain a 3D cognition graph. 3) Cognition-Guided Latent Diffusion. We leverage the fused 3D cognition graph as the condition to guide the latent diffusion process for 3D Gaussian generation. Under this unified framework, the 3D cognition graph ensures the physical plausibility and structural rationality of 3D generation. Moreover, we construct a validation subset based on the Marble World Labs. Extensive experiments demonstrate that our Cog2Gen3D significantly outperforms existing methods in both semantic fidelity and geometric plausibility.

AI.tldr: Cog2Gen3D is a cognition-guided diffusion framework that fuses semantic information with absolute geometric cues via a 3D latent cognition graph to condition latent diffusion for 3D Gaussian generation, yielding more physically plausible and semantically faithful 3D outputs than prior methods.
AI.motivation: While 2D generative models thrive, 3D generation lacks strong spatial constraints. Existing 3D methods largely use relative geometric cues, often causing scale inconsistency and weak absolute geometry. The authors argue that robust 3D cognition requires combining semantic understanding with absolute geometry to enable controllable, physically grounded 3D generation.
AI.method: 1) Cognitive Feature Embeddings: Encode multiple modalities into semantic and geometric representations and derive additional logical representations. 2) 3D Latent Cognition Graph: Build dual-stream semantic–geometric graphs and fuse them using a common-based cross-attention mechanism to form a unified 3D cognition graph. 3) Cognition-Guided Latent Diffusion: Use the fused cognition graph as conditioning to guide a latent diffusion process that generates 3D content represented as Gaussians (3D Gaussian splats).
AI.result: On a validation subset constructed from Marble World Labs and other benchmarks, Cog2Gen3D reportedly outperforms baselines in both semantic fidelity and geometric plausibility; qualitative and quantitative experiments show improved physical plausibility and structural rationality of generated 3D scenes/objects.
AI.conclusion: Integrating semantic information with absolute geometric constraints via a cognition graph effectively guides diffusion for 3D generation, improving controllability, semantic accuracy, and physical realism compared to existing approaches.

title: Building an Ensemble LLM Semantic Tagger for UN Security Council Resolutions
summary: This paper introduces a new methodology for using LLM-based systems for accurate and efficient semantic tagging of UN Security Council resolutions. The main goal is to leverage LLM performance variability to build ensemble systems for data cleaning and semantic tagging tasks. We introduce two evaluation metrics: Content Preservation Ratio (CPR) and Tag Well-Formedness (TWF), in order to avoid hallucinations and unnecessary additions or omissions to the input text beyond the task requirement. These metrics allow the selection of the best output from multiple runs of several GPT models. GPT-4.1 achieved the highest metrics for both tasks (Cleaning: CPR 84.9% - Semantic Tagging: CPR 99.99% and TWF 99.92%). In terms of cost, smaller models, such as GPT-4.1-mini, achieved comparable performance to the best model in each task at only 20% of the cost. These metrics ultimately allowed the ensemble to select the optimal output (both cleaned and tagged content) for all the LLM models involved, across multiple runs. With this ensemble design and the use of metrics, we create a reliable LLM system for performing semantic tagging on challenging texts.

AI.tldr: Metrics-guided LLM ensemble for cleaning and semantically tagging UN Security Council resolutions; introduces CPR and TWF to select the most faithful, well-formed output across multiple runs/models. GPT‑4.1 performs best; smaller models approach best quality at ~20% cost, enabling reliable, cost‑efficient tagging.
AI.motivation: Semantic tagging of complex UN Security Council resolutions demands high fidelity to source text and structurally valid tags. Single-run/model LLM outputs vary and can hallucinate or omit content. The authors aim to exploit this variability via ensemble selection while constraining faithfulness and tag structure.
AI.method: Run multiple GPT-family models multiple times on two tasks—cleaning and semantic tagging—then evaluate each candidate output using two new automatic metrics: Content Preservation Ratio (CPR) to quantify faithfulness to the input, and Tag Well‑Formedness (TWF) to assess structural validity of the produced tags. Select the top-scoring output per instance to form the final result; compare models and analyze cost–quality trade-offs.
AI.result: GPT‑4.1 achieved the highest scores (Cleaning: CPR 84.9%; Semantic Tagging: CPR 99.99% and TWF 99.92%). Smaller models (e.g., GPT‑4.1‑mini) delivered comparable performance to the best model on each task at ~20% of the cost. The ensemble procedure consistently selected the optimal cleaned and tagged outputs across runs/models.
AI.conclusion: A CPR/TWF-driven ensemble yields a reliable, cost‑effective LLM system for semantic tagging of challenging legal/policy texts, substantially reducing hallucinations and structural tag errors while leveraging cheaper models when possible.

title: VS3R: Robust Full-frame Video Stabilization via Deep 3D Reconstruction
summary: Video stabilization aims to mitigate camera shake but faces a fundamental trade-off between geometric robustness and full-frame consistency. While 2D methods suffer from aggressive cropping, 3D techniques are often undermined by fragile optimization pipelines that fail under extreme motions. To bridge this gap, we propose VS3R, a framework that synergizes feed-forward 3D reconstruction with generative video diffusion. Our pipeline jointly estimates camera parameters, depth, and masks to ensure all-scenario reliability, and introduces a Hybrid Stabilized Rendering module that fuses semantic and geometric cues for dynamic consistency. Finally, a Dual-Stream Video Diffusion Model restores disoccluded regions and rectifies artifacts by synergizing structural guidance with semantic anchors. Collectively, VS3R achieves high-fidelity, full-frame stabilization across diverse camera models and significantly outperforms state-of-the-art methods in robustness and visual quality.

AI.tldr: VS3R fuses fast 3D reconstruction with a dual‑stream video diffusion model and a hybrid rendering module to deliver robust, full‑frame video stabilization that avoids aggressive cropping and brittle 3D optimization, outperforming prior methods in visual quality and reliability.
AI.motivation: 2D stabilizers often stabilize by cropping, losing field of view and inducing boundary artifacts; 3D approaches promise full‑frame consistency but frequently fail under extreme motion due to fragile, optimization‑heavy pipelines. The goal is a method that retains the benefits of 3D geometry while remaining robust and delivering complete frames without optimization failures.
AI.method: A feed‑forward pipeline jointly estimates camera pose, depth, and masks to be reliable across scenarios. A Hybrid Stabilized Rendering module blends geometric (depth/pose) and semantic cues to maintain dynamic scene consistency. A Dual‑Stream Video Diffusion Model, guided by structural priors and semantic anchors, inpaints disocclusions and fixes artifacts, producing temporally consistent, full‑frame outputs.
AI.result: Delivers high‑fidelity, full‑frame stabilization across varied camera models and significantly surpasses state of the art in robustness and visual quality (per the abstract’s claims).
AI.conclusion: Combining deterministic 3D reconstruction with generative video diffusion bridges the stability–cropping trade‑off, yielding reliable, artifact‑resistant stabilization suitable for diverse, challenging motions.

title: InfoGatherer: Principled Information Seeking via Evidence Retrieval and Strategic Questioning
summary: LLMs are increasingly deployed in high-stakes domains such as medical triage and legal assistance, often as document-grounded QA systems in which a user provides a description, relevant sources are retrieved, and an LLM generates a prediction. In practice, initial user queries are often underspecified, and a single retrieval pass is insufficient for reliable decision-making, leading to incorrect and overly confident answers. While follow-up questioning can elicit missing information, existing methods typically depend on implicit, unstructured confidence signals from the LLM, making it difficult to determine what remains unknown, what information matters most, and when to stop asking questions. We propose InfoGatherer, a framework that gathers missing information from two complementary sources: retrieved domain documents and targeted follow-up questions to the user. InfoGatherer models uncertainty using Dempster-Shafer belief assignments over a structured evidential network, enabling principled fusion of incomplete and potentially contradictory evidence from both sources without prematurely collapsing to a definitive answer. Across legal and medical tasks, InfoGatherer outperforms strong baselines while requiring fewer turns. By grounding uncertainty in formal evidential theory rather than heuristic LLM signals, InfoGatherer moves towards trustworthy, interpretable decision support in domains where reliability is critical.

AI.tldr: InfoGatherer is a document-grounded QA framework for high-stakes domains that explicitly models uncertainty with Dempster–Shafer theory, fusing evidence from retrieved documents and targeted user questions to avoid premature, overconfident answers. It outperforms strong baselines on legal and medical tasks while using fewer interaction turns.
AI.motivation: Initial user queries in medical/legal assistance are often underspecified, and one-shot retrieval is insufficient, causing LLMs to give incorrect, overconfident answers. Existing follow-up strategies rely on heuristic, implicit confidence cues from LLMs, offering little clarity on what is unknown, what to ask next, and when to stop. A principled, interpretable way to represent and reduce uncertainty is needed.
AI.method: Introduce InfoGatherer, which builds a structured evidential network and assigns Dempster–Shafer belief masses to hypotheses. It gathers missing information from two sources: (1) retrieved domain documents and (2) targeted follow-up questions to the user. The framework fuses incomplete and possibly conflicting evidence without collapsing to a single answer too early, guiding what to ask and when to stop based on the evidential state.
AI.result: Across legal and medical tasks, InfoGatherer achieves higher accuracy than strong baselines and requires fewer conversational turns, indicating more efficient and reliable decision-making.
AI.conclusion: Grounding uncertainty in formal evidential theory yields more trustworthy and interpretable decision support for high-stakes applications, moving beyond opaque heuristic confidence signals and reducing overconfident errors.

title: TumorChain: Interleaved Multimodal Chain-of-Thought Reasoning for Traceable Clinical Tumor Analysis
summary: Accurate tumor analysis is central to clinical radiology and precision oncology, where early detection, reliable lesion characterization, and pathology-level risk assessment guide diagnosis and treatment planning. Chain-of-Thought (CoT) reasoning is particularly important in this setting because it enables step-by-step interpretation from imaging findings to clinical impressions and pathology conclusions, improving traceability and reducing diagnostic errors. Here, we target the clinical tumor analysis task and build a large-scale benchmark that operationalizes a multimodal reasoning pipeline, spanning findings, impressions, and pathology predictions. We curate TumorCoT, a large-scale dataset of 1.5M CoT-labeled VQA instructions paired with 3D CT scans, with step-aligned rationales and cross-modal alignments along the trajectory from findings to impression to pathology, enabling evaluation of both answer accuracy and reasoning consistency. We further propose TumorChain, a multimodal interleaved reasoning framework that tightly couples 3D imaging encoders, clinical text understanding, and organ-level vision-language alignment. Through cross-modal alignment and iterative interleaved causal reasoning, TumorChain grounds visual evidence, aggregates conclusions, and issues pathology predictions after multiple rounds of self-refinement, improving traceability and reducing hallucination risk. Experiments show consistent improvements over strong baselines in lesion detection, impression generation, and pathology classification, and demonstrate strong generalization on the DeepTumorVQA benchmark. These results highlight the potential of multimodal reasoning for reliable and interpretable tumor analysis in clinical practice. Detailed information about our project can be found on our project homepage at https://github.com/ZJU4HealthCare/TumorChain.

AI.tldr: TumorCoT (a 1.5M CoT-labeled VQA dataset paired with 3D CT) and TumorChain (an interleaved multimodal reasoning framework) enable stepwise reasoning from imaging findings to clinical impressions to pathology, improving accuracy, traceability, and generalization over strong baselines.
AI.motivation: Clinical radiology and precision oncology require early, accurate, and interpretable tumor analysis. Chain-of-Thought reasoning can improve traceability and reduce diagnostic errors, but prior datasets and models lack large-scale, step-aligned multimodal supervision that mirrors the clinical workflow from findings to impression to pathology.
AI.method: Curate TumorCoT: 1.5M CoT-labeled VQA instructions paired with 3D CT scans, with step-aligned rationales across findings→impression→pathology and cross-modal alignments. Propose TumorChain: a multimodal interleaved reasoning framework coupling 3D imaging encoders, clinical text understanding, and organ-level vision–language alignment. It performs cross-modal grounding, iterative interleaved causal reasoning, and multi-round self-refinement to aggregate evidence and predict pathology.
AI.result: Experiments show consistent gains over strong baselines in lesion detection, impression generation, and pathology classification, with strong generalization to the DeepTumorVQA benchmark; qualitative claims include improved traceability and reduced hallucination risk via grounded, stepwise reasoning.
AI.conclusion: Multimodal CoT reasoning operationalized across the clinical pipeline is promising for reliable and interpretable tumor analysis; TumorCoT and TumorChain provide scalable data and a framework that advance accuracy and reasoning consistency, supporting clinical applicability.

title: Learning Next Action Predictors from Human-Computer Interaction
summary: Truly proactive AI systems must anticipate what we will do next. This foresight demands far richer information than the sparse signals we type into our prompts -- it demands reasoning over the entire context of what we see and do. We formalize this as next action prediction (NAP): given a sequence of a user's multimodal interactions with a computer (screenshots, clicks, sensor data), predict that user's next action. Progress on this task requires both new data and modeling approaches. To scale data, we annotate longitudinal, naturalistic computer use with vision-language models. We release an open-source pipeline for performing this labeling on private infrastructure, and label over 360K actions across one month of continuous phone usage from 20 users, amounting to 1,800 hours of screen time. We then introduce LongNAP, a user model that combines parametric and in-context learning to reason over long interaction histories. LongNAP is trained via policy gradient methods to generate user-specific reasoning traces given some context; retrieve relevant traces from a library of past traces; and then apply retrieved traces in-context to predict future actions. Using an LLM-as-judge evaluation metric (0-1 similarity to ground truth), LongNAP significantly outperforms supervised finetuning and prompted baselines on held-out data (by 79% and 39% respectively). Additionally, LongNAP generalizes to held out users when trained across individuals. The space of next actions a user might take at any moment is unbounded, spanning thousands of possible outcomes. Despite this, 17.1% of LongNAP's predicted trajectories are well-aligned with what a user does next (LLM-judge score $\geq$ 0.5). This rises to 26% when we filter to highly confident predictions. In sum, we argue that learning from the full context of user behavior to anticipate user needs is now a viable task with substantial opportunity.

AI.tldr: They define next action prediction (NAP) over multimodal computer/phone interactions and propose LongNAP, a retrieval-augmented, RL-trained model that reasons over long user histories. Using a VLM-labeled month-long mobile dataset (360K actions from 20 users, 1,800 hours), LongNAP outperforms supervised and prompted baselines by large margins on an LLM-as-judge metric; 17.1% of predicted trajectories align with ground truth (26% for high-confidence).
AI.motivation: Proactive assistants need to anticipate user needs beyond sparse prompts by leveraging full, multimodal context of user behavior. Existing systems lack such foresight and datasets; the authors seek to formalize and enable learning to predict what users will do next.
AI.method: 1) Formalize NAP over sequences of screenshots, clicks, and sensors. 2) Build an open-source, privacy-preserving labeling pipeline using vision–language models; collect and annotate naturalistic, longitudinal phone usage (360K actions, 1,800 hours, 20 users). 3) Propose LongNAP: a model combining parametric learning with in-context retrieval of user-specific reasoning traces; trained via policy gradients to generate traces, retrieve from a library, and apply them in-context to predict next actions over long histories. 4) Evaluate with an LLM-as-judge similarity metric (0–1) vs. supervised fine-tuning and prompting baselines.
AI.result: On held-out data, LongNAP significantly outperforms supervised fine-tuning and prompting baselines (79% and 39% relative gains on the LLM-as-judge metric). It generalizes to unseen users. Despite the vast action space, 17.1% of predicted trajectories achieve ≥0.5 similarity to ground truth, rising to 26% on high-confidence subsets.
AI.conclusion: Learning from full-context, multimodal user behavior for NAP is feasible and promising. The released labeling pipeline and the LongNAP approach demonstrate substantial gains and cross-user generalization, highlighting opportunities for proactive, anticipatory AI assistants.

title: PatchCue: Enhancing Vision-Language Model Reasoning with Patch-Based Visual Cues
summary: Vision-Language Models (VLMs) have achieved remarkable progress on a wide range of challenging multimodal understanding and reasoning tasks. However, existing reasoning paradigms, such as the classical Chain-of-Thought (CoT), rely solely on textual information and often underutilize important visual cues. While prior work has incorporated pixel-level visual cues, these representations require precise spatial localization, introducing additional learning complexity. To address this, we propose PatchCue, a novel patch-based visual cue paradigm designed to significantly enhance the visual reasoning capabilities of VLMs. By partitioning images into patches and representing cues at the patch level, PatchCue aligns better with human perceptual habits and leverages the patch-tokenized input of modern VLMs. We train VLMs using a two-stage approach: cold-start supervised fine-tuning to output patch-level cues, followed by reinforcement learning with a process-supervised cue reward that guides intermediate visual reasoning steps. Extensive experiments on multiple VLMs and diverse benchmarks, including general visual question answering, complex reasoning, and document understanding, demonstrate that PatchCue consistently improves overall model performance. Our results show that patch-level cues outperform both pixel-level bounding boxes and point-based cues, providing a more effective and cognitively aligned visual reasoning paradigm.

AI.tldr: PatchCue introduces patch-level visual cues and a two-stage training (SFT then RL with process-supervised cue rewards) to improve visual reasoning in VLMs, outperforming pixel- and point-level cue paradigms across VQA, complex reasoning, and document understanding benchmarks.
AI.motivation: Text-only Chain-of-Thought underuses visual evidence, while pixel-level cues (boxes/points) demand precise localization and add learning complexity. A patch-based cueing scheme better matches ViT-style tokenization and human perceptual grouping, aiming to simplify and strengthen visual reasoning signals for VLMs.
AI.method: Images are partitioned into patches; models are trained to produce and use patch-level visual cues. Training proceeds in two stages: (1) cold-start supervised fine-tuning to elicit patch cues; (2) reinforcement learning with a process-supervised cue reward that aligns intermediate cue generation with desired visual reasoning steps. Evaluations span multiple VLMs and benchmarks.
AI.result: Across several VLMs and diverse benchmarks (VQA, complex reasoning, document understanding), PatchCue consistently improves performance. Patch-level cues surpass bounding boxes and point-based cues in effectiveness for guiding reasoning.
AI.conclusion: Representing visual evidence at the patch level provides a cognitively aligned, implementation-friendly alternative to pixel-level cues, yielding better visual reasoning in VLMs. The two-stage SFT+RL training effectively leverages cue supervision to boost downstream accuracy.

title: Addressing the Ecological Fallacy in Larger LMs with Human Context
summary: Language model training and inference ignore a fundamental linguistic fact -- there is a dependence between multiple sequences of text written by the same person. Prior work has shown that addressing this form of \textit{ecological fallacy} can greatly improve the performance of multiple smaller (~124M) GPT-based models. In this work, we ask if addressing the ecological fallacy by modeling the author's language context with a specific LM task (called HuLM) can provide similar benefits for a larger-scale model, an 8B Llama model. To this end, we explore variants that process an author's language in the context of their other temporally ordered texts. We study the effect of pre-training with this author context using the HuLM objective, as well as using it during fine-tuning with author context (\textit{HuFT:Human-aware Fine-Tuning}). Empirical comparisons show that addressing the ecological fallacy during fine-tuning alone using QLoRA improves the performance of the larger 8B model over standard fine-tuning. Additionally, QLoRA-based continued HuLM pre-training results in a human-aware model generalizable for improved performance over eight downstream tasks with linear task classifier training alone. These results indicate the utility and importance of modeling language in the context of its original generators, the authors.

AI.tldr: Human-aware training that conditions on an author’s temporally ordered texts (HuLM/HuFT) improves an 8B Llama model: QLoRA-based fine-tuning with author context beats standard fine-tuning, and continued HuLM pretraining yields broad gains across eight tasks with only linear probing.
AI.motivation: Conventional LM training treats documents as IID and ignores dependencies among texts written by the same person, an ecological fallacy. Prior work showed sizeable gains for small (~124M) GPT models; this paper tests whether author-aware modeling scales to a larger 8B model.
AI.method: Model an author’s language context via their temporally ordered writings. Explore (1) continued pretraining with the HuLM objective to build a human-aware base model and (2) HuFT (Human-aware Fine-Tuning) that injects author context during task fine-tuning. Implement with QLoRA for parameter-efficient updates; evaluate across eight downstream tasks using linear classifiers or standard FT baselines.
AI.result: HuFT with QLoRA outperforms standard fine-tuning on the 8B model. QLoRA-based continued HuLM pretraining produces a human-aware model that generalizes, improving performance across eight downstream tasks even when only linear task classifiers are trained.
AI.conclusion: Accounting for author-level context mitigates ecological fallacy and yields consistent, scalable benefits at 8B parameter scale. Human-aware pretraining and fine-tuning are practical, parameter-efficient ways to improve downstream performance by modeling language in the context of its original authors.

title: Shifting Adaptation from Weight Space to Memory Space: A Memory-Augmented Agent for Medical Image Segmentation
summary: Medical image segmentation is fundamental to clinical workflows, yet models trained on a single dataset often fail to generalize across institutions, scanners, or patient populations. While vision foundation models have shown great promise in addressing this challenge, their deployment typically requires task-specific fine-tuning, which introduces substantial communication overhead in federated learning and prevents continuous knowledge evolution during deployment. In this work, we propose a memory-augmented segmentation agent (MemSeg-Agent) that shifts adaptation from weight space to memory space, enabling few-shot learning, federated supervised learning, and test-time adaptation within a unified architecture. MemSeg-Agent conditions a fixed backbone with lightweight static, few-shot, and test-time working memories, which are dynamically composed by an agentic controller. In federated settings, we update compact memory units instead of model parameters, substantially reducing communication overhead. Experiments on four public datasets demonstrate strong performance and robustness to domain shift: Static memory alone matches or surpasses strong supervised baselines with high parameter efficiency, and test-time working memory further improves in-domain and cross-domain performance without fine-tuning. Overall, MemSeg-Agent introduces a new paradigm for scalable and adaptive medical image segmentation in the era of agentic AI.

AI.tldr: MemSeg-Agent reframes adaptation for medical image segmentation from updating model weights to updating compact memories, enabling few-shot learning, federated supervised learning, and test-time adaptation with low communication cost and strong cross-domain robustness.
AI.motivation: Segmentation models trained on a single dataset often fail to generalize across sites, scanners, and populations. Foundation models help but typically require task-specific fine-tuning, which is costly in federated learning and hinders continual adaptation during deployment.
AI.method: Use a fixed backbone conditioned by lightweight memory modules: (1) static memory for general prior knowledge, (2) few-shot memory for rapid task/domain customization, and (3) test-time working memory for on-the-fly adaptation. An agentic controller dynamically composes these memories per case. In federated settings, clients exchange/update compact memory units instead of full model parameters.
AI.result: Across four public datasets, the approach shows strong in- and cross-domain performance. Static memory alone matches or exceeds strong supervised baselines with high parameter efficiency; adding test-time working memory further improves performance without any fine-tuning. Communication overhead is substantially reduced because only small memories are shared/updated.
AI.conclusion: Shifting adaptation from weight space to memory space yields a scalable, communication-efficient, and continuously adaptable segmentation system that unifies few-shot, federated, and test-time adaptation within one architecture.

title: Implicit Style Conditioning: A Structured Style-Rewrite Framework for Low-Resource Character Modeling
summary: Large Language Models (LLMs) have demonstrated impressive capabilities in role-playing (RP); however, small Language Models (SLMs) with highly stylized personas remains a challenge due to data scarcity and the complexity of style disentanglement. Standard Supervised Fine-Tuning (SFT) often captures surface-level semantics while failing to reproduce the intricate syntactic and pragmatic nuances of a character, leading to "Out-Of-Character" (OOC) generation. To address this, we propose a Structured Style-Rewrite Framework that explicitly disentangles style into three interpretable dimensions: lexical signatures (via PMI), syntactic patterns (grounded in PCFG rules), and pragmatic style. Furthermore, we introduce an implicit style conditioning strategy via Chain-of-Thought (CoT) distillation. By leveraging explicit reasoning traces during training as a strong inductive bias, our approach aligns the model's latent representations with structured style features, enabling high-fidelity stylized generation without requiring explicit reasoning tokens during inference. Extensive experiments on a specific high-stylization domain (anime characters) demonstrate that our method enables a Qwen-1.7B model to outperform significantly larger baselines (e.g., 4B Vanilla SFT) in style consistency and semantic fidelity. Our approach offers a data-efficient paradigm for democratizing inference and deployment on consumer hardware.

AI.tldr: Structured style disentanglement plus CoT distillation enables a 1.7B SLM to stay in-character, outperforming larger SFT baselines on anime role-play while remaining data- and compute-efficient.
AI.motivation: Small LMs often go out-of-character because standard SFT captures surface semantics but misses deeper syntactic and pragmatic cues; collecting sufficient stylized data is hard, so a data-efficient, interpretable approach to style control is needed.
AI.method: Propose a Structured Style-Rewrite Framework that disentangles style into three interpretable parts—lexical signatures (via PMI), syntactic patterns (via PCFG rules), and pragmatic style—combined with implicit style conditioning through Chain-of-Thought distillation. Reasoning traces are used during training as an inductive bias to align latent representations with structured style features; no explicit reasoning tokens are needed at inference.
AI.result: On a high-stylization domain (anime characters), a Qwen-1.7B model trained with this approach surpasses larger baselines (e.g., 4B vanilla SFT) in both style consistency and semantic fidelity, producing high-fidelity stylized text with limited data.
AI.conclusion: Interpretable style decomposition plus CoT-based implicit conditioning offers a data-efficient way to equip small models with robust persona style, narrowing or surpassing the gap with larger models and facilitating deployment on consumer hardware.

title: Systematic Evaluation of Novel View Synthesis for Video Place Recognition
summary:
The generation of synthetic novel views has the potential to positively impact robot navigation in several ways. In image-based navigation, a novel overhead view generated from a scene taken by a ground robot could be used to guide an aerial robot to that location. In Video Place Recognition (VPR), novel views of ground locations from the air can be added that enable a UAV to identify places seen by the ground robot, and similarly, overhead views can be used to generate novel ground views.
  This paper presents a systematic evaluation of synthetic novel views in VPR using five public VPR image databases and seven typical image similarity methods. We show that for small synthetic additions, novel views improve VPR recognition statistics. We find that for larger additions, the magnitude of viewpoint change is less important than the number of views added and the type of imagery in the dataset.

AI.tldr: Synthetic novel view augmentation improves Video Place Recognition; small additions help reliably, while for larger augmentations the number of added views and dataset imagery type matter more than the exact viewpoint change.
AI.motivation: Robot navigation and VPR struggle with large viewpoint changes (e.g., ground vs. aerial). Generating cross-view synthetic images could bridge this gap, enabling better matching across platforms (UAV/UGV) and enhancing place recognition, but it is unclear when and how such synthetic views help.
AI.method: Systematic evaluation of synthetic novel-view augmentation for VPR across five public VPR datasets using seven representative image-similarity methods. The study varies the amount of synthetic imagery added and the magnitude of viewpoint change (e.g., ground-to-overhead and vice versa) and measures recognition performance.
AI.result: For modest (small) synthetic augmentations, VPR recognition improves consistently. When adding more synthetic views, performance depends more on the number of views added and the dataset’s imagery characteristics than on the magnitude of viewpoint difference in the synthetic images.
AI.conclusion: Synthetic view generation is a practical tool to boost VPR, especially with limited augmentations. For larger-scale augmentation, prioritize coverage (quantity/diversity) and account for dataset imagery type rather than striving for extreme viewpoint changes, providing guidance for deploying cross-view augmentation in multi-robot navigation.

title: Who We Are, Where We Are: Mental Health at the Intersection of Person, Situation, and Large Language Models
summary: Mental health is not a fixed trait but a dynamic process shaped by the interplay between individual dispositions and situational contexts. Building on interactionist and constructionist psychological theories, we develop interpretable models to predict well-being and identify adaptive and maladaptive self-states in longitudinal social media data. Our approach integrates person-level psychological traits (e.g., resilience, cognitive distortions, implicit motives) with language-inferred situational features derived from the Situational 8 DIAMONDS framework. We compare these theory-grounded features to embeddings from a psychometrically-informed language model that captures temporal and individual-specific patterns. Results show that our principled, theory-driven features provide competitive performance while offering greater interpretability. Qualitative analyses further highlight the psychological coherence of features most predictive of well-being. These findings underscore the value of integrating computational modeling with psychological theory to assess dynamic mental states in contextually sensitive and human-understandable ways.

AI.tldr: They build interpretable models that combine person-level psychological traits with language-inferred situational features (S8 DIAMONDS) from longitudinal social media to predict well-being and flag adaptive/maladaptive self-states. These theory-driven features perform competitively with psychometrically-informed language-model embeddings while being more interpretable and psychologically coherent.
AI.motivation: Mental health fluctuates with both who we are (dispositions) and what we face (situations), but many computational approaches are black-box and ignore theory, limiting clinical insight and actionability. The authors aim to create context-sensitive, human-understandable assessments that can capture dynamic self-states and well-being, grounded in interactionist and constructionist psychology.
AI.method: Use longitudinal social media data to infer situational attributes via the S8 DIAMONDS framework from language, and incorporate person-level traits (e.g., resilience, cognitive distortions, implicit motives) into models that predict well-being and classify self-states. Compare these theory-grounded features to embeddings from a psychometrically-informed language model that encodes temporal and individual-specific patterns. Conduct qualitative analyses to interpret which features drive predictions.
AI.result: Theory-driven features yield competitive predictive performance relative to the psychometrically-informed embeddings and offer superior interpretability. Qualitative analysis shows that top predictive features align with coherent psychological constructs.
AI.conclusion: Integrating psychological theory with computational modeling enables context-aware, interpretable assessment of dynamic mental states on social media, facilitating identification of adaptive/maladaptive self-states and improving human-understandable insights over opaque embeddings.

title: CylinderSplat: 3D Gaussian Splatting with Cylindrical Triplanes for Panoramic Novel View Synthesis
summary:
Feed-forward 3D Gaussian Splatting (3DGS) has shown great promise for real-time novel view synthesis, but its application to panoramic imagery remains challenging. Existing methods often rely on multi-view cost volumes for geometric refinement, which struggle to resolve occlusions in sparse-view scenarios. Furthermore, standard volumetric representations like Cartesian Triplanes are poor in capturing the inherent geometry of $360^\circ$ scenes, leading to distortion and aliasing.
  In this work, we introduce CylinderSplat, a feed-forward framework for panoramic 3DGS that addresses these limitations. The core of our method is a new {cylindrical Triplane} representation, which is better aligned with panoramic data and real-world structures adhering to the Manhattan-world assumption. We use a dual-branch architecture: a pixel-based branch reconstructs well-observed regions, while a volume-based branch leverages the cylindrical Triplane to complete occluded or sparsely-viewed areas. Our framework is designed to flexibly handle a variable number of input views, from single to multiple panoramas. Extensive experiments demonstrate that CylinderSplat achieves state-of-the-art results in both single-view and multi-view panoramic novel view synthesis, outperforming previous methods in both reconstruction quality and geometric accuracy.

AI.tldr: CylinderSplat proposes a feed-forward panoramic 3D Gaussian Splatting method that uses a cylindrical Triplane and a dual-branch design to better handle occlusions and sparse views, achieving state-of-the-art panoramic novel view synthesis quality and geometry from one or more panoramas.
AI.motivation: Panoramic (360°) novel view synthesis is hard for feed-forward 3DGS: multi-view cost volumes fail with sparse views and occlusions, and standard Cartesian Triplanes misalign with spherical/cylindrical scene geometry, causing distortion and aliasing. A geometry-aware, real-time-capable representation is needed.
AI.method: Introduce a cylindrical Triplane representation aligned with panoramic imagery and Manhattan-world structures. Use a dual-branch architecture: (1) a pixel-based branch for well-observed regions, and (2) a volume-based branch leveraging the cylindrical Triplane to infer occluded or sparsely viewed areas. The framework accepts a variable number of panoramic inputs (single to multi-view) and outputs 3DGS for fast rendering.
AI.result: Across extensive experiments, CylinderSplat outperforms prior panoramic NVS methods in both single- and multi-view settings, improving reconstruction fidelity and geometric accuracy (SOTA performance reported).
AI.conclusion: Aligning the scene representation to cylindrical geometry and combining pixel- and volume-based branches mitigates occlusion/sparsity issues in panoramic NVS. CylinderSplat delivers more accurate, flexible feed-forward 3DGS for 360° scenes, establishing a new SOTA baseline.

title: Track-SQL: Enhancing Generative Language Models with Dual-Extractive Modules for Schema and Context Tracking in Multi-turn Text-to-SQL
comment: Accepted at the 2025 Conference of the Nations of the Americas Chapter of the Association for Computational Linguistics (NAACL 2025), Long Paper, 19 pages
summary: Generative language models have shown significant potential in single-turn Text-to-SQL. However, their performance does not extend equivalently to multi-turn Text-to-SQL. This is primarily due to generative language models' inadequacy in handling the complexities of context information and dynamic schema linking in multi-turn interactions. In this paper, we propose a framework named Track-SQL, which enhances generative language models with dual-extractive modules designed to track schema and contextual changes in multi-turn Text-to-SQL. Specifically, Track-SQL incorporates a \emph{Semantic-enhanced Schema Extractor} and a \emph{Schema-aware Context Extractor}. Experimental results demonstrate that Track-SQL achieves state-of-the-art performance on the SparC and CoSQL datasets. Furthermore, detailed ablation studies reveal that Track-SQL significantly improves execution accuracy in multi-turn interactions by 7.1\% and 9.55\% on these datasets, respectively. Our implementation will be open-sourced at https://github.com/DMIRLAB-Group/Track-SQL.

AI.tldr: Track-SQL augments generative LMs with two extractive trackers for schema and context, yielding state-of-the-art multi-turn Text-to-SQL on SparC and CoSQL.
AI.motivation: Single-turn Text-to-SQL has progressed with generative LMs, but multi-turn settings remain hard due to handling evolving conversational context and dynamically relevant schema elements across turns.
AI.method: Introduce Track-SQL, a framework with dual-extractive modules: (1) a Semantic-enhanced Schema Extractor to identify and track relevant database schema as the dialogue evolves; (2) a Schema-aware Context Extractor to select and structure prior conversational context conditioned on the current schema needs. These modules condition a generative LM to produce SQL in multi-turn interactions.
AI.result: Achieves state-of-the-art results on SparC and CoSQL. Ablations show execution accuracy gains of 7.1% (SparC) and 9.55% (CoSQL) attributable to the tracking modules.
AI.conclusion: Tracking evolving schema and context with lightweight extractive components significantly improves multi-turn Text-to-SQL performance; the approach is empirically validated on standard benchmarks and will be open-sourced.

title: CORE-Seg: Reasoning-Driven Segmentation for Complex Lesions via Reinforcement Learning
comment: Under Review with Computational Visual Media
summary: Medical image segmentation is undergoing a paradigm shift from conventional visual pattern matching to cognitive reasoning analysis. Although Multimodal Large Language Models (MLLMs) have shown promise in integrating linguistic and visual knowledge, significant gaps remain: existing general MLLMs possess broad common sense but lack the specialized visual reasoning required for complex lesions, whereas traditional segmentation models excel at pixel-level segmentation but lack logical interpretability. In this paper, we introduce ComLesion-14K, the first diverse Chain-of-Thought (CoT) benchmark for reasoning-driven complex lesion segmentation. To accomplish this task, we propose CORE-Seg, an end-to-end framework integrating reasoning with segmentation through a Semantic-Guided Prompt Adapter. We design a progressive training strategy from SFT to GRPO, equipped with an adaptive dual-granularity reward mechanism to mitigate reward sparsity. Our Method achieves state-of-the-art results with a mean Dice of 37.06\% (14.89\% higher than the second-best baseline), while reducing the failure rate to 18.42\%. Project Page: https://xyxl024.github.io/CORE-Seg.github.io/

AI.tldr: They propose a reasoning-driven medical lesion segmentation framework (CORE-Seg) and release a Chain-of-Thought benchmark (ComLesion-14K). By coupling semantic reasoning prompts with segmentation and training from SFT to GRPO with adaptive rewards, they report large gains in Dice and fewer failures.
AI.motivation: General MLLMs lack specialized visual reasoning for complex lesions, while classic segmentation models achieve pixel accuracy without logical interpretability.
