The field needs a dataset and methods that tie explicit reasoning to segmentation to improve performance and transparency on challenging medical images.
AI.method: 1) ComLesion-14K: a diverse Chain-of-Thought benchmark for complex lesion segmentation with reasoning annotations. 2) CORE-Seg: an end-to-end model that integrates reasoning with segmentation via a Semantic-Guided Prompt Adapter. 3) Progressive training from supervised fine-tuning (SFT) to GRPO reinforcement learning, using an adaptive dual-granularity reward to alleviate reward sparsity and align reasoning and pixel-wise outputs.
AI.result: State-of-the-art performance on the benchmark with mean Dice of 37.06%, which is 14.89% higher than the second-best baseline, and a reduced failure rate to 18.42%.
AI.conclusion: Coupling explicit reasoning (via CoT and semantic prompts) with segmentation and optimizing with tailored rewards can substantially improve complex lesion segmentation while enhancing interpretability; the released dataset/benchmark enables further research.

title: PixARMesh: Autoregressive Mesh-Native Single-View Scene Reconstruction
summary: We introduce PixARMesh, a method to autoregressively reconstruct complete 3D indoor scene meshes directly from a single RGB image. Unlike prior methods that rely on implicit signed distance fields and post-hoc layout optimization, PixARMesh jointly predicts object layout and geometry within a unified model, producing coherent and artist-ready meshes in a single forward pass. Building on recent advances in mesh generative models, we augment a point-cloud encoder with pixel-aligned image features and global scene context via cross-attention, enabling accurate spatial reasoning from a single image. Scenes are generated autoregressively from a unified token stream containing context, pose, and mesh, yielding compact meshes with high-fidelity geometry. Experiments on synthetic and real-world datasets show that PixARMesh achieves state-of-the-art reconstruction quality while producing lightweight, high-quality meshes ready for downstream applications.

AI.tldr: PixARMesh autoregressively reconstructs full 3D indoor scene meshes from a single RGB image, jointly inferring layout and geometry to produce compact, artist-ready meshes and achieving state-of-the-art quality on synthetic and real datasets.
AI.motivation: Prior single-image 3D scene reconstruction often relies on implicit SDFs and separate, post-hoc layout optimization, which can produce incoherent scenes and heavy, impractical outputs. There is a need for a unified model that reasons about spatial layout and object geometry jointly and outputs lightweight meshes usable directly in downstream applications.
AI.method: Build on mesh generative models and encode the input with a point-cloud encoder augmented by pixel-aligned image features and global scene context via cross-attention. Generate scenes autoregressively from a unified token stream that includes context, camera/pose, and mesh tokens, enabling end-to-end joint prediction of object layout and detailed geometry in a single forward pass to yield compact meshes.
AI.result: On both synthetic and real-world datasets, the approach attains state-of-the-art reconstruction quality and produces lightweight, high-fidelity meshes suitable for practical use.
AI.conclusion: A single-image, unified autoregressive mesh generator can coherently recover indoor scene layout and geometry, delivering compact, high-quality, artist-ready meshes and outperforming prior SDF-based pipelines that require post-hoc optimization.

title: MASFactory: A Graph-centric Framework for Orchestrating LLM-Based Multi-Agent Systems with Vibe Graphing
comment: Submitted to ACL 2026 Demo Track. 10 pages, 6 figures. Code and documentation are available at: https://github.com/BUPT-GAMMA/MASFactory
summary: Large language model-based (LLM-based) multi-agent systems (MAS) are increasingly used to extend agentic problem solving via role specialization and collaboration. MAS workflows can be naturally modeled as directed computation graphs, where nodes execute agents/sub-workflows and edges encode dependencies and message passing. However, implementing complex graph workflows in current frameworks still requires substantial manual effort, offers limited reuse, and makes it difficult to integrate heterogeneous external context sources. To overcome these limitations, we present MASFactory, a graph-centric framework for orchestrating LLM-based MAS. It introduces Vibe Graphing, a human-in-the-loop approach that compiles natural-language intent into an editable workflow specification and then into an executable graph. In addition, the framework provides reusable components and pluggable context integration, as well as a visualizer for topology preview, runtime tracing, and human-in-the-loop interaction. We evaluate MASFactory on seven public benchmarks, validating both reproduction consistency for representative MAS methods and the effectiveness of Vibe Graphing. Our code (https://github.com/BUPT-GAMMA/MASFactory) and video (https://youtu.be/ANynzVfY32k) are publicly available.

AI.tldr: MASFactory is a graph-centric framework for orchestrating LLM-based multi-agent systems. It introduces “Vibe Graphing” to compile natural-language intents into editable workflow specs and executable computation graphs, with reusable components, pluggable context sources, and visualization/tracing. Evaluated on seven benchmarks, it shows consistent reproduction of representative MAS methods and effective human-in-the-loop workflow design.
AI.motivation: Implementing complex MAS workflows today is labor-intensive, hard to reuse, and difficult to connect with heterogeneous external context sources. A principled, graph-based orchestration with human-in-the-loop compilation from natural language could lower friction, improve reuse, and ease integration/observability.
AI.method: Model MAS as directed computation graphs where nodes are agents/sub-workflows and edges are dependencies/message passing. Introduce Vibe Graphing: a human-in-the-loop pipeline that compiles natural-language intent → editable workflow specification → executable graph. Provide reusable components, pluggable context integration, and a visualizer for topology preview, runtime tracing, and interactive oversight.
AI.result: On seven public benchmarks, MASFactory reproduces representative MAS methods consistently and demonstrates the effectiveness of Vibe Graphing for building/editing workflows. Code and demo video are released.
AI.conclusion: A graph-centric, human-in-the-loop approach simplifies MAS construction and orchestration, enhances reuse and integration with external context, and improves transparency/traceability. MASFactory operationalizes this approach and is validated empirically with public artifacts for reproducibility.

title: BlackMirror: Black-Box Backdoor Detection for Text-to-Image Models via Instruction-Response Deviation
comment: This paper is accepted by CVPR 2026
summary: This paper investigates the challenging task of detecting backdoored text-to-image models under black-box settings and introduces a novel detection framework BlackMirror. Existing approaches typically rely on analyzing image-level similarity, under the assumption that backdoor-triggered generations exhibit strong consistency across samples. However, they struggle to generalize to recently emerging backdoor attacks, where backdoored generations can appear visually diverse. BlackMirror is motivated by an observation: across backdoor attacks, {only partial semantic patterns within the generated image are steadily manipulated, while the rest of the content remains diverse or benign. Accordingly, BlackMirror consists of two components: MirrorMatch, which aligns visual patterns with the corresponding instructions to detect semantic deviations; and MirrorVerify, which evaluates the stability of these deviations across varied prompts to distinguish true backdoor behavior from benign responses. BlackMirror is a general, training-free framework that can be deployed as a plug-and-play module in Model-as-a-Service (MaaS) applications. Comprehensive experiments demonstrate that BlackMirror achieves accurate detection across a wide range of attacks. Code is available at https://github.com/Ferry-Li/BlackMirror.

AI.tldr: BlackMirror is a training‑free, black‑box framework to detect backdoored text‑to‑image models by finding stable, partial semantic deviations in generated images via instruction–image alignment and cross‑prompt consistency checks, achieving accurate detection across diverse attacks.
AI.motivation: Existing detectors assume backdoor generations are globally similar across samples, which breaks under modern attacks that yield visually diverse outputs. There is a need for a black‑box, general, deployable method that can flag backdoors even when only parts of an image are manipulated.
AI.method: Key observation: backdoors consistently manipulate only certain semantic patterns while other content remains benign/diverse. BlackMirror has two components: (1) MirrorMatch aligns visual patterns with the input instruction to surface semantic deviations (mismatched or extra patterns suggest potential triggers); (2) MirrorVerify tests whether these deviations persist across varied prompts to separate true, stable backdoor behavior from benign variability. The framework is training‑free and plug‑and‑play for MaaS settings.
AI.result: Comprehensive experiments show accurate detection across a wide range of backdoor attacks in black‑box settings, outperforming similarity‑based baselines that fail on diverse outputs. Code is released.
AI.conclusion: Leveraging partial, stable semantic manipulation as a signal enables reliable, training‑free black‑box detection of backdoored text‑to‑image models. BlackMirror is practical for MaaS deployment and robust to diverse attack forms.

title: InnoAds-Composer: Efficient Condition Composition for E-Commerce Poster Generation
summary: E-commerce product poster generation aims to automatically synthesize a single image that effectively conveys product information by presenting a subject, text, and a designed style. Recent diffusion models with fine-grained and efficient controllability have advanced product poster synthesis, yet they typically rely on multi-stage pipelines, and simultaneous control over subject, text, and style remains underexplored. Such naive multi-stage pipelines also show three issues: poor subject fidelity, inaccurate text, and inconsistent style. To address these issues, we propose InnoAds-Composer, a single-stage framework that enables efficient tri-conditional control tokens over subject, glyph, and style. To alleviate the quadratic overhead introduced by naive tri-conditional token concatenation, we perform importance analysis over layers and timesteps and route each condition only to the most responsive positions, thereby shortening the active token sequence. Besides, to improve the accuracy of Chinese text rendering, we design a Text Feature Enhancement Module (TFEM) that integrates features from both glyph images and glyph crops. To support training and evaluation, we also construct a high-quality e-commerce product poster dataset and benchmark, which is the first dataset that jointly contains subject, text, and style conditions. Extensive experiments demonstrate that InnoAds-Composer significantly outperforms existing product poster methods without obviously increasing inference latency.

AI.tldr: InnoAds-Composer is a single-stage diffusion framework that jointly controls subject, text (glyph), and style via tri-conditional tokens with importance-routed conditioning, plus a text feature enhancement module for Chinese; it delivers higher subject fidelity, text accuracy, and style consistency than prior multi-stage methods without adding noticeable latency, and introduces the first dataset covering all three conditions.
AI.motivation: Existing product poster generation relies on multi-stage diffusion pipelines that cannot reliably and efficiently control subject, text, and style at once—leading to subject drift, inaccurate (especially Chinese) text rendering, and style inconsistencies. A unified, efficient, and accurate solution is needed.
AI.method: Introduce tri-conditional control tokens (subject, glyph/text, style) within a single-stage diffusion model. To avoid quadratic complexity from naive token concatenation, perform importance analysis across layers and timesteps and route each condition only to the most responsive positions, shortening the active token sequence. Add a Text Feature Enhancement Module (TFEM) that fuses features from glyph images and glyph crops to improve Chinese text rendering. Provide a new dataset/benchmark that includes all three conditions for training and evaluation.
AI.result: Across extensive experiments, the method outperforms prior product-poster approaches on subject fidelity, text accuracy, and style consistency while keeping inference latency roughly unchanged.
AI.conclusion: Efficient importance-routed tri-conditional control, combined with TFEM, enables accurate and stylistically consistent single-shot poster synthesis. The accompanying dataset supports training and standardized evaluation, positioning this approach as a strong baseline for controllable poster generation.

title: ViewFusion: Structured Spatial Thinking Chains for Multi-View Reasoning
summary: Multi-view spatial reasoning remains difficult for current vision-language models. Even when multiple viewpoints are available, models often underutilize cross-view relations and instead rely on single-image shortcuts, leading to fragile performance on viewpoint transformation and occlusion-sensitive cases. We present ViewFusion, a two-stage framework that explicitly separates cross-view spatial pre-alignment from question answering. In the first stage, the model performs deliberate spatial pre-thinking to infer viewpoint relations and spatial transformations across views, forming an intermediate workspace that goes beyond a simple re-description. In the second stage, the model conducts question-driven reasoning conditioned on this workspace to produce the final prediction. We train ViewFusion with synthetic reasoning supervision followed by reinforcement learning using GRPO, which improves answer correctness while stabilizing the intended two-stage generation behavior. On MMSI-Bench, ViewFusion improves accuracy by 5.3\% over Qwen3-VL-4B-Instruct, with the largest gains on examples that require genuine cross-view alignment.

AI.tldr: ViewFusion is a two-stage VLM framework that first performs explicit cross-view spatial pre-alignment to build an intermediate workspace, then answers questions conditioned on it; trained with synthetic reasoning supervision and GRPO RL, it improves MMSI-Bench accuracy by 5.3% over Qwen3-VL-4B-Instruct, with the largest gains on alignment-intensive cases.
AI.motivation: Current vision-language models underuse cross-view relationships and lean on single-image shortcuts, causing brittleness under viewpoint changes and occlusions. The paper seeks a mechanism for deliberate multi-view spatial reasoning that avoids such shortcuts.
AI.method: A two-stage pipeline: (1) spatial pre-thinking to infer inter-view relations and transformations and construct an intermediate workspace beyond simple re-description; (2) question-driven reasoning conditioned on that workspace. Training uses synthetic reasoning supervision followed by GRPO-based reinforcement learning to boost correctness and enforce stable two-stage generation behavior.
AI.result: On MMSI-Bench, ViewFusion outperforms Qwen3-VL-4B-Instruct by 5.3% accuracy, with the largest improvements on problems requiring genuine cross-view alignment; RL further improves correctness and stabilizes the intended two-stage process.
AI.conclusion: Explicitly decoupling cross-view spatial alignment from downstream QA, combined with synthetic supervision and RL, enhances multi-view spatial reasoning and robustness to viewpoint changes and occlusions.

title: RAC: Rectified Flow Auto Coder
comment: 11 Figures, 4 Tables. Project Page at https://world-snapshot.github.io/RAC/
summary: In this paper, we propose a Rectified Flow Auto Coder (RAC) inspired by Rectified Flow to replace the traditional VAE: 1. It achieves multi-step decoding by applying the decoder to flow timesteps. Its decoding path is straight and correctable, enabling step-by-step refinement. 2. The model inherently supports bidirectional inference, where the decoder serves as the encoder through time reversal (hence Coder rather than encoder or decoder), reducing parameter count by nearly 41%. 3. This generative decoding method improves generation quality since the model can correct latent variables along the path, partially addressing the reconstruction--generation gap. Experiments show that RAC surpasses SOTA VAEs in both reconstruction and generation with approximately 70% lower computational cost.

AI.tldr: RAC is a rectified-flow-based auto-coder that replaces VAEs with a single, time-reversible “coder,” enabling multi-step, correctable decoding and bidirectional inference, improving both reconstruction and generation while using ~41% fewer parameters and ~70% less compute than SOTA VAEs.
AI.motivation: Address the reconstruction–generation gap and computational inefficiency of traditional VAEs (separate encoder/decoder, one-shot decoding), by leveraging rectified flow’s straight, correctable paths to enable iterative refinement and weight sharing for parameter efficiency.
AI.method: Introduce Rectified Flow Auto Coder (RAC): apply a shared coder across flow timesteps for multi-step decoding along a straightened trajectory; use time reversal so the same network acts as encoder and decoder (bidirectional inference); perform generative decoding that corrects latent variables along the path to narrow the reconstruction–generation gap.
AI.result: Empirically, RAC outperforms SOTA VAEs on both reconstruction and generation quality while cutting computational cost by ~70% and reducing parameter count by ~41% (datasets/metrics not specified in the abstract).
AI.conclusion: RAC offers an efficient, higher-quality alternative to VAEs by coupling rectified-flow dynamics with a single bidirectional coder, enabling stepwise, correctable inference that partially bridges the reconstruction–generation gap and lowers compute/parameter budgets.

title: Mitigating Bias in Concept Bottleneck Models for Fair and Interpretable Image Classification
summary: Ensuring fairness in image classification prevents models from perpetuating and amplifying bias. Concept bottleneck models (CBMs) map images to high-level, human-interpretable concepts before making predictions via a sparse, one-layer classifier. This structure enhances interpretability and, in theory, supports fairness by masking sensitive attribute proxies such as facial features. However, CBM concepts have been known to leak information unrelated to concept semantics and early results reveal only marginal reductions in gender bias on datasets like ImSitu. We propose three bias mitigation techniques to improve fairness in CBMs: 1. Decreasing information leakage using a top-k concept filter, 2. Removing biased concepts, and 3. Adversarial debiasing. Our results outperform prior work in terms of fairness-performance tradeoffs, indicating that our debiased CBM provides a significant step towards fair and interpretable image classification.

AI.tldr: They improve fairness in concept bottleneck models (CBMs) for image classification by limiting concept leakage (top‑k filtering), removing biased concepts, and applying adversarial debiasing, yielding better fairness–accuracy tradeoffs than prior work.
AI.motivation: CBMs are attractive for interpretability and could support fairness by operating on human concepts instead of raw features. However, concepts can leak sensitive proxy information and past CBM results show only small bias reduction (e.g., gender bias in ImSitu), motivating targeted debiasing of the concept space and predictor.
AI.method: Three techniques applied within the CBM pipeline: (1) top‑k concept filtering to reduce information leakage; (2) identification and removal of biased concepts from the concept set; (3) adversarial debiasing to discourage sensitive attribute information in concept representations or downstream predictor.
AI.result: Across evaluations (e.g., ImSitu), the debiased CBM achieves superior fairness–performance tradeoffs compared to prior approaches, with larger reductions in gender bias while maintaining competitive accuracy.
AI.conclusion: Combining concept filtering, concept pruning, and adversarial debiasing makes CBMs substantially fairer without sacrificing interpretability, representing a practical step toward fair, transparent image classification.

title: Evaluating Austrian A-Level German Essays with Large Language Models for Automated Essay Scoring
comment: To be presented at the SAC2026 and published in its symposium proceedings
summary: Automated Essay Scoring (AES) has been explored for decades with the goal to support teachers by reducing grading workload and mitigating subjective biases. While early systems relied on handcrafted features and statistical models, recent advances in Large Language Models (LLMs) have made it possible to evaluate student writing with unprecedented flexibility. This paper investigates the application of state-of-the-art open-weight LLMs for the grading of Austrian A-level German texts, with a particular focus on rubric-based evaluation. A dataset of 101 anonymised student exams across three text types was processed and evaluated. Four LLMs, DeepSeek-R1 32b, Qwen3 30b, Mixtral 8x7b and LLama3.3 70b, were evaluated with different contexts and prompting strategies. The LLMs were able to reach a maximum of 40.6% agreement with the human rater in the rubric-provided sub-dimensions, and only 32.8% of final grades matched the ones given by a human expert. The results indicate that even though smaller models are able to use standardised rubrics for German essay grading, they are not accurate enough to be used in a real-world grading environment.

AI.tldr: Open-weight LLMs can follow a standardized German A-level essay rubric but achieve only ~41% sub-dimension agreement and ~33% exact grade match with a human rater, so they are not yet suitable for high-stakes grading.
AI.motivation: Reduce teacher workload and subjective bias in essay grading by testing whether recent, flexible open-weight LLMs can reliably perform rubric-based assessment for German A-level exams.
AI.method: Use 101 anonymized Austrian A-level German exams across three text types; evaluate four open-weight LLMs (DeepSeek-R1 32B, Qwen3 30B, Mixtral 8x7B, LLaMA 3.3 70B) with varied context and prompting strategies; compare rubric sub-dimension ratings and final grades to a human rater.
AI.result: Best observed agreement: 40.6% on rubric-provided sub-dimensions; only 32.8% of final grades exactly matched the human expert’s grade.
AI.conclusion: Smaller open LLMs can operationalize rubrics but lack the accuracy needed for real-world deployment; improved reliability, calibration, fine-tuning, and likely human-in-the-loop use are required.

title: CollabOD: Collaborative Multi-Backbone with Cross-scale Vision for UAV Small Object Detection
summary: Small object detection in unmanned aerial vehicle (UAV) imagery is challenging, mainly due to scale variation, structural detail degradation, and limited computational resources. In high-altitude scenarios, fine-grained features are further weakened during hierarchical downsampling and cross-scale fusion, resulting in unstable localization and reduced robustness. To address this issue, we propose CollabOD, a lightweight collaborative detection framework that explicitly preserves structural details and aligns heterogeneous feature streams before multi-scale fusion. The framework integrates Structural Detail Preservation, Cross-Path Feature Alignment, and Localization-Aware Lightweight Design strategies. From the perspectives of image processing, channel structure, and lightweight design, it optimizes the architecture of conventional UAV perception models. The proposed design enhances representation stability while maintaining efficient inference. A unified detail-aware detection head further improves regression robustness without introducing additional deployment overhead. The code is available at: https://github.com/Bai-Xuecheng/CollabOD.

AI.tldr: CollabOD is a lightweight UAV small-object detector that preserves structural details and aligns heterogeneous feature streams before multi-scale fusion, using a detail-aware head to stabilize localization while keeping inference efficient.
AI.motivation: Small objects in UAV imagery suffer from scale variation, detail loss through downsampling and cross-scale fusion, and tight compute budgets—especially at high altitude—leading to unstable localization and reduced robustness.
AI.method: A collaborative framework combining: (1) Structural Detail Preservation to explicitly retain fine-grained information; (2) Cross-Path Feature Alignment to synchronize heterogeneous feature streams prior to multi-scale fusion; (3) a Localization-Aware Lightweight Design for efficient, robust regression. A unified, detail-aware detection head improves bounding-box regression without extra deployment overhead.
AI.result: Improved representation stability and localization robustness for small objects in UAV imagery, with efficient inference maintained. Code released at the provided GitHub link.
AI.conclusion: CollabOD refines conventional UAV perception architectures across image processing, channel structure, and lightweight design, delivering robust small-object detection suitable for resource-constrained deployment.

title: Experiences Build Characters: The Linguistic Origins and Functional Impact of LLM Personality
summary: Human problem-solving is enriched by a diversity of styles and personality traits, yet the development of Large Language Models (LLMs) has largely prioritized uniform performance benchmarks that favour specific behavioural tendencies such as assertiveness. To investigate how diverse experiences shape machine personality and influence problem-solving, this study employs continued pre-training to expose models to domain-specific texts in an unsupervised manner, simulating the accumulation of experience. By adapting the Big Five framework via the Machine Personality Inventory (MPI), we quantify the personality traits of these model variants and analyse their relationship to linguistic style and reasoning behaviour. The findings reveal that model competence is bimodal, peaking at "Expressive Generalists" and "Suppressed Specialists," while identifying a "Suppression Advantage" where reduced social traits enhance complex reasoning performance. This study further establishes a causal link between training data linguistics, such as imperative frequency, and lexical diversity, providing a roadmap for "Personality Engineering".

AI.tldr: Continued pre-training on domain-specific corpora shifts LLM “personality” (measured via an adapted Big Five MPI) and yields two competence peaks—Expressive Generalists and Suppressed Specialists—with evidence that lower social expressiveness can improve complex reasoning. Linguistic properties of training data (e.g., imperative frequency, lexical diversity) causally influence these traits and behaviors, enabling targeted “Personality Engineering.”
AI.motivation: LLM development emphasizes uniform benchmarks and assertive styles, overlooking the benefits of diverse human problem-solving styles. The authors aim to understand how experiential diversity (via data exposure) shapes machine personality and its link to reasoning and linguistic behavior.
AI.method: Use continued pre-training on domain-specific, unlabeled text to simulate experiential accumulation; quantify emergent traits with the Machine Personality Inventory (MPI) adapted from Big Five; analyze correlations between traits, linguistic style, and reasoning; probe causal effects of training-data linguistics (imperative frequency, lexical diversity) on personality/competence.
AI.result: Observed bimodal model competence with two clusters—“Expressive Generalists” (broad, expressive) and “Suppressed Specialists” (lower social traits, strong complex reasoning). Found a “Suppression Advantage,” where reduced social traits enhance complex reasoning performance. Demonstrated causal links between training-data stylistics (imperatives, lexical diversity) and resulting model personality/behavior.
AI.conclusion: Training-data linguistics can be engineered to tune LLM personalities and reasoning profiles. Designing corpora with specific stylistic features offers a practical path to “Personality Engineering,” and selectively dampening social traits may benefit complex reasoning tasks, though broader implications and trade-offs need consideration.

title: Facial Expression Recognition Using Residual Masking Network
summary: Automatic facial expression recognition (FER) has gained much attention due to its applications in human-computer interaction. Among the approaches to improve FER tasks, this paper focuses on deep architecture with the attention mechanism. We propose a novel Masking idea to boost the performance of CNN in facial expression task. It uses a segmentation network to refine feature maps, enabling the network to focus on relevant information to make correct decisions. In experiments, we combine the ubiquitous Deep Residual Network and Unet-like architecture to produce a Residual Masking Network. The proposed method holds state-of-the-art (SOTA) accuracy on the well-known FER2013 and private VEMO datasets. The source code is available at https://github.com/phamquiluan/ResidualMaskingNetwork.

AI.tldr: Residual Masking Network (ResMaskNet) enhances CNN-based facial expression recognition by learning mask-like attention maps via a U-Net-style segmentation branch fused with a ResNet backbone, yielding SOTA results on FER2013 and a private VEMO dataset.
AI.motivation: Facial expression recognition is challenged by cluttered backgrounds, occlusions, and intra-class variability. Attention mechanisms can help CNNs emphasize salient facial regions; the paper seeks a principled way to guide networks to focus on expression-relevant features to improve accuracy.
AI.method: Introduce a Masking mechanism: a segmentation-like network generates refinement masks that modulate intermediate feature maps of a deep residual CNN (ResNet). The overall architecture combines ResNet with a U-Net-style encoder–decoder to produce attention masks that focus the classifier on informative facial regions. End-to-end training is used to jointly learn masks and classifier.
AI.result: The proposed Residual Masking Network achieves state-of-the-art accuracy on FER2013 and on the authors’ private VEMO dataset. Code is publicly released.
AI.conclusion: Learning mask-based attention via a segmentation branch effectively refines CNN features for FER, improving performance over standard backbones and delivering SOTA results; the approach is practical and reproducible with the released code.

title: Beyond Geometry: Artistic Disparity Synthesis for Immersive 2D-to-3D
summary: Current 2D-to-3D conversion methods achieve geometric accuracy but are artistically deficient, failing to replicate the immersive and emotionally resonant experience of professional 3D cinema. This is because geometric reconstruction paradigms mistake deliberate artistic intent, such as strategic zero-plane shifts for pop-out effects and local depth sculpting, for data noise or ambiguity. This paper argues for a new paradigm: Artistic Disparity Synthesis, shifting the goal from physically accurate disparity estimation to artistically coherent disparity synthesis. We propose Art3D, a preliminary framework exploring this paradigm. Art3D uses a dual-path architecture to decouple global depth parameters (macro-intent) from local artistic effects (visual brushstrokes) and learns from professional 3D film data via indirect supervision. We also introduce a preliminary evaluation method to quantify cinematic alignment. Experiments show our approach demonstrates potential in replicating key local out-of-screen effects and aligning with the global depth styles of cinematic 3D content, laying the groundwork for a new class of artistically-driven conversion tools.

AI.tldr: Proposes a shift from physically accurate depth estimation to artistically coherent disparity synthesis for 2D-to-3D conversion. Introduces Art3D, a dual-path model that separates global cinematic depth style from local pop-out effects, trained with indirect supervision from professional 3D films, plus a preliminary metric for cinematic alignment. Early experiments indicate improved replication of cinematic depth style and out-of-screen effects.
AI.motivation: Conventional 2D→3D methods prioritize geometric correctness and treat deliberate artistic stereo choices (e.g., zero‑plane shifts and local depth sculpting) as noise, yielding conversions that lack the immersive and emotional qualities of professional 3D cinema. The goal is to capture artistic intent rather than only physical depth.
AI.method: Define a new paradigm—Artistic Disparity Synthesis—aiming to synthesize disparities that are artistically coherent. Propose Art3D, a dual-path architecture that decouples global depth parameters (macro-intent, such as overall depth budget/zero-plane placement) from local artistic manipulations (micro-intent, e.g., pop-out brushstrokes). Train using indirect supervision from professional 3D film data. Introduce a preliminary evaluation protocol to quantify alignment with cinematic depth style.
AI.result: Experiments suggest the model can reproduce key localized out-of-screen effects and better match global cinematic depth styles relative to geometry-first approaches, evidencing the feasibility of artistically driven conversion.
AI.conclusion: Art3D provides an initial framework for artistically guided 2D→3D conversion and supports a paradigm shift toward disparity synthesis aligned with cinematic intent, laying a foundation for future tools and more robust evaluation of artistic stereo quality.

title: Making Implicit Premises Explicit in Logical Understanding of Enthymemes
summary: Real-world arguments in text and dialogues are normally enthymemes (i.e. some of their premises and/or claims are implicit). Natural language processing (NLP) methods for handling enthymemes can potentially identify enthymemes in text but they do not decode their underlying logic, whereas logic-based approaches for handling them assume a knowledgebase with sufficient formulae that can be used to decode them via abduction. There is therefore a lack of a systematic method for translating textual components of an enthymeme into a logical argument and generating the logical formulae required for their decoding, and thereby showing logical entailment. To address this, we propose a pipeline that integrates: (1) a large language model (LLM) to generate intermediate implicit premises based on the explicit premise and claim; (2) another LLM to translate the natural language into logical formulas; and (3) a neuro-symbolic reasoner based on a SAT solver to determine entailment. We evaluate our pipeline on two enthymeme datasets, demonstrating promising performance in selecting the correct implicit premise, as measured by precision, recall, F1-score, and accuracy.

AI.tldr: They introduce a three-stage pipeline that fills in missing premises in enthymemes: one LLM invents implicit premises, another LLM converts text to logic, and a SAT-based neuro-symbolic reasoner checks entailment; tested on two datasets with promising P/R/F1/accuracy for selecting the correct implicit premise.
AI.motivation: NLP systems can flag enthymemes but typically cannot reconstruct the hidden logical steps, while logic-based approaches require a rich, often unavailable knowledge base. There is no systematic method to translate textual enthymemes into logical arguments and generate the missing formulae to demonstrate entailment.
AI.method: A pipeline with: (1) an LLM that proposes intermediate implicit premises from explicit premise and claim; (2) a separate LLM that translates natural language statements into logical formulae; (3) a neuro-symbolic SAT-solver-based reasoner to test whether the explicit plus generated premises entail the claim.
AI.result: On two enthymeme datasets, the pipeline achieves promising precision, recall, F1, and accuracy in selecting the correct implicit premise (no exact numbers provided in the abstract).
AI.conclusion: Combining LLMs for premise generation and text-to-logic translation with a SAT-based reasoner offers a practical path to decoding enthymemes and establishing entailment, narrowing the gap between data-driven NLP and formal logical reasoning.

title: Energy-Driven Adaptive Visual Token Pruning for Efficient Vision-Language Models
summary: Visual token reduction is critical for accelerating Vision-Language Models (VLMs), yet most existing approaches rely on a fixed budget shared across all inputs, overlooking the substantial variation in image information density. We propose E-AdaPrune, an energy-driven adaptive pruning framework that determines the token budget from the singular value spectrum of the visual features space. By preserving a certain proportion of spectral energy, our method allocates more tokens to information-dense scenes while aggressively compressing redundant ones, without introducing additional learnable parameters. We evaluate E-AdaPrune on nine benchmarks and three VLM backbones, LLaVA-1.5-7B, LLaVA-1.5-13B, and LLaVA-NeXT-8B. Under matched average token budgets, E-AdaPrune consistently yields an average improvement of up to 0.6\%, including a significant +5.1\% relative boost on the MMVet reasoning task. Using randomized singular value decomposition, the additional latency is limited to 8ms per image.

AI.tldr: E-AdaPrune adaptively prunes visual tokens per image by preserving a target proportion of spectral energy (from the singular value spectrum of visual features), delivering small but consistent accuracy gains over fixed-budget pruning at similar average token counts, with ~8 ms/image overhead and no extra learnable parameters.
AI.motivation: Fixed, one-size-fits-all visual token budgets ignore large variation in image information density, causing either wasted compute on simple scenes or accuracy loss on complex ones. The goal is to accelerate VLMs while maintaining or improving accuracy by allocating tokens adaptively per input without retraining.
AI.method: Compute the singular value spectrum of the visual feature space for each image; choose a token budget that preserves a specified proportion of cumulative spectral energy. This retains more tokens for information-dense scenes and prunes aggressively for redundant ones. Implemented with randomized SVD to bound latency; plug-and-play, training-free, and parameter-free. Evaluated on LLaVA-1.5-7B/13B and LLaVA-NeXT-8B across nine benchmarks.
AI.result: Under matched average token budgets, E-AdaPrune achieves average accuracy improvements up to 0.6% and a +5.1% relative gain on MMVet, with ~8 ms additional latency per image.
AI.conclusion: Energy-driven, per-image adaptive token pruning improves the efficiency–accuracy trade-off of VLMs with minimal overhead and no added parameters, suggesting a simple, general mechanism to tailor visual token counts to input complexity.

title: Pano3DComposer: Feed-Forward Compositional 3D Scene Generation from Single Panoramic Image
summary: Current compositional image-to-3D scene generation approaches construct 3D scenes by time-consuming iterative layout optimization or inflexible joint object-layout generation. Moreover, most methods rely on limited field-of-view perspective images, hindering the creation of complete 360-degree environments. To address these limitations, we design Pano3DComposer, an efficient feed-forward framework for panoramic images. To decouple object generation from layout estimation, we propose a plug-and-play Object-World Transformation Predictor. This module converts the 3D objects generated by off-the-shelf image-to-3D models from local to world coordinates. To achieve this, we adapt the VGGT architecture to Alignment-VGGT by using target object crop, multi-view object renderings and camera parameters to predict the transformation. The predictor is trained using pseudo-geometric supervision to address the shape discrepancy between generated and ground-truth objects. For input images from unseen domains, we further introduce a Coarse-to-Fine (C2F) alignment mechanism for Pano3DComposer that iteratively refines geometric consistency with feedback of scene rendering. Our method achieves superior geometric accuracy for image/text-to-3D tasks on synthetic and real-world datasets. It can generate a high-fidelity 3D scene in approximately 20 seconds on an RTX 4090 GPU. Project page: https://qiuzidian.github.io/pano3dcomposer-page/.

AI.tldr: Pano3DComposer is a fast feed-forward system that builds full 360° 3D scenes from panoramic inputs by decoupling object generation from layout and learning to transform per-object 3D assets into world coordinates via an Alignment-VGGT predictor, further refined with a coarse-to-fine alignment loop; it achieves superior geometric accuracy and ~20s runtime on an RTX 4090.
AI.motivation: Prior compositional image-to-3D methods are either slow due to iterative layout optimization or inflexible via joint object–layout generation, and most rely on narrow-FOV perspective images that fail to capture complete 360° environments.
AI.method: 1) Use off-the-shelf image-to-3D models to generate per-object 3D assets. 2) Predict each object’s local-to-world transformation with a plug-and-play Object-World Transformation Predictor based on an adapted VGGT (Alignment-VGGT) that ingests the object crop, multi-view renders, and camera parameters. 3) Train with pseudo-geometric supervision to mitigate shape discrepancies between generated and ground-truth objects. 4) Apply a Coarse-to-Fine alignment mechanism that iteratively refines geometry using rendered-scene feedback, improving robustness on unseen domains.
AI.result: Demonstrates superior geometric accuracy for both image- and text-to-3D tasks on synthetic and real datasets; can assemble high-fidelity 360° scenes in about 20 seconds on an RTX 4090 GPU.
AI.conclusion: Decoupling object synthesis from layout and predicting object-to-world transforms with Alignment-VGGT, plus C2F refinement, enables efficient, accurate panoramic 3D scene generation, overcoming FOV limitations and heavy optimization typical of prior work.

title: Diffusion Language Models Are Natively Length-Aware
summary: Unlike autoregressive language models, which terminate variable-length generation upon predicting an End-of-Sequence (EoS) token, Diffusion Language Models (DLMs) operate over a fixed maximum-length context window for a predetermined number of denoising steps. However, this process is independent of the required response length, resulting in computational waste for the majority of short responses common in reasoning and chat tasks. To address this problem, we conjecture that the latent prompt representation contains sufficient information to estimate the required output length. We provide empirical evidence for this phenomenon and propose a zero-shot mechanism to dynamically crop the context window before generation begins, leading to fewer diffusion steps and substantial computational savings. We evaluate our approach on four benchmarks with diverse tasks -- GSM8K (reasoning), HumanEval (code generation), IfEval (instruction following), and LongFormQA (question answering) -- revealing massive efficiency gains at minimal performance impact. We report significant reductions in FLOPs across all tasks, with no statistically significant performance degradation, and significant performance improvements in 2 out of 4 tasks.

AI.tldr: Predict output length from the prompt’s latent, crop the DLM’s context window before denoising, and cut compute dramatically with little to no quality loss (sometimes gains).
AI.motivation: Diffusion language models run a fixed number of denoising steps over a fixed-length window, wasting computation when the desired response is short—unlike autoregressive models that stop at EoS.
AI.method: Show that the prompt’s latent encodes the target output length and use this to zero-shot predict length and dynamically crop the context window before generation, thereby reducing the number of diffusion steps without additional training.
AI.result: Across GSM8K, HumanEval, IfEval, and LongFormQA, the method yields large FLOP reductions; no statistically significant performance degradation; significant gains on 2 of 4 benchmarks.
AI.conclusion: Adaptive, zero-shot context-window cropping guided by latent length estimates makes DLMs substantially more efficient while preserving or improving task performance, suggesting a practical route to length-aware diffusion generation.

title: A Causal Graph Approach to Oppositional Narrative Analysis
summary: Current methods for textual analysis rely on data annotated within predefined ontologies, often embedding human bias within black-box models. Despite achieving near-perfect performance, these approaches exploit unstructured, linear pattern recognition rather than modeling the structured interactions between entities that naturally emerge in discourse. In this work, we propose a graph-based framework for the detection, analysis, and classification of oppositional narratives and their underlying entities by representing narratives as entity-interaction graphs. Moreover, by incorporating causal estimation at the node level, our approach derives a causal representation of each contribution to the final classification by distilling the constructed sentence graph into a minimal causal subgraph. Building upon this representation, we introduce a classification pipeline that outperforms existing approaches to oppositional thinking classification task.

AI.tldr: Model narratives as entity-interaction graphs and use node-level causal estimation to extract minimal causal subgraphs, yielding interpretable, state-of-the-art classification of oppositional narratives.
AI.motivation: Prevailing text-classification methods depend on ontology-annotated data and black-box models that may encode annotator bias and focus on surface patterns rather than structured interactions among discourse entities. The goal is to capture the relational structure of narratives and provide causal, interpretable attributions for decisions.
AI.method: Construct entity-interaction (sentence) graphs from narratives; estimate node-level causal contributions; distill each graph into a minimal causal subgraph that explains the classification; feed this causal representation into a classification pipeline.
AI.result: The proposed pipeline surpasses prior methods on an oppositional thinking classification task and provides per-entity, per-interaction causal attributions for the final label.
AI.conclusion: A graph-based, causally grounded representation of narratives improves both predictive performance and interpretability over ontology-based black-box baselines, and it could generalize to broader discourse analysis tasks.

title: Skeleton-to-Image Encoding: Enabling Skeleton Representation Learning via Vision-Pretrained Models
comment: Submitted to IEEE TPAMI, under review
summary: Recent advances in large-scale pretrained vision models have demonstrated impressive capabilities across a wide range of downstream tasks, including cross-modal and multi-modal scenarios. However, their direct application to 3D human skeleton data remains challenging due to fundamental differences in data format. Moreover, the scarcity of large-scale skeleton datasets and the need to incorporate skeleton data into multi-modal action recognition without introducing additional model branches present significant research opportunities. To address these challenges, we introduce Skeleton-to-Image Encoding (S2I), a novel representation that transforms skeleton sequences into image-like data by partitioning and arranging joints based on body-part semantics and resizing to standardized image dimensions. This encoding enables, for the first time, the use of powerful vision-pretrained models for self-supervised skeleton representation learning, effectively transferring rich visual-domain knowledge to skeleton analysis. While existing skeleton methods often design models tailored to specific, homogeneous skeleton formats, they overlook the structural heterogeneity that naturally arises from diverse data sources. In contrast, our S2I representation offers a unified image-like format that naturally accommodates heterogeneous skeleton data. Extensive experiments on NTU-60, NTU-120, and PKU-MMD demonstrate the effectiveness and generalizability of our method for self-supervised skeleton representation learning, including under challenging cross-format evaluation settings.

AI.tldr: They convert 3D skeleton sequences into standardized images (S2I) so off‑the‑shelf vision-pretrained models can learn skeleton representations self-supervised, showing strong, generalizable results across NTU-60/120 and PKU-MMD, even when skeleton formats differ.
AI.motivation: Large vision models excel broadly but are hard to apply to skeleton data due to format mismatch, limited large-scale skeleton datasets, the desire to plug skeletons into multimodal systems without extra branches, and heterogeneity across skeleton sources.
AI.method: Skeleton-to-Image Encoding (S2I): partition joints by body-part semantics, arrange them spatially and temporally into image-like grids, resize to standard image sizes, then use vision-pretrained backbones for self-supervised representation learning. The unified image-like format accommodates heterogeneous skeleton schemas and enables transfer of visual-domain knowledge.
AI.result: On NTU-60, NTU-120, and PKU-MMD, S2I-based self-supervised learning is effective and generalizes well, including in cross-format evaluations. The abstract reports broad gains but no exact numbers.
AI.conclusion: Transforming skeletons into image-like inputs unlocks vision-pretrained models for skeleton analysis, yielding robust, generalizable representations and simplifying multimodal integration without adding task-specific skeleton branches.

title: CRIMSON: A Clinically-Grounded LLM-Based Metric for Generative Radiology Report Evaluation
summary: We introduce CRIMSON, a clinically grounded evaluation framework for chest X-ray report generation that assesses reports based on diagnostic correctness, contextual relevance, and patient safety. Unlike prior metrics, CRIMSON incorporates full clinical context, including patient age, indication, and guideline-based decision rules, and prevents normal or clinically insignificant findings from exerting disproportionate influence on the overall score. The framework categorizes errors into a comprehensive taxonomy covering false findings, missing findings, and eight attribute-level errors (e.g., location, severity, measurement, and diagnostic overinterpretation). Each finding is assigned a clinical significance level (urgent, actionable non-urgent, non-actionable, or expected/benign), based on a guideline developed in collaboration with attending cardiothoracic radiologists, enabling severity-aware weighting that prioritizes clinically consequential mistakes over benign discrepancies. CRIMSON is validated through strong alignment with clinically significant error counts annotated by six board-certified radiologists in ReXVal (Kendalls tau = 0.61-0.71; Pearsons r = 0.71-0.84), and through two additional benchmarks that we introduce. In RadJudge, a targeted suite of clinically challenging pass-fail scenarios, CRIMSON shows consistent agreement with expert judgment. In RadPref, a larger radiologist preference benchmark of over 100 pairwise cases with structured error categorization, severity modeling, and 1-5 overall quality ratings from three cardiothoracic radiologists, CRIMSON achieves the strongest alignment with radiologist preferences. We release the metric, the evaluation benchmarks, RadJudge and RadPref, and a fine-tuned MedGemma model to enable reproducible evaluation of report generation, all available at https://github.com/rajpurkarlab/CRIMSON.

AI.tldr: CRIMSON is a clinically grounded metric and benchmark suite for evaluating chest X‑ray report generation that prioritizes diagnostic correctness and patient safety by using full clinical context, a fine‑grained error taxonomy, and severity‑aware weighting; it aligns strongly with radiologist judgments and is released with datasets and a fine‑tuned model for reproducible evaluation.
AI.motivation: Existing text- or label-based metrics for chest X-ray report generation can reward superficial similarity or overcount benign discrepancies, failing to reflect clinical impact, context (age, indication), and safety. There is a need for an evaluation framework that captures clinically meaningful errors and aligns with expert radiologist judgment.
AI.method: CRIMSON evaluates generated reports using: (1) full clinical context (patient age, indication, guideline-driven decision rules); (2) a comprehensive error taxonomy spanning false positives, misses, and eight attribute-level errors (e.g., location, severity, measurement, diagnostic overinterpretation); and (3) severity-aware weighting based on a radiologist-derived guideline that categorizes findings as urgent, actionable non-urgent, non-actionable, or expected/benign. The authors also build two benchmarks—RadJudge (targeted pass–fail cases) and RadPref (pairwise preference dataset with structured errors and 1–5 ratings)—and release a fine-tuned MedGemma model.
AI.result: On ReXVal with six board-certified radiologists, CRIMSON’s scores correlate strongly with clinically significant error counts (Kendall’s τ = 0.61–0.71; Pearson’s r = 0.71–0.84). It shows consistent agreement with experts on RadJudge and yields the strongest alignment with radiologist preferences on RadPref among compared metrics.
AI.conclusion: CRIMSON provides a clinically faithful, safety-aware evaluation of chest X-ray report generation that better captures meaningful errors than prior metrics and aligns with expert judgment. The released metric, benchmarks (RadJudge, RadPref), and a fine-tuned MedGemma model enable reproducible, clinically grounded assessment for future research and model development.

title: Imagine How To Change: Explicit Procedure Modeling for Change Captioning
comment: Accepted to ICLR 2026. Code and models are available at https://github.com/BlueberryOreo/ProCap
summary: Change captioning generates descriptions that explicitly describe the differences between two visually similar images. Existing methods operate on static image pairs, thus ignoring the rich temporal dynamics of the change procedure, which is the key to understand not only what has changed but also how it occurs. We introduce ProCap, a novel framework that reformulates change modeling from static image comparison to dynamic procedure modeling. ProCap features a two-stage design: The first stage trains a procedure encoder to learn the change procedure from a sparse set of keyframes. These keyframes are obtained by automatically generating intermediate frames to make the implicit procedural dynamics explicit and then sampling them to mitigate redundancy. Then the encoder learns to capture the latent dynamics of these keyframes via a caption-conditioned, masked reconstruction task. The second stage integrates this trained encoder within an encoder-decoder model for captioning. Instead of relying on explicit frames from the previous stage -- a process incurring computational overhead and sensitivity to visual noise -- we introduce learnable procedure queries to prompt the encoder for inferring the latent procedure representation, which the decoder then translates into text. The entire model is then trained end-to-end with a captioning loss, ensuring the encoder's output is both temporally coherent and captioning-aligned. Experiments on three datasets demonstrate the effectiveness of ProCap. Code and pre-trained models are available at https://github.com/BlueberryOreo/ProCap

AI.tldr: ProCap reframes change captioning from static image-pair comparison to dynamic procedure modeling, learning a latent representation of how changes occur and decoding it into text with learnable queries, yielding stronger, temporally coherent captions across datasets.
AI.motivation: Static change-captioning models compare two images and miss the temporal dynamics of the change process, limiting their ability to capture not just what changed but how it happened and making them sensitive to noise and redundancy.
AI.method: Two-stage framework: (1) Procedure encoder pretraining on sparse keyframes derived from automatically generated intermediate frames; learns latent change dynamics via a caption-conditioned masked reconstruction objective. (2) Encoder integrated into an encoder–decoder captioner; learnable procedure queries prompt the encoder to infer the latent procedure without requiring explicit intermediate frames; end-to-end training with a captioning loss aligns temporal dynamics with text generation.
AI.result: On three datasets, ProCap is empirically effective, improving change-captioning performance; the query-based design avoids the computational overhead and noise sensitivity of explicit intermediate frames.
AI.conclusion: Modeling change as a latent procedure and decoding it via learned queries produces temporally coherent, caption-aligned descriptions and advances change captioning; code and models are released.

title: MAPO: Mixed Advantage Policy Optimization for Long-Horizon Multi-Turn Dialogue
summary: Subjective multi-turn dialogue tasks, such as emotional support, require conversational policies that adapt to evolving user states and optimize long-horizon interaction quality. However, reinforcement learning (RL) for such settings remains challenging due to the absence of reliable process supervision. Outcome-only training collapses credit assignment across turns into a single trajectory-level reward, while naïve turn-level group sampling incurs prohibitive rollout costs in interactive environments. We propose a critic-free and efficient RL algorithm named MAPO that leverages dense process feedback from a judge model and propagates long-horizon effects through Monte Carlo returns. To stabilize optimization, we introduce a mixed advantage estimator that combines turn-level normalization with batch-level normalization, enabling fine-grained yet scalable credit assignment. Across multiple subjective dialogue benchmarks, including EMPA, EmoBench, and EQ-Bench, and model scales ranging from 7B to 32B, our method consistently improves both training stability and final performance over outcome-only GRPO and single-level normalization baselines. On EMPA, we improve rates by up to 9 points and increase dialogue scores by as much as +43.2 over the 7B base model. Despite training only on EMPA-style environments, our approach generalizes well, yielding consistent improvements on unseen emotional-intelligence benchmarks, including up to +4 points on EmoBench and +3.5 on EQ-Bench. Together, these results demonstrate that dense process supervision combined with mixed-level normalization enables effective and scalable RL for subjective, open-ended multi-turn dialogue.

AI.tldr: MAPO is a critic-free, efficient RL method for subjective multi-turn dialogue that uses dense judge feedback and mixed-level normalization to assign long-horizon credit, yielding more stable training and better performance across benchmarks and model scales, with cross-benchmark generalization.
AI.motivation: Subjective, open-ended multi-turn tasks (e.g., emotional support) need policies that adapt over turns. Standard RL struggles because there is little reliable process supervision: outcome-only rewards collapse credit across the trajectory, while naïve turn-level rollouts are too costly.
AI.method: Introduce MAPO, a critic-free RL algorithm that: (1) leverages dense per-turn process feedback from a judge model; (2) propagates long-horizon effects via Monte Carlo returns; and (3) stabilizes optimization with a mixed advantage estimator combining turn-level and batch-level normalization for fine-grained yet scalable credit assignment.
AI.result: On EMPA, EmoBench, and EQ-Bench with models from 7B–32B, MAPO improves stability and final performance over outcome-only GRPO and single-level normalization baselines. Notably, on EMPA it yields up to +9 points in rates and +43.2 dialogue score over a 7B base model, and generalizes to unseen benchmarks with up to +4 on EmoBench and +3.5 on EQ-Bench.
AI.conclusion: Dense process supervision paired with mixed-level normalization enables effective, scalable RL for subjective multi-turn dialogue, delivering stable optimization, stronger long-horizon credit assignment, and generalization beyond the training environment.

title: Technical Report: Automated Optical Inspection of Surgical Instruments
summary: In the dynamic landscape of modern healthcare, maintaining the highest standards in surgical instruments is critical for clinical success. This report explores the diverse realm of surgical instruments and their associated manufacturing defects, emphasizing their pivotal role in ensuring the safety of surgical procedures. With potentially fatal consequences arising from even minor defects, precision in manufacturing is paramount.The report addresses the identification and rectification of critical defects such as cracks, rust, and structural irregularities. Such scrutiny prevents substantial financial losses for manufacturers and, more crucially, safeguards patient lives. The collaboration with industry leaders Daddy D Pro and Dr. Frigz International, renowned trailblazers in the Sialkot surgical cluster, provides invaluable insights into the analysis of defects in Pakistani-made instruments. This partnership signifies a commitment to advancing automated defect detection methodologies, specifically through the integration of deep learning architectures including YOLOv8, ResNet-152, and EfficientNet-b4, thereby elevating quality standards in the manufacturing process. The scope of this report is to identify various surgical instruments manufactured in Pakistan and analyze their associated defects using a newly developed dataset of 4,414 high-resolution images. By focusing on quality assurance through Automated Optical Inspection (AOI) tools, this document serves as a resource for manufacturers, healthcare professionals, and regulatory bodies. The insights gained contribute to the enhancement of instrument standards, ensuring a more reliable healthcare environment through industry expertise and cutting-edge technology.

AI.tldr: Report builds an AOI-based, deep-learning pipeline and dataset to detect manufacturing defects in Pakistani-made surgical instruments, aiming to raise quality standards and patient safety.
AI.motivation: Defects (cracks, rust, structural irregularities) in surgical instruments can cause patient harm and financial losses; current QC needs more precise, scalable, and objective automation.
AI.method: Industry-collaborative study (Daddy D Pro, Dr. Frigz International, Sialkot cluster) creating a 4,414-image high-resolution dataset of instruments/defects; evaluating automated optical inspection with deep learning models (YOLOv8 for detection, ResNet-152 and EfficientNet-b4 for classification/feature extraction).
AI.result: As presented, the work assembles a new dataset, analyzes common defect modes, and proposes an AOI pipeline integrating YOLOv8, ResNet-152, and EfficientNet-b4; the abstract does not report quantitative performance metrics.
AI.conclusion: Automated deep-learning AOI, supported by industry partnerships and curated data, can improve defect detection, reduce recalls/costs, and enhance patient safety; the report serves as a resource for manufacturers, clinicians, and regulators to elevate instrument quality.

title: Towards Driver Behavior Understanding: Weakly-Supervised Risk Perception in Driving Scenes
summary: Achieving zero-collision mobility remains a key objective for intelligent vehicle systems, which requires understanding driver risk perception-a complex cognitive process shaped by voluntary response of the driver to external stimuli and the attentiveness of surrounding road users towards the ego-vehicle. To support progress in this area, we introduce RAID (Risk Assessment In Driving scenes)-a large-scale dataset specifically curated for research on driver risk perception and contextual risk assessment. RAID comprises 4,691 annotated video clips, covering diverse traffic scenarios with labels for driver's intended maneuver, road topology, risk situations (e.g., crossing pedestrians), driver responses, and pedestrian attentiveness. Leveraging RAID, we propose a weakly supervised risk object identification framework that models the relationship between driver's intended maneuver and responses to identify potential risk sources. Additionally, we analyze the role of pedestrian attention in estimating risk and demonstrate the value of the proposed dataset. Experimental evaluations demonstrate that our method achieves 20.6% and 23.1% performance gains over prior state-of-the-art approaches on the RAID and HDDS datasets, respectively.

AI.tldr: Introduces RAID, a large-scale, richly annotated driving dataset for risk perception, and a weakly supervised framework that links driver intent and response (plus pedestrian attentiveness) to identify risk sources, achieving sizable gains over prior SOTA on RAID and HDDS.
AI.motivation: Advance toward zero-collision mobility requires modeling driver risk perception, which depends on both the ego driver’s voluntary responses to stimuli and surrounding users’ attentiveness. Existing datasets and models insufficiently capture this contextual, cognitive dimension of risk.
AI.method: 1) Curate RAID with 4,691 annotated clips spanning diverse traffic, labeled for intended maneuver, road topology, risk situations (e.g., crossing pedestrians), driver responses, and pedestrian attentiveness. 2) Propose a weakly supervised risk-object identification framework that models the relationship between driver’s intended maneuver and observed responses to infer likely risk sources; incorporate pedestrian attention signals. 3) Analyze the contribution of pedestrian attentiveness to risk estimation.
AI.result: The proposed method outperforms prior SOTA by 20.6% on RAID and 23.1% on HDDS, demonstrating improved identification of risk objects and validating the utility of the dataset and attention cues.
AI.conclusion: RAID provides a needed benchmark for contextual driver risk perception. Modeling intent–response relationships with weak supervision, augmented by pedestrian attentiveness, yields substantial performance improvements and generalizes across datasets; pedestrian attention is a key factor in risk estimation.

title: Wisdom of the AI Crowd (AI-CROWD) for Ground Truth Approximation in Content Analysis: A Research Protocol & Validation Using Eleven Large Language Models
summary: Large-scale content analysis is increasingly limited by the absence of observable ground truth or gold-standard labels, as creating such benchmarks through extensive human coding becomes impractical for massive datasets due to high time, cost, and consistency challenges. To overcome this barrier, we introduce the AI-CROWD protocol, which approximates ground truth by leveraging the collective outputs of an ensemble of large language models (LLMs). Rather than asserting that the resulting labels are true ground truth, the protocol generates a consensus-based approximation derived from convergent and divergent inferences across multiple models. By aggregating outputs via majority voting and interrogating agreement/disagreement patterns with diagnostic metrics, AI-CROWD identifies high-confidence classifications while flagging potential ambiguity or model-specific biases.

AI.tldr: AI-CROWD is a protocol that approximates ground truth for large-scale content analysis by ensembling multiple LLMs, using majority voting and agreement diagnostics to produce high-confidence labels and flag ambiguous or biased cases.
AI.motivation: Massive datasets lack feasible gold-standard labels because human coding is too costly, slow, and inconsistent; researchers need scalable labeling with explicit uncertainty to continue large-scale content analysis.
AI.method: Aggregate outputs from diverse LLMs; compute consensus via majority voting; analyze convergent/divergent patterns with diagnostic agreement metrics to quantify confidence and detect ambiguity or model-specific bias; treat outputs as an approximation, not definitive truth.
AI.result: Produces high-confidence classifications where models converge and identifies items with disagreement indicative of ambiguity or bias; enables scalable labeling with uncertainty cues. The abstract reports no quantitative benchmarks.
AI.conclusion: Consensus across multiple LLMs can serve as a practical, uncertainty-aware proxy for ground truth in large-scale content analysis, prioritizing human review for contentious cases and mitigating single-model bias, though it does not guarantee factual correctness.

title: MM-ISTS: Cooperating Irregularly Sampled Time Series Forecasting with Multimodal Vision-Text LLMs
summary: Irregularly sampled time series (ISTS) are widespread in real-world scenarios, exhibiting asynchronous observations on uneven time intervals across variables. Existing ISTS forecasting methods often solely utilize historical observations to predict future ones while falling short in learning contextual semantics and fine-grained temporal patterns. To address these problems, we achieve MM-ISTS, a multimodal framework augmented by vision-text large language models, that bridges temporal, visual, and textual modalities, facilitating ISTS forecasting. MM-ISTS encompasses a novel two-stage encoding mechanism. In particular, a cross-modal vision-text encoding module is proposed to automatically generate informative visual images and textual data, enabling the capture of intricate temporal patterns and comprehensive contextual understanding, in collaboration with multimodal LLMs (MLLMs). In parallel, ISTS encoding extracts complementary yet enriched temporal features from historical ISTS observations, including multi-view embedding fusion and a temporal-variable encoder. Further, we propose an adaptive query-based feature extractor to compress the learned tokens of MLLMs, filtering out small-scale useful knowledge, which in turn reduces computational costs. In addition, a multimodal alignment module with modality-aware gating is designed to alleviate the modality gap across ISTS, images, and text. Extensive experiments on real data offer insight into the effectiveness of the proposed solutions.

AI.tldr: MM-ISTS is a multimodal framework for irregularly sampled time-series forecasting that augments raw signals with automatically generated visual and textual representations processed by multimodal LLMs, aligns them with specialized ISTS encoders via a two-stage pipeline, compresses LLM tokens with adaptive queries, and reports improved accuracy and efficiency on real datasets.
AI.motivation: Conventional ISTS forecasters rely mainly on historical numeric observations and struggle to learn contextual semantics and fine-grained temporal patterns under asynchrony and uneven sampling. Leveraging vision–text LLMs could inject high-level context and richer temporal structure, but modality gaps and computational overhead must be addressed.
AI.method: Two-stage encoding: (1) Cross-modal vision–text encoding auto-generates images and text from ISTS and extracts semantics using multimodal LLMs; (2) Parallel ISTS encoding with multi-view embedding fusion and a temporal–variable encoder for enriched temporal features. An adaptive query-based feature extractor compresses MLLM tokens to reduce cost, and a multimodal alignment module with modality-aware gating fuses ISTS, image, and text representations for forecasting.
AI.result: On real-world datasets, the approach demonstrates effectiveness (improved forecasting performance) and reduced computation via token compression, though specific metrics are not given in the abstract.
AI.conclusion: Bridging temporal, visual, and textual modalities enables better capture of intricate temporal patterns and context for ISTS forecasting. Token compression and modality-aware alignment mitigate costs and modality gaps, suggesting multimodal augmentation is a promising direction for irregularly sampled time series.

title: Beyond Static Frames: Temporal Aggregate-and-Restore Vision Transformer for Human Pose Estimation
summary: Vision Transformers (ViTs) have recently achieved state-of-the-art performance in 2D human pose estimation due to their strong global modeling capability. However, existing ViT-based pose estimators are designed for static images and process each frame independently, thereby ignoring the temporal coherence that exists in video sequences. This limitation often results in unstable predictions, especially in challenging scenes involving motion blur, occlusion, or defocus. In this paper, we propose TAR-ViTPose, a novel Temporal Aggregate-and-Restore Vision Transformer tailored for video-based 2D human pose estimation. TAR-ViTPose enhances static ViT representations by aggregating temporal cues across frames in a plug-and-play manner, leading to more robust and accurate pose estimation. To effectively aggregate joint-specific features that are temporally aligned across frames, we introduce a joint-centric temporal aggregation (JTA) that assigns each joint a learnable query token to selectively attend to its corresponding regions from neighboring frames. Furthermore, we develop a global restoring attention (GRA) to restore the aggregated temporal features back into the token sequence of the current frame, enriching its pose representation while fully preserving global context for precise keypoint localization. Extensive experiments demonstrate that TAR-ViTPose substantially improves upon the single-frame baseline ViTPose, achieving a +2.3 mAP gain on the PoseTrack2017 benchmark. Moreover, our approach outperforms existing state-of-the-art video-based methods, while also achieving a noticeably higher runtime frame rate in real-world applications. Project page: https://github.com/zgspose/TARViTPose.

AI.tldr: TAR-ViTPose extends ViTPose to video by temporally aggregating joint-specific cues and restoring them into the current frame’s tokens, yielding more stable and accurate 2D pose estimates (+2.3 mAP on PoseTrack2017) while running faster than prior video methods.
AI.motivation: ViT-based pose estimators treat video frames independently, neglecting temporal coherence. This leads to unstable keypoints under motion blur, occlusion, or defocus. A method is needed to exploit temporal information without sacrificing ViT’s global context or adding heavy overhead.
AI.method: Temporal Aggregate-and-Restore architecture: (1) Joint-centric Temporal Aggregation (JTA) assigns each joint a learnable query token to selectively attend to its corresponding regions in neighboring frames, effectively aligning and aggregating joint-specific features across time; (2) Global Restoring Attention (GRA) injects the aggregated temporal features back into the current frame’s token sequence, enriching its pose representation while preserving global context. The design is plug-and-play on top of ViTPose.
AI.result: On PoseTrack2017, TAR-ViTPose improves over the single-frame ViTPose baseline by +2.3 mAP and surpasses existing state-of-the-art video-based methods, with a notably higher runtime frame rate in practical settings.
AI.conclusion: Exploiting temporal cues via joint-centric aggregation and globally contextual restoration stabilizes and boosts 2D video pose estimation under challenging conditions, delivering both accuracy gains and efficiency suitable for real-world deployment.

title: LIT-RAGBench: Benchmarking Generator Capabilities of Large Language Models in Retrieval-Augmented Generation
comment: Published as a conference paper at LREC 2026
summary: Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) is a framework in which a Generator, such as a Large Language Model (LLM), produces answers by retrieving documents from an external collection using a Retriever. In practice, Generators must integrate evidence from long contexts, perform multi-step reasoning, interpret tables, and abstain when evidence is missing. However, existing benchmarks for Generators provide limited coverage, with none enabling simultaneous evaluation of multiple capabilities under unified conditions. To bridge the gap between existing evaluations and practical use, we introduce LIT-RAGBench (the Logic, Integration, Table, Reasoning, and Abstention RAG Generator Benchmark), which defines five categories: Integration, Reasoning, Logic, Table, and Abstention, each further divided into practical evaluation aspects. LIT-RAGBench systematically covers patterns combining multiple aspects across categories. By using fictional entities and scenarios, LIT-RAGBench evaluates answers grounded in the provided external documents. The dataset consists of 114 human-constructed Japanese questions and an English version generated by machine translation with human curation. We use LLM-as-a-Judge for scoring and report category-wise and overall accuracy. Across API-based and open-weight models, no model exceeds 90% overall accuracy. By making strengths and weaknesses measurable within each category, LIT-RAGBench serves as a valuable metric for model selection in practical RAG deployments and for building RAG-specialized models. We release LIT-RAGBench, including the dataset and evaluation code, at https://github.com/Koki-Itai/LIT-RAGBench.

AI.tldr: LIT-RAGBench is a bilingual (JP→EN) benchmark for evaluating RAG generators across five practical capability areas—Integration, Reasoning, Logic, Table handling, and Abstention—using grounded, fictional documents, LLM-as-a-Judge scoring, and category-wise/overall accuracy; no tested model surpasses 90% overall.
AI.motivation: Real-world RAG requires integrating long contexts, multi-step reasoning, table interpretation, and the ability to abstain when evidence is missing, but existing generator benchmarks cover these only partially and not under unified, combined conditions.
AI.method: Define five categories (Integration, Reasoning, Logic, Table, Abstention) with practical sub-aspects; construct 114 human-authored Japanese questions and machine-translated, human-curated English counterparts; design tasks that systematically combine multiple aspects; use fictional entities to ensure answers must be grounded in the provided documents; evaluate API-based and open-weight models with LLM-as-a-Judge, reporting per-category and overall accuracy.
