AI.result: Across all tested models, none exceeds 90% overall accuracy; the benchmark exposes category-specific strengths and weaknesses. Dataset and evaluation code are released publicly (GitHub).
AI.conclusion: LIT-RAGBench bridges the gap between narrow evaluations and practical RAG needs, providing a fine-grained, unified metric for selecting models in deployment and for guiding the development of RAG-specialized systems.

title: Demystifying KAN for Vision Tasks: The RepKAN Approach
summary: Remote sensing image classification is essential for Earth observation, yet standard CNNs and Transformers often function as uninterpretable black-boxes. We propose RepKAN, a novel architecture that integrates the structural efficiency of CNNs with the non-linear representational power of KANs. By utilizing a dual-path design -- Spatial Linear and Spectral Non-linear -- RepKAN enables the autonomous discovery of class-specific spectral fingerprints and physical interaction manifolds. Experimental results on the EuroSAT and NWPU-RESISC45 datasets demonstrate that RepKAN provides explicit physically interpretable reasoning while outperforming state-of-the-art models. These findings indicate that RepKAN holds significant potential to serve as the backbone for future interpretable visual foundation models.

AI.tldr: RepKAN is a dual-path remote-sensing classifier that merges CNN-like spatial processing with KAN-based nonlinear spectral modeling to learn interpretable spectral fingerprints and achieves state-of-the-art results on EuroSAT and NWPU-RESISC45.
AI.motivation: Remote sensing stakeholders need models that are both accurate and physically interpretable; standard CNNs/Transformers behave as black boxes and do not expose spectral/physical reasoning used for decisions.
AI.method: A two-branch architecture: (1) a Spatial Linear path (CNN-style) for efficient spatial feature extraction, and (2) a Spectral Non-linear path leveraging Kolmogorov–Arnold Networks (KANs) to capture nonlinear spectral interactions. The branches are fused to enable discovery of class-specific spectral fingerprints and physical interaction manifolds, yielding interpretable reasoning.
AI.result: On EuroSAT and NWPU-RESISC45, RepKAN outperforms contemporary baselines while providing explicit, physically motivated interpretations of decisions. The abstract implies accuracy gains and improved interpretability but does not report exact metrics.
AI.conclusion: RepKAN delivers both performance and transparency, suggesting it could serve as an interpretable backbone for future visual foundation models in remote sensing.

title: FTSplat: Feed-forward Triangle Splatting Network
summary: High-fidelity three-dimensional (3D) reconstruction is essential for robotics and simulation. While Neural Radiance Fields (NeRF) and 3D Gaussian Splatting (3DGS) achieve impressive rendering quality, their reliance on time-consuming per-scene optimization limits real-time deployment. Emerging feed-forward Gaussian splatting methods improve efficiency but often lack explicit, manifold geometry required for direct simulation. To address these limitations, we propose a feed-forward framework for triangle primitive generation that directly predicts continuous triangle surfaces from calibrated multi-view images. Our method produces simulation-ready models in a single forward pass, obviating the need for per-scene optimization or post-processing. We introduce a pixel-aligned triangle generation module and incorporate relative 3D point cloud supervision to enhance geometric learning stability and consistency. Experiments demonstrate that our method achieves efficient reconstruction while maintaining seamless compatibility with standard graphics and robotic simulators.

AI.tldr: Feed-forward system that predicts explicit, continuous triangle meshes directly from calibrated multi-view images in a single pass, using pixel-aligned triangle generation and relative point-cloud supervision to produce simulator-ready reconstructions without per-scene optimization or post-processing.
AI.motivation: High-quality 3D reconstructions are needed for graphics, robotics, and simulation, which require explicit, manifold geometry. NeRF/3DGS offer great rendering but are slow due to per-scene optimization and are often implicit; existing feed-forward splatting is faster but lacks manifold meshes, limiting direct use in simulators.
AI.method: A feed-forward triangle primitive generation framework that directly outputs continuous triangle surfaces from multi-view images. It includes a pixel-aligned triangle generation module and trains with relative 3D point cloud supervision to stabilize and regularize geometry learning.
AI.result: On experimental benchmarks, the method reconstructs scenes efficiently in a single forward pass while producing explicit, manifold triangle meshes compatible with standard graphics and robotic simulators, avoiding per-scene optimization and post-processing.
AI.conclusion: Directly predicting triangle surfaces enables fast, simulator-ready 3D reconstruction, bridging the gap between efficient feed-forward methods and the need for explicit, manifold geometry in practical robotics and graphics pipelines.

title: FlashPrefill: Instantaneous Pattern Discovery and Thresholding for Ultra-Fast Long-Context Prefilling
summary: Long-context modeling is a pivotal capability for Large Language Models, yet the quadratic complexity of attention remains a critical bottleneck, particularly during the compute-intensive prefilling phase. While various sparse attention mechanisms have been explored, they typically suffer from either significant search latency or insufficient sparsity. In this paper, we propose FlashPrefill, a framework enabling ultra-fast prefilling via instantaneous pattern discovery and thresholding. FlashPrefill leverages a fast block-searching technique to simultaneously locate dynamic vertical, slash, and block-sparse attention patterns. Crucially, it introduces a dynamic thresholding mechanism that bypasses the prohibitive overhead of sorting or accumulating attention scores while effectively eliminating the long-tail distribution to enhance sparsity. Extensive evaluations demonstrate that FlashPrefill achieves a substantial leap in efficiency, delivering an unprecedented 27.78x speedup on 256K sequences. Notably, unlike existing methods that incur efficiency degradation on shorter contexts, FlashPrefill maintains a 1.71x speedup even at a 4K context length, demonstrating its robustness and practical utility across varying sequence scales.

AI.tldr: FlashPrefill accelerates the LLM prefilling stage by instantly discovering dynamic sparse attention patterns (vertical, slash, block) via fast block search and pruning with a lightweight dynamic threshold, avoiding sorting overhead; it reports up to 27.78x speedup at 256K tokens and 1.71x even at 4K.
AI.motivation: Quadratic attention makes long-context prefilling prohibitively expensive. Prior sparse attention methods either spend too much time finding sparse patterns (search latency) or fail to prune enough (insufficient sparsity), and often degrade on short contexts. A method is needed that quickly discovers effective sparsity without heavy overhead and works across sequence lengths.
AI.method: Introduce FlashPrefill: (1) a fast block-searching technique that simultaneously identifies dynamic vertical, slash, and block-sparse attention patterns; (2) a dynamic thresholding mechanism that prunes low attention scores on the fly, avoiding expensive sorting/accumulation and removing the long-tail distribution to increase sparsity. Together, these enable ultra-fast prefilling through instantaneous pattern discovery and thresholding.
AI.result: Extensive evaluations show large efficiency gains: up to 27.78x speedup on 256K-token sequences, while still achieving 1.71x speedup at 4K tokens—addressing the common efficiency drop on short contexts in competing methods.
AI.conclusion: FlashPrefill is a robust, practical approach to accelerate LLM prefilling via cheap, dynamic sparsity discovery and thresholding, delivering strong speedups for very long contexts without sacrificing efficiency on shorter ones.

title: OD-RASE: Ontology-Driven Risk Assessment and Safety Enhancement for Autonomous Driving
summary: Although autonomous driving systems demonstrate high perception performance, they still face limitations when handling rare situations or complex road structures. Such road infrastructures are designed for human drivers, safety improvements are typically introduced only after accidents occur. This reactive approach poses a significant challenge for autonomous systems, which require proactive risk mitigation. To address this issue, we propose OD-RASE, a framework for enhancing the safety of autonomous driving systems by detecting road structures that cause traffic accidents and connecting these findings to infrastructure development. First, we formalize an ontology based on specialized domain knowledge of road traffic systems. In parallel, we generate infrastructure improvement proposals using a large-scale visual language model (LVLM) and use ontology-driven data filtering to enhance their reliability. This process automatically annotates improvement proposals on pre-accident road images, leading to the construction of a new dataset. Furthermore, we introduce the Baseline approach (OD-RASE model), which leverages LVLM and a diffusion model to produce both infrastructure improvement proposals and generated images of the improved road environment. Our experiments demonstrate that ontology-driven data filtering enables highly accurate prediction of accident-causing road structures and the corresponding improvement plans. We believe that this work contributes to the overall safety of traffic environments and marks an important step toward the broader adoption of autonomous driving systems.

AI.tldr: OD-RASE is a framework that detects accident-causing road structures and proposes infrastructure improvements by combining a road-traffic ontology with LVLM- and diffusion-based generation, using ontology-driven filtering to ensure reliability and to build an auto-annotated dataset; experiments report high accuracy in predicting risky structures and viable fixes.
AI.motivation: Autonomous driving still struggles with rare corner cases and complex, human-oriented road designs. Current infrastructure safety improvements are reactive (post-accident), which is ill-suited for autonomous systems that need proactive risk mitigation. There is a need to connect perception-derived risk insights back to infrastructure planning to prevent accidents before they happen.
AI.method: 1) Formalize a domain ontology for road traffic systems. 2) Use a large visual-language model (LVLM) to propose infrastructure improvements for pre-accident road images. 3) Apply ontology-driven data filtering to validate and refine proposals, automatically annotating the images and constructing a new dataset. 4) Introduce a baseline OD-RASE model that combines an LVLM and a diffusion model to output both improvement proposals and synthesized images of the improved road environment.
AI.result: Ontology-driven filtering yields highly accurate predictions of accident-causing road structures and matching improvement plans; the process also creates a curated, auto-annotated dataset from pre-accident imagery. Generated improved-scene images illustrate proposed changes.
AI.conclusion: Linking ontology-grounded reasoning with generative LVLM/diffusion models can proactively identify risky infrastructure and propose actionable fixes, contributing to safer traffic environments and supporting broader adoption of autonomous driving systems.

title: SPOT: Span-level Pause-of-Thought for Efficient and Interpretable Latent Reasoning in Large Language Models
summary: Explicit Chain-of-Thought improves the reasoning performance of large language models but often incurs high inference cost due to verbose token-level traces. While recent approaches reduce this overhead via concise prompting or step pruning, they largely truncate what the model says rather than internalize what the model thinks. Latent reasoning offers a promising alternative by performing computation in the hidden space, yet prior methods face two critical challenges. Many existing approaches rely on rigid point-to-point alignment, forcing a latent token to approximate the final representation of a reasoning step, which can be insufficient to capture the dense, variable-length semantics of an entire reasoning segment. Furthermore, these methods often suffer from a lack of interpretability: latent states are commonly produced by unconstrained optimization or embedding mixing, yielding vectors that are difficult to decode or audit under the pretrained language head. We propose SPOT, a flexible framework that compresses explicit CoT into compact latent pause tokens without enforcing a fixed response template. At the core of SPOT is Span-level Semantic Alignment, a Sinkhorn optimal-transport objective that softly matches each pause token to the semantics of an entire reasoning segment, overcoming the rigidity of step-end alignment. To further improve interpretability, SPOT introduces a Frozen-Head Decoding Constraint that keeps latent states directly decodable as token distributions under the frozen pretrained LM head, enabling readable keyword interpretations of latent thoughts. Experiments on reasoning benchmarks demonstrate that SPOT improves accuracy by 2.3 points on average while reducing generated tokens by 37.5% and provides faithful semantic interpretations of the latent reasoning process.

AI.tldr: SPOT compresses explicit chain-of-thought into a few latent “pause tokens,” aligned to whole reasoning spans via optimal transport and constrained to be decodable by the frozen LM head, yielding higher accuracy with far fewer generated tokens and interpretable latent states.
AI.motivation: Explicit CoT boosts reasoning but is expensive at inference due to long token traces. Prior fixes mostly shorten outputs rather than truly internalize reasoning. Existing latent-reasoning methods suffer from (1) rigid point-to-point alignment that cannot capture variable-length, dense span semantics, and (2) poor interpretability because latent vectors are produced by unconstrained mixing/optimization and are hard to decode under the pretrained head.
AI.method: SPOT introduces compact latent pause tokens learned from explicit CoT without a fixed response template. It uses Span-level Semantic Alignment: a Sinkhorn optimal-transport objective that softly matches each pause token to the semantics of an entire reasoning segment, avoiding strict step-end alignment. To preserve interpretability, a Frozen-Head Decoding Constraint keeps latent states decodable as token distributions under the frozen pretrained LM head, enabling readable keyword-like interpretations of the latent thoughts.
AI.result: On reasoning benchmarks, SPOT increases accuracy by 2.3 points on average while reducing generated tokens by 37.5%, and the latent states yield faithful, human-readable summaries of the underlying reasoning segments.
AI.conclusion: Span-aligned, decode-constrained latent pause tokens can internalize reasoning more efficiently than explicit CoT, delivering a better accuracy–cost trade-off while remaining interpretable. SPOT provides a flexible, template-free framework for latent reasoning with both efficiency and auditability.

title: Probing Visual Concepts in Lightweight Vision-Language Models for Automated Driving
summary: The use of Vision-Language Models (VLMs) in automated driving applications is becoming increasingly common, with the aim of leveraging their reasoning and generalisation capabilities to handle long tail scenarios. However, these models often fail on simple visual questions that are highly relevant to automated driving, and the reasons behind these failures remain poorly understood. In this work, we examine the intermediate activations of VLMs and assess the extent to which specific visual concepts are linearly encoded, with the goal of identifying bottlenecks in the flow of visual information. Specifically, we create counterfactual image sets that differ only in a targeted visual concept and then train linear probes to distinguish between them using the activations of four state-of-the-art (SOTA) VLMs. Our results show that concepts such as the presence of an object or agent in a scene are explicitly and linearly encoded, whereas other spatial visual concepts, such as the orientation of an object or agent, are only implicitly encoded by the spatial structure retained by the vision encoder. In parallel, we observe that in certain cases, even when a concept is linearly encoded in the model's activations, the model still fails to answer correctly. This leads us to identify two failure modes. The first is perceptual failure, where the visual information required to answer a question is not linearly encoded in the model's activations. The second is cognitive failure, where the visual information is present but the model fails to align it correctly with language semantics. Finally, we show that increasing the distance of the object in question quickly degrades the linear separability of the corresponding visual concept. Overall, our findings improve our understanding of failure cases in VLMs on simple visual tasks that are highly relevant to automated driving.

AI.tldr: They probe the internal activations of four SOTA vision‑language models on counterfactual driving images with linear classifiers, showing which concepts are linearly encoded, identifying perceptual vs cognitive failure modes, and revealing that distance quickly degrades concept separability.
AI.motivation: VLMs are being adopted for automated driving to handle long‑tail scenarios, yet they often fail even on simple, safety‑critical visual questions. It is unclear whether such failures stem from missing visual representations or from misalignment between vision and language. Understanding these bottlenecks is essential to improve reliability.
AI.method: Construct counterfactual image sets that isolate a single visual concept (e.g., presence, orientation). Extract intermediate activations from four state‑of‑the‑art VLMs and train linear probes to test whether the target concept is linearly separable across layers. Compare probe accuracy and analyze errors to distinguish representation (perceptual) vs alignment (cognitive) failures; study how object distance affects separability.
AI.result: Object/agent presence is explicitly and linearly encoded in activations. Spatial concepts such as orientation are only implicitly represented via the vision encoder’s spatial structure and are not linearly separable. Even when a concept is linearly encoded, models can still answer incorrectly, indicating cognitive (alignment) failures; when it is not encoded, errors are perceptual. Increasing object distance rapidly reduces linear separability of the corresponding concept.
AI.conclusion: Current VLMs used for driving encode presence robustly but provide weak, fragile encodings for spatial attributes and for distant objects; moreover, misalignment between visual representations and language can cause errors even when the signal exists. Improving spatial representations, preserving high‑resolution features for far objects, and strengthening vision‑language alignment are promising directions; linear‑probe diagnostics offer a practical tool to localize bottlenecks.

title: Mind the Gap: Pitfalls of LLM Alignment with Asian Public Opinion
summary: Large Language Models (LLMs) are increasingly being deployed in multilingual, multicultural settings, yet their reliance on predominantly English-centric training data risks misalignment with the diverse cultural values of different societies. In this paper, we present a comprehensive, multilingual audit of the cultural alignment of contemporary LLMs including GPT-4o-Mini, Gemini-2.5-Flash, Llama 3.2, Mistral and Gemma 3 across India, East Asia and Southeast Asia. Our study specifically focuses on the sensitive domain of religion as the prism for broader alignment. To facilitate this, we conduct a multi-faceted analysis of every LLM's internal representations, using log-probs/logits, to compare the model's opinion distributions against ground-truth public attitudes. We find that while the popular models generally align with public opinion on broad social issues, they consistently fail to accurately represent religious viewpoints, especially those of minority groups, often amplifying negative stereotypes. Lightweight interventions, such as demographic priming and native language prompting, partially mitigate but do not eliminate these cultural gaps. We further show that downstream evaluations on bias benchmarks (such as CrowS-Pairs, IndiBias, ThaiCLI, KoBBQ) reveal persistent harms and under-representation in sensitive contexts. Our findings underscore the urgent need for systematic, regionally grounded audits to ensure equitable global deployment of LLMs.

AI.tldr: Multilingual audit finds major LLMs align with broad social attitudes but misrepresent religious viewpoints—especially for minority groups—amplifying stereotypes; prompt-level fixes only partially help; persistent bias appears in benchmarks, motivating regionally grounded audits.
AI.motivation: LLMs trained largely on English-centric data risk cultural misalignment when deployed globally. Religion, as a sensitive and value-laden domain, serves as an effective probe of broader cultural alignment across diverse societies.
AI.method: Evaluate multiple contemporary LLMs (GPT-4o-Mini, Gemini-2.5-Flash, Llama 3.2, Mistral, Gemma 3) across India, East Asia, and Southeast Asia. Use internal probability signals (log-probs/logits) to derive opinion distributions and compare against ground-truth public attitudes. Test lightweight mitigations (demographic priming, native-language prompting). Validate downstream impacts using bias benchmarks (CrowS-Pairs, IndiBias, ThaiCLI, KoBBQ).
AI.result: Models generally track public opinion on broad social issues but fail to accurately represent religious viewpoints, particularly for minority groups, and often amplify negative stereotypes. Demographic priming and native-language prompting reduce but do not eliminate gaps. Benchmarks reveal persistent harms and under-representation in sensitive contexts.
AI.conclusion: State-of-the-art LLMs are not reliably culturally aligned on religion across studied regions; minor prompt-level interventions are insufficient. Systematic, regionally grounded audits and deeper data/model interventions are needed for equitable global deployment.

title: TempoSyncDiff: Distilled Temporally-Consistent Diffusion for Low-Latency Audio-Driven Talking Head Generation
summary: Diffusion models have recently advanced photorealistic human synthesis, although practical talking-head generation (THG) remains constrained by high inference latency, temporal instability such as flicker and identity drift, and imperfect audio-visual alignment under challenging speech conditions. This paper introduces TempoSyncDiff, a reference-conditioned latent diffusion framework that explores few-step inference for efficient audio-driven talking-head generation. The approach adopts a teacher-student distillation formulation in which a diffusion teacher trained with a standard noise prediction objective guides a lightweight student denoiser capable of operating with significantly fewer inference steps to improve generation stability. The framework incorporates identity anchoring and temporal regularization designed to mitigate identity drift and frame-to-frame flicker during synthesis, while viseme-based audio conditioning provides coarse lip motion control. Experiments on the LRS3 dataset report denoising-stage component-level metrics relative to VAE reconstructions and preliminary latency characterization, including CPU-only and edge computing measurements and feasibility estimates for edge deployment. The results suggest that distilled diffusion models can retain much of the reconstruction behaviour of a stronger teacher while enabling substantially lower latency inference. The study is positioned as an initial step toward practical diffusion-based talking-head generation under constrained computational settings. GitHub: https://mazumdarsoumya.github.io/TempoSyncDiff

AI.tldr: TempoSyncDiff proposes a distilled, reference‑conditioned latent diffusion model for audio‑driven talking‑head generation that targets few‑step inference, reduced flicker/identity drift, and better latency for constrained devices, while maintaining much of a stronger teacher’s reconstruction behavior.
AI.motivation: Current diffusion‑based talking‑head generation suffers from high latency, temporal instability (flicker, identity drift), and imperfect audio‑visual alignment, making deployment on CPUs/edge devices impractical. The work aims to enable practical, lower‑latency THG without sacrificing core visual and lip‑sync fidelity.
AI.method: A teacher‑student distillation framework trains a lightweight student denoiser to mimic a standard diffusion teacher, enabling generation in far fewer denoising steps. The model operates in a reference‑conditioned latent space, adds identity anchoring and temporal regularization to stabilize identity and reduce frame‑to‑frame flicker, and uses viseme‑based audio conditioning for coarse lip‑motion control. Evaluations on LRS3 include denoising‑stage component metrics vs VAE reconstructions and latency on CPU/edge settings, plus feasibility estimates for deployment.
AI.result: Distilled students retain much of the teacher’s reconstruction behavior while achieving substantially lower latency; preliminary CPU‑only and edge measurements indicate improved feasibility. Reported metrics are at the denoising/component level relative to VAE reconstructions, with an emphasis on latency characterization rather than exhaustive perceptual benchmarks.
AI.conclusion: Distillation can make diffusion‑based talking‑head generation more practical under compute constraints, with reduced inference steps and improved temporal stability. The work is an initial step toward deployable THG, calling for broader benchmarks, stronger baselines, and end‑to‑end evaluations to validate real‑world utility.

title: SLER-IR: Spherical Layer-wise Expert Routing for All-in-One Image Restoration
summary: Image restoration under diverse degradations remains challenging for unified all-in-one frameworks due to feature interference and insufficient expert specialization. We propose SLER-IR, a spherical layer-wise expert routing framework that dynamically activates specialized experts across network layers. To ensure reliable routing, we introduce a Spherical Uniform Degradation Embedding with contrastive learning, which maps degradation representations onto a hypersphere to eliminate geometry bias in linear embedding spaces. In addition, a Global-Local Granularity Fusion (GLGF) module integrates global semantics and local degradation cues to address spatially non-uniform degradations and the train-test granularity gap. Experiments on three-task and five-task benchmarks demonstrate that SLER-IR achieves consistent improvements over state-of-the-art methods in both PSNR and SSIM. Code and models will be publicly released.

AI.tldr: SLER-IR improves all-in-one image restoration by dynamically routing layer-wise experts using spherical degradation embeddings and a global–local fusion module, yielding higher PSNR/SSIM than prior methods on multi-task benchmarks.
AI.motivation: Unified restoration across diverse degradations suffers from feature interference and insufficient specialization; routing must be reliable, and models must handle spatially non-uniform degradations and a train–test granularity gap.
AI.method: Introduce SLER-IR: (1) spherical layer-wise expert routing that activates specialized experts across network layers; (2) Spherical Uniform Degradation Embedding with contrastive learning to map degradation representations onto a hypersphere, removing geometry bias of linear embeddings; (3) Global-Local Granularity Fusion (GLGF) to integrate global semantics with local degradation cues for spatially varying degradations and to bridge granularity mismatch.
AI.result: Across three-task and five-task benchmarks, SLER-IR consistently outperforms state-of-the-art methods in both PSNR and SSIM.
AI.conclusion: Combining dynamic expert routing with spherical degradation embeddings and GLGF mitigates interference and granularity issues in all-in-one restoration, delivering state-of-the-art quality; code and models will be released.

title: The Art That Poses Back: Assessing AI Pastiches after Contemporary Artworks
summary: This study explores artificial visual creativity, focusing on ChatGPT's ability to generate new images intentionally pastiching original artworks such as paintings, drawings, sculptures and installations. The process involved twelve artists from Romania, Bulgaria, France, Austria, and the United Kingdom, each invited to contribute with three of their artworks and to grade and comment on the AI-generated versions. The analysis combines human evaluation with computational methods aimed at detecting visual and stylistic similarities or divergences between the original works and their AI-produced renditions. The results point to a significant gap between color and texture-based similarity and compositional, conceptual, and perceptual one. Consequently, we advocate for the use of a "style transfer dashboard" of complementary metrics to evaluate the similarity between pastiches and originals, rather than using a single style metric. The artists' comments revealed limitations of ChatGPT's pastiches after contemporary artworks, which were perceived by the authors of the originals as lacking dimensionality, context, and intentional sense, and seeming more of a paraphrase or an approximate quotation rather than as a valuable, emotion-evoking artwork.

AI.tldr: Study evaluates ChatGPT’s capacity to create pastiches of contemporary artworks; finds AI matches surface style (color/texture) but misses deeper compositional, conceptual, and perceptual aspects; proposes a multi-metric “style transfer dashboard.”
AI.motivation: Assess whether AI can intentionally pastiche living artists’ works in a way that meaningfully reflects style and intent, and determine how similarity should be measured beyond single, surface-level metrics.
AI.method: Twelve artists (from RO, BG, FR, AT, UK) each provided three artworks. ChatGPT generated corresponding pastiches. Artists graded and commented on the AI outputs. Computational analyses compared originals and pastiches across visual/stylistic dimensions to detect similarities/divergences.
AI.result: Marked discrepancy between color/texture similarity and higher-level compositional, conceptual, and perceptual similarity. Artists judged AI pastiches as lacking dimensionality, context, and intentionality—coming off as paraphrases/approximate quotations, not emotion-evoking works. This motivates a composite evaluation via complementary metrics.
AI.conclusion: A single style metric is inadequate for judging AI pastiche quality. A dashboard of complementary metrics is recommended. Current AI pastiches struggle to capture deeper artistic intent and structure, limiting their perceived artistic value.

title: Adaptive Radial Projection on Fourier Magnitude Spectrum for Document Image Skew Estimation
comment: This paper has been accepted to ICIP 2022
summary: Skew estimation is one of the vital tasks in document processing systems, especially for scanned document images, because its performance impacts subsequent steps directly. Over the years, an enormous number of researches focus on this challenging problem in the rise of digitization age. In this research, we first propose a novel skew estimation method that extracts the dominant skew angle of the given document image by applying an Adaptive Radial Projection on the 2D Discrete Fourier Magnitude spectrum. Second, we introduce a high quality skew estimation dataset DISE-2021 to assess the performance of different estimators. Finally, we provide comprehensive analyses that focus on multiple improvement aspects of Fourier-based methods. Our results show that the proposed method is robust, reliable, and outperforms all compared methods. The source code is available at https://github.com/phamquiluan/jdeskew.

AI.tldr: They propose a Fourier-spectrum approach that estimates a document’s dominant skew angle via an Adaptive Radial Projection on the 2D DFT magnitude, introduce a new evaluation dataset (DISE-2021), analyze improvements for Fourier-based methods, and report state-of-the-art results with public code.
AI.motivation: Skew degrades downstream document processing (e.g., OCR and layout analysis); scanned images are often rotated, so accurate, robust, and fast skew estimation is critical. Existing methods are numerous but have limitations and lack a strong, shared benchmark for fair comparison, motivating a new method and dataset.
AI.method: Compute the 2D Discrete Fourier Transform of the image, take the magnitude spectrum, and apply an Adaptive Radial Projection to detect the dominant orientation corresponding to the document’s skew angle; complement with comprehensive analyses targeting enhancements to Fourier-based estimators and validate using a new dataset (DISE-2021).
AI.result: Across evaluations, the proposed approach is robust and reliable, outperforming all compared skew estimators on the introduced DISE-2021 dataset; implementation is released at https://github.com/phamquiluan/jdeskew.
AI.conclusion: An adaptive Fourier-magnitude projection yields accurate skew estimation, paired with a new benchmark and analyses that advance Fourier-based solutions; results suggest this method is a strong, practical choice for document deskewing in real-world pipelines.

title: Transparent AI for Mathematics: Transformer-Based Large Language Models for Mathematical Entity Relationship Extraction with XAI
summary: Mathematical text understanding is a challenging task due to the presence of specialized entities and complex relationships between them. This study formulates mathematical problem interpretation as a Mathematical Entity Relation Extraction (MERE) task, where operands are treated as entities and operators as their relationships. Transformer-based models are applied to automatically extract these relations from mathematical text, with Bidirectional Encoder Representations from Transformers (BERT) achieving the best performance, reaching an accuracy of 99.39%. To enhance transparency and trust in the model's predictions, Explainable Artificial Intelligence (XAI) is incorporated using Shapley Additive Explanations (SHAP). The explainability analysis reveals how specific textual and mathematical features influence relation prediction, providing insights into feature importance and model behavior. By combining transformer-based learning, a task-specific dataset, and explainable modeling, this work offers an effective and interpretable framework for MERE, supporting future applications in automated problem solving, knowledge graph construction, and intelligent educational systems.

AI.tldr: They recast mathematical text understanding as a mathematical entity–relation extraction task (operands as entities, operators as relations), use transformers (BERT best at 99.39% accuracy) to extract relations, and apply SHAP to explain predictions, yielding an accurate and interpretable framework for downstream math-automation applications.
AI.motivation: Mathematical text contains specialized symbols and dense relational structure that standard NLP struggles to capture. There is also a need for transparent, trustworthy models to support tasks like automated problem solving, knowledge graph construction, and educational tooling.
AI.method: Formulate Mathematical Entity Relation Extraction (MERE) where operands are entities and operators are relations; construct a task-specific dataset; train and compare transformer-based models for relation extraction, with BERT as the top performer; integrate Explainable AI via SHAP to attribute predictions to textual/mathematical features and analyze feature importance.
AI.result: BERT achieves 99.39% accuracy on MERE. SHAP analyses identify how specific tokens and mathematical features drive relation predictions, clarifying model behavior and feature salience.
AI.conclusion: Combining transformers with SHAP-based explanations provides an effective and interpretable solution for MERE. The approach offers a viable foundation for automated math problem solving, math knowledge graph construction, and intelligent tutoring systems, improving both performance and transparency.

title: Text-Driven Emotionally Continuous Talking Face Generation
summary: Talking Face Generation (TFG) strives to create realistic and emotionally expressive digital faces. While previous TFG works have mastered the creation of naturalistic facial movements, they typically express a fixed target emotion in synthetic videos and lack the ability to exhibit continuously changing and natural expressions like humans do when conveying information. To synthesize realistic videos, we propose a novel task called Emotionally Continuous Talking Face Generation (EC-TFG), which takes a text segment and an emotion description with varying emotions as driving data, aiming to generate a video where the person speaks the text while reflecting the emotional changes within the description. Alongside this, we introduce a customized model, i.e., Temporal-Intensive Emotion Modulated Talking Face Generation (TIE-TFG), which innovatively manages dynamic emotional variations by employing Temporal-Intensive Emotion Fluctuation Modeling, allowing it to provide emotion variation sequences corresponding to the input text to drive continuous facial expression changes in synthesized videos. Extensive evaluations demonstrate our method's exceptional ability to produce smooth emotion transitions and uphold high-quality visuals and motion authenticity across diverse emotional states.

AI.tldr: They define a new task (EC-TFG) and propose a model (TIE-TFG) that generates talking-face videos with continuously varying, text-aligned emotions, yielding smooth transitions, realistic motion, and high visual quality.
AI.motivation: Most talking-face methods fix a single target emotion per video; they cannot portray the natural, temporally evolving expressions humans show while speaking. A system that follows changing emotions over time would make synthesized videos more realistic and expressive.
AI.method: Introduce Emotionally Continuous Talking Face Generation (EC-TFG): input is a text segment plus an emotion description that varies over time. Propose Temporal-Intensive Emotion–Modulated TFG (TIE-TFG), which uses Temporal-Intensive Emotion Fluctuation Modeling to produce an emotion-variation sequence aligned to the input text and modulate the face generator to drive continuous expression changes and smooth transitions.
AI.result: On extensive evaluations, the approach delivers smooth emotion transitions while maintaining high image quality and motion authenticity across a range of emotional states.
AI.conclusion: Defining EC-TFG and introducing TIE-TFG effectively model dynamic emotional variations during speech, enabling realistic, continuously expressive talking-face videos with strong temporal coherence and visual fidelity.

title: LucidNFT: LR-Anchored Multi-Reward Preference Optimization for Generative Real-World Super-Resolution
summary: Generative real-world image super-resolution (Real-ISR) can synthesize visually convincing details from severely degraded low-resolution (LR) inputs, yet its stochastic sampling makes a critical failure mode hard to avoid: outputs may look sharp but be unfaithful to the LR evidence (semantic and structural hallucination), while such LR-anchored faithfulness is difficult to assess without HR ground truth. Preference-based reinforcement learning (RL) is a natural fit because each LR input yields a rollout group of candidates to compare. However, effective alignment in Real-ISR is hindered by (i) the lack of a degradation-robust LR-referenced faithfulness signal, and (ii) a rollout-group optimization bottleneck where naive multi-reward scalarization followed by normalization compresses objective-wise contrasts, causing advantage collapse and weakening the reward-weighted updates in DiffusionNFT-style forward fine-tuning. Moreover, (iii) limited coverage of real degradations restricts rollout diversity and preference signal quality. We propose LucidNFT, a multi-reward RL framework for flow-matching Real-ISR. LucidNFT introduces LucidConsistency, a degradation-robust semantic evaluator that makes LR-anchored faithfulness measurable and optimizable; a decoupled advantage normalization strategy that preserves objective-wise contrasts within each LR-conditioned rollout group before fusion, preventing advantage collapse; and LucidLR, a large-scale collection of real-world degraded images to support robust RL fine-tuning. Experiments show that LucidNFT consistently improves strong flow-based Real-ISR baselines, achieving better perceptual-faithfulness trade-offs with stable optimization dynamics across diverse real-world scenarios.

AI.tldr: LucidNFT is a multi-reward, preference-based RL framework for flow-matching real-world image super-resolution that reduces hallucinations by introducing a degradation-robust faithfulness evaluator, a new advantage normalization to avoid reward collapse, and a large real-degradation dataset; it yields more faithful yet perceptually sharp reconstructions and more stable optimization than strong baselines.
AI.motivation: Real-ISR models often hallucinate structures not supported by the LR input, and faithfulness is hard to measure without HR ground truth. Preference-based RL is attractive due to per-input rollout comparisons, but existing approaches lack (1) an LR-referenced, degradation-robust faithfulness signal; (2) sound multi-reward optimization, as naive scalarization and post-hoc normalization compress contrasts and cause advantage collapse; and (3) sufficient coverage of real degradations to ensure diverse rollouts and reliable preferences.
AI.method: LucidNFT aligns flow-matching Real-ISR via multi-reward RL. It proposes: (a) LucidConsistency, a semantic evaluator robust to degradations that scores LR-anchored faithfulness; (b) a decoupled advantage normalization that preserves per-objective contrasts within each LR-conditioned rollout group before fusing rewards, avoiding advantage collapse in DiffusionNFT-style forward fine-tuning; and (c) LucidLR, a large-scale corpus of real degraded images to enhance rollout diversity and preference signal quality.
AI.result: Across diverse real-world scenarios, LucidNFT consistently improves strong flow-based Real-ISR baselines, delivering a better trade-off between perceptual quality and LR faithfulness and exhibiting more stable optimization dynamics.
AI.conclusion: By making LR-anchored faithfulness measurable and optimizing it with contrast-preserving multi-reward RL and richer real degradations, LucidNFT reduces semantic/structural hallucinations in Real-ISR while maintaining perceptual sharpness. The framework appears broadly applicable to flow-based SR and potentially extensible to other generative restorations.

title: Evaluation of Deontic Conditional Reasoning in Large Language Models: The Case of Wason's Selection Task
comment: To appear in the Proceedings of EACL 2026
summary: As large language models (LLMs) advance in linguistic competence, their reasoning abilities are gaining increasing attention. In humans, reasoning often performs well in domain specific settings, particularly in normative rather than purely formal contexts. Although prior studies have compared LLM and human reasoning, the domain specificity of LLM reasoning remains underexplored. In this study, we introduce a new Wason Selection Task dataset that explicitly encodes deontic modality to systematically distinguish deontic from descriptive conditionals, and use it to examine LLMs' conditional reasoning under deontic rules. We further analyze whether observed error patterns are better explained by confirmation bias (a tendency to seek rule-supporting evidence) or by matching bias (a tendency to ignore negation and select items that lexically match elements of the rule). Results show that, like humans, LLMs reason better with deontic rules and display matching-bias-like errors. Together, these findings suggest that the performance of LLMs varies systematically across rule types and that their error patterns can parallel well-known human biases in this paradigm.

AI.tldr: They introduce a deontic Wason Selection Task dataset and show that LLMs, like humans, reason better under deontic (normative) rules than descriptive ones and exhibit matching-bias-like errors.
AI.motivation: Human reasoning is domain-sensitive and often improves in deontic contexts; prior LLM–human comparisons have not isolated domain specificity or tested whether LLM errors reflect human biases (confirmation vs matching).
AI.method: Construct a Wason Selection Task dataset that explicitly encodes deontic modality to separate deontic from descriptive conditionals. Evaluate LLMs’ conditional reasoning on these items and analyze error patterns to adjudicate between confirmation bias and matching bias explanations.
AI.result: LLMs perform better on deontic rules than on descriptive rules and their errors align more with matching bias than with confirmation bias, paralleling human performance patterns.
AI.conclusion: LLM reasoning is systematically affected by rule type; deontic framing boosts performance and LLMs display human-like matching-bias error patterns. The dataset offers a targeted probe for domain-specific reasoning in LLMs.

title: From Prompting to Preference Optimization: A Comparative Study of LLM-based Automated Essay Scoring
summary: Large language models (LLMs) have recently reshaped Automated Essay Scoring (AES), yet prior studies typically examine individual techniques in isolation, limiting understanding of their relative merits for English as a Second Language (L2) writing. To bridge this gap, we presents a comprehensive comparison of major LLM-based AES paradigms on IELTS Writing Task~2. On this unified benchmark, we evaluate four approaches: (i) encoder-based classification fine-tuning, (ii) zero- and few-shot prompting, (iii) instruction tuning and Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG), and (iv) Supervised Fine-Tuning combined with Direct Preference Optimization (DPO) and RAG. Our results reveal clear accuracy-cost-robustness trade-offs across methods, the best configuration, integrating k-SFT and RAG, achieves the strongest overall results with F1-Score 93%. This study offers the first unified empirical comparison of modern LLM-based AES strategies for English L2, promising potential in auto-grading writing tasks. Code is public at https://github.com/MinhNguyenDS/LLM_AES-EnL2

AI.tldr: Unified benchmark of LLM-based Automated Essay Scoring for IELTS Task 2 (L2 English) finds supervised fine-tuning with k-shot data plus RAG yields best overall performance (F1 93%) amid clear accuracy–cost–robustness trade-offs.
AI.motivation: Prior AES work with LLMs tested techniques in isolation, leaving unclear which approaches work best for English L2 writing and how they trade off accuracy, cost, and robustness.
AI.method: Construct a unified benchmark on IELTS Writing Task 2 and compare four paradigms: (i) encoder-based classification fine-tuning, (ii) zero-/few-shot prompting, (iii) instruction tuning with RAG, and (iv) supervised fine-tuning (SFT) combined with Direct Preference Optimization (DPO) and RAG. Evaluate accuracy, cost, and robustness.
AI.result: Across methods, there are pronounced trade-offs; the top configuration—k-shot SFT augmented with RAG—achieves the strongest overall results with an F1 score of 93%.
AI.conclusion: A unified empirical study shows that modern LLM-based AES can effectively grade L2 essays, with SFT+RAG being most effective under the studied conditions. The work provides a comparative foundation and open codebase for future AES research.

title: Unify the Views: View-Consistent Prototype Learning for Few-Shot Segmentation
summary: Few-shot segmentation (FSS) has gained significant attention for its ability to generalize to novel classes with limited supervision, yet remains challenged by structural misalignment and cross-view inconsistency under large appearance or viewpoint variations. This paper tackles these challenges by introducing VINE (View-Informed NEtwork), a unified framework that jointly models structural consistency and foreground discrimination to refine class-specific prototypes. Specifically, VINE introduces a spatial-view graph on backbone features, where the spatial graph captures local geometric topology and the view graph connects features from different perspectives to propagate view-invariant structural semantics. To further alleviate foreground ambiguity, we derive a discriminative prior from the support-query feature discrepancy to capture category-specific contrast, which reweights SAM features by emphasizing salient regions and recalibrates backbone activations for improved structural focus. The foreground-enhanced SAM features and structurally enriched ResNet features are progressively integrated through masked cross-attention, yielding class-consistent prototypes used as adaptive prompts for the SAM decoder to generate accurate masks. Extensive experiments on multiple FSS benchmarks validate the effectiveness and robustness of VINE, particularly under challenging scenarios with viewpoint shifts and complex structures. The code is available at https://github.com/HongliLiu1/VINE-main.

AI.tldr: VINE is a few-shot segmentation framework that refines class prototypes by enforcing view-invariant structural consistency and stronger foreground discrimination, fusing SAM and ResNet features via a spatial–view graph and masked cross‑attention to yield accurate masks under viewpoint shifts.
AI.motivation: Few-shot segmentation often fails under large appearance or viewpoint changes due to structural misalignment and cross-view inconsistency, and it struggles with foreground ambiguity when supervision is scarce. The paper seeks a unified way to make prototypes both structure-aware (view-invariant) and foreground-discriminative.
AI.method: 1) Build a spatial–view graph over backbone features: a spatial graph encodes local geometric topology; a view graph links features from different perspectives to propagate view-invariant structural semantics. 2) Compute a discriminative prior from support–query feature discrepancy to capture category-specific contrast; use it to reweight SAM features (emphasize salient regions) and recalibrate backbone activations for structural focus. 3) Progressively integrate the foreground-enhanced SAM features and structurally enriched ResNet features via masked cross-attention to form class-consistent prototypes, which serve as adaptive prompts to the SAM decoder to produce masks.
AI.result: Across multiple FSS benchmarks, VINE improves accuracy and robustness, especially with viewpoint shifts and complex structures, outperforming baselines (exact metrics not provided in the abstract).
AI.conclusion: Jointly modeling structural consistency and foreground discrimination, and using the resulting prototypes to prompt SAM, yields more reliable few-shot segmentation under challenging view and structure variations. Code is released, suggesting reproducibility and practical utility.

title: Abductive Reasoning with Syllogistic Forms in Large Language Models
comment: Published in Proceedings of the 3rd International Conference on Human and Artificial Rationalities (HAR 2024), LNCS 15504, pp. 3-17
summary: Research in AI using Large-Language Models (LLMs) is rapidly evolving, and the comparison of their performance with human reasoning has become a key concern. Prior studies have indicated that LLMs and humans share similar biases, such as dismissing logically valid inferences that contradict common beliefs. However, criticizing LLMs for these biases might be unfair, considering our reasoning not only involves formal deduction but also abduction, which draws tentative conclusions from limited information. Abduction can be regarded as the inverse form of syllogism in its basic structure, that is, a process of drawing a minor premise from a major premise and conclusion. This paper explores the accuracy of LLMs in abductive reasoning by converting a syllogistic dataset into one suitable for abduction. It aims to investigate whether the state-of-the-art LLMs exhibit biases in abduction and to identify potential areas for improvement, emphasizing the importance of contextualized reasoning beyond formal deduction. This investigation is vital for advancing the understanding and application of LLMs in complex reasoning tasks, offering insights into bridging the gap between machine and human cognition.

AI.tldr: The paper reframes syllogistic reasoning tasks into abductive ones to test whether state-of-the-art LLMs show human-like biases in abduction and to highlight the need for contextualized reasoning beyond strict deduction.
AI.motivation: Comparisons between LLMs and humans often emphasize similar biases in deductive tasks (e.g., rejecting valid but belief-incongruent inferences). Since human reasoning also relies on abduction, it may be unfair to judge LLMs solely on deductive benchmarks. The authors aim to assess LLMs on abductive reasoning to better align evaluation with human cognition.
AI.method: Treat abduction as the inverse of a syllogism (inferring a plausible minor premise from a major premise and a conclusion). Convert an existing syllogistic dataset into an abductive format and evaluate state-of-the-art LLMs for accuracy and bias patterns under this abductive setup.
AI.result: Specific empirical results are not provided in the abstract; the work sets up an evaluation to probe LLM abductive accuracy and bias, intending to identify where LLMs succeed or fail relative to human-like abductive reasoning.
AI.conclusion: Contextualized, non-deductive reasoning is crucial for fairly evaluating and improving LLMs. Abductive benchmarks can reveal biases and guide methods that narrow the gap between machine and human cognition in complex reasoning tasks.

title: OVGGT: O(1) Constant-Cost Streaming Visual Geometry Transformer
summary: Reconstructing 3D geometry from streaming video requires continuous inference under bounded resources. Recent geometric foundation models achieve impressive reconstruction quality through all-to-all attention, yet their quadratic cost confines them to short, offline sequences. Causal-attention variants such as StreamVGGT enable single-pass streaming but accumulate an ever-growing KV cache, exhausting GPU memory within hundreds of frames and precluding the long-horizon deployment that motivates streaming inference in the first place. We present OVGGT, a training-free framework that bounds both memory and compute to a fixed budget regardless of sequence length. Our approach combines Self-Selective Caching, which leverages FFN residual magnitudes to compress the KV cache while remaining fully compatible with FlashAttention, with Dynamic Anchor Protection, which shields coordinate-critical tokens from eviction to suppress geometric drift over extended trajectories. Extensive experiments on indoor, outdoor, and ultra-long sequence benchmarks demonstrate that OVGGT processes arbitrarily long videos within a constant VRAM envelope while achieving state-of-the-art 3D geometric accuracy.

AI.tldr: OVGGT is a training‑free, streaming 3D reconstruction framework that caps memory and compute to a fixed budget by selectively compressing the attention KV cache (via FFN‑residual magnitudes) and protecting geometry‑critical tokens, enabling arbitrarily long videos with state‑of‑the‑art accuracy under constant VRAM.
AI.motivation: Streaming 3D geometry from long videos must run under tight GPU memory/compute. All‑to‑all attention models are quadratic and offline; causal streaming avoids recomputation but grows an unbounded KV cache (e.g., StreamVGGT), exhausting memory and causing drift—undermining real long‑horizon deployment.
AI.method: Introduce a training‑free wrapper around causal‑attention VGGT: (1) Self‑Selective Caching compresses the KV cache by ranking tokens using FFN residual magnitudes, remaining compatible with FlashAttention; (2) Dynamic Anchor Protection identifies and preserves coordinate‑critical tokens (anchors) from eviction to limit geometric drift. Both together enforce a fixed memory/compute budget independent of sequence length.
AI.result: Across indoor, outdoor, and ultra‑long benchmarks, OVGGT processes arbitrarily long sequences within a constant VRAM envelope and achieves state‑of‑the‑art 3D geometric accuracy compared to prior streaming and foundation models.
AI.conclusion: OVGGT delivers practical, bounded‑resource streaming 3D reconstruction without retraining, maintaining accuracy while preventing cache bloat and long‑term drift—thereby enabling long‑horizon deployment.

title: PONTE: Personalized Orchestration for Natural Language Trustworthy Explanations
summary: Explainable Artificial Intelligence (XAI) seeks to enhance the transparency and accountability of machine learning systems, yet most methods follow a one-size-fits-all paradigm that neglects user differences in expertise, goals, and cognitive needs. Although Large Language Models can translate technical explanations into natural language, they introduce challenges related to faithfulness and hallucinations. To address these challenges, we present PONTE (Personalized Orchestration for Natural language Trustworthy Explanations), a human-in-the-loop framework for adaptive and reliable XAI narratives. PONTE models personalization as a closed-loop validation and adaptation process rather than prompt engineering. It combines: (i) a low-dimensional preference model capturing stylistic requirements; (ii) a preference-conditioned generator grounded in structured XAI artifacts; and (iii) verification modules enforcing numerical faithfulness, informational completeness, and stylistic alignment, optionally supported by retrieval-grounded argumentation. User feedback iteratively updates the preference state, enabling quick personalization. Automatic and human evaluations across healthcare and finance domains show that the verification-refinement loop substantially improves completeness and stylistic alignment over validation-free generation. Human studies further confirm strong agreement between intended preference vectors and perceived style, robustness to generation stochasticity, and consistently positive quality assessments.

AI.tldr: PONTE is a human‑in‑the‑loop framework that personalizes XAI narratives via a preference model, a grounded generator, and verification modules to ensure faithfulness, completeness, and stylistic fit; across healthcare and finance, it outperforms validation‑free generation and remains robust and well‑perceived by users.
AI.motivation: Current XAI methods often ignore user diversity (expertise, goals, cognitive needs), while LLM-based explanations risk unfaithfulness and hallucinations. There is a need for trustworthy, user-tailored explanations that remain faithful to underlying model evidence.
AI.method: Personalization is framed as a closed-loop validation and adaptation process. PONTE includes: (i) a low-dimensional preference model encoding stylistic requirements; (ii) a preference-conditioned natural-language generator grounded in structured XAI artifacts; and (iii) verification modules that enforce numerical faithfulness, informational completeness, and stylistic alignment, optionally augmented with retrieval-grounded argumentation. User feedback iteratively updates the preference state to enable rapid personalization.
AI.result: Automatic and human evaluations in healthcare and finance show the verification–refinement loop substantially improves completeness and stylistic alignment versus generation without validation. Human studies indicate strong agreement between intended preference vectors and perceived style, robustness to generation stochasticity, and consistently positive quality ratings.
AI.conclusion: Closed-loop, verification-backed personalization can deliver adaptive, reliable XAI narratives that better match user needs without sacrificing faithfulness, with evidence of cross-domain applicability and efficient personalization through feedback.

title: Place-it-R1: Unlocking Environment-aware Reasoning Potential of MLLM for Video Object Insertion
summary: Modern video editing techniques have achieved high visual fidelity when inserting video objects. However, they focus on optimizing visual fidelity rather than physical causality, leading to edits that are physically inconsistent with their environment. In this work, we present Place-it-R$1$, an end-to-end framework for video object insertion that unlocks the environment-aware reasoning potential of Multimodal Large Language Models (MLLMs). Our framework leverages the Chain-of-Thought (CoT) reasoning of MLLMs to orchestrate video diffusion, following a Think-then-Place paradigm. To bridge cognitive reasoning and generative execution, we introduce three key innovations: First, MLLM performs physical scene understanding and interaction reasoning, generating environment-aware chain-of-thought tokens and inferring valid insertion regions to explicitly guide the diffusion toward physically plausible insertion. Then, we introduce MLLM-guided Spatial Direct Preference Optimization (DPO), where diffusion outputs are fed back to the MLLM for scoring, enabling visual naturalness. During inference, the MLLM iteratively triggers refinement cycles and elicits adaptive adjustments from the diffusion model, forming a closed-loop that progressively enhances editing quality. Furthermore, we provide two user-selectable modes: a plausibility-oriented flexible mode that permits environment modifications (\eg, generating support structures) to enhance physical plausibility, and a fidelity-oriented standard mode that preserves scene integrity for maximum fidelity, offering users explicit control over the plausibility-fidelity trade-off. Extensive experiments demonstrate Place-it-R1 achieves physically-coherent video object insertion compared with state-of-the-art solutions and commercial models.

AI.tldr: Place-it-R1 couples an MLLM’s chain-of-thought with a video diffusion editor to insert objects into videos in a way that respects physical context, using iterative, closed-loop guidance and preference optimization; it outperforms prior methods on physical coherence while letting users trade off plausibility vs fidelity.
AI.motivation: Current video object insertion focuses on visual realism but often violates physical causality (e.g., unsupported objects, wrong interactions). The authors aim to bring environment-aware, physics-consistent reasoning into generative editing to avoid such inconsistencies.
AI.method: An end-to-end “Think-then-Place” pipeline: (1) an MLLM performs physical scene understanding and interaction reasoning, producing environment-aware chain-of-thought tokens and valid insertion regions to guide video diffusion; (2) MLLM-guided Spatial DPO feeds diffusion outputs back to the MLLM for spatially-aware scoring, aligning outputs with visual naturalness; (3) a closed-loop inference where the MLLM iteratively triggers refinement and adaptive adjustments to the diffusion model. Two user modes: (a) plausibility-oriented flexible mode that can alter the environment (e.g., add supports) and (b) fidelity-oriented standard mode that preserves scene integrity.
AI.result: Across extensive experiments, Place-it-R1 achieves more physically coherent video object insertions than state-of-the-art academic and commercial baselines, per the authors’ evaluations.
AI.conclusion: Integrating MLLM chain-of-thought reasoning with diffusion editing and spatial preference optimization yields physically plausible object insertions and offers controllable plausibility–fidelity trade-offs. The closed-loop MLLM feedback is central to progressively improving edit quality.

title: Exploring Open-Vocabulary Object Recognition in Images using CLIP
summary: To address the limitations of existing open-vocabulary object recognition methods, specifically high system complexity, substantial training costs, and limited generalization, this paper proposes a novel Open-Vocabulary Object Recognition (OVOR) framework based on a streamlined two-stage strategy: object segmentation followed by recognition. The framework eliminates the need for complex retraining and labor-intensive annotation. After cropping object regions, we generate object-level image embeddings alongside category-level text embeddings using CLIP, which facilitates arbitrary vocabularies. To reduce reliance on CLIP and enhance encoding flexibility, we further introduce a CNN/MLP-based method that extracts convolutional neural network (CNN) feature maps and utilizes a multilayer perceptron (MLP) to align visual features with text embeddings. These embeddings are concatenated and processed via Singular Value Decomposition (SVD) to construct a shared representation space. Finally, recognition is performed through embedding similarity matching. Experiments on COCO, Pascal VOC, and ADE20K demonstrate that training-free, CLIP-based encoding without SVD achieves the highest average AP, outperforming current state-of-the-art methods. Simultaneously, the results highlight the potential of CNN/MLP-based image encoding for OVOR.

AI.tldr: A simple two-stage OV open-vocabulary object recognition pipeline—segment first, then recognize via CLIP image/text similarity—works training-free and beats SOTA AP; an alternative CNN/MLP+SVD alignment is explored but underperforms CLIP-only.
AI.motivation: Existing OVOR methods are complex, require costly retraining and annotations, and generalize poorly. The goal is a flexible, training-free approach that supports arbitrary vocabularies with lower system overhead.
AI.method: Two-stage framework: (1) segment objects and crop regions; (2) recognize by matching embeddings. Use CLIP to create object-level image embeddings and category-level text embeddings for arbitrary label sets. To reduce dependence on CLIP, propose a CNN/MLP encoder that extracts CNN features and aligns them to text embeddings via an MLP; concatenate embeddings and apply SVD to form a shared representation; perform recognition via similarity matching.
AI.result: On COCO, Pascal VOC, and ADE20K, a training-free CLIP-based encoding without SVD attains the highest average AP and outperforms state-of-the-art methods. The CNN/MLP-based encoder shows potential but does not surpass CLIP-only in current experiments.
AI.conclusion: A lightweight segmentation-then-recognition design, leveraging CLIP without retraining, can outperform more complex OVOR systems. Additional SVD-based fusion and CNN/MLP alignment are optional and currently less effective, though promising for future flexibility.

title: Beyond Rows to Reasoning: Agentic Retrieval for Multimodal Spreadsheet Understanding and Editing
summary: Recent advances in multimodal Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) enable Large Language Models (LLMs) to analyze enterprise spreadsheet workbooks containing millions of cells, cross-sheet dependencies, and embedded visual artifacts. However, state-of-the-art approaches exclude critical context through single-pass retrieval, lose data resolution through compression, and exceed LLM context windows through naive full-context injection, preventing reliable multi-step reasoning over complex enterprise workbooks. We introduce Beyond Rows to Reasoning (BRTR), a multimodal agentic framework for spreadsheet understanding that replaces single-pass retrieval with an iterative tool-calling loop, supporting end-to-end Excel workflows from complex analysis to structured editing. Supported by over 200 hours of expert human evaluation, BRTR achieves state-of-the-art performance across three frontier spreadsheet understanding benchmarks, surpassing prior methods by 25 percentage points on FRTR-Bench, 7 points on SpreadsheetLLM, and 32 points on FINCH. We evaluate five multimodal embedding models, identifying NVIDIA NeMo Retriever 1B as the top performer for mixed tabular and visual data, and vary nine LLMs. Ablation experiments confirm that the planner, retrieval, and iterative reasoning each contribute substantially, and cost analysis shows GPT-5.2 achieves the best efficiency-accuracy trade-off. Throughout all evaluations, BRTR maintains full auditability through explicit tool-call traces.

AI.tldr: BRTR is a multimodal, agentic RAG framework that iteratively retrieves and reasons over large, complex spreadsheets—preserving fidelity and auditability—and achieves state-of-the-art results across leading benchmarks.
AI.motivation: Enterprise spreadsheets contain millions of cells, cross-sheet formulas, and embedded visuals. Existing multimodal RAG methods either miss key context via single-pass retrieval, lose detail via compression, or overflow LLM context windows with naive full-context injection—hindering reliable multi-step reasoning and trustworthy editing.
AI.method: Introduce Beyond Rows to Reasoning (BRTR), a multimodal agent with an iterative tool-calling loop that plans, retrieves, and reasons over spreadsheet content (tabular + visual) across steps. It supports end-to-end Excel workflows, from complex analysis to structured editing, maintains explicit tool-call traces for auditability, and systematically evaluates multiple multimodal embedding models and nine LLMs. Ablations isolate contributions of the planner, retrieval strategy, and iterative reasoning.
AI.result: BRTR sets new SOTA: +25 percentage points on FRTR-Bench, +7 on SpreadsheetLLM, and +32 on FINCH, supported by 200+ hours of expert human evaluation. Among five multimodal embedding models, NVIDIA NeMo Retriever 1B performs best for mixed tabular-visual data. Cost analysis shows GPT-5.2 gives the best efficiency-accuracy trade-off; ablations confirm each component (planner, retrieval, iterative loop) substantially contributes.
AI.conclusion: Iterative, tool-driven multimodal RAG over spreadsheets overcomes context loss and window limits, enabling accurate, auditable multi-step reasoning and editing. BRTR reliably advances spreadsheet understanding and offers a practical, cost-efficient path for enterprise-scale workflows.

title: VLM-RobustBench: A Comprehensive Benchmark for Robustness of Vision-Language Models
summary: Vision-language models (VLMs) achieve strong performance on standard, high-quality datasets, but we still do not fully understand how they perform under real-world image distortions. We present VLM-RobustBench, a benchmark spanning 49 augmentation types across noise, blur, weather, digital, and geometric perturbations, evaluated under graded severities (low/mid/high) and binary transforms, yielding 133 corrupted settings. We evaluate VLMs from four families (Qwen, InternVL, Molmo, Gemma) on two complementary benchmarks: MMBench (visually grounded) and MMMU-Pro (reasoning-oriented). Our results reveal that visual severity is a weak predictor of difficulty: low-severity spatial perturbations often degrade performance more than visually severe photometric corruptions. In particular, low-severity glass_blur reduces MMBench accuracy by about 8 pp on average across models, while the largest drops arise from resampling and geometric distortions (e.g., upsample, elastic_transform), reaching up to 34 pp. Overall, our findings suggest current VLMs are semantically strong but spatially fragile, motivating the definition of novel robustness evaluation protocols and training regimes that emphasize resampling and geometric invariances.

AI.tldr: They introduce VLM-RobustBench, a large corruption benchmark (49 augmentation types, 133 settings) and show that current vision–language models are far more vulnerable to mild spatial/resampling/geometric perturbations than to visually severe photometric noise, implying a gap in spatial robustness.
AI.motivation: VLMs excel on clean, curated data, but real-world inputs contain distortions. There is no comprehensive, standardized way to quantify how VLMs degrade under such corruptions, hampering understanding and progress on robustness.
AI.method: Construct a benchmark covering noise, blur, weather, digital, and geometric corruptions at multiple severities plus binary transforms; test four VLM families (Qwen, InternVL, Molmo, Gemma) on two evaluation suites (MMBench for visual grounding, MMMU-Pro for reasoning) and analyze accuracy changes versus corruption type/severity.
AI.result: Perceptual/visual severity correlates weakly with difficulty: small spatial changes can degrade performance more than dramatic photometric ones. Low-severity glass blur reduces MMBench by ~8 percentage points on average; resampling/geometric distortions (e.g., upsample, elastic transform) cause the biggest drops, up to 34 points. Overall, models appear semantically competent but spatially fragile.
AI.conclusion: Robustness work should prioritize spatial and resampling invariances. The community needs new evaluation protocols and training regimes that explicitly target geometric robustness rather than focusing mainly on photometric noise.

title: Speak in Context: Multilingual ASR with Speech Context Alignment via Contrastive Learning
summary: Automatic speech recognition (ASR) has benefited from advances in pretrained speech and language models, yet most systems remain constrained to monolingual settings and short, isolated utterances. While recent efforts in context-aware ASR show promise, two key challenges persist: limited multilingual support and the absence of principled alignment between speech and contextual representations. In this paper, we introduce a context-aware multilingual ASR framework that supports diverse languages and accents while preserving the modularity of pretrained models. Our approach combines a frozen speech encoder and a decoder-only language model via a lightweight projection module, allowing structured context prompts, including dialogue history and biasing words, to guide transcription. To improve interaction between speech and context, we employ a contrastive learning objective that aligns their representations in a shared embedding space. Evaluations on over 1,500 hours of real-world conversational speech across 11 languages and 5 English dialects show that contextual input consistently improves recognition quality. Contrastive alignment provides additional gains when applied to different context types, with an overall performance gain of over 5%. These results highlight the importance of both contextual modeling and cross-modal alignment in multilingual ASR.

AI.tldr: Proposes a modular, context-aware multilingual ASR that fuses a frozen speech encoder with a decoder-only LM via a lightweight projection, uses structured context prompts (dialogue history, biasing words), and aligns speech/context with contrastive learning; evaluated on 1.5k+ hours across 11 languages and 5 English dialects, yielding consistent gains and >5% overall improvement.
AI.motivation: Despite strong pretrained speech and language models, most ASR remains monolingual and optimized for short, isolated utterances. Existing context-aware ASR has limited multilingual coverage and lacks a principled alignment between speech and contextual inputs, constraining effectiveness in real conversational settings.
AI.method: Keep a pretrained speech encoder frozen and connect it to a decoder-only language model through a small projection layer. Feed structured contextual prompts (e.g., dialogue history, biasing terms) to guide decoding. Train with a contrastive learning objective to align speech and context representations in a shared embedding space, improving their interaction without heavy finetuning.
AI.result: On >1,500 hours of real conversational speech spanning 11 languages and 5 English dialects, adding contextual input consistently improves recognition. Applying contrastive alignment across different context types brings additional gains, with an overall improvement exceeding 5% relative performance.
AI.conclusion: Contextual modeling and cross-modal representation alignment are both crucial for multilingual ASR. The proposed modular framework leverages pretrained components effectively and scales across languages and accents, delivering measurable accuracy improvements.

title: Reflective Flow Sampling Enhancement
summary: The growing demand for text-to-image generation has led to rapid advances in generative modeling. Recently, text-to-image diffusion models trained with flow matching algorithms, such as FLUX, have achieved remarkable progress and emerged as strong alternatives to conventional diffusion models. At the same time, inference-time enhancement strategies have been shown to improve the generation quality and text-prompt alignment of text-to-image diffusion models. However, these techniques are mainly applicable to conventional diffusion models and usually fail to perform well on flow models. To bridge this gap, we propose Reflective Flow Sampling (RF-Sampling), a theoretically-grounded and training-free inference enhancement framework explicitly designed for flow models, especially for the CFG-distilled variants (i.e., models distilled from CFG guidance techniques), like FLUX. Departing from heuristic interpretations, we provide a formal derivation proving that RF-Sampling implicitly performs gradient ascent on the text-image alignment score. By leveraging a linear combination of textual representations and integrating them with flow inversion, RF-Sampling allows the model to explore noise spaces that are more consistent with the input prompt. Extensive experiments across multiple benchmarks demonstrate that RF-Sampling consistently improves both generation quality and prompt alignment. Moreover, RF-Sampling is also the first inference enhancement method that can exhibit test-time scaling ability to some extent on FLUX.

AI.tldr: RF-Sampling is a training-free, theoretically grounded inference procedure for flow-matched text-to-image models (e.g., FLUX/CFG-distilled) that implicitly performs gradient ascent on text–image alignment by combining textual representations with flow inversion, yielding better image quality, stronger prompt adherence, and test-time scaling on FLUX.
AI.motivation: Inference-time enhancement techniques that boost quality and prompt alignment in conventional diffusion models often fail on newer flow-matched text-to-image models (especially CFG-distilled variants). There is a need for a principled, training-free method tailored to flow models to close this performance gap.
AI.method: Introduce Reflective Flow Sampling (RF-Sampling), derived to implicitly optimize a text–image alignment score via gradient-ascent-like updates during sampling.
