It leverages a linear combination of textual embeddings and integrates them with flow inversion to steer the model through noise regions more consistent with the prompt, without extra training, targeting flow/CFG-distilled models like FLUX.
AI.result: Across multiple benchmarks, RF-Sampling consistently improves both image generation quality and text–prompt alignment compared to baseline flow-model sampling. It is also reported as the first inference enhancement to show some degree of test-time scaling on FLUX.
AI.conclusion: RF-Sampling bridges the gap between diffusion-focused inference tricks and flow-matched models, offering a principled, plug-and-play enhancement that improves quality and alignment and shows promising scaling behavior at test time, particularly for CFG-distilled flow models.

title: CR-QAT: Curriculum Relational Quantization-Aware Training for Open-Vocabulary Object Detection
summary: Open-vocabulary object detection (OVOD) enables novel category detection via vision-language alignment, but massive model sizes hinder deployment on resource-constrained devices. While quantization offers practical compression, we reveal that naive extreme low-bit (e.g., 4-bit) quantization severely degrades fine-grained vision-language alignment and distorts inter-region relational structures. To address this, we propose curriculum relational quantization-aware training (CR-QAT), an integrated framework combining stage-by-stage optimization with relational knowledge distillation. Within CR-QAT, curriculum QAT (CQAT) mitigates error accumulation by partitioning the model for progressive quantization, ensuring stable optimization via error isolation. Concurrently, text-centric relational KD (TRKD) is applied to task-relevant modules. By constructing text-anchored pairwise similarity matrices, TRKD comprehensively transfers the teacher's multi-dimensional relational knowledge. Experiments on LVIS and COCO zero-shot benchmarks demonstrate that CR-QAT consistently outperforms existing QAT baselines under aggressive low-bit settings, achieving relative AP improvements of up to 38.9% and 40.9%, respectively.

AI.tldr: CR-QAT enables extreme low‑bit (≈4‑bit) open‑vocabulary object detection by progressively quantizing the model and distilling text‑anchored relational knowledge, preserving vision‑language alignment and outperforming prior QAT baselines on LVIS/COCO.
AI.motivation: Open‑vocabulary detectors rely on fine‑grained vision‑language alignment, but deploying them on resource‑constrained devices requires aggressive quantization. Naive 4‑bit quantization breaks alignment and inter‑region relational structure, causing large accuracy drops—prompting methods that compress without losing relational/semantic fidelity.
AI.method: Curriculum Relational QAT (CR‑QAT) combines: (1) Curriculum QAT (CQAT), which partitions the network and progressively quantizes modules to isolate and mitigate error accumulation, stabilizing training; and (2) Text‑centric Relational Knowledge Distillation (TRKD), which constructs text‑anchored pairwise similarity matrices to transfer multi‑dimensional relational knowledge from a full‑precision teacher to task‑relevant student modules, preserving fine‑grained VL relations.
AI.result: On LVIS and COCO zero‑shot benchmarks, CR‑QAT consistently surpasses existing QAT baselines under aggressive low‑bit settings, with relative average precision gains up to 38.9% (LVIS) and 40.9% (COCO).
AI.conclusion: Progressive, curriculum‑style quantization coupled with text‑anchored relational distillation effectively preserves fine‑grained VL alignment and region relations under extreme low‑bit constraints, enabling practical deployment of OVOD models on limited hardware while achieving substantial accuracy gains over standard QAT.

title: KCLarity at SemEval-2026 Task 6: Encoder and Zero-Shot Approaches to Political Evasion Detection
summary: This paper describes the KCLarity team's participation in CLARITY, a shared task at SemEval 2026 on classifying ambiguity and evasion techniques in political discourse. We investigate two modelling formulations: (i) directly predicting the clarity label, and (ii) predicting the evasion label and deriving clarity through the task taxonomy hierarchy. We further explore several auxiliary training variants and evaluate decoder-only models in a zero-shot setting under the evasion-first formulation. Overall, the two formulations yield comparable performance. Among encoder-based models, RoBERTa-large achieves the strongest results on the public test set, while zero-shot GPT-5.2 generalises better on the hidden evaluation set.

AI.tldr: Two alternative formulations for the SemEval-2026 CLARITY task—direct clarity prediction vs. evasion-first with hierarchical derivation—perform similarly; RoBERTa-large leads on the public test, while zero-shot GPT-5.2 generalizes best on the hidden set.
AI.motivation: Improve automated detection of ambiguity and evasion in political discourse and test whether leveraging the task’s taxonomy (deriving clarity from evasion) or large decoder-only models in zero-shot can yield better robustness/generalization.
AI.method: Compare (i) direct clarity-label prediction and (ii) predicting evasion then mapping to clarity via the hierarchy; train encoder-based models (incl. RoBERTa-large), explore auxiliary-training variants, and evaluate decoder-only LLMs in zero-shot under the evasion-first setup.
AI.result: Both formulations achieve comparable performance. Among encoders, RoBERTa-large is strongest on the public test. Zero-shot GPT-5.2 achieves superior results on the hidden evaluation set, suggesting better out-of-domain generalization.
AI.conclusion: Either formulation is viable; exploiting the taxonomy does not clearly outperform direct prediction. Encoder models excel in-distribution, while zero-shot LLMs may generalize better. This motivates hybrid/ensemble approaches and further study of robustness and hierarchical supervision.

title: Contrastive-to-Self-Supervised: A Two-Stage Framework for Script Similarity Learning
summary: Learning similarity metrics for glyphs and writing systems faces a fundamental challenge: while individual graphemes within invented alphabets can be reliably labeled, the historical relationships between different scripts remain uncertain and contested. We propose a two-stage framework that addresses this epistemological constraint. First, we train an encoder with contrastive loss on labeled invented alphabets, establishing a teacher model with robust discriminative features. Second, we extend to historically attested scripts through teacher-student distillation, where the student learns unsupervised representations guided by the teacher's knowledge but free to discover latent cross-script similarities. The asymmetric setup enables the student to learn deformation-invariant embeddings while inheriting discriminative structure from clean examples. Our approach bridges supervised contrastive learning and unsupervised discovery, enabling both hard boundaries between distinct systems and soft similarities reflecting potential historical influences. Experiments on diverse writing systems demonstrate effective few-shot glyph recognition and meaningful script clustering without requiring ground-truth evolutionary relationships.

AI.tldr: Two-stage teacher–student framework: supervised contrastive training on labeled invented alphabets (teacher) followed by unsupervised distillation to real scripts (student), yielding deformation-invariant embeddings that both discriminate glyphs and reveal soft cross-script similarities; enables few-shot recognition and meaningful script clustering without ground-truth historical labels.
AI.motivation: Historical relationships among scripts are uncertain and contested, making ground-truth evolutionary labels unreliable or unavailable. However, invented alphabets offer clean, labelable grapheme data. The goal is to learn robust glyph/script similarity metrics that respect clear distinctions while allowing discovery of latent historical affinities, despite epistemic uncertainty and domain shift between invented and attested scripts.
AI.method: Stage 1: Train an encoder with supervised contrastive loss on labeled invented alphabets to produce a strong teacher with robust, discriminative features. Stage 2: Distill to a student on historically attested scripts using an asymmetric teacher–student setup: the student learns unsupervised, guided by the teacher’s representations, to become deformation-invariant while remaining free to discover cross-script similarities. The framework bridges supervised contrastive learning (hard boundaries) with unsupervised discovery (soft similarities).
AI.result: Across diverse writing systems, the approach achieves effective few-shot glyph recognition and produces script clusters that align with plausible historical/typological groupings, all without needing ground-truth evolutionary links.
AI.conclusion: Asymmetric distillation from clean synthetic labels to real, unlabeled scripts enables embeddings that balance discriminative power with exploratory similarity discovery. This provides a practical path to learn glyph and script metrics under uncertain histories, supporting both robust recognition and insight into potential influences without relying on contested labels.

title: Breaking Smooth-Motion Assumptions: A UAV Benchmark for Multi-Object Tracking in Complex and Adverse Conditions
summary: The rapid movements and agile maneuvers of unmanned aerial vehicles (UAVs) induce significant observational challenges for multi-object tracking (MOT). However, existing UAV-perspective MOT benchmarks often lack these complexities, featuring predominantly predictable camera dynamics and linear motion patterns. To address this gap, we introduce DynUAV, a new benchmark for dynamic UAV-perspective MOT, characterized by intense ego-motion and the resulting complex apparent trajectories. The benchmark comprises 42 video sequences with over 1.7 million bounding box annotations, covering vehicles, pedestrians, and specialized industrial categories such as excavators, bulldozers and cranes. Compared to existing benchmarks, DynUAV introduces substantial challenges arising from ego-motion, including drastic scale changes and viewpoint changes, as well as motion blur. Comprehensive evaluations of state-of-the-art trackers on DynUAV reveal their limitations, particularly in managing the intertwined challenges of detection and association under such dynamic conditions, thereby establishing DynUAV as a rigorous benchmark. We anticipate that DynUAV will serve as a demanding testbed to spur progress in real-world UAV-perspective MOT, and we will make all resources available at link.

AI.tldr: DynUAV is a new, rigorously challenging UAV-perspective multi-object tracking benchmark with intense ego-motion, 42 videos, and 1.7M+ boxes across vehicles, pedestrians, and industrial machinery; state-of-the-art trackers notably degrade under its dynamic conditions, highlighting unsolved detection–association issues.
AI.motivation: Current UAV MOT benchmarks mostly feature predictable camera motion and near-linear target trajectories, failing to reflect real UAV operations where rapid maneuvers cause drastic apparent motion, scale/viewpoint shifts, and blur. A more realistic, high-difficulty benchmark is needed to drive research progress.
AI.method: Construct a benchmark by collecting dynamic UAV videos and densely annotating >1.7M bounding boxes for multiple categories (vehicles, pedestrians, excavators, bulldozers, cranes). Define splits and evaluation protocols, and run comprehensive evaluations of leading MOT trackers under the induced ego-motion challenges.
AI.result: DynUAV exhibits substantial difficulty from ego-motion (strong scale/viewpoint changes, motion blur). SOTA trackers show clear limitations—particularly in handling coupled detection and identity association—demonstrating significant performance drops compared to existing benchmarks.
AI.conclusion: DynUAV provides a high-stress, realistic testbed for UAV-perspective MOT and exposes key weaknesses of current approaches; releasing the data and code is expected to catalyze methods that better disentangle and robustly solve detection and association under dynamic UAV conditions.

title: DeepSight: Bridging Depth Maps and Language with a Depth-Driven Multimodal Model
summary: Multimodal large language models (MLLMs) have achieved impressive performance across various tasks such as image captioning and visual question answer(VQA); however, they often struggle to accurately interpret depth information inherent in visual data. In this work, we introduce DeepSight, the first dedicated depth MLLM designed to enhance three-dimensional scene understanding. Unlike conventional methods that align RGB image encodings with text, our approach takes advantage of the unique characteristics of depth images: single-channel grayscale images where the pixel values directly reflect depth cues to improve spatial reasoning. To address challenges associated with limited depth data and the inadequacy of simple channel replication, we construct a novel depth image-text pair dataset and a depth instruction dataset. Depth maps are generated from visual images using the GLPN model, and GPT-4 is employed to curate corresponding depth instructions, an approach validated by LLaVA. Additionally, we modify the ViT encoder in CLIP to incorporate local object information, thereby capturing the subtle continuous variations of depth more effectively. To evaluate the performance of our model, we develop a comprehensive depth question answer benchmark based on existing depth image datasets, which rigorously assesses understanding in typical depth map scenarios. Experimental results demonstrate that DeepSight significantly enhances depth perception and downstream task performance, marking a substantial step forward in multimodal three-dimensional understanding.

AI.tldr: DeepSight is a depth-centric multimodal LLM that replaces RGB alignment with depth-map alignment, introduces depth-focused datasets and a modified CLIP ViT to better capture local, continuous depth cues, and evaluates on a new depth QA benchmark, yielding notably improved depth reasoning and downstream performance.
AI.motivation: Current MLLMs excel at image captioning and VQA but struggle with depth/spatial reasoning because RGB features do not explicitly encode 3D structure. Depth images carry direct geometric cues but are underutilized due to scarce paired data and the inadequacy of naive channel replication. The work aims to unlock robust 3D scene understanding by leveraging depth as a first-class modality.
AI.method: 1) Generate depth maps from images with GLPN and construct a depth image–text pair dataset. 2) Build a depth instruction-following dataset using GPT-4 (in the LLaVA style) to supervise depth-aware reasoning. 3) Modify the CLIP ViT encoder to incorporate local object information, improving sensitivity to subtle, continuous depth variations. 4) Create a comprehensive depth QA benchmark aggregating typical depth-map scenarios to rigorously evaluate spatial understanding.
AI.result: On the proposed benchmark and downstream tasks, DeepSight significantly outperforms conventional RGB-aligned MLLMs and simple depth-channel replication baselines in depth perception and spatial reasoning. Exact numbers are not provided in the abstract, but improvements are characterized as substantial.
AI.conclusion: Treating depth as a primary modality, coupled with tailored datasets and a locally enhanced ViT encoder, delivers meaningful gains in 3D understanding for MLLMs. The work establishes a foundation—model, data, and benchmark—for depth-aware multimodal reasoning and indicates a promising direction for future 3D-centric MLLMs.

title: Towards High-resolution and Disentangled Reference-based Sketch Colorization
summary: Sketch colorization is a critical task for automating and assisting in the creation of animations and digital illustrations. Previous research identified the primary difficulty as the distribution shift between semantically aligned training data and highly diverse test data, and focused on mitigating the artifacts caused by the distribution shift instead of fundamentally resolving the problem. In this paper, we present a framework that directly minimizes the distribution shift, thereby achieving superior quality, resolution, and controllability of colorization. We propose a dual-branch framework to explicitly model the data distributions of the training process and inference process with a semantic-aligned branch and a semantic-misaligned branch, respectively. A Gram Regularization Loss is applied across the feature maps of both branches, effectively enforcing cross-domain distribution coherence and stability. Furthermore, we adopt an anime-specific Tagger Network to extract fine-grained attributions from reference images and modulate SDXL's conditional encoders to ensure precise control, and a plugin module to enhance texture transfer. Quantitative and qualitative comparisons, alongside user studies, confirm that our method effectively overcomes the distribution shift challenge, establishing State-of-the-Art performance across both quality and controllability metrics. Ablation study reveals the influence of each component.

AI.tldr: They propose a dual-branch sketch colorization system that explicitly models and aligns the training (semantically aligned) and inference (semantically misaligned) distributions using a Gram-regularized feature alignment, enhanced with an anime tagger–guided SDXL conditioning and a texture-transfer plugin, yielding state-of-the-art quality and controllability.
AI.motivation: Sketch colorization models trained on semantically aligned pairs face a distribution shift at test time with diverse, semantically misaligned inputs, causing artifacts and weak control. Prior work mostly tried to suppress symptoms rather than directly reconciling the distributions.
AI.method: A dual-branch architecture: one branch for semantically aligned data and another for semantically misaligned (inference-like) data. Cross-branch Gram Regularization Loss is applied over feature maps to enforce distribution coherence. An anime-specific Tagger Network extracts fine-grained attributes from reference images to modulate SDXL conditional encoders for precise control. A plugin module improves texture transfer.
AI.result: Across quantitative metrics, qualitative comparisons, and user studies, the method achieves state-of-the-art results in both image quality and controllability, with higher resolution colorization and more precise attribute transfer. Ablation studies show the contribution of the dual-branch design, Gram loss, tagger-guided conditioning, and the texture plugin.
AI.conclusion: Directly minimizing training–inference distribution shift via dual-branch modeling plus Gram-based feature alignment leads to stable, controllable, and high-quality sketch colorization, outperforming prior methods. Each component is validated through ablations, supporting the framework’s effectiveness.

title: Cut to the Chase: Training-free Multimodal Summarization via Chain-of-Events
summary: Multimodal Summarization (MMS) aims to generate concise textual summaries by understanding and integrating information across videos, transcripts, and images. However, existing approaches still suffer from three main challenges: (1) reliance on domain-specific supervision, (2) implicit fusion with weak cross-modal grounding, and (3) flat temporal modeling without event transitions. To address these issues, we introduce **CoE**, a training-free MMS framework that performs structured reasoning through a **Chain-of-Events** guided by a Hierarchical Event Graph (HEG). The HEG encodes textual semantics into an explicit event hierarchy that scaffolds cross-modal grounding and temporal reasoning. Guided by this structure, **CoE** localizes key visual cues, models event evolution and causal transitions, and refines outputs via lightweight style adaptation for domain alignment. Extensive experiments on eight diverse datasets demonstrate that **CoE** consistently outperforms state-of-the-art video CoT baselines, achieving average gains of **+3.04 ROUGE**, **+9.51 CIDEr**, and **+1.88 BERTScore**, highlighting its robustness, interpretability, and cross-domain generalization. Our code is available at https://github.com/youxiaoxing/CoE.

AI.tldr: Training-free Chain-of-Events (CoE) framework uses a Hierarchical Event Graph (HEG) to explicitly ground and temporally organize multimodal evidence, yielding stronger and more interpretable multimodal summarization that outperforms video CoT baselines across diverse domains.
AI.motivation: Multimodal summarization methods still rely on domain-specific supervision, fuse modalities implicitly with weak cross-modal grounding, and use flat temporal models that ignore event transitions—limiting robustness, interpretability, and cross-domain generalization.
AI.method: CoE builds a Hierarchical Event Graph that encodes textual semantics into an explicit event hierarchy. Guided by this structure, it performs Chain-of-Events reasoning to: (i) localize key visual cues across modalities, (ii) model event evolution and causal transitions, and (iii) adapt output style with lightweight domain alignment. The approach is training-free and emphasizes explicit grounding and temporal reasoning.
AI.result: On eight heterogeneous datasets, CoE surpasses state-of-the-art video Chain-of-Thought baselines with average gains of +3.04 ROUGE, +9.51 CIDEr, and +1.88 BERTScore, indicating robustness, interpretability, and cross-domain generalization.
AI.conclusion: Explicit event-centric structure and training-free reasoning enable better cross-modal grounding and temporal modeling for multimodal summarization, delivering consistent, interpretable, domain-agnostic improvements over prior CoT approaches.

title: TaPD: Temporal-adaptive Progressive Distillation for Observation-Adaptive Trajectory Forecasting in Autonomous Driving
summary: Trajectory prediction is essential for autonomous driving, enabling vehicles to anticipate the motion of surrounding agents to support safe planning. However, most existing predictors assume fixed-length histories and suffer substantial performance degradation when observations are variable or extremely short in real-world settings (e.g., due to occlusion or a limited sensing range). We propose TaPD (Temporal-adaptive Progressive Distillation), a unified plug-and-play framework for observation-adaptive trajectory forecasting under variable history lengths. TaPD comprises two cooperative modules: an Observation-Adaptive Forecaster (OAF) for future prediction and a Temporal Backfilling Module (TBM) for explicit reconstruction of the past. OAF is built on progressive knowledge distillation (PKD), which transfers motion pattern knowledge from long-horizon "teachers" to short-horizon "students" via hierarchical feature regression, enabling short observations to recover richer motion context. We further introduce a cosine-annealed distillation weighting scheme to balance forecasting supervision and feature alignment, improving optimization stability and cross-length consistency. For extremely short histories where implicit alignment is insufficient, TBM backfills missing historical segments conditioned on scene evolution, producing context-rich trajectories that strengthen PKD and thereby improve OAF. We employ a decoupled pretrain-reconstruct-finetune protocol to preserve real-motion priors while adapting to backfilled inputs. Extensive experiments on Argoverse 1 and Argoverse 2 show that TaPD consistently outperforms strong baselines across all observation lengths, delivers especially large gains under very short inputs, and improves other predictors (e.g., HiVT) in a plug-and-play manner. Code will be available at https://github.com/zhouhao94/TaPD.

AI.tldr: TaPD introduces a plug-and-play, observation-adaptive trajectory prediction framework that remains robust under variable and very short history lengths by combining progressive knowledge distillation (from long- to short-horizon models) with a temporal backfilling module to reconstruct missing past motion; it achieves consistent SOTA gains on Argoverse 1/2 and boosts other predictors.
AI.motivation: Most trajectory forecasters assume fixed-length observation histories; real-world driving produces variable or very short histories due to occlusions and sensing limits, causing sharp performance drops. A method is needed to adapt predictions to whatever history length is available while preserving motion context.
AI.method: Two cooperative modules: (1) Observation-Adaptive Forecaster (OAF) trained via progressive knowledge distillation (PKD), transferring motion-pattern features from long-horizon “teachers” to short-horizon “students” through hierarchical feature regression; a cosine-annealed distillation weight balances forecasting loss and feature alignment for stability and cross-length consistency. (2) Temporal Backfilling Module (TBM) that reconstructs missing historical segments conditioned on scene evolution to provide context-rich pseudo-histories, especially for extremely short inputs. A decoupled pretrain–reconstruct–finetune protocol preserves real-motion priors while adapting to backfilled inputs.
AI.result: On Argoverse 1 and 2, TaPD consistently outperforms strong baselines across all observation lengths, with especially large gains for very short histories, and can improve external predictors (e.g., HiVT) in a plug-and-play manner.
AI.conclusion: Temporal-adaptive progressive distillation with explicit backfilling enables robust, observation-adaptive trajectory forecasting, recovers motion context from short inputs, and generalizes as a plug-in to other models, delivering consistent state-of-the-art performance across history lengths.

title: RePer-360: Releasing Perspective Priors for 360$^\circ$ Depth Estimation via Self-Modulation
summary: Recent depth foundation models trained on perspective imagery achieve strong performance, yet generalize poorly to 360$^\circ$ images due to the substantial geometric discrepancy between perspective and panoramic domains. Moreover, fully fine-tuning these models typically requires large amounts of panoramic data. To address this issue, we propose RePer-360, a distortion-aware self-modulation framework for monocular panoramic depth estimation that adapts depth foundation models while preserving powerful pretrained perspective priors. Specifically, we design a lightweight geometry-aligned guidance module to derive a modulation signal from two complementary projections (i.e., ERP and CP) and use it to guide the model toward the panoramic domain without overwriting its pretrained perspective knowledge. We further introduce a Self-Conditioned AdaLN-Zero mechanism that produces pixel-wise scaling factors to reduce the feature distribution gap between the perspective and panoramic domains. In addition, a cubemap-domain consistency loss further improves training stability and cross-projection alignment. By shifting the focus from complementary-projection fusion to panoramic domain adaptation under preserved pretrained perspective priors, RePer-360 surpasses standard fine-tuning methods while using only 1\% of the training data. Under the same in-domain training setting, it further achieves an approximately 20\% improvement in RMSE. Code will be released upon acceptance.

AI.tldr: RePer-360 is a distortion-aware self-modulation approach that adapts perspective-trained depth foundation models to 360° panoramas using ERP/CP-guided modulation, a Self-Conditioned AdaLN-Zero for pixel-wise feature scaling, and a cubemap consistency loss—beating standard fine-tuning with only 1% data and yielding ~20% RMSE gains.
AI.motivation: Depth models trained on perspective images generalize poorly to panoramic imagery due to severe geometric mismatch; fully fine-tuning them demands large amounts of 360° data. A data-efficient method is needed that leverages and preserves powerful pretrained perspective priors while adapting to the panoramic domain.
AI.method: RePer-360 introduces (1) a lightweight geometry-aligned guidance module that derives a modulation signal from equirectangular (ERP) and cubemap (CP) projections to steer features toward the panoramic domain without overwriting perspective knowledge; (2) a Self-Conditioned AdaLN-Zero mechanism that outputs pixel-wise scaling factors to reduce cross-domain feature distribution gaps; and (3) a cubemap-domain consistency loss to stabilize training and align predictions across projections. The focus is on panoramic domain adaptation rather than complementary-projection fusion.
AI.result: The approach surpasses standard fine-tuning baselines while using only 1% of panoramic training data and achieves approximately a 20% RMSE improvement under the same in-domain training conditions.
AI.conclusion: Distortion-aware self-modulation can efficiently adapt perspective-trained depth foundation models to 360° images while preserving pretrained priors, delivering superior accuracy with minimal data. Code will be released upon acceptance.

title: GazeMoE: Perception of Gaze Target with Mixture-of-Experts
summary: Estimating human gaze target from visible images is a critical task for robots to understand human attention, yet the development of generalizable neural architectures and training paradigms remains challenging. While recent advances in pre-trained vision foundation models offer promising avenues for locating gaze targets, the integration of multi-modal cues -- including eyes, head poses, gestures, and contextual features -- demands adaptive and efficient decoding mechanisms. Inspired by Mixture-of-Experts (MoE) for adaptive domain expertise in large vision-language models, we propose GazeMoE, a novel end-to-end framework that selectively leverages gaze-target-related cues from a frozen foundation model through MoE modules. To address class imbalance in gaze target classification (in-frame vs. out-of-frame) and enhance robustness, GazeMoE incorporates a class-balancing auxiliary loss alongside strategic data augmentations, including region-specific cropping and photometric transformations. Extensive experiments on benchmark datasets demonstrate that our GazeMoE achieves state-of-the-art performance, outperforming existing methods on challenging gaze estimation tasks. The code and pre-trained models are released at https://huggingface.co/zdai257/GazeMoE

AI.tldr: GazeMoE adds Mixture-of-Experts adapters atop frozen vision foundation model features to adaptively fuse eyes/head/gesture/context cues for gaze target estimation, plus class-balanced loss and targeted augmentations, achieving state of the art on benchmarks.
AI.motivation: Robots need reliable gaze target estimation, but integrating multiple visual cues and generalizing across scenes is hard. Pre-trained vision foundation models are promising, yet require adaptive, efficient mechanisms to selectively use relevant cues and to handle class imbalance (in-frame vs. out-of-frame).
AI.method: An end-to-end framework that keeps the foundation vision backbone frozen and introduces MoE modules to route and combine gaze-relevant signals (eyes, head pose, gestures, context). Training adds a class-balancing auxiliary loss for in/out-of-frame classification and robustness-driven augmentations (region-specific cropping and photometric transforms).
AI.result: On standard gaze-estimation benchmarks, the approach outperforms prior methods and sets new state-of-the-art performance. The authors release code and pre-trained models.
AI.conclusion: Selective cue utilization via MoE over frozen foundation features, together with balanced training and targeted augmentations, yields a robust, generalizable gaze-target estimator suitable for practical deployment.

title: HiPP-Prune: Hierarchical Preference-Conditioned Structured Pruning for Vision-Language Models
summary: Pruning vision-language models (VLMs) for efficient deployment is challenging because compression can affect not only task utility but also visual grounding, often amplifying object hallucinations even at the same sparsity level. We present HiPP-Prune, a hierarchical preference-conditioned structured pruning framework that treats pruning as conditional resource allocation under multiple objectives. HiPP-Prune makes plan-level decisions: a single policy invocation outputs a global pruning blueprint by factorizing decisions into an overall sparsity budget and a layer-wise allocation, enabling queryable trade-offs via a user-specified preference vector. To account for VLM-specific failure modes, our policy state integrates a visual sensitivity signal derived from attention flow between vision tokens and language hidden states, discouraging over-pruning of vision-critical layers that facilitate cross-modal fusion. We optimize pruning plans with plan-level Group Relative Policy Optimization (GRPO) under a multi-objective return that combines task utility, hallucination robustness (POPE), compression, and a synaptic-flow-inspired stability proxy to reduce unproductive exploration in high-sparsity regimes. Experiments on LLaVA with POPE and ScienceQA demonstrate that HiPP-Prune discovers diverse non-dominated pruning plans and provides controllable robustness--utility trade-offs under matched sparsity budgets.

AI.tldr: HiPP-Prune is a hierarchical, preference-conditioned structured pruning method for vision–language models that allocates a global sparsity budget across layers using a policy optimized with multi-objective GRPO, incorporating a visual-sensitivity signal to preserve grounding and reduce hallucinations; it yields controllable robustness–utility trade-offs and Pareto-diverse pruning plans on LLaVA (POPE, ScienceQA).
AI.motivation: Standard pruning of VLMs can degrade not only task accuracy but also visual grounding, often increasing object hallucinations even at the same sparsity. Practitioners need pruning methods that explicitly balance accuracy, robustness to hallucinations, and compression while respecting VLM-specific cross-modal fusion dynamics, and allow user-controllable trade-offs.
AI.method: Treat pruning as conditional resource allocation solved by a hierarchical policy that outputs a plan-level pruning blueprint: (1) choose an overall sparsity budget; (2) allocate layer-wise sparsity. The policy’s state includes a visual-sensitivity signal from attention flow between vision tokens and language hidden states to avoid over-pruning cross-modal layers. Plans are optimized with plan-level Group Relative Policy Optimization (GRPO) under a multi-objective return combining task utility, hallucination robustness (POPE), compression, and a synaptic-flow-inspired stability proxy to stabilize exploration at high sparsity. Users provide a preference vector to query different trade-offs.
AI.result: On LLaVA evaluated with POPE and ScienceQA, HiPP-Prune discovers diverse non-dominated (Pareto) pruning plans and enables controllable robustness–utility trade-offs under matched sparsity budgets, suggesting improved hallucination robustness without sacrificing utility compared to naive pruning at similar sparsity.
AI.conclusion: Reframing VLM pruning as preference-conditioned, plan-level resource allocation with visual-sensitivity-aware policies and multi-objective GRPO yields practical, controllable compression that better preserves cross-modal grounding and mitigates hallucinations, producing Pareto-efficient options for deployment constraints.

title: EffectMaker: Unifying Reasoning and Generation for Customized Visual Effect Creation
summary: Visual effects (VFX) are essential for enhancing the expressiveness and creativity of video content, yet producing high-quality effects typically requires expert knowledge and costly production pipelines. Existing AIGC systems face significant challenges in VFX generation due to the scarcity of effect-specific data and the inherent difficulty of modeling supernatural or stylized effects. Moreover, these approaches often require per-effect fine-tuning, which severely limits their scalability and generalization to novel VFX. In this work, we present EffectMaker, a unified reasoning-generation framework that enables reference-based VFX customization. EffectMaker employs a multimodal large language model to interpret high-level effect semantics and reason about how they should adapt to a target subject, while a diffusion transformer leverages in-context learning to capture fine-grained visual cues from reference videos. These two components form a semantic-visual dual-path guidance mechanism that enables accurate, controllable, and effect-consistent synthesis without per-effect fine-tuning. Furthermore, we construct EffectData, the largest high-quality synthetic dataset containing 130k videos across 3k VFX categories, to improve generalization and scalability. Experiments show that EffectMaker achieves superior visual quality and effect consistency over state-of-the-art baselines, offering a scalable and flexible paradigm for customized VFX generation. Project page: https://effectmaker.github.io

AI.tldr: EffectMaker is a unified, reference-based VFX generation framework that couples a multimodal LLM for semantic reasoning with a diffusion transformer for visual cue extraction, enabling accurate, controllable, effect-consistent video synthesis without per-effect fine-tuning. The authors also release EffectData, a 130k-video synthetic dataset across 3k VFX categories, achieving state-of-the-art quality and consistency.
AI.motivation: High-quality VFX typically demand expert skill and costly pipelines. Current AIGC tools struggle due to scarce effect-specific data, difficulty modeling stylized/supernatural effects, and the need for per-effect fine-tuning, which hampers scalability and generalization to new effects.
AI.method: EffectMaker introduces a semantic-visual dual-path guidance mechanism: (1) a multimodal large language model interprets high-level effect semantics and reasons about how to adapt them to a target subject; (2) a diffusion transformer performs in-context learning from reference videos to capture fine-grained visual cues. Together, they guide generation for reference-based VFX customization without per-effect fine-tuning. The authors also construct EffectData (130k synthetic videos, 3k categories) to train/improve generalization.
AI.result: Experiments indicate superior visual quality and effect consistency compared to state-of-the-art baselines. The system provides accurate, controllable, and effect-consistent synthesis and shows improved scalability and generalization, aided by the large synthetic dataset.
AI.conclusion: EffectMaker offers a scalable, flexible paradigm for customized VFX generation by combining semantic reasoning and visual in-context guidance, eliminating per-effect fine-tuning and outperforming existing approaches. EffectData further strengthens generalization and supports broad coverage of VFX categories.

title: DEX-AR: A Dynamic Explainability Method for Autoregressive Vision-Language Models
summary: As Vision-Language Models (VLMs) become increasingly sophisticated and widely used, it becomes more and more crucial to understand their decision-making process. Traditional explainability methods, designed for classification tasks, struggle with modern autoregressive VLMs due to their complex token-by-token generation process and intricate interactions between visual and textual modalities. We present DEX-AR (Dynamic Explainability for AutoRegressive models), a novel explainability method designed to address these challenges by generating both per-token and sequence-level 2D heatmaps highlighting image regions crucial for the model's textual responses. The proposed method offers to interpret autoregressive VLMs-including varying importance of layers and generated tokens-by computing layer-wise gradients with respect to attention maps during the token-by-token generation process. DEX-AR introduces two key innovations: a dynamic head filtering mechanism that identifies attention heads focused on visual information, and a sequence-level filtering approach that aggregates per-token explanations while distinguishing between visually-grounded and purely linguistic tokens. Our evaluation on ImageNet, VQAv2, and PascalVOC, shows a consistent improvement in both perturbation-based metrics, using a novel normalized perplexity measure, as well as segmentation-based metrics.

AI.tldr: DEX-AR is a gradient-on-attention explainability method for autoregressive vision–language models that produces per-token and sequence-level heatmaps by dynamically selecting visually focused attention heads and aggregating token attributions, yielding better perturbation and segmentation metrics on ImageNet, VQAv2, and PascalVOC.
AI.motivation: Existing XAI techniques are tailored to single-shot classification and struggle with autoregressive VLMs, where outputs are generated token-by-token and visual–textual interactions span layers, heads, and time. Practitioners need faithful, temporally aware, and modality-sensitive explanations that separate visually grounded evidence from purely linguistic generation.
AI.method: During token-by-token decoding, compute layer-wise gradients with respect to attention maps to estimate each token’s dependence on image regions. Introduce (1) dynamic head filtering to identify attention heads that attend to visual content, and (2) sequence-level filtering to aggregate per-token attributions while distinguishing visually grounded tokens from language-only tokens, producing both per-token and whole-sequence 2D heatmaps.
AI.result: Across ImageNet, VQAv2, and PascalVOC, DEX-AR consistently outperforms baselines on perturbation-based evaluations—using a new normalized perplexity metric—and on segmentation-based metrics, indicating more faithful and localized visual explanations.
AI.conclusion: DEX-AR provides dynamic, fine-grained, and modality-aware explanations for autoregressive VLMs by capturing varying layer and token importance and separating visual from linguistic signals. Its empirical gains suggest improved faithfulness and usability for interpreting VLM decisions in diverse tasks.

title: MOSIV: Multi-Object System Identification from Videos
summary: We introduce the challenging problem of multi-object system identification from videos, for which prior methods are ill-suited due to their focus on single-object scenes or discrete material classification with a fixed set of material prototypes. To address this, we propose MOSIV, a new framework that directly optimizes for continuous, per-object material parameters using a differentiable simulator guided by geometric objectives derived from video. We also present a new synthetic benchmark with contact-rich, multi-object interactions to facilitate evaluation. On this benchmark, MOSIV substantially improves grounding accuracy and long-horizon simulation fidelity over adapted baselines, establishing it as a strong baseline for this new task. Our analysis shows that object-level fine-grained supervision and geometry-aligned objectives are critical for stable optimization in these complex, multi-object settings. The source code and dataset will be released.

AI.tldr: MOSIV tackles multi-object system identification from video by optimizing continuous, per-object material parameters with a differentiable simulator guided by geometry-derived objectives, and shows clear gains over baselines on a new synthetic, contact-rich benchmark.
AI.motivation: Existing approaches largely handle single-object scenes or treat materials as discrete classes tied to fixed prototypes, which fails for realistic, contact-rich, multi-object interactions requiring continuous, object-specific physical parameters estimated from video.
AI.method: Introduce MOSIV: differentiate through a physics simulator to directly optimize continuous material parameters per object. The optimization is driven by geometry-aligned objectives extracted from video and leverages object-level, fine-grained supervision. A synthetic benchmark with multi-object, contact-heavy scenarios is provided for evaluation.
AI.result: On the new benchmark, MOSIV substantially improves grounding accuracy (associating correct parameters to objects) and long-horizon simulation fidelity (rollout stability/accuracy) compared with adapted baselines, establishing it as a strong baseline for this task.
AI.conclusion: Object-level fine-grained supervision and geometry-aligned objectives are crucial for stable optimization in complex multi-object settings. Differentiable, geometry-driven simulation enables accurate system identification at scale; released code and dataset aim to catalyze further work.

title: K-MaT: Knowledge-Anchored Manifold Transport for Cross-Modal Prompt Learning in Medical Imaging
summary: Large-scale biomedical vision-language models (VLMs) adapted on high-end imaging (e.g., CT) often fail to transfer to frontline low-end modalities (e.g., radiography), collapsing into modality-specific shortcuts. We propose K-MaT (Knowledge-Anchored Manifold Transport), a prompt-learning framework that transfers decision structures to low-end modalities without requiring low-end training images. K-MaT factorizes prompts, anchors them to clinical text descriptions, and aligns the low-end prompt manifold to the visually-grounded high-end space using Fused Gromov-Wasserstein optimal transport. We evaluate K-MaT on four cross-modal benchmarks, including dermoscopy, mammography to ultrasound, and CT to chest X-ray. K-MaT achieves state-of-the-art results, improving the average harmonic mean of accuracy to 44.1% (from BiomedCoOp's 42.0%) and macro-F1 to 36.2%. Notably, on the challenging breast imaging task, it mitigates the catastrophic forgetting seen in standard methods like CoOp (which drops to 27.0% accuracy on the low-end), preserving robust performance across modalities. Aligning prompt manifolds via optimal transport provides a highly effective route for the zero-shot cross-modal deployment of medical VLMs.

AI.tldr: K-MaT is a prompt-learning framework that transfers the decision structure of medical VLMs from high-end imaging (e.g., CT) to low-end modalities (e.g., X-ray) without using any low-end images, by factorizing and clinically anchoring prompts and aligning their manifolds via Fused Gromov-Wasserstein optimal transport, achieving state-of-the-art zero-shot cross-modal performance.
AI.motivation: Biomedical VLMs fine-tuned on high-end modalities often fail on low-end imaging due to modality-specific shortcuts and domain gaps; acquiring and labeling low-end data can be difficult. The goal is robust zero-shot cross-modal transfer that preserves high-end performance while enabling reliable low-end deployment.
AI.method: K-MaT factorizes prompts and anchors them to clinical text descriptions, then aligns the resulting low-end prompt manifold with the high-end, visually grounded prompt space using Fused Gromov-Wasserstein optimal transport. This manifold transport enforces structural consistency across modalities without requiring any low-end training images.
AI.result: Across four cross-modal benchmarks (including dermoscopy, mammography→ultrasound, and CT→chest X-ray), K-MaT improves the average harmonic mean accuracy to 44.1% (vs. 42.0% for BiomedCoOp) and macro-F1 to 36.2%. On a challenging breast imaging task, it mitigates catastrophic forgetting observed in standard prompt-learning (e.g., CoOp dropping to 27% on low-end), maintaining robust performance across modalities.
AI.conclusion: Aligning prompt manifolds with optimal transport, coupled with clinically anchored prompt factorization, is an effective strategy for zero-shot cross-modal deployment of medical VLMs. K-MaT delivers state-of-the-art results and preserves performance across modalities without access to low-end images during training.

title: StruVis: Enhancing Reasoning-based Text-to-Image Generation via Thinking with Structured Vision
summary: Reasoning-based text-to-image (T2I) generation requires models to interpret complex prompts accurately. Existing reasoning frameworks can be broadly categorized into two types: (1) Text-Only Reasoning, which is computationally efficient but lacks access to visual context, often resulting in the omission of critical spatial and visual elements; and (2) Text-Image Interleaved Reasoning, which leverages a T2I generator to provide visual references during the reasoning process. While this approach enhances visual grounding, it incurs substantial computational costs and constrains the reasoning capacity of MLLMs to the representational limitations of the generator. To this end, we propose StruVis, a novel framework that enhances T2I generation through Thinking with Structured Vision. Instead of relying on intermediate image generation, StruVis employs text-based structured visual representations as intermediate reasoning states, thereby enabling the MLLM to effectively "perceive" visual structure within a purely text-based reasoning process. Powered by this, the reasoning potential for T2I generation of the MLLM is unlocked through structured-vision-guided reasoning. Additionally, as a generator-agnostic reasoning framework, our proposed StruVis can be seamlessly integrated with diverse T2I generators and efficiently enhance their performance in reasoning-based T2I generation. Extensive experiments demonstrate that StruVis achieves significant performance improvements on reasoning-based T2I benchmarks, e.g., a 4.61% gain on T2I-ReasonBench and a 4% gain on WISE.

AI.tldr: StruVis is a generator-agnostic framework that improves reasoning-based text-to-image generation by replacing intermediate image rendering with text-based structured visual representations, yielding better visual grounding at lower cost and showing 4–5% gains on reasoning benchmarks.
AI.motivation: Current reasoning pipelines either use text-only reasoning (fast but blind to visual/spatial constraints) or interleave images during reasoning (better grounding but expensive and limited by the generator’s representational bias). A method is needed that gives multimodal LLMs access to visual structure during reasoning without incurring image-generation overhead or locking reasoning to a specific generator.
AI.method: Introduce Thinking with Structured Vision: produce text-based structured visual representations (e.g., layouts, object-attribute relations, spatial graphs) as intermediate reasoning states. Use these structures to guide prompt planning and control signals for any downstream T2I generator, avoiding intermediate image synthesis and keeping the reasoning stage purely textual. The framework is generator-agnostic and aims to unlock the MLLM’s reasoning potential via structured-vision-guided reasoning.
AI.result: Across reasoning-oriented T2I benchmarks, StruVis yields significant improvements: +4.61% on T2I-ReasonBench and +4% on WISE, while being more computationally efficient than interleaved image reasoning.
AI.conclusion: Structured, text-based visual intermediates can provide the benefits of visual grounding for reasoning without the cost and constraints of iterative image generation, enabling plug-and-play gains for diverse T2I generators in reasoning-heavy prompts.

title: Dynamic Chunking Diffusion Transformer
summary: Diffusion Transformers process images as fixed-length sequences of tokens produced by a static $\textit{patchify}$ operation. While effective, this design spends uniform compute on low- and high-information regions alike, ignoring that images contain regions of varying detail and that the denoising process progresses from coarse structure at early timesteps to fine detail at late timesteps. We introduce the Dynamic Chunking Diffusion Transformer (DC-DiT), which augments the DiT backbone with a learned encoder-router-decoder scaffold that adaptively compresses the 2D input into a shorter token sequence in a data-dependent manner using a chunking mechanism learned end-to-end with diffusion training. The mechanism learns to compress uniform background regions into fewer tokens and detail-rich regions into more tokens, with meaningful visual segmentations emerging without explicit supervision. Furthermore, it also learns to adapt its compression across diffusion timesteps, using fewer tokens at noisy stages and more tokens as fine details emerge. On class-conditional ImageNet $256{\times}256$, DC-DiT consistently improves FID and Inception Score over both parameter-matched and FLOP-matched DiT baselines across $4{\times}$ and $16{\times}$ compression, showing this is a promising technique with potential further applications to pixel-space, video and 3D generation. Beyond accuracy, DC-DiT is practical: it can be upcycled from pretrained DiT checkpoints with minimal post-training compute (up to $8{\times}$ fewer training steps) and composes with other dynamic computation methods to further reduce generation FLOPs.

AI.tldr: DC-DiT replaces static patchify in Diffusion Transformers with a learned dynamic chunking module that adaptively allocates tokens across space and timesteps, yielding better FID/IS at 4×–16× compression and practical speed/compute benefits via upcycling and composition with other dynamic compute methods.
AI.motivation: Standard DiT spends uniform compute on all image patches and across all diffusion timesteps, despite images having heterogeneous detail and the denoising process progressing from coarse to fine. This wastes computation on low-information regions and early/noisy steps, limiting efficiency–accuracy trade-offs.
AI.method: Augment the DiT backbone with a learned encoder–router–decoder scaffold. The router aggregates the 2D input into variable-sized chunks, mapping smooth/background regions to fewer tokens and detail-rich regions to more tokens. Routing is learned end-to-end during diffusion training and adapts across timesteps (coarse allocation early, finer later). The approach can be initialized from pretrained DiT ('upcycled') and composes with other dynamic computation methods.
AI.result: On class-conditional ImageNet 256×256, DC-DiT consistently improves FID and Inception Score versus parameter- and FLOP-matched DiT baselines under 4× and 16× compression. The learned routing exhibits meaningful unsupervised visual segmentations, uses fewer tokens at noisy steps and more at later steps with finer details. Practically, upcycling requires up to 8× fewer training steps and the method further reduces generation FLOPs when combined with other dynamic compute techniques.
AI.conclusion: Adaptive tokenization aligned with spatial detail and diffusion timestep improves the efficiency–quality frontier for diffusion transformers. DC-DiT is retrofit-friendly and general, suggesting applicability beyond class-conditional images to pixel-space, video, and 3D generation, and opens avenues for broader adaptive-token diffusion architectures.

title: Occlusion-Aware SORT: Observing Occlusion for Robust Multi-Object Tracking
comment: The IEEE/CVF Conference on Computer Vision and Pattern Recognition 2026 (CVPR2026)
summary: Multi-object tracking (MOT) involves analyzing object trajectories and counting the number of objects in video sequences. However, 2D MOT faces challenges due to positional cost confusion arising from partial occlusion. To address this issue, we present the novel Occlusion-Aware SORT (OA-SORT) framework, a plug-and-play and training-free framework that includes the Occlusion-Aware Module (OAM), the Occlusion-Aware Offset (OAO), and the Bias-Aware Momentum (BAM). Specifically, OAM analyzes the occlusion status of objects, where a Gaussian Map (GM) is introduced to reduce background influence. In contrast, OAO and BAM leverage the OAM-described occlusion status to mitigate cost confusion and suppress estimation instability. Comprehensive evaluations on the DanceTrack, SportsMOT, and MOT17 datasets demonstrate the importance of occlusion handling in MOT. On the DanceTrack test set, OA-SORT achieves 63.1% and 64.2% in HOTA and IDF1, respectively. Furthermore, integrating the Occlusion-Aware framework into the four additional trackers improves HOTA and IDF1 by an average of 2.08% and 3.05%, demonstrating the reusability of the occlusion awareness.

AI.tldr: OA-SORT is a training-free, plug-and-play occlusion-aware extension to SORT that estimates occlusion status and uses it to adjust association and motion updates, reducing cost confusion from partial occlusions and improving MOT metrics across datasets.
AI.motivation: 2D multi-object tracking often fails under partial occlusion, where positional costs become ambiguous and motion updates get unstable, leading to ID switches and degraded tracking. A lightweight, retraining-free approach to explicitly handle occlusions is needed.
AI.method: Introduce an Occlusion-Aware Module (OAM) that infers per-object occlusion status and builds a Gaussian Map to attenuate background effects; use Occlusion-Aware Offset (OAO) to adjust association costs based on occlusion; and apply Bias-Aware Momentum (BAM) to stabilize state estimation during occlusions. The framework is plug-and-play and training-free, augmenting existing SORT-style pipelines.
AI.result: On DanceTrack test, OA-SORT achieves 63.1% HOTA and 64.2% IDF1. Across DanceTrack, SportsMOT, and MOT17, adding the occlusion-aware framework to four other trackers yields average gains of +2.08% HOTA and +3.05% IDF1, indicating consistent improvements.
AI.conclusion: Explicit occlusion modeling within a simple, training-free framework effectively mitigates association confusion and estimation bias, improving tracking accuracy and generalizing across trackers and datasets, making it a reusable enhancement for 2D MOT.

title: Prompt Group-Aware Training for Robust Text-Guided Nuclei Segmentation
summary: Foundation models such as Segment Anything Model 3 (SAM3) enable flexible text-guided medical image segmentation, yet their predictions remain highly sensitive to prompt formulation. Even semantically equivalent descriptions can yield inconsistent masks, limiting reliability in clinical and pathology workflows. We reformulate prompt sensitivity as a group-wise consistency problem. Semantically related prompts are organized into \emph{prompt groups} sharing the same ground-truth mask, and a prompt group-aware training framework is introduced for robust text-guided nuclei segmentation. The approach combines (i) a quality-guided group regularization that leverages segmentation loss as an implicit ranking signal, and (ii) a logit-level consistency constraint with a stop-gradient strategy to align predictions within each group. The method requires no architectural modification and leaves inference unchanged. Extensive experiments on multi-dataset nuclei benchmarks show consistent gains under textual prompting and markedly reduced performance variance across prompt quality levels. On six zero-shot cross-dataset tasks, our method improves Dice by an average of 2.16 points. These results demonstrate improved robustness and generalization for vision-language segmentation in computational pathology.

AI.tldr: They reduce prompt sensitivity in text-guided nuclei segmentation by grouping semantically equivalent prompts and enforcing group-wise consistency during training, yielding steadier masks and +2.16 Dice on zero-shot cross-dataset tasks without changing architecture or inference.
AI.motivation: Text prompts that are semantically the same can lead to different segmentations with foundation models (e.g., SAM3), undermining reliability in clinical/pathology workflows. There is a need to make text-guided medical image segmentation robust to prompt formulation and to generalize across datasets.
AI.method: Reformulate prompt sensitivity as a group-wise consistency problem: organize semantically related prompts into prompt groups that share a ground-truth mask. Train with (i) quality-guided group regularization using segmentation loss as an implicit ranking signal, and (ii) a logit-level consistency constraint with a stop-gradient strategy to align predictions within each group. No architectural changes; inference pipeline unchanged.
AI.result: On multi-dataset nuclei benchmarks, the method consistently improves performance under text prompts and reduces variance across prompt quality levels. In six zero-shot cross-dataset tasks, it improves Dice by an average of 2.16 points.
AI.conclusion: Group-aware, consistency-regularized training makes vision–language segmentation more robust and generalizable in computational pathology, stabilizing predictions across semantically equivalent prompts without added inference complexity.

title: Ensemble Learning with Sparse Hypercolumns
comment: presented at 33rd International Conference on Artificial Intelligence and Cognitive Science (AICS 2025)
summary: Directly inspired by findings in biological vision, high-dimensional hypercolumns are feature vectors built by concatenating multi-scale activations of convolutional neural networks for a single image pixel location. Together with powerful classifiers, they can be used for image segmentation i.e. pixel classification. However, in practice, there are only very few works dedicated to the use of hypercolumns. One reason is the computational complexity of processing concatenated dense hypercolumns that grows linearly with the size $N$ of the training set. In this work, we address this challenge by applying stratified subsampling to the VGG16 based hypercolumns. Furthermore, we investigate the performance of ensemble learning on sparse hypercolumns. Our experiments on a brain tumor dataset show that stacking and voting ensembles deliver competitive performance, but in the extreme low-shot case of $N \leq 20$, a simple Logistic Regression classifier is the most effective method. For 10% stratified subsampling rate, our best average Dice score is 0.66 for $N=20$. This is a statistically significant improvement of 24.53% over the standard multi-scale UNet baseline ($p$-value = $[3.07e-11]$, Wilcoxon signed-rank test), which is less effective due to overfitting.

AI.tldr: Sparse VGG16 hypercolumns with stratified subsampling enable low-shot brain-tumor segmentation; a simple logistic regression on 10% sampled hypercolumns reaches Dice 0.66 at N=20, outperforming a multi-scale U-Net by 24.5% (p≈3.1e-11).
AI.motivation: Dense, concatenated multi-scale CNN hypercolumns are powerful for pixel-wise segmentation but computationally heavy and underused; in low-shot regimes, complex end-to-end models (e.g., U-Net) overfit, motivating lighter, sample-efficient alternatives.
AI.method: Build per-pixel hypercolumns from multi-scale VGG16 activations; reduce computational load via stratified subsampling (e.g., 10%); train pixel-level classifiers on these sparse hypercolumns and evaluate ensemble strategies (stacking, voting) versus simple logistic regression on a brain tumor dataset under very small training sets (N≤20).
AI.result: Ensembles are competitive overall, but in the extreme low-shot case (N≤20), logistic regression is most effective. With 10% subsampling and N=20, average Dice=0.66, a statistically significant +24.53% over a multi-scale U-Net baseline (Wilcoxon signed-rank, p=3.07e-11), attributed to reduced overfitting in the simpler model.
AI.conclusion: Stratified subsampling of hypercolumns paired with simple linear classifiers yields a strong, computationally efficient baseline for low-shot medical image segmentation, outperforming a more complex U-Net in this regime; ensembles may help, but the simplest classifier can be best when data are scarce.

title: Physical Simulator In-the-Loop Video Generation
summary: Recent advances in diffusion-based video generation have achieved remarkable visual realism but still struggle to obey basic physical laws such as gravity, inertia, and collision. Generated objects often move inconsistently across frames, exhibit implausible dynamics, or violate physical constraints, limiting the realism and reliability of AI-generated videos. We address this gap by introducing Physical Simulator In-the-loop Video Generation (PSIVG), a novel framework that integrates a physical simulator into the video diffusion process. Starting from a template video generated by a pre-trained diffusion model, PSIVG reconstructs the 4D scene and foreground object meshes, initializes them within a physical simulator, and generates physically consistent trajectories. These simulated trajectories are then used to guide the video generator toward spatio-temporally physically coherent motion. To further improve texture consistency during object movement, we propose a Test-Time Texture Consistency Optimization (TTCO) technique that adapts text and feature embeddings based on pixel correspondences from the simulator. Comprehensive experiments demonstrate that PSIVG produces videos that better adhere to real-world physics while preserving visual quality and diversity. Project Page: https://vcai.mpi-inf.mpg.de/projects/PSIVG/

AI.tldr: PSIVG integrates a physics simulator into the diffusion-based video generation loop, reconstructs a 4D scene with object meshes, simulates physically valid trajectories, and uses them to guide generation; a test-time texture optimization (TTCO) further enforces appearance consistency, yielding videos that better obey physics without sacrificing quality.
AI.motivation: Despite strong visual realism, current video diffusion models often violate basic physics (gravity, inertia, collisions), causing frame-to-frame inconsistencies and implausible dynamics. The paper aims to bridge this realism–physicality gap to improve reliability of AI-generated videos.
AI.method: 1) Generate a template video using a pre-trained diffusion model. 2) Reconstruct the 4D scene and foreground object meshes from the template. 3) Initialize these objects in a physical simulator to produce physically consistent trajectories. 4) Use the simulated trajectories to guide the diffusion process for spatio-temporally coherent motion. 5) Apply Test-Time Texture Consistency Optimization (TTCO) to adapt text and feature embeddings using simulator-derived pixel correspondences, improving texture stability during motion.
AI.result: Comprehensive experiments indicate that PSIVG yields videos that better adhere to real-world physics while maintaining visual quality and diversity compared with standard diffusion baselines.
AI.conclusion: Embedding a physics simulator in the video diffusion loop, complemented by test-time texture consistency, leads to more physically plausible and visually consistent generated videos, advancing the realism and reliability of video synthesis.

title: FontUse: A Data-Centric Approach to Style- and Use-Case-Conditioned In-Image Typography
summary: Recent text-to-image models can generate high-quality images from natural-language prompts, yet controlling typography remains challenging: requested typographic appearance is often ignored or only weakly followed. We address this limitation with a data-centric approach that trains image generation models using targeted supervision derived from a structured annotation pipeline specialized for typography. Our pipeline constructs a large-scale typography-focused dataset, FontUse, consisting of about 70K images annotated with user-friendly prompts, text-region locations, and OCR-recognized strings. The annotations are automatically produced using segmentation models and multimodal large language models (MLLMs). The prompts explicitly combine font styles (e.g., serif, script, elegant) and use cases (e.g., wedding invitations, coffee-shop menus), enabling intuitive specification even for novice users. Fine-tuning existing generators with these annotations allows them to consistently interpret style and use-case conditions as textual prompts without architectural modification. For evaluation, we introduce a Long-CLIP-based metric that measures alignment between generated typography and requested attributes. Experiments across diverse prompts and layouts show that models trained with our pipeline produce text renderings more consistent with prompts than competitive baselines. The source code for our annotation pipeline is available at https://github.com/xiaxinz/FontUSE.

AI.tldr: A typography-focused, data-centric pipeline (FontUse, ~70K images) provides targeted annotations and prompts to fine-tune existing text-to-image models for reliable control of font style and use-case via natural language; a Long-CLIP metric evaluates alignment, and the fine-tuned models outperform baselines. Code is released.
AI.motivation: Current text-to-image generators poorly control typography, often ignoring requested font styles and usage contexts, which limits real-world design workflows. There is a need for intuitive prompt-based control over typographic appearance and a principled way to measure it.
AI.method: Automatically construct a large-scale dataset (FontUse, ~70K images) via a structured annotation pipeline that uses segmentation models and MLLMs to produce: (1) user-friendly prompts that explicitly combine font styles (e.g., serif, script, elegant) with use cases (e.g., wedding invitations, coffee-shop menus), (2) text-region locations, and (3) OCR-recognized strings. Fine-tune existing text-to-image generators on this supervision so they learn to follow style/use-case prompts without changing architectures. Introduce a Long-CLIP-based metric to quantify alignment between generated typography and requested attributes.
AI.result: When fine-tuned with FontUse annotations, models generate text that aligns more consistently with requested styles and use-cases across varied prompts and layouts, surpassing competitive baselines according to the Long-CLIP metric and qualitative assessments.
AI.conclusion: Targeted, typography-specific supervision enables general text-to-image models to achieve controllable, prompt-driven typography without architectural changes. The FontUse dataset and Long-CLIP metric support training and evaluation, and the released pipeline facilitates reproducibility and extension.

title: CLoPA: Continual Low Parameter Adaptation of Interactive Segmentation for Medical Image Annotation
summary: Interactive segmentation enables clinicians to guide annotation, but existing zero-shot models like nnInteractive fail to consistently reach expert-level performance across diverse medical imaging tasks. Because annotation campaigns produce a growing stream of task-specific labelled data, online adaptation of the segmentation model is a natural complement to zero-shot inference. We propose CLoPA, a continual adaptation strategy that tunes a small fraction of nnInteractive's parameters on the annotation cache, triggered by lightweight episode scheduling. CLoPA requires no new parameters or changes to the inference pipeline, and operates entirely within the existing annotation workflow. Across eight Medical Segmentation Decathlon tasks spanning diverse anatomical targets and imaging characteristics, CLoPA rapidly elevates performance to expert-level, even for tasks where nnInteractive previously failed, with the majority of gains realised after a single training episode. We show that the benefits of tuning different parameter groups depends on task characteristics and data regimes. Also, that for targets with complex geometries (e.g., hepatic vessels), instance normalisation and low-level feature tuning saturates, suggesting a need for deeper feature-representation alignment in the most challenging scenarios.

AI.tldr: CLoPA is a lightweight continual adaptation strategy that fine-tunes a small subset of nnInteractive’s parameters using ongoing annotation caches, quickly raising zero-shot interactive segmentation to expert-level across varied medical imaging tasks without changing the inference pipeline.
AI.motivation: Zero-shot interactive segmentation (e.g., nnInteractive) often fails to consistently reach expert-level accuracy across diverse medical imaging tasks. Since annotation campaigns naturally produce a stream of labeled data, leveraging these data online to adapt the model can close the performance gap while fitting seamlessly into existing clinical annotation workflows.
AI.method: Introduce CLoPA, a continual learning scheme that: (1) schedules lightweight adaptation episodes during routine annotation; (2) fine-tunes only a small fraction of nnInteractive’s parameters (e.g., instance normalization and low-level features) on a cached set of recent annotations; (3) requires no additional parameters, architecture changes, or inference-time modifications; and (4) analyzes which parameter groups to tune under different task and data regimes.
AI.result: Across eight diverse Medical Segmentation Decathlon tasks, CLoPA rapidly improves performance to expert-level—even where nnInteractive previously underperformed—with most gains achieved after a single adaptation episode. The effectiveness of tuning depends on task characteristics and data availability. For complex geometries (e.g., hepatic vessels), focusing on instance normalization and low-level features saturates, indicating diminishing returns without deeper representation updates.
AI.conclusion: Continual, in-workflow fine-tuning of a small parameter subset effectively complements zero-shot interactive segmentation, delivering expert-level performance quickly and broadly. However, the hardest anatomical targets may require deeper feature-representation alignment beyond low-level or normalization-layer tuning.

title: Learning to Generate via Understanding: Understanding-Driven Intrinsic Rewarding for Unified Multimodal Models
summary: Recently, unified multimodal models (UMMs) have made remarkable progress in integrating visual understanding and generation, demonstrating strong potential for complex text-to-image (T2I) tasks. Despite their theoretical promise, a persistent capability gap exists: UMMs typically exhibit superior visual understanding but comparatively weaker generative capabilities. This discrepancy arises largely from the intrinsic decoupling between the understanding and generation processes. While a UMM can accurately interpret fine-grained visual details, it often struggles to produce semantically coherent images from complex textual prompts. To address this challenge, we explore UMMs' internal understanding capability to enhance generation quality. We propose a token-level intrinsic text-image alignment reward mechanism, GvU, enabling the UMM to act simultaneously as teacher and student: it evaluates its own outputs using the understanding branch to guide the generations accordingly. Building upon this, we design a self-supervised reinforcement learning framework, allowing UMMs to iteratively improve their generation quality through understanding-based intrinsic reward signals--without reliance on external supervision. Experimental results show that our method substantially boosts UMMs' generation, which in turn strengthens their fine-grained visual understanding, narrowing the capability gap between UMMs' visual understanding and generation.

AI.tldr: They introduce GvU, a token-level intrinsic text–image alignment reward that lets a unified multimodal model (UMM) use its own visual-understanding branch to judge and improve its text-to-image generation via self-supervised reinforcement learning, substantially improving generation and also refining understanding.
AI.motivation: UMMs excel at visual understanding but lag in image generation because understanding and generation are decoupled; the goal is to leverage the model’s strong internal understanding to guide and align its weaker generation without external supervision.
AI.method: Design a token-level intrinsic alignment reward (GvU) computed by the UMM’s understanding branch over generated image–text pairs; treat the UMM as both teacher (evaluator) and student (generator); optimize generation with a self-supervised reinforcement learning loop that uses the intrinsic reward to iteratively improve outputs.
AI.result: Experiments report marked gains in text-to-image generation quality and improved fine-grained understanding, reducing the capability gap between understanding and generation in UMMs.
AI.conclusion: Intrinsic, understanding-driven rewards coupled with self-supervised RL can align and co-improve the two branches of UMMs, offering a practical path to stronger, more coherent generation without external annotations.

title: GenHOI: Towards Object-Consistent Hand-Object Interaction with Temporally Balanced and Spatially Selective Object Injection
summary: Hand-Object Interaction (HOI) remains a core challenge in digital human video synthesis, where models must generate physically plausible contact and preserve object identity across frames. Although recent HOI reenactment approaches have achieved progress, they are typically trained and evaluated in-domain and fail to generalize to complex, in-the-wild scenarios. In contrast, all-in-one video editing models exhibit broader robustness but still struggle with HOI-specific issues such as inconsistent object appearance. In this paper, we present GenHOI, a lightweight augmentation to pretrained video generation models that injects reference-object information in a temporally balanced and spatially selective manner. For temporal balancing, we propose Head-Sliding RoPE, which assigns head-specific temporal offsets to reference tokens, distributing their influence evenly across frames and mitigating the temporal decay of 3D RoPE to improve long-range object consistency. For spatial selectivity, we design a two-level spatial attention gate that concentrates object-conditioned attention on HOI regions and adaptively scales its strength, preserving background realism while enhancing interaction fidelity. Extensive qualitative and quantitative evaluations on unseen, in-the-wild scenes demonstrate that GenHOI significantly outperforms state-of-the-art HOI reenactment and all-in-one video editing methods. Project page: https://xuanhuang0.github.io/GenHOI/

AI.tldr: GenHOI is a lightweight add-on to pretrained video generators that injects reference-object cues with temporally balanced and spatially selective attention, yielding more consistent, physically plausible hand–object interactions in the wild.
AI.motivation: HOI video synthesis requires stable object identity and realistic contact over time, but HOI-specific models overfit to domains and general video editors suffer from object inconsistency and poor interaction fidelity. A generalizable yet HOI-aware mechanism is needed.
AI.method: Augment pretrained video diffusion/generation models with: (1) Head-Sliding RoPE—assign head-specific temporal offsets to reference-object tokens to counter temporal decay in 3D RoPE and evenly propagate reference influence across frames; (2) a two-level spatial attention gate—focus object-conditioned attention on HOI regions and adaptively scale it to enhance interaction while preserving background realism.
AI.result: On unseen, in-the-wild scenes, the approach improves long-range object appearance consistency and HOI realism, significantly outperforming state-of-the-art HOI reenactment and all-in-one video editing baselines in both qualitative and quantitative evaluations.
