AI.conclusion: Injecting temporally balanced and spatially selective object conditioning into pretrained video models effectively addresses HOI-specific failures, offering a lightweight, generalizable path to robust, realistic hand–object interaction synthesis.

title: Do Foundation Models Know Geometry? Probing Frozen Features for Continuous Physical Measurement
summary: Vision-language models encode continuous geometry that their text pathway fails to express: a 6,000-parameter linear probe extracts hand joint angles at 6.1 degrees MAE from frozen features, while the best text output achieves only 20.0 degrees -- a 3.3x bottleneck. LoRA fine-tuning (r=16, 2,000 images) narrows this gap to 6.5 degrees, providing evidence for a pathway-training deficit rather than a representational one. Training objective determines accuracy more than architecture: five encoders spanning self-supervised, contrastive, and hybrid paradigms converge to statistically equivalent accuracy (R^2 approximately 0.55, TOST-equivalent at delta=0.03) despite sharing as little as CKA=0.41 representational similarity -- functional convergence without representational convergence. Autoregressive generation damages geometric fidelity, but the damage originates in the generation process, not in language alignment: Qwen2.5-VL's LLM layers actually improve probe accuracy over its raw vision encoder. Layer-wise analysis reveals a universal mid-network accuracy peak across all architectures, with attention heads in layers 18-22 carrying disproportionate geometric signal. These findings enable a single frozen backbone to function as a multi-task geometric sensor through lightweight probes, without fine-tuning or text generation.

AI.tldr: VLMs already encode precise continuous 3D geometry, but the text/generation pathway cannot express it: tiny probes recover near-expert hand pose accuracy while text outputs lag; training objective matters more than backbone design; mid-network attention heads carry the strongest geometric signal, enabling frozen backbones to act as multi-task geometric sensors via lightweight probes.
AI.motivation: Test whether modern vision-language models intrinsically represent fine-grained geometry (e.g., hand joint angles), identify where performance bottlenecks arise (representation vs text pathway), assess the relative impact of training objectives versus architectures, and localize where in the network geometric information resides to guide practical usage without costly fine-tuning or brittle text generation.
AI.method: Probe frozen VLM features with a ~6k-parameter linear regressor for hand joint angle estimation and compare against text-generation-based predictions; apply LoRA (r=16) with 2k images to the language/generation pathway; evaluate five encoders trained with self-supervised, contrastive, and hybrid objectives; test statistical equivalence of accuracy via TOST at delta=0.03 and measure representational similarity with CKA; analyze the effect of autoregressive generation and the contribution of LLM layers (e.g., Qwen2.5-VL) to probe accuracy; perform layer- and head-wise probing to map where geometric signal concentrates.
AI.result: - Linear probe: 6.1° MAE on hand joint angles from frozen features; best text output: 20.0° MAE (≈3.3× worse). - LoRA fine-tuning narrows to 6.5° MAE, implicating a pathway/training deficit rather than representational limits. - Across five encoders with diverse objectives, accuracy converges (R^2 ≈ 0.55) and is statistically equivalent (TOST at Δ=0.03) despite low representational similarity (CKA as low as 0.41): functional convergence without representational convergence. - Autoregressive generation degrades geometric fidelity; degradation is due to generation itself, not language alignment; LLM layers in Qwen2.5-VL improve probe accuracy vs the raw vision encoder. - Layer-wise: universal mid-network accuracy peak; attention heads in layers 18–22 carry disproportionate geometric signal.
AI.conclusion: VLMs contain strong, accessible geometric representations; the main bottleneck is the autoregressive/text pathway rather than the vision backbone. Training objective shapes accuracy more than architectural choice, and geometric information concentrates mid-network in specific attention heads. Therefore, a single frozen VLM backbone can serve as a robust multi-task geometric sensor via lightweight linear/probing heads, avoiding text generation and heavy fine-tuning.

title: Devil is in Narrow Policy: Unleashing Exploration in Driving VLA Models
summary: We identify a fundamental Narrow Policy limitation undermining the performance of autonomous VLA models, where driving Imitation Learning (IL) tends to collapse exploration and limit the potential of subsequent Reinforcement Learning (RL) stages, which often saturate prematurely due to insufficient feedback diversity. Thereby, we propose Curious-VLA, a framework that alleviates the exploit-explore dilemma through a two-stage design. During IL, we introduce a Feasible Trajectory Expansion (FTE) strategy to generate multiple physically valid trajectories and a step-wise normalized trajectory representation to adapt this diverse data. In the RL stage, we present Adaptive Diversity-Aware Sampling (ADAS) that prioritizes high-diversity samples and introduce Spanning Driving Reward (SDR) with a focal style weighting to amplify reward's value span for improving sensitivity to driving quality. On the Navsim benchmark, Curious-VLA achieves SoTA results (PDMS 90.3, EPDMS 85.4) and a Best-of-N PDMS of 94.8, demonstrating its effectiveness in unlocking the exploratory potential of VLA models. Code: https://github.com/Mashiroln/curious_vla.git.

AI.tldr: Curious-VLA tackles the “narrow policy” problem in VLA driving by expanding feasible trajectories in IL and enforcing diversity-aware sampling and a spanning reward in RL, yielding SOTA results on Navsim (PDMS 90.3, EPDMS 85.4; Best-of-N 94.8).
AI.motivation: In autonomous VLA models, imitation learning tends to collapse exploration, producing narrow policies; subsequent RL then saturates early due to low feedback diversity. The work aims to resolve this exploit–explore tension to unlock better driving quality and robustness.
AI.method: Two-stage framework. (1) IL stage: Feasible Trajectory Expansion (FTE) generates multiple physically valid alternatives; a step-wise normalized trajectory representation enables learning from this diversified set. (2) RL stage: Adaptive Diversity-Aware Sampling (ADAS) prioritizes high-diversity samples; Spanning Driving Reward (SDR) with focal-style weighting enlarges the reward value span and sensitivity to driving quality, improving gradient signal during RL.
AI.result: On the Navsim benchmark, Curious-VLA reaches SOTA: PDMS 90.3, EPDMS 85.4, and Best-of-N PDMS 94.8, indicating stronger exploration and better overall driving performance than prior VLA baselines.
AI.conclusion: By counteracting exploration collapse in IL and promoting diversity-sensitive learning in RL, Curious-VLA mitigates the narrow-policy limitation and substantially improves autonomous VLA driving. The approach appears practical and reproducible (public code) and may generalize to other exploration-limited VLA tasks.

title: Artificial Intelligence for Detecting Fetal Orofacial Clefts and Advancing Medical Education
summary: Orofacial clefts are among the most common congenital craniofacial abnormalities, yet accurate prenatal detection remains challenging due to the scarcity of experienced specialists and the relative rarity of the condition. Early and reliable diagnosis is essential to enable timely clinical intervention and reduce associated morbidity. Here we show that an artificial intelligence system, trained on over 45,139 ultrasound images from 9,215 fetuses across 22 hospitals, can diagnose fetal orofacial clefts with sensitivity and specificity exceeding 93% and 95% respectively, matching the performance of senior radiologists and substantially outperforming junior radiologists. When used as a medical copilot, the system raises junior radiologists' sensitivity by more than 6%. Beyond direct diagnostic assistance, the system also accelerates the development of clinical expertise. A pilot study involving 24 radiologists and trainees demonstrated that the model can improve the expertise development for rare conditions. This dual-purpose approach offers a scalable solution for improving both diagnostic accuracy and specialist training in settings where experienced radiologists are scarce.

AI.tldr: Multi-center AI system trained on >45k prenatal ultrasound images detects fetal orofacial clefts with >93% sensitivity and >95% specificity, matching senior radiologists, outperforming juniors, and improving junior performance and learning.
AI.motivation: Prenatal detection of orofacial clefts is clinically important but difficult due to rarity of cases and shortage of specialists. A scalable tool is needed to deliver early, reliable diagnosis and to support training in low-resource settings.
AI.method: Develop an AI model using 45,139 ultrasound images from 9,215 fetuses collected across 22 hospitals. Benchmark its diagnostic performance against senior and junior radiologists. Evaluate its effect as a decision-support copilot for juniors and run a pilot study with 24 radiologists/trainees to assess impact on expertise development for rare conditions.
AI.result: The AI achieved sensitivity >93% and specificity >95%, matching senior radiologists and substantially outperforming juniors. As a copilot, it increased junior radiologists’ sensitivity by >6%. A pilot involving 24 readers indicated improved expertise development for rare conditions.
AI.conclusion: An accurate, dual-purpose AI offers scalable gains: immediate diagnostic assistance comparable to experts and measurable support for training, potentially improving outcomes where experienced radiologists are limited.

title: SUREON: A Benchmark and Vision-Language-Model for Surgical Reasoning
summary: Surgeons don't just see -- they interpret. When an expert observes a surgical scene, they understand not only what instrument is being used, but why it was chosen, what risk it poses, and what comes next. Current surgical AI cannot answer such questions, largely because training data that explicitly encodes surgical reasoning is immensely difficult to annotate at scale. Yet surgical video lectures already contain exactly this -- explanations of intent, rationale, and anticipation, narrated by experts for the purpose of teaching. Though inherently noisy and unstructured, these narrations encode the reasoning that surgical AI currently lacks. We introduce SUREON, a large-scale video QA dataset that systematically harvests this training signal from surgical academic videos. SUREON defines 12 question categories covering safety assessment, decision rationale, and forecasting, and uses a multi-agent pipeline to extract and structure supervision at scale. Across 134.7K clips and 170 procedure types, SUREON yields 206.8k QA pairs and an expert-validated benchmark of 354 examples. To evaluate the extent to which this supervision translates to surgical reasoning ability, we introduce two models: SureonVLM, a vision-language model adapted through supervised fine-tuning, and SureonVLM-R1, a reasoning model trained with Group Relative Policy Optimization. Both models can answer complex questions about surgery and substantially outperform larger general-domain models, exceeding 84% accuracy on the SUREON benchmark while outperforming general-domain models on standard surgical perception tasks. Qualitative analysis of SureonVLM-R1 reveals explicit reasoning behavior, such as inferring operative intent from visual context.

AI.tldr: They build SUREON, a large video–QA dataset mined from narrated surgical lectures to capture expert intent, rationale, safety, and anticipation, then train two vision–language models (SFT and GRPO-based) that surpass larger general models (>84% on their benchmark) and exhibit explicit reasoning.
AI.motivation: Surgical AI lacks interpretable, anticipatory reasoning because such supervision is hard to annotate at scale. Expert lecture narrations already contain this reasoning (intent, risk, rationale), providing an untapped, scalable signal.
AI.method: Harvest narrated surgical academic videos and use a multi‑agent pipeline to extract and structure 12 categories of reasoning-centric QA (safety, rationale, forecasting). Assemble 206.8k QA pairs from 134.7k clips across 170 procedures and create a 354‑example expert‑validated benchmark. Train two models: SureonVLM via supervised fine‑tuning and SureonVLM‑R1 via Group Relative Policy Optimization to enhance stepwise reasoning.
AI.result: Both models answer complex surgery questions, exceed 84% accuracy on the SUREON benchmark, and outperform larger general-domain models on standard surgical perception tasks. Qualitative analyses show explicit reasoning, e.g., inferring operative intent from context.
AI.conclusion: Narrated surgical lectures can be systematically mined to provide scalable supervision for surgical reasoning. SUREON and the proposed VLMs demonstrate that this supervision yields measurable gains and more explicit reasoning behavior, pointing toward more capable, safety‑aware surgical AI.

title: Transforming Omnidirectional RGB-LiDAR data into 3D Gaussian Splatting
comment: This work has been submitted to the 2026 IEEE/RSJ International Conference on Intelligent Robots and Systems (IROS) for possible publication
summary: The demand for large-scale digital twins is rapidly growing in robotics and autonomous driving. However, constructing these environments with 3D Gaussian Splatting (3DGS) usually requires expensive, purpose-built data collection. Meanwhile, deployed platforms routinely collect extensive omnidirectional RGB and LiDAR logs, but a significant portion of these sensor data is directly discarded or strictly underutilized due to transmission constraints and the lack of scalable reuse pipeline. In this paper, we present an omnidirectional RGB-LiDAR reuse pipeline that transforms these archived logs into robust initialization assets for 3DGS. Direct conversion of such raw logs introduces practical bottlenecks: inherent non-linear distortion leads to unreliable Structure-from-Motion (SfM) tracking, and dense, unorganized LiDAR clouds cause computational overhead during 3DGS optimization. To overcome these challenges, our pipeline strategically integrates an ERP-to-cubemap conversion module for deterministic spatial anchoring, alongside PRISM-a color stratified downsampling strategy. By bridging these multi-modal inputs via Fast Point Feature Histograms (FPFH) based global registration and Iterative Closest Point (ICP), our pipeline successfully repurposes a considerable fraction of discarded data into usable SfM geometry. Furthermore, our LiDAR-reinforced initialization consistently enhances the final 3DGS rendering fidelity in structurally complex scenes compared to vision-only baselines. Ultimately, this work provides a deterministic workflow for creating simulation-grade digital twins from standard archived sensor logs.

AI.tldr: They propose a deterministic, scalable pipeline to repurpose archived omnidirectional RGB and LiDAR logs into strong initialization for 3D Gaussian Splatting, overcoming SfM instability and LiDAR overhead, and yielding higher-fidelity digital twins, especially in complex scenes.
AI.motivation: Building large-scale digital twins with 3DGS typically needs costly, purpose-built capture. Meanwhile, deployed robots/vehicles already collect vast omnidirectional RGB and LiDAR logs that are often discarded due to bandwidth limits and lack of a reuse pipeline. The work aims to unlock this underused data for high-quality 3DGS initialization.
AI.method: A reuse pipeline that: (1) converts equirectangular (ERP) omnidirectional images to cubemaps for deterministic spatial anchoring; (2) applies PRISM, a color-stratified downsampling, to tame dense, unorganized LiDAR; (3) performs multi-modal alignment via FPFH-based global registration followed by ICP refinement; and (4) feeds the resulting SfM-ready geometry as initialization assets for 3DGS.
AI.result: The pipeline converts a sizable portion of archived logs into reliable SfM geometry and, with LiDAR-reinforced initialization, consistently improves 3DGS rendering fidelity over vision-only baselines, particularly in structurally complex environments. It also reduces practical bottlenecks tied to non-linear imaging distortion and LiDAR computational overhead.
AI.conclusion: A practical, deterministic workflow for turning standard archived omnidirectional RGB-LiDAR logs into simulation-grade digital twins via improved 3DGS initialization, enhancing robustness and fidelity while improving the scalability of environment construction.

title: BEVLM: Distilling Semantic Knowledge from LLMs into Bird's-Eye View Representations
comment: 4 figures, 6 tables in the main paper, 32 pages in total
summary: The integration of Large Language Models (LLMs) into autonomous driving has attracted growing interest for their strong reasoning and semantic understanding abilities, which are essential for handling complex decision-making and long-tail scenarios. However, existing methods typically feed LLMs with tokens from multi-view and multi-frame images independently, leading to redundant computation and limited spatial consistency. This separation in visual processing hinders accurate 3D spatial reasoning and fails to maintain geometric coherence across views. On the other hand, Bird's-Eye View (BEV) representations learned from geometrically annotated tasks (e.g., object detection) provide spatial structure but lack the semantic richness of foundation vision encoders. To bridge this gap, we propose BEVLM, a framework that connects a spatially consistent and semantically distilled BEV representation with LLMs. Through extensive experiments, we show that BEVLM enables LLMs to reason more effectively in cross-view driving scenes, improving accuracy by 46%, by leveraging BEV features as unified inputs. Furthermore, by distilling semantic knowledge from LLMs into BEV representations, BEVLM significantly improves closed-loop end-to-end driving performance by 29% in safety-critical scenarios.

AI.tldr: BEVLM fuses a spatially consistent, semantically distilled Bird’s‑Eye View (BEV) representation with Large Language Models to enable stronger 3D reasoning for autonomous driving, yielding large gains in cross‑view understanding and closed‑loop safety.
AI.motivation: LLMs are promising for decision‑making and handling rare scenarios, but feeding them independent tokens from multi‑view/multi‑frame images causes redundant computation and poor spatial consistency, harming 3D reasoning. Traditional BEV features offer geometric structure yet lack rich semantics. A bridge is needed to combine BEV’s spatial coherence with foundation‑model semantics for driving tasks.
AI.method: Introduce BEVLM, which connects LLMs to a unified, spatially consistent BEV feature representation. The system uses BEV features as the sole visual input to the LLM for cross‑view reasoning and distills semantic knowledge from the LLM back into the BEV encoder to enrich its semantics. This couples geometric BEV structure with LLM‑level semantics and reduces token redundancy across views/frames.
AI.result: Experiments show BEVLM improves cross‑view driving scene reasoning accuracy by 46% when using BEV features as unified inputs, and boosts closed‑loop end‑to‑end driving performance in safety‑critical scenarios by 29% via semantic knowledge distillation.
AI.conclusion: Coupling LLMs with semantically enriched, spatially coherent BEV features enhances 3D reasoning and safety‑critical driving performance, demonstrating that bridging geometric structure and semantic understanding addresses limitations of prior multi‑view tokenization approaches.

title: Lyapunov Probes for Hallucination Detection in Large Foundation Models
summary: We address hallucination detection in Large Language Models (LLMs) and Multimodal Large Language Models (MLLMs) by framing the problem through the lens of dynamical systems stability theory. Rather than treating hallucination as a straightforward classification task, we conceptualize (M)LLMs as dynamical systems, where factual knowledge is represented by stable equilibrium points within the representation space. Our main insight is that hallucinations tend to arise at the boundaries of knowledge-transition regions separating stable and unstable zones. To capture this phenomenon, we propose Lyapunov Probes: lightweight networks trained with derivative-based stability constraints that enforce a monotonic decay in confidence under input perturbations. By performing systematic perturbation analysis and applying a two-stage training process, these probes reliably distinguish between stable factual regions and unstable, hallucination-prone regions. Experiments on diverse datasets and models demonstrate consistent improvements over existing baselines.

AI.tldr: Reframes hallucination detection as a stability problem in (multimodal) LLMs and introduces Lyapunov Probes—lightweight, derivative-constrained probes that detect unstable, hallucination-prone regions via monotonic confidence decay under input perturbations—achieving improvements over prior detectors.
AI.motivation: Hallucinations persist in LLM/MLLM outputs and are hard to identify reliably with simple classifiers or confidence heuristics. The authors hypothesize that factual knowledge corresponds to stable equilibria in a model’s representation space, and hallucinations occur near boundaries of stability. A stability-theoretic approach could yield more principled and generalizable detection.
AI.method: Model (M)LLMs as dynamical systems with stable equilibria for true facts. Train small Lyapunov Probes on top of internal representations with derivative-based constraints ensuring the probe’s confidence decreases monotonically under structured perturbations away from stable regions. Use systematic perturbation analysis and a two-stage training procedure to separate stable (factual) from unstable (hallucination-prone) regions. Apply to multiple datasets and both LLMs and MLLMs.
AI.result: The probes reliably distinguish factual vs. unstable regions and consistently outperform existing hallucination-detection baselines across diverse datasets and models, per the abstract (no specific numbers reported).
AI.conclusion: Viewing hallucination through dynamical stability and enforcing Lyapunov-style monotonicity yields a lightweight, effective, and seemingly model-agnostic detector. Results suggest this framework can improve reliability across modalities, with potential for broader stability-guided safety tools.

title: FedARKS: Federated Aggregation via Robust and Discriminative Knowledge Selection and Integration for Person Re-identification
summary: The application of federated domain generalization in person re-identification (FedDG-ReID) aims to enhance the model's generalization ability in unseen domains while protecting client data privacy. However, existing mainstream methods typically rely on global feature representations and simple averaging operations for model aggregation, leading to two limitations in domain generalization: (1) Using only global features makes it difficult to capture subtle, domain-invariant local details (such as accessories or textures); (2) Uniform parameter averaging treats all clients as equivalent, ignoring their differences in robust feature extraction capabilities, thereby diluting the contributions of high quality clients. To address these issues, we propose a novel federated learning framework, Federated Aggregation via Robust and Discriminative Knowledge Selection and Integration (FedARKS), comprising two mechanisms: RK (Robust Knowledge) and KS (Knowledge Selection).

AI.tldr: FedARKS is a federated learning framework for person re-ID that boosts domain generalization by integrating robust local feature knowledge and client-aware knowledge selection, overcoming the limits of global-only features and uniform model averaging.
AI.motivation: FedDG-ReID needs to generalize to unseen domains under privacy constraints, but (1) relying on global features misses subtle, domain-invariant local cues (e.g., accessories/textures), and (2) uniform parameter averaging treats heterogeneous clients equally, diluting contributions from higher-quality, more robust clients.
AI.method: Introduce FedARKS with two mechanisms: (a) Robust Knowledge (RK) to learn/aggregate discriminative and domain-invariant representations with emphasis on local details (likely multi-granular or part-aware features) alongside global cues; (b) Knowledge Selection (KS) to perform client-aware aggregation that selects or weights client updates by their robustness/discriminativeness instead of naive averaging, thereby emphasizing high-quality clients during federation.
AI.result: Compared to standard FedAvg-style methods using global features, FedARKS is expected to yield higher cross-domain re-ID performance on unseen target domains while preserving data privacy; likely shows consistent gains over mainstream FedDG baselines, especially where local, subtle attributes matter.
AI.conclusion: By combining robust, locality-aware representation learning with client-quality-aware aggregation, FedARKS addresses key shortcomings in FedDG-ReID and improves generalization without sharing raw data, offering a promising direction for privacy-preserving, domain-robust person re-identification.

title: Cross-Resolution Distribution Matching for Diffusion Distillation
summary: Diffusion distillation is central to accelerating image and video generation, yet existing methods are fundamentally limited by the denoising process, where step reduction has largely saturated. Partial timestep low-resolution generation can further accelerate inference, but it suffers noticeable quality degradation due to cross-resolution distribution gaps. We propose Cross-Resolution Distribution Matching Distillation (RMD), a novel distillation framework that bridges cross-resolution distribution gaps for high-fidelity, few-step multi-resolution cascaded inference. Specifically, RMD divides the timestep intervals for each resolution using logarithmic signal-to-noise ratio (logSNR) curves, and introduces logSNR-based mapping to compensate for resolution-induced shifts. Distribution matching is conducted along resolution trajectories to reduce the gap between low-resolution generator distributions and the teacher's high-resolution distribution. In addition, a predicted-noise re-injection mechanism is incorporated during upsampling to stabilize training and improve synthesis quality. Quantitative and qualitative results show that RMD preserves high-fidelity generation while accelerating inference across various backbones. Notably, RMD achieves up to 33.4X speedup on SDXL and 25.6X on Wan2.1-14B, while preserving high visual fidelity.

AI.tldr: RMD distills diffusion models for few-step, multi-resolution cascaded inference by aligning cross-resolution noise schedules with logSNR-based mappings and stabilizing upsampling via predicted-noise re-injection, yielding 25–33× speedups with high fidelity.
AI.motivation: Inference acceleration for diffusion models has hit limits with pure step reduction; partial low-resolution generation is fast but degrades quality due to distribution shifts between resolutions. The paper seeks to close this cross-resolution gap to enable high-fidelity, few-step generation.
AI.method: Cross-Resolution Distribution Matching Distillation (RMD): (1) partition timesteps per resolution using logSNR curves; (2) apply logSNR-based timestep mappings to compensate resolution-induced shifts; (3) perform distribution matching along resolution trajectories to align low-res generator distributions with a high-res teacher; (4) use predicted-noise re-injection during upsampling to stabilize training and improve synthesis. Enables few-step, multi-resolution cascaded inference across backbones.
AI.result: Maintains high visual fidelity while substantially accelerating inference: up to 33.4× speedup on SDXL and 25.6× on Wan2.1-14B, supported by quantitative and qualitative evaluations across architectures.
AI.conclusion: Bridging cross-resolution distribution gaps with logSNR-aware distillation enables robust, few-step cascaded generation. RMD generalizes across backbones, stabilizes upsampling, and delivers large practical speedups without noticeable quality loss.

title: Spatial Colour Mixing Illusions as a Perception Stress Test for Vision-Language Models
summary: Vision-language models (VLMs) achieve strong benchmark results, yet can exhibit systematic perceptual weaknesses: structured, large changes to pixel values can cause confident yet nonsensical predictions, even when the underlying scene remains easily recognizable to humans. We study this gap using Spatial Colour Mixing, a programmatic family of colour distortions that overlays structured patterns (in both RGB and Ostwald colour systems) onto natural images. We introduce a framework of eight spatial colour mixing variants and evaluate nine VLMs across three model families on four datasets. Across models and datasets, accuracy degrades sharply with increasing distortion, and scaling the language model does not reliably mitigate the failure. In a human study with 61 participants on an animal recognition dataset, humans substantially outperform VLMs under the same distortions. Finally, we show that a simple human-inspired preprocessing step recovers a meaningful portion of performance for several distortion types, motivating perception-aware preprocessing and tool-use as practical strategies for improving VLM robustness.

AI.tldr: VLMs are brittle to structured color distortions; performance collapses under spatial color mixing, humans remain robust, and simple preprocessing can partially restore VLM accuracy.
AI.motivation: Despite strong benchmarks, VLMs make confident, nonsensical predictions when images undergo large but human-tolerable color changes. The authors aim to quantify this human–model robustness gap and probe whether scaling mitigates it.
AI.method: They design Spatial Colour Mixing (SCM), eight programmatic color-overlay variants in RGB and Ostwald systems that impose structured patterns on natural images. They evaluate nine VLMs across three model families on four datasets, run a human study with 61 participants on animal recognition, and test a simple human-inspired preprocessing step to mitigate distortions.
AI.result: Across datasets and models, accuracy drops sharply as SCM distortion increases; larger LMs do not reliably fix the issue. Humans greatly outperform VLMs under identical distortions. A lightweight preprocessing pipeline recovers a meaningful share of lost performance for several SCM variants.
AI.conclusion: Current VLMs have significant perceptual vulnerabilities to structured color perturbations. Robustness does not come for free with scale. Perception-aware preprocessing and tool-use are promising, practical stopgaps, highlighting the need for robustness-oriented training and evaluation.

title: Longitudinal NSCLC Treatment Progression via Multimodal Generative Models
summary: Predicting tumor evolution during radiotherapy is a clinically critical challenge, particularly when longitudinal changes are driven by both anatomy and treatment. In this work, we introduce a Virtual Treatment (VT) framework that formulates non-small cell lung cancer (NSCLC) progression as a dose-aware multimodal conditional image-to-image translation problem. Given a CT scan, baseline clinical variables, and a specified radiation dose increment, VT aims to synthesize plausible follow-up CT images reflecting treatment-induced anatomical changes. We evaluate the proposed framework on a longitudinal dataset of 222 stage III NSCLC patients, comprising 895 CT scans acquired during radiotherapy under irregular clinical schedules. The generative process is conditioned on delivered dose increments together with demographic and tumor-related clinical variables. Representative GAN-based and diffusion-based models are benchmarked across 2D and 2.5D configurations. Quantitative and qualitative results indicate that diffusion-based models benefit more consistently from multimodal, dose-aware conditioning and produce more stable and anatomically plausible tumor evolution trajectories than GAN-based baselines, supporting the potential of VT as a tool for in-silico treatment monitoring and adaptive radiotherapy research in NSCLC.

AI.tldr: Virtual Treatment (VT) reframes NSCLC tumor evolution during radiotherapy as a dose-aware, multimodal conditional image-to-image translation task. Conditioning on baseline CT, clinical variables, and specified dose increments, diffusion models synthesize plausible follow-up CTs and outperform GAN baselines in stability and anatomical realism.
AI.motivation: Clinicians need to anticipate treatment-driven anatomical changes during radiotherapy to enable timely monitoring and adaptation. Existing models struggle to account for the joint influence of anatomy and delivered radiation dose over irregular longitudinal schedules.
AI.method: VT conditions generative models on baseline CT, demographic and tumor-related clinical variables, and delivered radiation dose increments to synthesize follow-up CTs reflecting treatment-induced changes. The framework benchmarks representative GAN- and diffusion-based models in 2D and 2.5D settings on a longitudinal cohort of 222 stage III NSCLC patients (895 CT scans) acquired during radiotherapy under irregular schedules.
AI.result: Across quantitative and qualitative evaluations, diffusion-based models more consistently leverage multimodal, dose-aware conditioning, producing more stable and anatomically plausible tumor evolution trajectories than GAN-based baselines.
AI.conclusion: Dose-aware, multimodal conditioning enables realistic in-silico CT synthesis of treatment evolution, with diffusion models particularly effective. VT shows promise for treatment monitoring and adaptive radiotherapy research in NSCLC.

title: FreeOcc: Training-free Panoptic Occupancy Prediction via Foundation Models
summary: Semantic and panoptic occupancy prediction for road scene analysis provides a dense 3D representation of the ego vehicle's surroundings. Current camera-only approaches typically rely on costly dense 3D supervision or require training models on data from the target domain, limiting deployment in unseen environments. We propose FreeOcc, a training-free pipeline that leverages pretrained foundation models to recover both semantics and geometry from multi-view images. FreeOcc extracts per-view panoptic priors with a promptable foundation segmentation model and prompt-to-taxonomy rules, and reconstructs metric 3D points with a reconstruction foundation model. Depth- and confidence- aware filtering lifts reliable labels into 3D, which are fused over time and voxelized with a deterministic refinement stack. For panoptic occupancy, instances are recovered by fitting and merging robust current-view 3D box candidates, enabling instance-aware occupancy without any learned 3D model. On Occ3D-nuScenes, FreeOcc achieves 16.9 mIoU and 16.5 RayIoU train-free, on par with state-of-the-art weakly supervised methods. When employed as a pseudo-label generation pipeline for training downstream models, it achieves 21.1 RayIoU, surpassing the previous state-of-the-art weakly supervised baseline. Furthermore, FreeOcc sets new baselines for both train-free and weakly supervised panoptic occupancy prediction, achieving 3.1 RayPQ and 3.9 RayPQ, respectively. These results highlight foundation-model-driven perception as a practical route to training-free 3D scene understanding.

AI.tldr: FreeOcc is a fully training-free pipeline that uses pretrained foundation models to generate semantic and panoptic 3D occupancy from multi-view cameras, matching or surpassing weakly supervised baselines and serving as strong pseudo-labels for downstream training.
AI.motivation: Camera-only 3D occupancy prediction typically needs dense 3D supervision or target-domain training, which is costly and limits generalization. The paper aims to enable deployment in unseen environments without additional training by leveraging general-purpose foundation models for both semantics and geometry.
AI.method: Extract per-view panoptic priors using a promptable foundation segmentation model mapped via prompt-to-taxonomy rules; reconstruct metric 3D points using a reconstruction foundation model; apply depth- and confidence-aware filtering to lift reliable 2D labels into 3D; temporally fuse and voxelize with a deterministic refinement stack. For panoptic occupancy, estimate instances by fitting and merging robust current-view 3D box candidates, enabling instance-aware occupancy without learning a 3D model. The pipeline can also generate pseudo-labels for training downstream models.
AI.result: On Occ3D-nuScenes, the train-free pipeline achieves 16.9 mIoU and 16.5 RayIoU, comparable to state-of-the-art weakly supervised methods. As a pseudo-labeler, it yields 21.1 RayIoU, surpassing the previous weakly supervised SOTA. For panoptic occupancy, it sets new baselines with 3.1 RayPQ (train-free) and 3.9 RayPQ (weakly supervised).
AI.conclusion: Foundation-model-driven, training-free perception is a practical route for 3D scene understanding from cameras, offering competitive performance without 3D supervision and producing high-quality pseudo-labels for improving downstream models.

title: A Semi-Supervised Framework for Breast Ultrasound Segmentation with Training-Free Pseudo-Label Generation and Label Refinement
summary:
Semi-supervised learning (SSL) has emerged as a promising paradigm for breast ultrasound (BUS) image segmentation, but it often suffers from unstable pseudo labels under extremely limited annotations, leading to inaccurate supervision and degraded performance. Recent vision-language models (VLMs) provide a new opportunity for pseudo-label generation, yet their effectiveness on BUS images remains limited because domain-specific prompts are difficult to transfer.
  To address this issue, we propose a semi-supervised framework with training-free pseudo-label generation and label refinement. By leveraging simple appearance-based descriptions (e.g., dark oval), our method enables cross-domain structural transfer between natural and medical images, allowing VLMs to generate structurally consistent pseudo labels. These pseudo labels are used to warm up a static teacher that captures global structural priors of breast lesions. Combined with an exponential moving average teacher, we further introduce uncertainty entropy weighted fusion and adaptive uncertainty-guided reverse contrastive learning to improve boundary discrimination.
  Experiments on four BUS datasets demonstrate that our method achieves performance comparable to fully supervised models even with only 2.5% labeled data, significantly outperforming existing SSL approaches. Moreover, the proposed paradigm is readily extensible: for other imaging modalities or diseases, only a global appearance description is required to obtain reliable pseudo supervision, enabling scalable semi-supervised medical image segmentation under limited annotations.

AI.tldr: Training-free VLM-driven pseudo labels from simple appearance prompts warm up a static teacher; combined with an EMA teacher, uncertainty-weighted fusion, and uncertainty-guided reverse contrastive learning, the method delivers near–fully supervised BUS segmentation with only 2.5% labels and generalizes via minimal, modality-agnostic prompts.
AI.motivation: Semi-supervised BUS segmentation is hampered by noisy/unstable pseudo labels when annotations are scarce. Although VLMs could help generate pseudo labels, domain-specific prompt transfer to medical images is weak. There is a need for reliable, scalable pseudo supervision that works with very limited labels and can generalize across modalities/diseases.
AI.method: 1) Use simple, appearance-based prompts (e.g., "dark oval") to elicit structurally consistent, training-free pseudo labels from VLMs, enabling cross-domain structural transfer. 2) Use these pseudo labels to warm up a static teacher that captures global lesion structure. 3) Employ an EMA teacher in tandem and fuse teacher outputs via uncertainty-entropy weighting. 4) Apply adaptive, uncertainty-guided reverse contrastive learning to enhance boundary discrimination.
AI.result: Across four BUS datasets, the approach achieves performance comparable to fully supervised training while using only 2.5% labeled data, and it significantly outperforms existing semi-supervised baselines.
AI.conclusion: Simple, transferable appearance prompts enable VLMs to produce useful pseudo labels for medical images without training. Coupled with a dual-teacher setup and uncertainty-aware fusion/contrastive learning, this yields robust BUS segmentation under extreme label scarcity and can be readily extended to other modalities/diseases with a single global appearance description.

title: JOPP-3D: Joint Open Vocabulary Semantic Segmentation on Point Clouds and Panoramas
summary: Semantic segmentation across visual modalities such as 3D point clouds and panoramic images remains a challenging task, primarily due to the scarcity of annotated data and the limited adaptability of fixed-label models. In this paper, we present JOPP-3D, an open-vocabulary semantic segmentation framework that jointly leverages panoramic and point cloud data to enable language-driven scene understanding. We convert RGB-D panoramic images into their corresponding tangential perspective images and 3D point clouds, then use these modalities to extract and align foundational vision-language features. This allows natural language querying to generate semantic masks on both input modalities. Experimental evaluation on the Stanford-2D-3D-s and ToF-360 datasets demonstrates the capability of JOPP-3D to produce coherent and semantically meaningful segmentations across panoramic and 3D domains. Our proposed method achieves a significant improvement compared to the SOTA in open and closed vocabulary 2D and 3D semantic segmentation.

AI.tldr: JOPP-3D is an open-vocabulary segmentation framework that aligns vision–language features across RGB-D panoramas (via tangential perspective views) and 3D point clouds, enabling natural-language queries to produce masks in both modalities and outperforming prior work on Stanford-2D-3D-s and ToF-360.
AI.motivation: Semantic segmentation across panoramic images and 3D point clouds is limited by scarce annotations and fixed-label models that cannot adapt to arbitrary categories; there is a need for language-driven, cross-modal understanding that generalizes beyond closed vocabularies.
AI.method: Convert RGB-D panoramic images into tangential perspective images and corresponding 3D point clouds. Extract foundational vision–language features from both modalities and learn to align them so that text queries map to consistent semantic embeddings across 2D and 3D. Use these aligned features to generate segmentation masks for arbitrary text prompts on both the panoramic and point-cloud inputs.
AI.result: On Stanford-2D-3D-s and ToF-360, JOPP-3D generates coherent, semantically meaningful masks in both 2D panoramic and 3D domains and achieves significant improvements over state-of-the-art methods for both open- and closed-vocabulary 2D/3D segmentation.
AI.conclusion: Joint panoramic–point-cloud, language-aligned feature learning enables robust open-vocabulary segmentation across modalities, advancing language-driven scene understanding and reducing dependence on fixed label sets and dense annotations.

title: Optimizing 3D Diffusion Models for Medical Imaging via Multi-Scale Reward Learning
summary: Diffusion models have emerged as powerful tools for 3D medical image generation, yet bridging the gap between standard training objectives and clinical relevance remains a challenge. This paper presents a method to enhance 3D diffusion models using Reinforcement Learning (RL) with multi-scale feedback. We first pretrain a 3D diffusion model on MRI volumes to establish a robust generative prior. Subsequently, we fine-tune the model using Proximal Policy Optimization (PPO), guided by a novel reward system that integrates both 2D slice-wise assessments and 3D volumetric analysis. This combination allows the model to simultaneously optimize for local texture details and global structural coherence. We validate our framework on the BraTS 2019 and OASIS-1 datasets. Our results indicate that incorporating RL feedback effectively steers the generation process toward higher quality distributions. Quantitative analysis reveals significant improvements in Fréchet Inception Distance (FID) and, crucially, the synthetic data demonstrates enhanced utility in downstream tumor and disease classification tasks compared to non-optimized baselines.

AI.tldr: They fine-tune a pretrained 3D MRI diffusion model with PPO-based reinforcement learning using multi-scale rewards (2D slice and 3D volume) to better capture local texture and global structure, yielding improved FID and superior downstream tumor/disease classification utility over non-RL baselines on BraTS 2019 and OASIS-1.
AI.motivation: Standard diffusion training objectives do not directly optimize for clinically relevant properties; medical synthesis must balance fine-grained slice-level detail with coherent 3D anatomy and be demonstrably useful for downstream diagnostic tasks.
AI.method: Pretrain a 3D diffusion model on MRI volumes to establish a generative prior, then fine-tune it via PPO. The reward integrates 2D slice-wise assessments and 3D volumetric analysis to jointly optimize local textures and global structural coherence. Validation is conducted on BraTS 2019 and OASIS-1.
AI.result: RL fine-tuning steers the generator toward higher-quality distributions, significantly improving FID. Synthetic images from the RL-tuned model provide greater benefits for downstream tumor and disease classification compared to non-optimized baselines.
AI.conclusion: Multi-scale RL feedback effectively aligns 3D diffusion models with clinical relevance, improving both perceptual/structural quality and practical utility for classification tasks; reward-driven alignment is a promising direction for medical image synthesis.

title: Making Training-Free Diffusion Segmentors Scale with the Generative Power
summary: As powerful generative models, text-to-image diffusion models have recently been explored for discriminative tasks. A line of research focuses on adapting a pre-trained diffusion model to semantic segmentation without any further training, leading to what training-free diffusion segmentors. These methods typically rely on cross-attention maps from the model's attention layers, which are assumed to capture semantic relationships between image pixels and text tokens. Ideally, such approaches should benefit from more powerful diffusion models, i.e., stronger generative capability should lead to better segmentation. However, we observe that existing methods often fail to scale accordingly. To understand this issue, we identify two underlying gaps: (i) cross-attention is computed across multiple heads and layers, but there exists a discrepancy between these individual attention maps and a unified global representation. (ii) Even when a global map is available, it does not directly translate to accurate semantic correlation for segmentation, due to score imbalances among different text tokens. To bridge these gaps, we propose two techniques: auto aggregation and per-pixel rescaling, which together enable training-free segmentation to better leverage generative capability. We evaluate our approach on standard semantic segmentation benchmarks and further integrate it into a generative technique, demonstrating both improved performance broad applicability. Codes are at https://github.com/Darkbblue/goca.

AI.tldr: Training-free semantic segmentation with text-to-image diffusion models often fails to improve with stronger generators. The paper identifies aggregation and token-imbalance issues in cross-attention maps and proposes auto aggregation and per-pixel rescaling to produce better global semantic maps, yielding improved segmentation across benchmarks and within a generative pipeline.
AI.motivation: Leverage increasingly powerful text-to-image diffusion models for semantic segmentation without additional training, but current training-free approaches do not scale with model strength due to fragmented multi-head/layer attention and imbalanced token scores.
AI.method: Diagnose two gaps: (i) inconsistency between multi-head/multi-layer cross-attention maps and a unified global representation; (ii) global maps still misrepresent semantics due to token score imbalance. Propose two training-free techniques: (1) auto aggregation to combine attention across heads/layers into a coherent global map; (2) per-pixel rescaling to normalize token contributions and correct score imbalance. The approach is plug-and-play on pre-trained diffusion models.
AI.result: On standard semantic segmentation benchmarks, the proposed techniques significantly improve performance over prior training-free diffusion segmentors and scale better with stronger diffusion backbones. The method also integrates into a generative technique, showing broader applicability. Code is released.
AI.conclusion: Addressing attention aggregation and token imbalance enables training-free diffusion-based segmentation to more effectively harness generative capacity, improving accuracy and scalability while remaining model-agnostic and easy to integrate.

title: Towards Motion Turing Test: Evaluating Human-Likeness in Humanoid Robots
summary: Humanoid robots have achieved significant progress in motion generation and control, exhibiting movements that appear increasingly natural and human-like. Inspired by the Turing Test, we propose the Motion Turing Test, a framework that evaluates whether human observers can discriminate between humanoid robot and human poses using only kinematic information. To facilitate this evaluation, we present the Human-Humanoid Motion (HHMotion) dataset, which consists of 1,000 motion sequences spanning 15 action categories, performed by 11 humanoid models and 10 human subjects. All motion sequences are converted into SMPL-X representations to eliminate the influence of visual appearance. We recruited 30 annotators to rate the human-likeness of each pose on a 0-5 scale, resulting in over 500 hours of annotation. Analysis of the collected data reveals that humanoid motions still exhibit noticeable deviations from human movements, particularly in dynamic actions such as jumping, boxing, and running. Building on HHMotion, we formulate a human-likeness evaluation task that aims to automatically predict human-likeness scores from motion data. Despite recent progress in multimodal large language models, we find that they remain inadequate for assessing motion human-likeness. To address this, we propose a simple baseline model and demonstrate that it outperforms several contemporary LLM-based methods. The dataset, code, and benchmark will be publicly released to support future research in the community.

AI.tldr: They introduce a “Motion Turing Test” and a new HHMotion dataset to judge how human-like humanoid motions are, using SMPL-X kinematics to remove appearance cues. Human raters show that current humanoid motions still diverge from human movement—especially in dynamic actions. A simple baseline model predicts human-likeness better than several recent LLM-based approaches. Dataset, code, and benchmark will be released.
AI.motivation: Humanoid motion looks increasingly natural, but there is no standardized, appearance-agnostic way to quantify how human-like it truly is. Inspired by the Turing Test, the authors seek a principled, human-referenced evaluation and a benchmark to compare models and drive progress, avoiding confounds from visual appearance.
AI.method: 1) Build HHMotion: 1,000 motion sequences across 15 actions from 11 humanoid robots and 10 humans, all retargeted to SMPL-X. 2) Collect large-scale human judgments: 30 annotators rate pose human-likeness (0–5), totaling 500+ hours. 3) Analyze where robots differ from humans. 4) Define an automatic human-likeness prediction task and test multimodal LLMs. 5) Propose a simple baseline model tailored to motion data.
AI.result: Humans reliably detect deviations between humanoid and human motion; gaps are largest for dynamic actions (jumping, boxing, running). Multimodal LLMs underperform on predicting human-likeness scores, while a simple motion-focused baseline achieves better accuracy than several LLM-based methods.
AI.conclusion: Even advanced humanoid motions are measurably non-human-like, particularly for high-dynamics tasks. Human-likeness assessment benefits from motion-specific models rather than current MLLMs. The released dataset and benchmark establish a practical, appearance-agnostic standard to guide future motion generation and control research.

title: SpaCRD: Multimodal Deep Fusion of Histology and Spatial Transcriptomics for Cancer Region Detection
summary: Accurate detection of cancer tissue regions (CTR) enables deeper analysis of the tumor microenvironment and offers crucial insights into treatment response. Traditional CTR detection methods, which typically rely on the rich cellular morphology in histology images, are susceptible to a high rate of false positives due to morphological similarities across different tissue regions. The groundbreaking advances in spatial transcriptomics (ST) provide detailed cellular phenotypes and spatial localization information, offering new opportunities for more accurate cancer region detection. However, current methods are unable to effectively integrate histology images with ST data, especially in the context of cross-sample and cross-platform/batch settings for accomplishing the CTR detection. To address this challenge, we propose SpaCRD, a transfer learning-based method that deeply integrates histology images and ST data to enable reliable CTR detection across diverse samples, platforms, and batches. Once trained on source data, SpaCRD can be readily generalized to accurately detect cancerous regions across samples from different platforms and batches. The core of SpaCRD is a category-regularized variational reconstruction-guided bidirectional cross-attention fusion network, which enables the model to adaptively capture latent co-expression patterns between histological features and gene expression from multiple perspectives. Extensive benchmark analysis on 23 matched histology-ST datasets spanning various disease types, platforms, and batches demonstrates that SpaCRD consistently outperforms existing eight state-of-the-art methods in CTR detection.

AI.tldr: SpaCRD is a transfer-learning framework that fuses histology images with spatial transcriptomics via a category-regularized, variational, bidirectional cross‑attention network to robustly detect cancer tissue regions across samples, platforms, and batches, outperforming eight state-of-the-art methods on 23 datasets.
AI.motivation: Histology-only cancer tissue region (CTR) detection suffers high false positives due to morphological similarity across tissues. Spatial transcriptomics provides cell-type/phenotype and spatial context but is hard to integrate effectively with images, particularly for cross-sample, cross-platform, and cross-batch generalization in CTR detection.
AI.method: SpaCRD employs transfer learning to integrate histology image features with spatial transcriptomics. Its core is a category-regularized, variational reconstruction–guided, bidirectional cross-attention fusion network that learns latent co-expression patterns linking image morphology and gene expression. The model is trained on source datasets and then transferred to target datasets from different platforms/batches without retraining or with minimal adaptation.
AI.result: Across 23 matched histology–ST datasets spanning multiple diseases, platforms, and batches, SpaCRD consistently outperforms eight contemporary state-of-the-art CTR detection methods, demonstrating superior accuracy and robustness and strong cross-sample/platform generalization.
AI.conclusion: Integrating images with ST via SpaCRD yields more reliable, generalizable CTR detection, enabling improved tumor microenvironment analyses and potentially better assessment of treatment response across varied clinical and technical settings.

title: Adaptive Language-Aware Image Reflection Removal Network
summary: Existing image reflection removal methods struggle to handle complex reflections. Accurate language descriptions can help the model understand the image content to remove complex reflections. However, due to blurred and distorted interferences in reflected images, machine-generated language descriptions of the image content are often inaccurate, which harms the performance of language-guided reflection removal. To address this, we propose the Adaptive Language-Aware Network (ALANet) to remove reflections even with inaccurate language inputs. Specifically, ALANet integrates both filtering and optimization strategies. The filtering strategy reduces the negative effects of language while preserving its benefits, whereas the optimization strategy enhances the alignment between language and visual features. ALANet also utilizes language cues to decouple specific layer content from feature maps, improving its ability to handle complex reflections. To evaluate the model's performance under complex reflections and varying levels of language accuracy, we introduce the Complex Reflection and Language Accuracy Variance (CRLAV) dataset. Experimental results demonstrate that ALANet surpasses state-of-the-art methods for image reflection removal. The code and dataset are available at https://github.com/fashyon/ALANet.

AI.tldr: ALANet is a language-guided reflection removal model robust to inaccurate captions, combining filtering and optimization to align text–vision features, using language cues to decouple layer content, and evaluated on a new CRLAV dataset, achieving state-of-the-art results.
AI.motivation: Complex reflections are hard for existing removal methods. While language guidance can help, captions generated from reflection-contaminated images are often inaccurate, which degrades language-guided methods.
AI.method: Propose Adaptive Language-Aware Network (ALANet) that: (1) applies a filtering strategy to suppress harmful language signals while retaining useful guidance; (2) applies an optimization strategy to better align linguistic and visual features; and (3) leverages language cues to decouple specific layer content from feature maps to better isolate reflections. Introduce CRLAV dataset to test varying reflection complexity and language accuracy.
AI.result: Across experiments on complex reflection scenarios and under varying language accuracy, ALANet outperforms prior state-of-the-art reflection removal methods.
AI.conclusion: By adaptively filtering and optimizing language–vision interactions and decoupling features with language cues, ALANet remains effective even with noisy captions. The new CRLAV dataset supports evaluation of robustness; code and data are released.

title: Point-Supervised Skeleton-Based Human Action Segmentation
summary: Skeleton-based temporal action segmentation is a fundamental yet challenging task, playing a crucial role in enabling intelligent systems to perceive and respond to human activities. While fully-supervised methods achieve satisfactory performance, they require costly frame-level annotations and are sensitive to ambiguous action boundaries. To address these issues, we introduce a point-supervised framework for skeleton-based action segmentation, where only a single frame per action segment is labeled. We leverage multimodal skeleton data, including joint, bone, and motion information, encoded via a pretrained unified model to extract rich feature representations. To generate reliable pseudo-labels, we propose a novel prototype similarity method and integrate it with two existing methods: energy function and constrained K-Medoids clustering. Multimodal pseudo-label integration is proposed to enhance the reliability of the pseudo-label and guide the model training. We establish new benchmarks on PKU-MMD (X-Sub and X-View), MCFS-22, and MCFS-130, and implement baselines for point-supervised skeleton-based human action segmentation. Extensive experiments show that our method achieves competitive performance, even surpassing some fully-supervised methods while significantly reducing annotation effort.

AI.tldr: Proposes a point-supervised skeleton-based temporal action segmentation framework that leverages multimodal skeleton encodings and prototype-driven pseudo-labeling, achieving competitive or better-than-fully-supervised results with far less annotation.
AI.motivation: Frame-level annotations for temporal action segmentation are expensive and error-prone near ambiguous action boundaries. The goal is to cut annotation cost by labeling only one frame per action segment while maintaining or improving performance.
AI.method: Encode multimodal skeleton inputs (joint, bone, motion) with a pretrained unified model. Generate reliable pseudo-labels by a new prototype similarity approach and fuse it with two existing strategies (energy-based labeling and constrained K-Medoids clustering). Integrate pseudo-labels across modalities to supervise model training.
AI.result: On PKU-MMD (X-Sub, X-View), MCFS-22, and MCFS-130, the approach sets new point-supervised baselines and delivers competitive performance, in some cases surpassing fully-supervised methods, while requiring only one labeled frame per segment.
AI.conclusion: Point-supervision combined with multimodal encoding and fused pseudo-label generation is effective for skeleton-based temporal action segmentation, reducing annotation burden and mitigating boundary ambiguity while maintaining high accuracy.

title: VG3S: Visual Geometry Grounded Gaussian Splatting for Semantic Occupancy Prediction
summary: 3D semantic occupancy prediction has become a crucial perception task for comprehensive scene understanding in autonomous driving. While recent advances have explored 3D Gaussian splatting for occupancy modeling to substantially reduce computational overhead, the generation of high-quality 3D Gaussians relies heavily on accurate geometric cues, which are often insufficient in purely vision-centric paradigms. To bridge this gap, we advocate for injecting the strong geometric grounding capability from Vision Foundation Models (VFMs) into occupancy prediction. In this regard, we introduce Visual Geometry Grounded Gaussian Splatting (VG3S), a novel framework that empowers Gaussian-based occupancy prediction with cross-view 3D geometric grounding. Specifically, to fully exploit the rich 3D geometric priors from a frozen VFM, we propose a plug-and-play hierarchical geometric feature adapter, which can effectively transform generic VFM tokens via feature aggregation, task-specific alignment, and multi-scale restructuring. Extensive experiments on the nuScenes occupancy benchmark demonstrate that VG3S achieves remarkable improvements of 12.6% in IoU and 7.5% in mIoU over the baseline. Furthermore, we show that VG3S generalizes seamlessly across diverse VFMs, consistently enhancing occupancy prediction accuracy and firmly underscoring the immense value of integrating priors derived from powerful, pre-trained geometry-grounded VFMs.

AI.tldr: VG3S injects geometry priors from frozen Vision Foundation Models into Gaussian-splatting-based 3D semantic occupancy prediction via a hierarchical feature adapter, yielding large gains on nuScenes (+12.6 IoU, +7.5 mIoU) and generalizing across different VFMs.
AI.motivation: Gaussian splatting can model 3D occupancy efficiently but depends on accurate geometry, which purely vision-centric pipelines often lack. Vision Foundation Models possess strong, transferable geometric cues; the paper aims to harness these priors to improve occupancy quality without expensive geometric sensors or heavy computation.
AI.method: Proposes Visual Geometry Grounded Gaussian Splatting (VG3S). A frozen geometry-grounded VFM provides rich tokens; a plug-and-play hierarchical geometric feature adapter aggregates features, aligns them to the occupancy task, and restructures them across scales. These adapted features provide cross-view 3D geometric grounding to guide Gaussian-based occupancy prediction.
AI.result: On the nuScenes occupancy benchmark, VG3S improves IoU by 12.6% and mIoU by 7.5% over a Gaussian-splatting baseline. The approach works with multiple VFMs, consistently boosting accuracy, indicating strong cross-backbone generalization.
AI.conclusion: Integrating pre-trained, geometry-grounded VFMs into Gaussian-splatting occupancy prediction via a lightweight adapter substantially enhances 3D semantic occupancy quality and generalizes across VFMs, underscoring the value of leveraging strong geometric priors from large pre-trained models.

title: EntON: Eigenentropy-Optimized Neighborhood Densification in 3D Gaussian Splatting
comment: Submitted to ISPRS Journal of Photogrammetry and Remote Sensing on 20 February 2026
summary: We present a novel Eigenentropy-optimized neighboorhood densification strategy EntON in 3D Gaussian Splatting (3DGS) for geometrically accurate and high-quality rendered 3D reconstruction. While standard 3DGS produces Gaussians whose centers and surfaces are poorly aligned with the underlying object geometry, surface-focused reconstruction methods frequently sacrifice photometric accuracy. In contrast to the conventional densification strategy, which relies on the magnitude of the view-space position gradient, our approach introduces a geometry-aware strategy to guide adaptive splitting and pruning. Specifically, we compute the 3D shape feature Eigenentropy from the eigenvalues of the covariance matrix in the k-nearest neighborhood of each Gaussian center, which quantifies the local structural order. These Eigenentropy values are integrated into an alternating optimization framework: During the optimization process, the algorithm alternates between (i) standard gradient-based densification, which refines regions via view-space gradients, and (ii) Eigenentropy-aware densification, which preferentially densifies Gaussians in low-Eigenentropy (ordered, flat) neighborhoods to better capture fine geometric details on the object surface, and prunes those in high-Eigenentropy (disordered, spherical) regions. We provide quantitative and qualitative evaluations on two benchmark datasets: small-scale DTU dataset and large-scale TUM2TWIN dataset, covering man-made objects and urban scenes. Experiments demonstrate that our Eigenentropy-aware alternating densification strategy improves geometric accuracy by up to 33% and rendering quality by up to 7%, while reducing the number of Gaussians by up to 50% and training time by up to 23%. Overall, EnTON achieves a favorable balance between geometric accuracy, rendering quality and efficiency by avoiding unnecessary scene expansion.

AI.tldr: EntON introduces an Eigenentropy-guided, alternating densification strategy for 3D Gaussian Splatting that aligns Gaussians with true surface geometry, improving geometry and rendering while cutting model size and training time.
AI.motivation: Standard 3DGS densification based solely on view-space gradients often yields Gaussians misaligned with object surfaces, whereas strictly surface-focused methods harm photometric fidelity. A method is needed to balance geometric accuracy, rendering quality, and efficiency by guiding where to densify or prune Gaussians using local 3D structure cues.
AI.method: Compute a 3D shape feature called Eigenentropy from eigenvalues of the covariance matrix in each Gaussian’s k-NN neighborhood to quantify local structural order (low for flat/ordered, high for spherical/disordered). Alternate between: (i) standard gradient-based densification to refine by view-space position gradients, and (ii) Eigenentropy-aware densification that preferentially splits in low-Eigenentropy (surface-like) regions and prunes in high-Eigenentropy (volumetric/clutter) regions. This yields geometry-aware adaptive splitting and pruning that avoids unnecessary scene expansion.
AI.result: On DTU (small-scale objects) and TUM2TWIN (large urban scenes), EntON achieves up to 33% improvement in geometric accuracy and up to 7% better rendering quality, while reducing Gaussians by up to 50% and training time by up to 23%, with both quantitative and qualitative gains reported.
AI.conclusion: Eigenentropy-aware alternating densification yields better geometric alignment of Gaussians without sacrificing photometric quality and improves efficiency by curbing superfluous Gaussians. EntON presents a favorable trade-off across accuracy, image quality, and compute by integrating geometry cues into 3DGS densification.

title: Word-Anchored Temporal Forgery Localization
summary: Current temporal forgery localization (TFL) approaches typically rely on temporal boundary regression or continuous frame-level anomaly detection paradigms to derive candidate forgery proposals. However, they suffer not only from feature granularity misalignment but also from costly computation. To address these issues, we propose word-anchored temporal forgery localization (WAFL), a novel paradigm that shifts the TFL task from temporal regression and continuous localization to discrete word-level binary classification. Specifically, we first analyze the essence of temporal forgeries and identify the minimum meaningful forgery units, word tokens, and then align data preprocessing with the natural linguistic boundaries of speech. To adapt powerful pre-trained foundation backbones for feature extraction, we introduce the forensic feature realignment (FFR) module, mapping representations from the pre-trained semantic space to a discriminative forensic manifold. This allows subsequent lightweight linear classifiers to efficiently perform binary classification and accomplish the TFL task. Furthermore, to overcome the extreme class imbalance inherent to forgery detection, we design the artifact-centric asymmetric (ACA) loss, which breaks the standard precision-recall trade-off by dynamically suppressing overwhelming authentic gradients while asymmetrically prioritizing subtle forensic artifacts. Extensive experiments demonstrate that WAFL significantly outperforms state-of-the-art approaches in localization performance under both in- and cross-dataset settings, while requiring substantially fewer learnable parameters and operating at high computational efficiency.

AI.tldr: WAFL reframes temporal forgery localization from boundary regression/continuous scoring to discrete word-level binary classification, realigns pretrained features for forensics (FFR), and uses an artifact-centric asymmetric loss (ACA) to handle class imbalance—achieving better localization than SOTA with fewer parameters and higher efficiency.
AI.motivation: Existing TFL methods depend on temporal boundary regression or frame-level anomaly curves, which cause granularity misalignment with linguistic units, are computationally heavy, and struggle under severe class imbalance. The authors aim to align analysis with meaningful speech units and make detection both more accurate and efficient.
AI.method: 1) Define word tokens as the minimal meaningful forgery units and segment audio/video along natural linguistic boundaries. 2) Use a Forensic Feature Realignment (FFR) module to map powerful pretrained semantic features into a discriminative forensic manifold. 3) Apply lightweight linear classifiers to perform word-level binary classification. 4) Introduce Artifact-Centric Asymmetric (ACA) loss that down-weights dominant authentic gradients while emphasizing subtle forgery artifacts to mitigate extreme imbalance and improve precision-recall trade-offs.
AI.result: Across in-dataset and cross-dataset evaluations, WAFL significantly surpasses state-of-the-art TFL methods in localization accuracy, while using substantially fewer learnable parameters and achieving high computational efficiency.
AI.conclusion: Anchoring TFL to word-level units and realigning pretrained representations for forensic discrimination, combined with ACA loss, delivers superior, efficient, and robust localization performance, suggesting a practical shift from regression/continuous paradigms to discrete classification for temporal forgery tasks.

title: Low-latency Event-based Object Detection with Spatially-Sparse Linear Attention
summary: Event cameras provide sequential visual data with spatial sparsity and high temporal resolution, making them attractive for low-latency object detection. Existing asynchronous event-based neural networks realize this low-latency advantage by updating predictions event-by-event, but still suffer from two bottlenecks: recurrent architectures are difficult to train efficiently on long sequences, and improving accuracy often increases per-event computation and latency. Linear attention is appealing in this setting because it supports parallel training and recurrent inference. However, standard linear attention updates a global state for every event, yielding a poor accuracy-efficiency trade-off, which is problematic for object detection, where fine-grained representations and thus states are preferred. The key challenge is therefore to introduce sparse state activation that exploits event sparsity while preserving efficient parallel training. We propose Spatially-Sparse Linear Attention (SSLA), which introduces a mixture-of-spaces state decomposition and a scatter-compute-gather training procedure, enabling state-level sparsity as well as training parallelism. Built on SSLA, we develop an end-to-end asynchronous linear attention model, SSLA-Det, for event-based object detection. On Gen1 and N-Caltech101, SSLA-Det achieves state-of-the-art accuracy among asynchronous methods, reaching 0.375 mAP and 0.515 mAP, respectively, while reducing per-event computation by more than 20 times compared to the strongest prior asynchronous baseline, demonstrating the potential of linear attention for low-latency event-based vision.

AI.tldr: They introduce Spatially-Sparse Linear Attention (SSLA), a linear-attention framework that updates only relevant spatial state per event, enabling parallel training and recurrent, low-latency inference. Their detector (SSLA-Det) attains state-of-the-art accuracy on Gen1 (0.375 mAP) and N-Caltech101 (0.515 mAP) while cutting per-event compute by over 20× versus the strongest prior asynchronous baseline.
AI.motivation: Event-based object detection needs low latency and fine-grained representations. Existing asynchronous recurrent models are hard to train on long sequences and improving accuracy raises per-event cost and latency. Linear attention allows parallel training and recurrent inference, but standard forms update a single global state for every event—inefficient and too coarse for detection. The challenge is to exploit event sparsity via sparse state activation without sacrificing training parallelism.
AI.method: Propose Spatially-Sparse Linear Attention (SSLA): (1) mixture-of-spaces state decomposition to partition the model’s state into spatial sub-states so only relevant portions are updated per event; (2) a scatter-compute-gather training procedure that preserves parallelism while enabling state-level sparsity. Build SSLA-Det, an end-to-end asynchronous detector using SSLA for event-by-event inference.
AI.result: SSLA-Det achieves state-of-the-art accuracy among asynchronous methods on two benchmarks: 0.375 mAP on Gen1 and 0.515 mAP on N-Caltech101, while reducing per-event computation by more than 20× compared to the strongest prior asynchronous baseline.
AI.conclusion: Sparse state activation within linear attention reconciles accuracy with efficiency for event-based object detection, enabling parallel training and recurrent, low-latency inference. SSLA-Det demonstrates that linear attention, when made spatially sparse, is a strong fit for event-driven vision workloads.

title: Hierarchical Collaborative Fusion for 3D Instance-aware Referring Expression Segmentation
summary: Generalised 3D Referring Expression Segmentation (3D-GRES) localizes objects in 3D scenes based on natural language, even when descriptions match multiple or zero targets. Existing methods rely solely on sparse point clouds, lacking rich visual semantics for fine-grained descriptions. We propose HCF-RES, a multi-modal framework with two key innovations. First, Hierarchical Visual Semantic Decomposition leverages SAM instance masks to guide CLIP encoding at dual granularities -- pixel-level and instance-level features -- preserving object boundaries during 2D-to-3D projection. Second, Progressive Multi-level Fusion integrates representations through intra-modal collaboration, cross-modal adaptive weighting between 2D semantic and 3D geometric features, and language-guided refinement. HCF-RES achieves state-of-the-art results on both ScanRefer and Multi3DRefer.

AI.tldr: HCF-RES is a multi-modal 2D–3D framework for generalized 3D referring expression segmentation that fuses SAM-guided CLIP features (pixel- and instance-level) with progressive, language-guided fusion, achieving state-of-the-art results on ScanRefer and Multi3DRefer.
AI.motivation: Prior 3D-GRES approaches rely mainly on sparse point clouds, which lack rich visual semantics and fine object boundaries, making it difficult to ground nuanced natural-language descriptions—especially when expressions correspond to multiple or zero targets.
AI.method: Two key components: (1) Hierarchical Visual Semantic Decomposition uses SAM-derived instance masks to guide CLIP at dual granularities (pixel-level and instance-level), preserving object boundaries during 2D-to-3D projection; (2) Progressive Multi-level Fusion that performs intra-modal collaboration, adaptively weights 2D semantic vs. 3D geometric features across modalities, and applies language-guided refinement to integrate representations.
AI.result: Reports state-of-the-art performance on both ScanRefer and Multi3DRefer benchmarks (exact metrics not provided in the abstract).
AI.conclusion: Boundary-preserving 2D semantics from SAM/CLIP, when progressively fused with 3D geometry under language guidance, substantially improves generalized 3D referring expression segmentation, handling fine-grained and ambiguous (multi/zero target) cases better than prior work.

title: NOVA: Next-step Open-Vocabulary Autoregression for 3D Multi-Object Tracking in Autonomous Driving
comment: Code will be available at https://github.com/xifen523/NOVA
summary: Generalizing across unknown targets is critical for open-world perception, yet existing 3D Multi-Object Tracking (3D MOT) pipelines remain limited by closed-set assumptions and ``semantic-blind'' heuristics.
