To address this, we propose Next-step Open-Vocabulary Autoregression (NOVA), an innovative paradigm that shifts 3D tracking from traditional fragmented distance-based matching toward generative spatio-temporal semantic modeling. NOVA reformulates 3D trajectories as structured spatio-temporal semantic sequences, enabling the simultaneous encoding of physical motion continuity and deep linguistic priors. By leveraging the autoregressive capabilities of Large Language Models (LLMs), we transform the tracking task into a principled process of next-step sequence completion. This mechanism allows the model to explicitly utilize the hierarchical structure of language space to resolve fine-grained semantic ambiguities and maintain identity consistency across complex long-range sequences through high-level commonsense reasoning. Extensive experiments on nuScenes, V2X-Seq-SPD, and KITTI demonstrate the superior performance of NOVA. Notably, on the nuScenes dataset, NOVA achieves an AMOTA of 22.41% for Novel categories, yielding a significant 20.21% absolute improvement over the baseline. These gains are realized through a compact 0.5B autoregressive model. Code will be available at https://github.com/xifen523/NOVA.

AI.tldr: NOVA reframes 3D multi-object tracking as an open-vocabulary, next-step autoregressive sequence modeling problem that fuses motion dynamics with language semantics, yielding large gains on novel categories (nuScenes AMOTA 22.41%, +20.21 abs. over baseline) using a compact 0.5B model.
AI.motivation: Closed-set and heuristic matching in 3D MOT fail to generalize to unseen categories and lack semantic reasoning. Open-world perception needs trackers that can recognize and maintain identities across novel objects using richer priors than distance metrics.
AI.method: Represent each 3D trajectory as a structured spatio-temporal semantic sequence that jointly encodes motion continuity and linguistic cues. Use a large language model in an autoregressive manner to perform next-step sequence completion, leveraging hierarchical language structure and commonsense for disambiguation and identity maintenance over long horizons.
AI.result: Across nuScenes, V2X-Seq-SPD, and KITTI, NOVA outperforms baselines; on nuScenes it achieves 22.41% AMOTA for novel categories, a 20.21% absolute improvement. The approach operates with a 0.5B-parameter autoregressive model.
AI.conclusion: Generative spatio-temporal semantic modeling via LLM autoregression is a viable and effective paradigm for open-vocabulary 3D MOT, improving generalization to unseen categories and long-range identity consistency. Code release is planned, suggesting reproducibility and future extensions.

title: ODD-SEC: Onboard Drone Detection with a Spinning Event Camera
summary: The rapid proliferation of drones requires balancing innovation with regulation. To address security and privacy concerns, techniques for drone detection have attracted significant attention.Passive solutions, such as frame camera-based systems, offer versatility and energy efficiency under typical conditions but are fundamentally constrained by their operational principles in scenarios involving fast-moving targets or adverse illumination.Inspired by biological vision, event cameras asynchronously detect per-pixel brightness changes, offering high dynamic range and microsecond-level responsiveness that make them uniquely suited for drone detection in conditions beyond the reach of conventional frame-based cameras.However, the design of most existing event-based solutions assumes a static camera, greatly limiting their applicability to moving carriers--such as quadrupedal robots or unmanned ground vehicles--during field operations.In this paper, we introduce a real-time drone detection system designed for deployment on moving carriers. The system utilizes a spinning event-based camera, providing a 360° horizontal field of view and enabling bearing estimation of detected drones. A key contribution is a novel image-like event representation that operates without motion compensation, coupled with a lightweight neural network architecture for efficient spatiotemporal learning. Implemented on an onboard Jetson Orin NX, the system can operate in real time. Outdoor experimental results validate reliable detection with a mean angular error below 2° under challenging conditions, underscoring its suitability for real-world surveillance applications. We will open-source our complete pipeline to support future research.

AI.tldr: Real-time, event-camera-based drone detector for moving platforms using a spinning sensor to achieve 360° FOV and sub-2° bearing accuracy without motion compensation, running efficiently on a Jetson Orin NX.
AI.motivation: Conventional frame cameras struggle with fast targets and adverse illumination, and most event-based detectors assume a static camera—limiting deployment on mobile carriers amid rising needs for secure drone detection.
AI.method: Mount a spinning event-based camera to obtain a 360° horizontal field of view and estimate target bearing. Introduce an image-like event representation that forgoes motion compensation, paired with a lightweight neural network for spatiotemporal learning. Deploy the full pipeline on an embedded Jetson Orin NX for real-time inference.
AI.result: Outdoor experiments demonstrate reliable detection under challenging conditions with mean angular (bearing) error below 2°, and real-time operation on-board the Orin NX.
AI.conclusion: The approach is well-suited for mobile, real-world surveillance, extending event-based drone detection to moving carriers; the authors plan to open-source the full pipeline to catalyze further research.

title: Spectral and Trajectory Regularization for Diffusion Transformer Super-Resolution
summary: Diffusion transformer (DiT) architectures show great potential for real-world image super-resolution (Real-ISR). However, their computationally expensive iterative sampling necessitates one-step distillation. Existing one-step distillation methods struggle with Real-ISR on DiT. They suffer from fundamental trajectory mismatch and generate severe grid-like periodic artifacts. To tackle these challenges, we propose StrSR, a novel one-step adversarial distillation framework featuring spectral and trajectory regularization. Specifically, we propose an asymmetric discriminative distillation architecture to bridge the trajectory gap. Additionally, we design a frequency distribution matching strategy to effectively suppress DiT-specific periodic artifacts caused by high-frequency spectral leakage. Extensive experiments demonstrate that StrSR achieves state-of-the-art performance in Real-ISR, across both quantitative metrics and visual perception. The code and models will be released at https://github.com/jkwang28/StrSR .

AI.tldr: StrSR distills a diffusion transformer (DiT) into a single-step Real-ISR model using asymmetric adversarial distillation and frequency-spectrum matching, fixing trajectory mismatch and grid artifacts to reach state-of-the-art quality and perception.
AI.motivation: DiT models perform well for real-world image super-resolution but are impractically slow due to iterative sampling. One-step distillation is needed, yet existing approaches fail on DiT-based Real-ISR because the student’s sampling trajectory mismatches the teacher’s and because DiTs exhibit high-frequency spectral leakage that causes grid-like periodic artifacts.
AI.method: A one-step adversarial distillation framework with two core components: (1) an asymmetric discriminative distillation architecture that aligns the student’s generation trajectory with the teacher’s, reducing trajectory mismatch; and (2) a frequency distribution matching strategy that aligns high-frequency spectra to suppress DiT-specific periodic artifacts arising from spectral leakage. Together, these act as trajectory and spectral regularizers.
AI.result: Across extensive experiments, StrSR achieves state-of-the-art performance for Real-ISR on both quantitative benchmarks and perceptual quality, while greatly mitigating grid artifacts.
AI.conclusion: StrSR makes single-step, high-fidelity Real-ISR feasible for DiT models by addressing trajectory mismatch and spectral leakage. It delivers faster inference without sacrificing quality, and the authors will release code and models.

title: Can we Trust Unreliable Voxels? Exploring 3D Semantic Occupancy Prediction under Label Noise
comment: The benchmark and source code will be made publicly available at https://github.com/mylwx/OccNL
summary: 3D semantic occupancy prediction is a cornerstone of robotic perception, yet real-world voxel annotations are inherently corrupted by structural artifacts and dynamic trailing effects. This raises a critical but underexplored question: can autonomous systems safely rely on such unreliable occupancy supervision? To systematically investigate this issue, we establish OccNL, the first benchmark dedicated to 3D occupancy under occupancy-asymmetric and dynamic trailing noise. Our analysis reveals a fundamental domain gap: state-of-the-art 2D label noise learning strategies collapse catastrophically in sparse 3D voxel spaces, exposing a critical vulnerability in existing paradigms. To address this challenge, we propose DPR-Occ, a principled label noise-robust framework that constructs reliable supervision through dual-source partial label reasoning. By synergizing temporal model memory with representation-level structural affinity, DPR-Occ dynamically expands and prunes candidate label sets to preserve true semantics while suppressing noise propagation. Extensive experiments on SemanticKITTI demonstrate that DPR-Occ prevents geometric and semantic collapse under extreme corruption. Notably, even at 90% label noise, our method achieves significant performance gains (up to 2.57% mIoU and 13.91% IoU) over existing label noise learning baselines adapted to the 3D occupancy prediction task. By bridging label noise learning and 3D perception, OccNL and DPR-Occ provide a reliable foundation for safety-critical robotic perception in dynamic environments. The benchmark and source code will be made publicly available at https://github.com/mylwx/OccNL.

AI.tldr: They introduce OccNL, a benchmark exposing how noisy 3D voxel labels (structural artifacts, dynamic trails) break existing 2D label-noise methods, and propose DPR-Occ, a noise-robust framework that fuses temporal memory with structural affinity to maintain correct occupancy semantics. On SemanticKITTI, it resists collapse and outperforms adapted baselines even with 90% noisy labels.
AI.motivation: Real-world 3D occupancy supervision is highly unreliable due to asymmetric occupancy errors and motion-induced trailing artifacts, but current training pipelines assume clean labels. The field lacks a dedicated benchmark and robust methods, and 2D noise-learning ideas may not transfer to sparse 3D voxels. The authors ask whether autonomous systems can safely learn from such corrupted supervision and how to make learning robust.
AI.method: 1) Build OccNL, a benchmark simulating occupancy-asymmetric and dynamic trailing noise for 3D semantic occupancy. 2) Empirically show that state-of-the-art 2D label-noise approaches catastrophically fail in sparse 3D voxel settings. 3) Propose DPR-Occ: a dual-source partial label reasoning scheme that forms reliable supervision by combining temporal model memory with representation-level structural affinity; it dynamically expands/prunes candidate label sets to retain true semantics and curb noise propagation.
AI.result: On SemanticKITTI under extreme label corruption, DPR-Occ prevents geometric/semantic collapse and yields notable gains over adapted label-noise baselines; at 90% label noise, improvements reach up to +2.57% mIoU and +13.91% IoU.
AI.conclusion: OccNL exposes a critical domain gap for learning under noisy 3D occupancy labels, and DPR-Occ offers a principled, noise-robust solution. Together they advance safety-critical robotic perception in dynamic environments; code and benchmark will be released.

title: Attribute Distribution Modeling and Semantic-Visual Alignment for Generative Zero-shot Learning
summary: Generative zero-shot learning (ZSL) synthesizes features for unseen classes, leveraging semantic conditions to transfer knowledge from seen classes. However, it also introduces two intrinsic challenges: (1) class-level attributes fails to capture instance-specific visual appearances due to substantial intra-class variability, thus causing the class-instance gap; (2) the substantial mismatch between semantic and visual feature distributions, manifested in inter-class correlations, gives rise to the semantic-visual domain gap. To address these challenges, we propose an Attribute Distribution Modeling and Semantic-Visual Alignment (ADiVA) approach, jointly modeling attribute distributions and performing explicit semantic-visual alignment. Specifically, our ADiVA consists of two modules: an Attribute Distribution Modeling (ADM) module that learns a transferable attribute distribution for each class and samples instance-level attributes for unseen classes, and a Visual-Guided Alignment (VGA) module that refines semantic representations to better reflect visual structures. Experiments on three widely used benchmark datasets demonstrate that ADiVA significantly outperforms state-of-the-art methods (e.g., achieving gains of 4.7% and 6.1% on AWA2 and SUN, respectively). Moreover, our approach can serve as a plugin to enhance existing generative ZSL methods.

AI.tldr: ADiVA is a generative zero-shot learning approach that jointly models class-wise attribute distributions and explicitly aligns semantic and visual spaces via two modules (ADM and VGA), yielding state-of-the-art gains on standard benchmarks and serving as a plug-in to existing generative ZSL methods.
AI.motivation: Generative ZSL often relies on coarse, class-level attributes that overlook instance-level variability (class–instance gap) and suffers from a mismatch between semantic and visual feature distributions (semantic–visual domain gap). The paper seeks to mitigate both gaps to improve synthesis of unseen-class features and downstream recognition.
AI.method: ADiVA comprises: (1) Attribute Distribution Modeling (ADM), which learns a transferable attribute distribution per class from seen data and samples instance-level attributes for unseen classes; and (2) Visual-Guided Alignment (VGA), which refines/warps semantic representations to better match the structure of the visual feature space. These are integrated into a generative ZSL pipeline that synthesizes visual features for unseen classes conditioned on refined, instance-level attributes.
AI.result: On three standard ZSL benchmarks, ADiVA achieves significant improvements over prior SOTA, including +4.7% on AWA2 and +6.1% on SUN (metrics unspecified in abstract). The method also functions as a plug-in that boosts existing generative ZSL models.
AI.conclusion: Jointly modeling attribute distributions and enforcing semantic–visual alignment alleviates both class–instance and domain gaps in generative ZSL. ADiVA is empirically effective, generalizable as a plug-in, and advances SOTA on multiple datasets.

title: FlowMotion: Training-Free Flow Guidance for Video Motion Transfer
summary: Video motion transfer aims to generate a target video that inherits motion patterns from a source video while rendering new scenes. Existing training-free approaches focus on constructing motion guidance based on the intermediate outputs of pre-trained T2V models, which results in heavy computational overhead and limited flexibility. In this paper, we present FlowMotion, a novel training-free framework that enables efficient and flexible motion transfer by directly leveraging the predicted outputs of flow-based T2V models. Our key insight is that early latent predictions inherently encode rich temporal information. Motivated by this, we propose flow guidance, which extracts motion representations based on latent predictions to align motion patterns between source and generated videos. We further introduce a velocity regularization strategy to stabilize optimization and ensure smooth motion evolution. By operating purely on model predictions, FlowMotion achieves superior time and resource efficiency as well as competitive performance compared with state-of-the-art methods.

AI.tldr: FlowMotion is a training-free video motion transfer framework that exploits early latent predictions from flow-based T2V models to guide motion, adds velocity regularization for stability, and delivers competitive results with much better time/resource efficiency.
AI.motivation: Training-free motion transfer methods typically rely on intermediate outputs of pre-trained T2V models, incurring heavy computation and constraining flexibility. There is a need for a more efficient and adaptable way to transfer motion across videos without training.
AI.method: Directly leverage predicted outputs of flow-based T2V models. Key idea: early latent predictions encode rich temporal cues. Propose flow guidance to extract motion representations from these latent predictions to align source and target motion; introduce velocity regularization to stabilize optimization and ensure smooth motion evolution; operate purely on model predictions (no training).
AI.result: Achieves superior time and resource efficiency while maintaining competitive motion transfer quality compared to state-of-the-art training-free approaches (no specific metrics provided).
AI.conclusion: By harnessing temporal information in early latent predictions and regularizing motion velocity, FlowMotion offers an efficient, flexible, and training-free solution for video motion transfer that competes with SOTA quality while reducing computational overhead.

title: 3D CBCT Artefact Removal Using Perpendicular Score-Based Diffusion Models
summary: Cone-beam computed tomography (CBCT) is a widely used 3D imaging technique in dentistry, offering high-resolution images while minimising radiation exposure for patients. However, CBCT is highly susceptible to artefacts arising from high-density objects such as dental implants, which can compromise image quality and diagnostic accuracy. To reduce artefacts, implant inpainting in the sequence of projections plays a crucial role in many artefact reduction approaches. Recently, diffusion models have achieved state-of-the-art results in image generation and have widely been applied to image inpainting tasks. However, to our knowledge, existing diffusion-based methods for implant inpainting operate on independent 2D projections. This approach neglects the correlations among individual projections, resulting in inconsistencies in the reconstructed images. To address this, we propose a 3D dental implant inpainting approach based on perpendicular score-based diffusion models, each trained in two different planes and operating in the projection domain. The 3D distribution of the projection series is modelled by combining the two 2D score-based diffusion models in the sampling scheme. Our results demonstrate the method's effectiveness in producing high-quality, artefact-reduced 3D CBCT images, making it a promising solution for improving clinical imaging.

AI.tldr: They propose a projection-domain dental implant inpainting method for CBCT that enforces 3D consistency by combining two orthogonally trained score-based diffusion models, yielding artifact-reduced, higher-quality 3D reconstructions.
AI.motivation: CBCT images suffer from severe metal artifacts from dental implants. Existing diffusion-based inpainting operates on each 2D projection independently, ignoring inter-projection correlations and causing 3D inconsistencies after reconstruction. A method that respects 3D structure while working in the projection domain is needed.
AI.method: Train two 2D score-based diffusion models on perpendicular planes of the projection stack. During sampling, fuse the two models’ scores to approximate the 3D distribution of the full projection series and inpaint implant-affected regions in the projections, promoting cross-view consistency before reconstruction.
AI.result: The approach produces visually consistent, artifact-reduced 3D CBCT volumes from inpainted projections, outperforming independent 2D inpainting by reducing inconsistencies and improving image quality (no quantitative metrics reported in the abstract).
AI.conclusion: Modeling inter-projection dependencies via perpendicular score-based diffusion in the projection domain is effective for implant artifact reduction in CBCT, offering a promising path to more reliable clinical imaging.

title: Latent Transfer Attack: Adversarial Examples via Generative Latent Spaces
summary: Adversarial attacks are a central tool for probing the robustness of modern vision models, yet most methods optimize perturbations directly in pixel space under $\ell_\infty$ or $\ell_2$ constraints. While effective in white-box settings, pixel-space optimization often produces high-frequency, texture-like noise that is brittle to common preprocessing (e.g., resizing and cropping) and transfers poorly across architectures. We propose $\textbf{LTA}$ ($\textbf{L}$atent $\textbf{T}$ransfer $\textbf{A}$ttack), a transfer-based attack that instead optimizes perturbations in the latent space of a pretrained Stable Diffusion VAE. Given a clean image, we encode it into a latent code and optimize the latent representation to maximize a surrogate classifier loss, while softly enforcing a pixel-space $\ell_\infty$ budget after decoding. To improve robustness to resolution mismatch and standard input pipelines, we incorporate Expectation Over Transformations (EOT) via randomized resizing, interpolation, and cropping, and apply periodic latent Gaussian smoothing to suppress emerging artifacts and stabilize optimization. Across a suite of CNN and vision-transformer targets, LTA achieves strong transfer attack success while producing spatially coherent, predominantly low-frequency perturbations that differ qualitatively from pixel-space baselines and occupy a distinct point in the transfer-quality trade-off. Our results highlight pretrained generative latent spaces as an effective and structured domain for adversarial optimization, bridging robustness evaluation with modern generative priors.

AI.tldr: Introduces LTA, an adversarial attack that optimizes perturbations in the latent space of a Stable Diffusion VAE, enforcing a pixel-space l∞ budget after decoding. It yields spatially coherent, low-frequency, and more transferable perturbations than standard pixel-space attacks, especially under common input transformations.
AI.motivation: Pixel-space adversarial optimization typically produces brittle, high-frequency noise that breaks under resizing/cropping and transfers poorly across architectures. Robustness evaluations need stronger, more realistic transfer attacks that survive standard preprocessing and resolution changes. Leveraging generative model priors may produce structured, resilient perturbations.
AI.method: Encode a clean image into the latent of a pretrained Stable Diffusion VAE. Optimize the latent representation to maximize a surrogate classifier loss while softly constraining the decoded image to a pixel-space l∞ budget. Improve transformation robustness via EOT with randomized resizing, interpolation, and cropping. Apply periodic Gaussian smoothing in latent space to suppress artifacts and stabilize optimization.
AI.result: Across CNN and ViT targets, LTA achieves strong transfer success and robustness to resolution/input-pipeline variations. The resulting perturbations are spatially coherent and predominantly low-frequency, qualitatively distinct from pixel-space noise, and occupy a new point in the transfer–quality trade-off.
AI.conclusion: Pretrained generative latent spaces are effective domains for adversarial optimization, producing transferable, structured perturbations and suggesting tighter links between robustness evaluation and modern generative priors.

title: WMoE-CLIP: Wavelet-Enhanced Mixture-of-Experts Prompt Learning for Zero-Shot Anomaly Detection
summary: Vision-language models have recently shown strong generalization in zero-shot anomaly detection (ZSAD), enabling the detection of unseen anomalies without task-specific supervision. However, existing approaches typically rely on fixed textual prompts, which struggle to capture complex semantics, and focus solely on spatial-domain features, limiting their ability to detect subtle anomalies. To address these challenges, we propose a wavelet-enhanced mixture-of-experts prompt learning method for ZSAD. Specifically, a variational autoencoder is employed to model global semantic representations and integrate them into prompts to enhance adaptability to diverse anomaly patterns. Wavelet decomposition extracts multi-frequency image features that dynamically refine textual embeddings through cross-modal interactions. Furthermore, a semantic-aware mixture-of-experts module is introduced to aggregate contextual information. Extensive experiments on 14 industrial and medical datasets demonstrate the effectiveness of the proposed method.

AI.tldr: They introduce a wavelet-enhanced, mixture-of-experts prompt learning framework for zero-shot anomaly detection that adaptively builds text prompts using VAE-derived global semantics and refines them with multi-frequency visual cues, showing strong performance on 14 industrial and medical datasets.
AI.motivation: Fixed textual prompts in VLM-based ZSAD fail to capture complex, dataset-specific semantics, and reliance on only spatial-domain features hampers detection of subtle, fine-grained anomalies. The field needs adaptive prompt mechanisms and richer feature representations that include frequency information and contextual aggregation.
AI.method: Three key components: (1) a variational autoencoder learns global semantic representations of normal/abnormal patterns and conditions prompt generation to adapt to diverse anomalies; (2) wavelet decomposition extracts multi-frequency image features that dynamically refine textual embeddings through cross-modal interactions; (3) a semantic-aware mixture-of-experts aggregates contextual information to better align vision and language features for anomaly scoring. The framework plugs into a VLM backbone for zero-shot inference.
AI.result: Extensive experiments across 14 industrial and medical datasets indicate the proposed method is effective, presumably outperforming fixed-prompt and spatial-only baselines and improving sensitivity to subtle anomalies.
AI.conclusion: Adaptive, semantics-informed prompts combined with frequency-aware visual features and context-aggregating MoE improve zero-shot anomaly detection and generalize across domains, particularly for subtle and diverse anomaly patterns.

title: P-SLCR: Unsupervised Point Cloud Semantic Segmentation via Prototypes Structure Learning and Consistent Reasoning
summary: Current semantic segmentation approaches for point cloud scenes heavily rely on manual labeling, while research on unsupervised semantic segmentation methods specifically for raw point clouds is still in its early stages. Unsupervised point cloud learning poses significant challenges due to the absence of annotation information and the lack of pre-training. The development of effective strategies is crucial in this context. In this paper, we propose a novel prototype library-driven unsupervised point cloud semantic segmentation strategy that utilizes Structure Learning and Consistent Reasoning (P-SLCR). First, we propose a Consistent Structure Learning to establish structural feature learning between consistent points and the library of consistent prototypes by selecting high-quality features. Second, we propose a Semantic Relation Consistent Reasoning that constructs a prototype inter-relation matrix between consistent and ambiguous prototype libraries separately. This process ensures the preservation of semantic consistency by imposing constraints on consistent and ambiguous prototype libraries through the prototype inter-relation matrix. Finally, our method was extensively evaluated on the S3DIS, SemanticKITTI, and Scannet datasets, achieving the best performance compared to unsupervised methods. Specifically, the mIoU of 47.1% is achieved for Area-5 of the S3DIS dataset, surpassing the classical fully supervised method PointNet by 2.5%.

AI.tldr: P-SLCR is an unsupervised point cloud semantic segmentation framework that builds and reasons over prototype libraries to learn structure-consistent features, achieving state-of-the-art unsupervised results and even surpassing a classic supervised baseline on S3DIS Area-5.
AI.motivation: Manual annotation for point cloud segmentation is costly, and unsupervised learning on raw point clouds lacks labels and pretraining signals. The authors aim to uncover and enforce semantic structure without supervision by leveraging prototype representations and consistency constraints, addressing the gap in effective unsupervised strategies.
AI.method: Prototype library–driven framework (P-SLCR) with two key components: (1) Consistent Structure Learning selects high-quality features and aligns consistent points with a library of consistent prototypes to learn robust structural features; (2) Semantic Relation Consistent Reasoning builds prototype inter-relation matrices separately for consistent and ambiguous prototype libraries, imposing constraints that preserve semantic consistency and disambiguate categories via relational reasoning.
AI.result: Extensive evaluation on S3DIS, SemanticKITTI, and ScanNet shows best performance among unsupervised methods. On S3DIS Area-5, P-SLCR attains 47.1% mIoU, exceeding the classic fully supervised PointNet by 2.5%.
AI.conclusion: Prototype-based structure learning plus relation-consistent reasoning yields strong unsupervised point cloud segmentation, narrowing the gap with supervised methods and demonstrating that carefully designed prototype libraries and consistency constraints can extract meaningful semantics without labels.

title: WorldCache: Accelerating World Models for Free via Heterogeneous Token Caching
summary: Diffusion-based world models have shown strong potential for unified world simulation, but the iterative denoising remains too costly for interactive use and long-horizon rollouts. While feature caching can accelerate inference without training, we find that policies designed for single-modal diffusion transfer poorly to world models due to two world-model-specific obstacles: \emph{token heterogeneity} from multi-modal coupling and spatial variation, and \emph{non-uniform temporal dynamics} where a small set of hard tokens drives error growth, making uniform skipping either unstable or overly conservative. We propose \textbf{WorldCache}, a caching framework tailored to diffusion world models. We introduce \textit{Curvature-guided Heterogeneous Token Prediction}, which uses a physics-grounded curvature score to estimate token predictability and applies a Hermite-guided damped predictor for chaotic tokens with abrupt direction changes. We also design \textit{Chaotic-prioritized Adaptive Skipping}, which accumulates a curvature-normalized, dimensionless drift signal and recomputes only when bottleneck tokens begin to drift. Experiments on diffusion world models show that WorldCache delivers up to \textbf{3.7$\times$} end-to-end speedups while maintaining \textbf{98\%} rollout quality, demonstrating the vast advantages and practicality of WorldCache in resource-constrained scenarios. Our code is released in https://github.com/FofGofx/WorldCache.

AI.tldr: WorldCache accelerates diffusion-based world models by selectively reusing cached features and recomputing only for hard, drifting tokens, guided by a curvature-based predictability score and a Hermite-damped predictor. It yields up to 3.7× speedups with ~98% rollout quality, enabling more practical interactive and long-horizon simulation.
AI.motivation: Iterative denoising in diffusion world models is too slow for interactive control and long rollouts. Off-the-shelf caching/skipping policies from single-modal diffusion break down because (1) tokens differ widely across modalities and space (heterogeneity), and (2) a small set of unstable tokens drives error growth over time (non-uniform temporal dynamics), so uniform skipping is either unstable or overly conservative.
AI.method: WorldCache introduces two components: (1) Curvature-guided Heterogeneous Token Prediction uses a physics-inspired curvature score to estimate token predictability and applies a Hermite-guided damped predictor for chaotic tokens with abrupt direction changes; (2) Chaotic-prioritized Adaptive Skipping accumulates a curvature-normalized, dimensionless drift signal and triggers recomputation only when bottleneck tokens start to drift, enabling non-uniform, token-aware skipping/caching in diffusion rollouts.
AI.result: On diffusion-based world models, WorldCache achieves up to 3.7× end-to-end speedups while maintaining 98% rollout quality, suggesting stable, accurate long-horizon simulation under tighter resource budgets. Code is released for reproduction.
AI.conclusion: Token-aware, chaos-aware caching tailored to world models can substantially reduce diffusion inference cost without retraining while preserving rollout fidelity. Curvature-driven prediction and drift-triggered recomputation are key to safe acceleration and make diffusion world models more viable in resource-constrained and interactive settings.

title: LATO: 3D Mesh Flow Matching with Structured TOpology Preserving LAtents
summary: In this paper, we introduce LATO, a novel topology-preserving latent representation that enables scalable, flow matching-based synthesis of explicit 3D meshes. LATO represents a mesh as a Vertex Displacement Field (VDF) anchored on surface, incorporating a sparse voxel Variational Autoencoder (VAE) to compress this explicit signal into a structured, topology-aware voxel latent. To decapsulate the mesh, the VAE decoder progressively subdivides and prunes latent voxels to instantiate precise vertex locations. In the end, a dedicated connection head queries the voxel latent to predict edge connectivity between vertex pairs directly, allowing mesh topology to be recovered without isosurface extraction or heuristic meshing. For generative modeling, LATO adopts a two-stage flow matching process, first synthesizing the structure voxels and subsequently refining the voxel-wise topology features. Compared to prior isosurface/triangle-based diffusion models and autoregressive generation approaches, LATO generates meshes with complex geometry, well-formed topology while being highly efficient in inference.

AI.tldr: LATO is a topology-preserving latent representation and flow-matching framework that directly generates explicit 3D meshes by encoding a vertex displacement field into a sparse, topology-aware voxel latent, decoding precise vertices via progressive voxel subdivision/pruning, and predicting edges to recover mesh connectivity—achieving complex, well-formed meshes with efficient inference.
AI.motivation: Existing 3D generative models often rely on isosurface extraction or triangle-based diffusion and heuristic meshing, which can distort topology and be computationally heavy; autoregressive mesh generation is slow and struggles with complex, coherent topology. There is a need for a scalable, topology-aware latent that yields explicit meshes with reliable connectivity and efficient sampling.
AI.method: Represent meshes as a Vertex Displacement Field (VDF) anchored on surface. Encode this explicit signal into a structured voxel latent using a sparse-voxel VAE. The decoder progressively subdivides and prunes latent voxels to produce accurate vertex positions. A connection head queries the voxel latent to directly predict edge connectivity between vertex pairs, removing the need for isosurface extraction or heuristic meshing. For generation, employ two-stage flow matching: first synthesize structural voxels, then refine voxel-wise topology features.
AI.result: Compared to prior isosurface/triangle-based diffusion models and autoregressive approaches, LATO produces meshes with more complex geometry, better-formed topology, and significantly improved inference efficiency.
AI.conclusion: A topology-aware voxel latent coupled with progressive decoding and direct edge prediction enables scalable, high-quality explicit mesh synthesis without isosurface extraction. LATO establishes an efficient, accurate pathway for generative 3D mesh modeling and may serve as a foundation for future topology-preserving 3D generation systems.

title: Computer vision-based estimation of invertebrate biomass
summary: The ability to estimate invertebrate biomass using only images could help scaling up quantitative biodiversity monitoring efforts. Computer vision-based methods have the potential to omit the manual, time-consuming, and destructive process of dry weighing specimens. We present two approaches for dry mass estimation that do not require additional manual effort apart from imaging the specimens: fitting a linear model with novel predictors, automatically calculated by an imaging device, and training a family of end-to-end deep neural networks for the task, using single-view, multi-view, and metadata-aware architectures. We propose using area and sinking speed as predictors. These can be calculated with BIODISCOVER, which is a dual-camera system that captures image sequences of specimens sinking in an ethanol column. For this study, we collected a large dataset of dry mass measurement and image sequence pairs to train and evaluate models. We show that our methods can estimate specimen dry mass even with complex and visually diverse specimen morphologies. Combined with automatic taxonomic classification, our approach is an accurate method for group-level dry mass estimation, with a median percentage error of 10-20% for individuals. We highlight the importance of choosing appropriate evaluation metrics, and encourage using both percentage errors and absolute errors as metrics, because they measure different properties. We also explore different optimization losses, data augmentation methods, and model architectures for training deep-learning models.

AI.tldr: They show that invertebrate dry mass can be estimated from images alone—using simple features (area, sinking speed) or end-to-end deep learning—achieving roughly 10–20% median percentage error per individual and enabling accurate group-level biomass estimates.
AI.motivation: Scaling biodiversity monitoring needs fast, non-destructive, and automated biomass estimation to replace laborious, destructive dry weighing of specimens.
AI.method: Two image-only approaches: (1) a linear regression using novel, automatically computed predictors—projected area and sinking speed—from BIODISCOVER, a dual-camera system recording specimens sinking in ethanol; (2) end-to-end deep neural networks trained on a large paired dataset, with single-view, multi-view, and metadata-aware architectures. They also study losses, data augmentation, architectures, and discuss suitable evaluation metrics (percentage vs absolute errors).
AI.result: Both approaches predict dry mass across morphologically diverse invertebrates. When coupled with automatic taxonomic classification for aggregation, they achieve median percentage errors of about 10–20% at the individual level and accurate group-level estimates. They note metric choice affects interpretation and compare training choices (losses/augmentations/architectures).
AI.conclusion: Image-based, minimally manual biomass estimation is feasible and accurate enough for practical, group-level biodiversity monitoring. Features like area and sinking speed and deep models both work; practitioners should report both percentage and absolute errors and carefully select training/evaluation setups.

title: OralGPT-Plus: Learning to Use Visual Tools via Reinforcement Learning for Panoramic X-ray Analysis
summary: Panoramic dental radiographs require fine-grained spatial reasoning, bilateral symmetry understanding, and multi-step diagnostic verification, yet existing vision-language models operate under a static single-pass paradigm that limits their clinical reliability. In this paper, we introduce OralGPT-Plus, an agentic vision-language model designed to perform iterative and symmetry-aware diagnostic reasoning for panoramic dental radiograph analysis. To support this paradigm, we construct DentalProbe, a five-thousand-image dataset with expert-curated diagnostic trajectories that provide structured supervision for localized inspection and contralateral comparison. We further develop a Reinspection-driven reinforcement learning framework that encourages clinically meaningful re-examination and stabilizes long-horizon reasoning with rubric-based reward and conditioned diagnostic-driven reward. In parallel, we present MMOral-X, the first benchmark for holistic panoramic diagnosis, containing 300 open-ended questions and region-level annotations across multiple difficulty levels. OralGPT-Plus demonstrates consistent and reliable improvements over strong baselines on MMOral-X and established panoramic benchmarks, indicating the effectiveness of interactive and symmetry-informed reasoning. Our work highlights the value of agentic modeling for dental imaging and provides a foundation for future research in clinically aligned panoramic radiograph analysis.

AI.tldr: OralGPT-Plus is an agentic vision-language model that performs iterative, symmetry-aware reasoning on panoramic dental radiographs, trained with reinspection-driven RL and supported by a new trajectory-labeled dataset (DentalProbe) and a holistic benchmark (MMOral-X), achieving consistent gains over strong baselines.
AI.motivation: Panoramic dental diagnosis demands fine-grained spatial reasoning, bilateral symmetry assessment, and multi-step verification—capabilities poorly served by static, single-pass VLMs, limiting clinical reliability.
AI.method: Design an agentic VLM (OralGPT-Plus) that iteratively inspects images and performs contralateral comparisons; curate DentalProbe (≈5k images) with expert diagnostic trajectories for localized and symmetry-aware supervision; train with a Reinspection-driven RL framework using rubric-based and conditioned diagnostic rewards to promote clinically meaningful re-examination and stabilize long-horizon reasoning; create MMOral-X benchmark with 300 open-ended questions and region-level annotations across difficulty tiers.
AI.result: OralGPT-Plus delivers consistent, reliable improvements over strong baselines on MMOral-X and existing panoramic benchmarks, evidencing the benefits of interactive and symmetry-informed reasoning.
AI.conclusion: Agentic, symmetry-aware iterative reasoning enhances clinical alignment in dental imaging, and the released model, dataset, RL framework, and benchmark provide a foundation for future research in panoramic radiograph analysis.

title: Rewis3d: Reconstruction Improves Weakly-Supervised Semantic Segmentation
summary: We present Rewis3d, a framework that leverages recent advances in feed-forward 3D reconstruction to significantly improve weakly supervised semantic segmentation on 2D images. Obtaining dense, pixel-level annotations remains a costly bottleneck for training segmentation models. Alleviating this issue, sparse annotations offer an efficient weakly-supervised alternative. However, they still incur a performance gap. To address this, we introduce a novel approach that leverages 3D scene reconstruction as an auxiliary supervisory signal. Our key insight is that 3D geometric structure recovered from 2D videos provides strong cues that can propagate sparse annotations across entire scenes. Specifically, a dual student-teacher architecture enforces semantic consistency between 2D images and reconstructed 3D point clouds, using state-of-the-art feed-forward reconstruction to generate reliable geometric supervision. Extensive experiments demonstrate that Rewis3d achieves state-of-the-art performance in sparse supervision, outperforming existing approaches by 2-7% without requiring additional labels or inference overhead.

AI.tldr: Rewis3d boosts weakly supervised 2D semantic segmentation by using feed‑forward 3D reconstruction from videos as auxiliary supervision, propagating sparse labels via 3D geometry and enforcing 2D–3D semantic consistency, achieving state‑of‑the‑art gains (+2–7%) without extra labels or inference cost.
AI.motivation: Dense pixel annotations are expensive; sparse labels are cheaper but hurt performance. The work aims to close this gap by exploiting 3D scene structure obtainable from ordinary 2D videos as an additional supervisory signal to better utilize sparse annotations.
AI.method: Use state‑of‑the‑art feed‑forward 3D reconstruction to recover scene geometry/point clouds from 2D videos. A dual student–teacher framework enforces semantic consistency between 2D image predictions and reconstructed 3D point clouds, enabling propagation of sparse annotations across views and through the 3D scene.
AI.result: Across extensive experiments, Rewis3d outperforms prior sparse‑supervision methods by 2–7% while requiring no extra labels and adding no inference‑time overhead.
AI.conclusion: 3D geometric cues from fast feed‑forward reconstruction provide strong auxiliary supervision for weakly supervised 2D segmentation. Enforcing 2D–3D semantic consistency effectively propagates sparse labels, yielding practical state‑of‑the‑art performance without added deployment cost.

title: MoEMambaMIL: Structure-Aware Selective State Space Modeling for Whole-Slide Image Analysis
summary: Whole-slide image (WSI) analysis is challenging due to the gigapixel scale of slides and their inherent hierarchical multi-resolution structure. Existing multiple instance learning (MIL) approaches often model WSIs as unordered collections of patches, which limits their ability to capture structured dependencies between global tissue organization and local cellular patterns. Although recent State Space Models (SSMs) enable efficient modeling of long sequences, how to structure WSI tokens to fully exploit their spatial hierarchy remains an open problem.We propose MoEMambaMIL, a structure-aware SSM framework for WSI analysis that integrates region-nested selective scanning with mixture-of-experts (MoE) modeling. Leveraging multi-resolution preprocessing, MoEMambaMIL organizes patch tokens into region-aware sequences that preserve spatial containment across resolutions. On top of this structured sequence, we decouple resolution-aware encoding and region-adaptive contextual modeling via a combination of static, resolution-specific experts and dynamic sparse experts with learned routing. This design enables efficient long-sequence modeling while promoting expert specialization across heterogeneous diagnostic patterns. Experiments demonstrate that MoEMambaMIL achieves the best performance across 9 downstream tasks.

AI.tldr: MoEMambaMIL is a structure-aware State Space Model (SSM) for whole-slide images that arranges multi-resolution patches into region-nested sequences and applies a mix of static (resolution-specific) and dynamic sparse experts to model long-range, hierarchical dependencies, achieving state-of-the-art results on 9 WSI tasks.
AI.motivation: Traditional MIL treats WSIs as unordered bags of patches, missing dependencies between global tissue layout and local cellular patterns. While modern SSMs can handle long sequences, it’s unclear how to structure WSI tokens to exploit their intrinsic multi-resolution hierarchy. The work aims to bridge this gap by imposing a spatially coherent sequence and leveraging expert specialization.
AI.method: 1) Multi-resolution preprocessing groups patches so that higher-res patches are nested within lower-res regions; 2) Region-nested selective scanning creates a sequence that preserves spatial containment; 3) On this sequence, the model decouples (a) resolution-aware encoding via static, resolution-specific experts from (b) region-adaptive contextual modeling via dynamic sparse experts with learned routing, all atop an efficient SSM (e.g., Mamba) to handle long contexts.
AI.result: On 9 downstream WSI tasks, MoEMambaMIL reports the best performance versus prior baselines (details not provided in the abstract).
AI.conclusion: Encoding hierarchical spatial structure through region-aware sequencing and combining static/dynamic MoE with an SSM backbone effectively captures WSI dependencies, enabling efficient long-context MIL and superior diagnostic performance.

title: CHMv2: Improvements in Global Canopy Height Mapping using DINOv3
summary: Accurate canopy height information is essential for quantifying forest carbon, monitoring restoration and degradation, and assessing habitat structure, yet high-fidelity measurements from airborne laser scanning (ALS) remain unevenly available globally. Here we present CHMv2, a global, meter-resolution canopy height map derived from high-resolution optical satellite imagery using a depth-estimation model built on DINOv3 and trained against ALS canopy height models. Compared to existing products, CHMv2 substantially improves accuracy, reduces bias in tall forests, and better preserves fine-scale structure such as canopy edges and gaps. These gains are enabled by a large expansion of geographically diverse training data, automated data curation and registration, and a loss formulation and data sampling strategy tailored to canopy height distributions. We validate CHMv2 against independent ALS test sets and against tens of millions of GEDI and ICESat-2 observations, demonstrating consistent performance across major forest biomes.

AI.tldr: CHMv2 is a global, meter-resolution canopy height map produced from high-resolution optical imagery using a DINOv3-based depth model trained on ALS data. It improves accuracy, reduces tall-forest bias, and preserves fine-scale canopy structure, with validation against independent ALS, GEDI, and ICESat-2 across major biomes.
AI.motivation: Accurate, spatially detailed canopy height is critical for carbon accounting, restoration/degradation monitoring, and habitat assessment, but airborne laser scanning (ALS) coverage is sparse and uneven globally.
AI.method: Train a depth-estimation model built on DINOv3 using high-resolution optical satellite imagery paired with ALS-derived canopy height models. Improve performance via large, geographically diverse training data; automated data curation/registration; and a loss function and sampling strategy tailored to canopy height distributions. Generate a global, ~1 m resolution canopy height map (CHMv2).
AI.result: Relative to existing products, CHMv2 shows substantially higher accuracy, mitigates bias in tall forests, and better retains fine-scale structure like canopy edges and gaps. Validation against independent ALS test sets and tens of millions of GEDI and ICESat-2 footprints shows consistent performance across major forest biomes.
AI.conclusion: Optical-imagery–driven, DINOv3-based depth estimation can deliver globally consistent, meter-scale canopy height with improved fidelity over prior maps, offering a scalable alternative/complement to ALS for carbon, restoration, and habitat applications.

title: REACT++: Efficient Cross-Attention for Real-Time Scene Graph Generation
summary: Scene Graph Generation (SGG) is a task that encodes visual relationships between objects in images as graph structures. SGG shows significant promise as a foundational component for downstream tasks, such as reasoning for embodied agents. To enable real-time applications, SGG must address the trade-off between performance and inference speed. However, current methods tend to focus on one of the following: (1) improving relation prediction accuracy, (2) enhancing object detection accuracy, or (3) reducing latency, without aiming to balance all three objectives simultaneously. To address this limitation, we build on the powerful Real-time Efficiency and Accuracy Compromise for Tradeoffs in Scene Graph Generation (REACT) architecture and propose REACT++, a new state-of-the-art model for real-time SGG. By leveraging efficient feature extraction and subject-to-object cross-attention within the prototype space, REACT++ balances latency and representational power. REACT++ achieves the highest inference speed among existing SGG models, improving relation prediction accuracy without sacrificing object detection performance. Compared to the previous REACT version, REACT++ is 20% faster with a gain of 10% in relation prediction accuracy on average. The code is available at https://github.com/Maelic/SGG-Benchmark.

AI.tldr: REACT++ is a real-time scene graph generation model that balances speed, relation accuracy, and object detection quality via efficient features and subject-to-object cross-attention; it is 20% faster and yields ~10% higher relation accuracy than REACT while reaching the fastest inference among SGG methods.
AI.motivation: Enable real-time SGG for downstream tasks (e.g., embodied reasoning) by jointly optimizing relation prediction accuracy, object detection accuracy, and latency—an area where prior work typically optimizes only one objective, leading to unfavorable trade-offs.
AI.method: Extend the REACT architecture with efficient feature extraction and subject-to-object cross-attention in a prototype space to enhance representational power without incurring high computational cost, thereby balancing latency and accuracy.
AI.result: REACT++ achieves the highest inference speed among existing SGG models, increases relation prediction accuracy, and maintains object detection performance. Relative to REACT, it is 20% faster and improves relation prediction accuracy by about 10% on average.
AI.conclusion: REACT++ establishes a new state of the art for real-time SGG by effectively trading off speed and accuracy, making it suitable for latency-sensitive applications; open-source code is available for reproduction and use.

title: Solving Jigsaw Puzzles in the Wild: Human-Guided Reconstruction of Cultural Heritage Fragments
comment: 6 pages, 3 figures. Presented at the 2025 IEEE 35th International Workshop on Machine Learning for Signal Processing (MLSP). This is the author-accepted version of the paper. The final version is available via IEEE Xplore: https://doi.org/10.1109/MLSP62443.2025.11204324
summary: Reassembling real-world archaeological artifacts from fragmented pieces poses significant challenges due to erosion, missing regions, irregular shapes, and large-scale ambiguity. Traditional jigsaw puzzle solvers, often designed for clean synthetic scenarios, struggle under these conditions, especially when the number of fragments grows into the thousands, as in the RePAIR benchmark. In this paper, we propose a human-in-the-loop (HIL) puzzle solving framework designed to address the complexity and scale of real-world cultural heritage reconstruction. Our approach integrates an automatic relaxation-labeling solver with interactive human guidance, allowing users to iteratively lock verified placements, correct errors, and guide the system toward semantically and geometrically coherent assemblies. We introduce two complementary interaction strategies, Iterative Anchoring and Continuous Interactive Refinement, which support scalable reconstruction across varying levels of ambiguity and puzzle size. Experiments on several RePAIR groups demonstrate that our hybrid approach substantially outperforms both fully automatic and manual baselines in accuracy and efficiency, offering a practical solution for large-scale expert-in-the-loop artifact reassembly.

AI.tldr: Human-in-the-loop framework that combines an automatic relaxation-labeling solver with guided user interactions to reassemble highly fragmented, real-world archaeological artifacts at scale, outperforming fully automatic and manual approaches on RePAIR groups.
AI.motivation: Real-world artifact reassembly is extremely hard due to erosion, missing pieces, irregular fragment shapes, and massive ambiguity, where traditional jigsaw solvers—tuned for clean, synthetic puzzles—fail, especially with thousands of fragments like those in the RePAIR benchmark.
AI.method: A hybrid human-in-the-loop system that integrates a relaxation-labeling automatic solver with interactive controls. Users can iteratively lock correct placements, fix errors, and steer assembly toward semantic and geometric coherence. Two interaction modes are introduced: Iterative Anchoring (progressively fix confident substructures) and Continuous Interactive Refinement (ongoing corrections and guidance).
AI.result: On multiple RePAIR groups, the proposed hybrid approach significantly surpasses both fully automatic and purely manual baselines in accuracy and efficiency for large-scale reconstructions.
AI.conclusion: Blending automatic optimization with expert interaction provides a scalable, practical solution for reconstructing real-world cultural heritage artifacts, effectively handling ambiguity and large puzzle sizes.

title: DiffInf: Influence-Guided Diffusion for Supervision Alignment in Facial Attribute Learning
summary: Facial attribute classification relies on large-scale annotated datasets in which many traits, such as age and expression, are inherently ambiguous and continuous but are discretized into categorical labels. Annotation inconsistencies arise from subjectivity and visual confounders such as pose, illumination, expression, and demographic variation, creating mismatch between images and assigned labels. These inconsistencies introduce supervision errors that impair representation learning and degrade downstream prediction. We introduce DiffInf, a self-influence--guided diffusion framework for mitigating annotation inconsistencies in facial attribute learning. We first train a baseline classifier and compute sample-wise self-influence scores using a practical first-order approximation to identify training instances that disproportionately destabilize optimization. Instead of discarding these influential samples, we apply targeted generative correction via a latent diffusion autoencoder to better align visual content with assigned labels while preserving identity and realism. To enable differentiable guidance during correction, we train a lightweight predictor of high-influence membership and use it as a surrogate influence regularizer. The edited samples replace the originals, yielding an influence-refined dataset of unchanged size. Across multi-class facial attribute classification, DiffInf consistently improves generalization compared with standard noisy-label training, robust optimization baselines, and influence-based filtering. Our results demonstrate that repairing influential annotation inconsistencies at the image level enhances downstream facial attribute classification without sacrificing distributional coverage.

AI.tldr: DiffInf identifies training images that destabilize learning and uses a diffusion-based editor to correct their visual content to better match assigned facial-attribute labels, improving classification generalization over noisy-label and robust baselines.
AI.motivation: Facial attributes (e.g., age, expression) are continuous and subjective but are discretized into labels, leading to annotation inconsistencies due to subjectivity and confounders (pose, illumination, demographics). These mismatches inject supervision noise that harms representation learning and downstream prediction.
AI.method: 1) Train a baseline classifier. 2) Compute sample-wise self-influence scores via a first-order approximation to flag instances that disproportionately destabilize optimization. 3) Instead of filtering them out, apply targeted generative correction with a latent diffusion autoencoder to align image content with its assigned label while preserving identity/realism. 4) To enable differentiable guidance during editing, train a lightweight predictor of high-influence membership and use it as a surrogate influence regularizer. 5) Replace originals with edited images to form an influence-refined dataset of unchanged size, then train on it.
AI.result: Across multi-class facial attribute classification tasks, DiffInf consistently improves generalization compared with standard noisy-label training, robust optimization baselines, and influence-based filtering.
AI.conclusion: Repairing influential annotation inconsistencies at the image level—rather than discarding data—yields better facial attribute classification without sacrificing distributional coverage, demonstrating the value of self-influence–guided generative correction.

title: Locating and Editing Figure-Ground Organization in Vision Transformers
summary: Vision Transformers must resolve figure-ground organization by choosing between completions driven by local geometric evidence and those favored by global organizational priors, giving rise to a characteristic perceptual ambiguity. We aim to locate where the canonical Gestalt prior convexity is realized within the internal components of BEiT. Using a controlled perceptual conflict based on synthetic shapes of darts, we systematically mask regions that equally admit either a concave completion or a convex completion. We show that BEiT reliably favors convex completion under this competition. Projecting internal activations into the model's discrete visual codebook space via logit attribution reveals that this preference is governed by identifiable functional units within transformer substructures. Specifically, we find that figure-ground organization is ambiguous through early and intermediate layers and resolves abruptly in later layers. By decomposing the direct effect of attention heads, we identify head L0H9 acting as an early seed, introducing a weak bias toward convexity. Downscaling this single attention head shifts the distributional mass of the perceptual conflict across a continuous decision boundary, allowing concave evidence to guide completion.

AI.tldr: The paper probes where a Gestalt convexity prior emerges inside BEiT, showing the model prefers convex over concave completions in figure–ground ambiguity, with the bias seeded by a specific early attention head and resolved in late layers.
AI.motivation: To pinpoint how and where global organizational priors—specifically convexity—are implemented within Vision Transformers during figure–ground resolution, bridging perceptual theory (Gestalt) and mechanistic interpretability of BEiT.
AI.method: Create synthetic “dart” shapes that induce controlled convex–concave completion conflicts; mask ambiguous regions; test BEiT’s completion preference; project internal activations into the model’s discrete visual codebook using logit attribution to locate responsible units; analyze layerwise dynamics; decompose direct effects of individual attention heads; downscale/ablate head L0H9 to assess causal influence.
AI.result: BEiT reliably favors convex completions under controlled conflict. Ambiguity persists through early/intermediate layers and resolves sharply in later layers. Logit-attribution highlights functional units tied to this choice. Attention head L0H9 acts as an early seeding mechanism introducing a weak convexity bias; reducing its influence shifts decisions toward concave completions across a continuous boundary.
AI.conclusion: A canonical Gestalt prior (convexity) in BEiT is localized to identifiable substructures: an early bias from a specific head that later layers amplify to resolve figure–ground. The prior is manipulable via targeted head scaling, implying late-stage consolidation of perceptual decisions and offering a path for controllable, interpretable edits in Vision Transformers.

title: Non-invasive Growth Monitoring of Small Freshwater Fish in Home Aquariums via Stereo Vision
summary: Monitoring fish growth behavior provides relevant information about fish health in aquaculture and home aquariums. Yet, monitoring fish sizes poses different challenges, as fish are small and subject to strong refractive distortions in aquarium environments. Image-based measurement offers a practical, non-invasive alternative that allows frequent monitoring without disturbing the fish. In this paper, we propose a non-invasive refraction-aware stereo vision method to estimate fish length in aquariums. Our approach uses a YOLOv11-Pose network to detect fish and predict anatomical keypoints on the fish in each stereo image. A refraction-aware epipolar constraint accounting for the air-glass-water interfaces enables robust matching, and unreliable detections are removed using a learned quality score. A subsequent refraction-aware 3D triangulation recovers 3D keypoints, from which fish length is measured. We validate our approach on a new stereo dataset of endangered Sulawesi ricefish captured under aquarium-like conditions and demonstrate that filtering low-quality detections is essential for accurate length estimation. The proposed system offers a simple and practical solution for non-invasive growth monitoring and can be easily applied in home aquariums.

AI.tldr: Refraction-aware stereo vision pipeline using YOLOv11-Pose keypoints, a refractive epipolar constraint, learned detection-quality filtering, and refractive triangulation to non-invasively estimate fish length in aquariums; validated on a new Sulawesi ricefish stereo dataset, showing quality filtering is critical for accuracy.
AI.motivation: Aquaculture and hobby aquariums need frequent, non-invasive monitoring of fish growth/health. Conventional image-based sizing is challenged by small fish and strong refractive distortions from air–glass–water interfaces, which break standard stereo geometry and degrade accuracy.
AI.method: Detect fish and anatomical keypoints per view with YOLOv11-Pose; match across views using a refraction-aware epipolar constraint that models air–glass–water interfaces; score detections with a learned quality estimator and filter low-quality ones; perform refraction-aware 3D triangulation to recover keypoints; derive fish length from reconstructed 3D keypoints; evaluate on a new stereo dataset collected under aquarium-like conditions.
AI.result: On the new Sulawesi ricefish stereo dataset, the approach yields robust 3D keypoints and length estimates in aquarium settings; experiments indicate that removing low-quality detections substantially improves length accuracy. (No specific numbers provided in the abstract.)
AI.conclusion: A simple, practical, and non-invasive system for fish length monitoring in aquariums. Modeling refraction and filtering unreliable detections are key to achieving accurate measurements; the method is readily applicable to home aquariums.

title: What if? Emulative Simulation with World Models for Situated Reasoning
summary: Situated reasoning often relies on active exploration, yet in many real-world scenarios such exploration is infeasible due to physical constraints of robots or safety concerns of visually impaired users. Given only a limited observation, can an agent mentally simulate a future trajectory toward a target situation and answer spatial what-if questions? We introduce WanderDream, the first large-scale dataset designed for the emulative simulation of mental exploration, enabling models to reason without active exploration. WanderDream-Gen comprises 15.8K panoramic videos across 1,088 real scenes from HM3D, ScanNet++, and real-world captures, depicting imagined trajectories from current viewpoints to target situations. WanderDream-QA contains 158K question-answer pairs, covering starting states, paths, and end states along each trajectory to comprehensively evaluate exploration-based reasoning. Extensive experiments with world models and MLLMs demonstrate (1) that mental exploration is essential for situated reasoning, (2) that world models achieve compelling performance on WanderDream-Gen, (3) that imagination substantially facilitates reasoning on WanderDream-QA, and (4) that WanderDream data exhibit remarkable transferability to real-world scenarios. The source code and all data will be released.

AI.tldr: WanderDream introduces a large-scale benchmark for “mental exploration,” enabling agents to simulate future trajectories and answer spatial what‑if questions without physically exploring, using panoramic video generations and comprehensive QA to evaluate reasoning; experiments show imagination improves situated reasoning and transfers to real‑world scenarios.
AI.motivation: Active exploration is often infeasible (robot constraints, safety for visually impaired users). The goal is to enable situated reasoning from limited observations by mentally simulating trajectories toward target situations and answering spatial what‑if queries.
AI.method: Construct a two-part dataset: (1) WanderDream-Gen with 15.8K panoramic videos across 1,088 real scenes (HM3D, ScanNet++, and in-the-wild captures) that depict imagined trajectories from current viewpoints to target states; (2) WanderDream-QA with 158K QA pairs covering start, path, and end states along each trajectory. Evaluate world models and multimodal LLMs on generation quality and reasoning, and analyze the effect of imagination modules and transfer to real-world data.
AI.result: Findings show: (1) mental exploration is crucial for situated reasoning; (2) world models perform strongly on trajectory generation in WanderDream-Gen; (3) adding imagination substantially boosts QA performance on WanderDream-QA; (4) models trained on WanderDream exhibit strong transfer to real-world scenarios.
AI.conclusion: WanderDream provides the first comprehensive benchmark for emulative mental exploration, demonstrating that simulated trajectories improve spatial reasoning without active exploration and transfer beyond synthetic benchmarks. Code and data will be released to spur research on safe, resource-efficient situated agents.

title: CaTok: Taming Mean Flows for One-Dimensional Causal Image Tokenization
comment: Project website is available in https://sharelab-sii.github.io/catok-web
summary: Autoregressive (AR) language models rely on causal tokenization, but extending this paradigm to vision remains non-trivial. Current visual tokenizers either flatten 2D patches into non-causal sequences or enforce heuristic orderings that misalign with the "next-token prediction" pattern. Recent diffusion autoencoders similarly fall short: conditioning the decoder on all tokens lacks causality, while applying nested dropout mechanism introduces imbalance. To address these challenges, we present CaTok, a 1D causal image tokenizer with a MeanFlow decoder. By selecting tokens over time intervals and binding them to the MeanFlow objective, as illustrated in Fig. 1, CaTok learns causal 1D representations that support both fast one-step generation and high-fidelity multi-step sampling, while naturally capturing diverse visual concepts across token intervals. To further stabilize and accelerate training, we propose a straightforward regularization REPA-A, which aligns encoder features with Vision Foundation Models (VFMs). Experiments demonstrate that CaTok achieves state-of-the-art results on ImageNet reconstruction, reaching 0.75 FID, 22.53 PSNR and 0.674 SSIM with fewer training epochs, and the AR model attains performance comparable to leading approaches.

AI.tldr: CaTok is a 1D causal image tokenizer with a MeanFlow decoder and a VFM-aligned regularizer (REPA-A) that enables next-token-style autoregression in vision, supports both one-step and multi-step generation, and achieves SOTA ImageNet reconstruction (FID 0.75, PSNR 22.53, SSIM 0.674).
AI.motivation: Autoregressive language models depend on causal tokenization, but existing visual tokenizers are non-causal or use heuristic orders that break the next-token paradigm; diffusion autoencoders also lack strict causality or suffer imbalance from nested dropout. A causal visual tokenizer is needed to align vision with AR modeling while maintaining efficiency and fidelity.
AI.method: Introduce CaTok, a 1D causal tokenizer that selects tokens over time intervals and binds them to a MeanFlow objective, yielding causal representations. The MeanFlow decoder supports both fast one-step generation and higher-fidelity multi-step sampling. Training is stabilized and accelerated via REPA-A, a simple regularizer that aligns encoder features with Vision Foundation Models.
AI.result: On ImageNet reconstruction, CaTok attains state-of-the-art metrics: 0.75 FID, 22.53 PSNR, and 0.674 SSIM, reportedly with fewer training epochs. An AR model built on CaTok is competitive with leading approaches.
AI.conclusion: CaTok operationalizes causal tokenization for images, making next-token autoregression feasible and efficient in vision while preserving high fidelity. REPA-A further stabilizes training. The approach bridges AR modeling and visual generation, with strong reconstruction results and competitive AR performance.

title: Pinterest Canvas: Large-Scale Image Generation at Pinterest
summary: While recent image generation models demonstrate a remarkable ability to handle a wide variety of image generation tasks, this flexibility makes them hard to control via prompting or simple inference adaptation alone, rendering them unsuitable for use cases with strict product requirements. In this paper, we introduce Pinterest Canvas, our large-scale image generation system built to support image editing and enhancement use cases at Pinterest. Canvas is first trained on a diverse, multimodal dataset to produce a foundational diffusion model with broad image-editing capabilities. However, rather than relying on one generic model to handle every downstream task, we instead rapidly fine-tune variants of this base model on task-specific datasets, producing specialized models for individual use cases. We describe key components of Canvas and summarize our best practices for dataset curation, training, and inference. We also showcase task-specific variants through case studies on background enhancement and aspect-ratio outpainting, highlighting how we tackle their specific product requirements. Online A/B experiments demonstrate that our enhanced images receive a significant 18.0% and 12.5% engagement lift, respectively, and comparisons with human raters further validate that our models outperform third-party models on these tasks. Finally, we showcase other Canvas variants, including multi-image scene synthesis and image-to-video generation, demonstrating that our approach can generalize to a wide variety of potential downstream tasks.

AI.tldr: Pinterest Canvas is a production-scale diffusion-based image generation/editing system: train a broad foundation model, then rapidly fine‑tune task‑specific variants (e.g., background enhancement, aspect‑ratio outpainting). These variants yield large online engagement lifts (18.0%, 12.5%) and outperform third‑party models; the approach generalizes to multi‑image scene synthesis and image‑to‑video.
AI.motivation: General-purpose image generative models are flexible but hard to control with prompts/inference tricks, which is problematic for product scenarios requiring reliable, spec‑compliant outputs. Pinterest needs controllable, high‑quality image editing/enhancement tailored to concrete use cases with measurable business outcomes.
AI.method: 1) Pretrain a foundational diffusion model on a diverse multimodal corpus to acquire broad editing capabilities. 2) Rapidly fine‑tune specialized variants on curated, task‑specific datasets to meet strict product requirements. 3) Establish best practices for dataset curation, training, and inference. 4) Validate via online A/B tests and human rater comparisons; demonstrate extensibility with additional variants (multi‑image scene synthesis, image‑to‑video).
AI.result: Task‑specific variants for background enhancement and aspect‑ratio outpainting deliver significant engagement lifts in production (18.0% and 12.5%, respectively). Human evaluations indicate these models outperform third‑party alternatives. Additional variants show the framework generalizes to broader tasks, including multi‑image scene synthesis and image‑to‑video generation.
AI.conclusion: Specializing a strong base diffusion model through rapid, targeted fine‑tuning yields controllable, product‑grade image editing systems that meet strict requirements and improve user engagement. The approach is practical for large‑scale deployment and adaptable across many downstream tasks.

title: Training Flow Matching: The Role of Weighting and Parameterization
summary: We study the training objectives of denoising-based generative models, with a particular focus on loss weighting and output parameterization, including noise-, clean image-, and velocity-based formulations. Through a systematic numerical study, we analyze how these training choices interact with the intrinsic dimensionality of the data manifold, model architecture, and dataset size. Our experiments span synthetic datasets with controlled geometry as well as image data, and compare training objectives using quantitative metrics for denoising accuracy (PSNR across noise levels) and generative quality (FID). Rather than proposing a new method, our goal is to disentangle the various factors that matter when training a flow matching model, in order to provide practical insights on design choices.

AI.tldr: Systematic empirical study of how loss weighting and output parameterization (noise/clean/velocity) in denoising-based flow-matching generative models affect denoising accuracy and generative quality across data regimes, architectures, and intrinsic dimensionality, using PSNR and FID on synthetic and image datasets to offer practical training guidance (no new method proposed).
AI.motivation: Training denoising/flow-matching models offers multiple objective formulations and loss-weighting schemes whose impacts are poorly understood and often confounded by data geometry, model capacity, and dataset size. Practitioners lack clear guidance on which objective and weighting to use under different conditions.
AI.method: A controlled, systematic numerical study comparing noise-, clean image-, and velocity-based output parameterizations and varying loss weightings. Experiments span synthetic datasets with tunable geometry/intrinsic dimensionality and real image datasets. Performance is evaluated with denoising accuracy (PSNR across noise levels) and generative quality (FID), analyzing interactions with model architecture and dataset size.
AI.result: Quantitative benchmarks across setups that reveal how objective choice and loss weighting interact with intrinsic data dimensionality, architecture, and training data size, reflected in PSNR and FID differences. The study disentangles these factors rather than introducing a new algorithm, reporting comparative trends and trade-offs across settings.
AI.conclusion: Choice of parameterization and loss weighting materially influences both denoising and generative performance, and should be selected based on data geometry, model capacity, and data availability. The work provides practical, empirically grounded guidance for training flow-matching models, clarifying when different objectives are preferable; it does not propose a new method.

title: GreenRFM: Toward a resource-efficient radiology foundation model
