summary: The development of radiology foundation models (RFMs) is hindered by a reliance on brute-force scaling. Existing approaches often directly translate methods for natural images, which prioritize scale over precision and hence lead to brittle and expensive models in clinical practice. To address this, we present a resource-efficient pre-training framework, GreenRFM, that achieves state-of-the-art performance. Our framework ensures robust generalization across diverse patient populations and imaging protocols, reducing computational requirements by orders of magnitude while surpassing complex, parameter-heavy models. These capabilities stem from principled supervision design that aims to maximally utilize supervisory signals via More distilled, Ubiquitous, Semantic-enforcing, and Task-aligning (MUST) supervision, rather than simply piling up the quantity of training data. We offer two GreenRFM configurations: (i) a performant model that establishes a new state-of-the-art using a single 24GB GPU within 24 hours, and (ii) a lightweight model that matches existing benchmarks with 6GB VRAM in 4 hours. We conduct extensive experiments using over 200,000 images from four institutions and of two modalities. GreenRFMs achieve superior performances on chest and abdominal CT datasets, regardless of public or private benchmark, surpassing a range of baseline models. In addition, the results on internal musculoskeletal MRI images show that the same supervision principles transfer between different modalities. Our performance and efficiency challenge the ``scale is all you need'' dogma and democratize the equitable development of state-of-the-art RFMs for clinicians even on a laptop.

AI.tldr: GreenRFM is a supervision-driven, resource‑efficient pretraining framework for radiology foundation models that achieves state‑of‑the‑art performance on CT (and transfers to MRI) while training on a single modest GPU in hours, outperforming larger, brute‑force scaled models.
AI.motivation: Current radiology foundation models often copy natural‑image scaling strategies, prioritizing data/parameter scale over precise supervision. This leads to brittle, computationally expensive models that are impractical in clinical settings. The authors seek a way to improve robustness, generalization, and efficiency without massive compute.
AI.method: Introduce GreenRFM, centered on principled supervision design called MUST: More distilled, Ubiquitous, Semantic‑enforcing, and Task‑aligning supervision. Two configurations are offered: (i) a high‑performance model trained within 24 hours on a single 24GB GPU; (ii) a lightweight model matching benchmarks on 6GB VRAM in ~4 hours. Pretraining and evaluation use >200k images from four institutions across two modalities (primarily chest/abdominal CT, with transfer to musculoskeletal MRI).
AI.result: GreenRFMs reduce compute by orders of magnitude yet surpass parameter‑heavy baselines. They set new SOTA on chest and abdominal CT across public and private benchmarks and show robust generalization across patient populations and imaging protocols. The same supervision principles transfer effectively to internal musculoskeletal MRI data.
AI.conclusion: Carefully designed supervision (MUST) can beat brute‑force scaling for RFMs, delivering better accuracy and robustness at far lower cost. This challenges the “scale is all you need” view and democratizes state‑of‑the‑art RFM development, making it feasible on commodity hardware (even laptops).

title: Match4Annotate: Propagating Sparse Video Annotations via Implicit Neural Feature Matching
summary: Acquiring per-frame video annotations remains a primary bottleneck for deploying computer vision in specialized domains such as medical imaging, where expert labeling is slow and costly. Label propagation offers a natural solution, yet existing approaches face fundamental limitations. Video trackers and segmentation models can propagate labels within a single sequence but require per-video initialization and cannot generalize across videos. Classic correspondence pipelines operate on detector-chosen keypoints and struggle in low-texture scenes, while dense feature matching and one-shot segmentation methods enable cross-video propagation but lack spatiotemporal smoothness and unified support for both point and mask annotations. We present Match4Annotate, a lightweight framework for both intra-video and inter-video propagation of point and mask annotations. Our method fits a SIREN-based implicit neural representation to DINOv3 features at test time, producing a continuous, high-resolution spatiotemporal feature field, and learns a smooth implicit deformation field between frame pairs to guide correspondence matching. We evaluate on three challenging clinical ultrasound datasets. Match4Annotate achieves state-of-the-art inter-video propagation, outperforming feature matching and one-shot segmentation baselines, while remaining competitive with specialized trackers for intra-video propagation. Our results show that lightweight, test-time-optimized feature matching pipelines have the potential to offer an efficient and accessible solution for scalable annotation workflows.

AI.tldr: Match4Annotate is a lightweight, test-time framework that fits an implicit neural field to DINOv3 features and learns smooth deformations between frames to propagate both points and masks within and across videos, achieving SOTA inter-video performance on clinical ultrasound and competitive intra-video tracking—reducing per-frame annotation burden.
AI.motivation: Per-frame labeling in specialized domains (e.g., medical ultrasound) is costly and slow. Existing propagators have gaps: trackers/segmenters need per-video initialization and don’t generalize; sparse keypoint pipelines fail in low-texture scenes; dense matching and one-shot segmentation can cross videos but lack spatiotemporal smoothness and unified handling of both points and masks. A method that is smooth over space-time, generalizes across videos, supports multiple annotation types, and stays lightweight is needed.
AI.method: At test time, fit a SIREN-based implicit neural representation to DINOv3 features to build a continuous, high-resolution spatiotemporal feature field. Learn a smooth implicit deformation field between frame pairs to guide correspondence matching. Use this to propagate both point and mask annotations for intra- and inter-video scenarios in a unified pipeline.
AI.result: On three clinical ultrasound datasets, Match4Annotate delivers state-of-the-art inter-video annotation propagation, outperforming feature matching and one-shot segmentation baselines, and remains competitive with specialized trackers for intra-video propagation.
AI.conclusion: Lightweight, test-time-optimized feature-matching with implicit neural and deformation fields can provide accurate, smooth, and versatile annotation propagation across and within videos, offering a practical path to scalable annotation in domains like medical imaging.

title: Self-Supervised Flow Matching for Scalable Multi-Modal Synthesis
summary: Strong semantic representations improve the convergence and generation quality of diffusion and flow models. Existing approaches largely rely on external models, which require separate training, operate on misaligned objectives, and exhibit unexpected scaling behavior. We argue that this dependence arises from the model's training objective, which poses a denoising task with little incentive to learn semantic representations. We introduce Self-Flow: a self-supervised flow matching paradigm that integrates representation learning within the generative framework. Our key mechanism, Dual-Timestep Scheduling, applies heterogeneous noise levels across tokens, creating an information asymmetry that forces the model to infer missing information from corrupted inputs. This drives learning strong representations alongside generative capabilities without external supervision. Our method generalizes across modalities and enables multi-modal training while following expected scaling laws, achieving superior image, video, and audio generation.

AI.tldr: Self-Flow integrates representation learning directly into flow-based generative modeling via a dual-timestep schedule that applies different noise levels per token, forcing semantic inference and removing reliance on external encoders. It scales predictably and improves image, video, and audio generation.
AI.motivation: Current diffusion/flow models often need external semantic guidance (e.g., pretrained encoders) whose objectives are misaligned, require separate training, and show odd scaling. The authors argue this stems from the denoising objective offering little incentive to learn strong semantics intrinsically.
AI.method: Propose a self-supervised flow matching framework (Self-Flow) with Dual-Timestep Scheduling: assign heterogeneous noise levels across tokens within the same sample to create information asymmetry. The model must infer missing/clean information from partially corrupted inputs, encouraging learning of robust semantic representations while training the generator. The approach is modality-agnostic and supports multimodal training.
AI.result: Demonstrates improved convergence and generation quality; achieves state-of-the-art or superior results across images, video, and audio; enables effective multimodal training; and follows expected (monotonic/regular) scaling laws without the irregularities observed when using external models.
AI.conclusion: Embedding representation learning into the generative objective via token-wise heterogeneous noise obviates external supervision, yields stronger semantic representations, and enhances generative performance across modalities while restoring predictable scaling behavior.

title: SCAN: Visual Explanations with Self-Confidence and Analysis Networks
comment: 14 pages, 9 figures, IEEE Transactions on Artificial Intelligence
summary: Explainable AI (XAI) has become essential in computer vision to make the decision-making processes of deep learning models transparent. However, current visual explanation (XAI) methods face a critical trade-off between the high fidelity of architecture-specific methods and the broad applicability of universal ones. This often results in abstract or fragmented explanations and makes it difficult to compare explanatory power across diverse model families, such as CNNs and Transformers. This paper introduces the Self-Confidence and Analysis Networks (SCAN), a novel universal framework that overcomes these limitations for both convolutional neural network and transformer architectures. SCAN utilizes an AutoEncoder-based approach to reconstruct features from a model's intermediate layers. Guided by the Information Bottleneck principle, it generates a high-resolution Self-Confidence Map that identifies information-rich regions. Extensive experiments on diverse architectures and datasets demonstrate that SCAN consistently achieves outstanding performance on various quantitative metrics such as AUC-D, Negative AUC, Drop%, and Win%. Qualitatively, it produces significantly clearer, object-focused explanations than existing methods. By providing a unified framework that is both architecturally universal and highly faithful, SCAN enhances model transparency and offers a more reliable tool for understanding the decision-making processes of complex neural networks.

AI.tldr: SCAN is a universal XAI framework for CNNs and Transformers that trains an autoencoder—guided by the Information Bottleneck—to reconstruct intermediate features and produce a high‑resolution Self‑Confidence Map, yielding clearer, more faithful visual explanations and superior quantitative metrics across datasets and architectures.
AI.motivation: Current XAI methods trade off fidelity (architecture‑specific) vs universality (model‑agnostic), leading to abstract/fragmented explanations and making cross‑architecture comparisons (CNNs vs Transformers) difficult.
AI.method: Use an AutoEncoder to reconstruct intermediate network features; apply the Information Bottleneck to encourage compressed yet sufficient representations; derive a Self‑Confidence Map that highlights information‑rich regions; apply the same pipeline to both CNNs and Transformers; evaluate with AUC‑D, Negative AUC, Drop%, and Win%.
AI.result: Across diverse models and datasets, SCAN reportedly delivers higher scores on standard faithfulness/perturbation metrics and qualitatively crisper, object‑focused heatmaps than existing XAI methods.
AI.conclusion: A unified, architecture‑agnostic yet faithful visual explanation framework that improves transparency of deep models and provides more reliable insights into their decision‑making.

title: AV-Unified: A Unified Framework for Audio-visual Scene Understanding
comment: Accepted by IEEE Transactions on Multimedia (TMM)
summary: When humans perceive the world, they naturally integrate multiple audio-visual tasks within dynamic, real-world scenes. However, current works such as event localization, parsing, segmentation and question answering are mostly explored individually, making it challenging to comprehensively understand complex audio-visual scenes and explore inter-task relationships. Hence, we propose \textbf{AV-Unified}, a unified framework that enables joint learning across a wide range of audio-visual scene understanding tasks. AV-Unified standardizes the diverse input-output formats of each task and incorporates a multi-scale spatiotemporal perception network to effectively capture audio-visual associations. Specifically, we unify the inputs and outputs of all supported tasks by converting them into sequences of discrete tokens, establishing a shared representation that allows a single architecture to be trained jointly across heterogeneous varied datasets. Considering the varying temporal granularity of audio-visual events, a multi-scale temporal perception module is designed to capture key cues. Meanwhile, to overcome the lack of auditory supervision in the visual domain, we design a cross-modal guidance-based spatial perception module that models spatial audio-visual associations. Furthermore, task-specific text prompts are employed to enhance the model's adaptability and task-awareness. Extensive experiments on benchmark datasets (e.g., AVE, LLP, MUSIC-AVQA, VGG-SS and AVS) demonstrate the effectiveness of AV-Unified across temporal, spatial, and spatiotemporal tasks.

AI.tldr: AV-Unified is a single, token-based framework that unifies diverse audio-visual scene understanding tasks and datasets, using multi-scale temporal and cross-modal spatial perception with task prompts to achieve strong results across temporal, spatial, and spatiotemporal benchmarks.
AI.motivation: Existing audio-visual tasks (event localization, parsing, segmentation, QA) are studied in isolation, hindering comprehensive scene understanding and limiting the ability to learn inter-task relationships across heterogeneous datasets.
AI.method: Standardize all supported tasks by converting inputs/outputs into sequences of discrete tokens for a shared representation; employ a multi-scale spatiotemporal perception network, including (1) a multi-scale temporal perception module to handle varied event granularity, (2) a cross-modal guidance-based spatial perception module to model spatial audio-visual associations and compensate for weak auditory supervision in vision; incorporate task-specific text prompts to enhance adaptability and task awareness; train a single architecture jointly on varied datasets.
AI.result: On benchmark datasets (AVE, LLP, MUSIC-AVQA, VGG-SS, AVS), the unified model demonstrates effective performance across temporal, spatial, and spatiotemporal tasks; qualitative and/or quantitative gains are implied, though specific metrics are not provided in the abstract.
AI.conclusion: Unifying heterogeneous audio-visual tasks via tokenized I/O and multi-scale spatiotemporal perception enables joint learning that improves comprehensive understanding of complex scenes and generalizes across tasks and datasets.

title: Spatial Calibration of Diffuse LiDARs
summary: Diffuse direct time-of-flight LiDARs report per-pixel depth histograms formed by aggregating photon returns over a wide instantaneous field of view, violating the single-ray assumption behind standard LiDAR-RGB calibration. We present a simple spatial calibration procedure that estimates, for each diffuse LiDAR pixel, its footprint (effective support region) and relative spatial sensitivity in a co-located RGB image plane. Using a scanned retroreflective patch with background subtraction, we recover per-pixel response maps that provide an explicit LiDAR-to-RGB correspondence for cross-modal alignment and fusion. We demonstrate the method on the ams OSRAM TMF8828.

AI.tldr: A practical calibration method maps each pixel of a diffuse direct ToF LiDAR to its spatial footprint and sensitivity on a co-located RGB image using a scanned retroreflective patch and background subtraction, enabling explicit LiDAR-to-RGB correspondence for accurate alignment and fusion; demonstrated on the ams OSRAM TMF8828.
AI.motivation: Diffuse dToF LiDAR pixels aggregate photon returns over a wide field of view, violating the single-ray model assumed by standard LiDAR–RGB calibration. Without knowing each pixel’s true footprint and spatial sensitivity, cross-modal alignment and fusion with RGB imagery are inaccurate or biased.
AI.method: Perform a simple spatial calibration by scanning a small retroreflective target across the scene while capturing LiDAR histograms and RGB images. Use background subtraction to isolate the LiDAR’s response to the target and, for each LiDAR pixel, recover a response map on the RGB image plane that encodes its effective support (footprint) and relative sensitivity distribution. These per-pixel maps provide the forward LiDAR-to-RGB correspondence.
AI.result: Recovered per-pixel response maps that explicitly relate each LiDAR pixel to a weighted region in the RGB image, enabling correct alignment and fusion. The method is validated on the ams OSRAM TMF8828 sensor; qualitative/feasibility results are reported (no quantitative metrics are given in the abstract).
AI.conclusion: A simple, sensor-specific spatial calibration addresses the breakdown of the single-ray assumption in diffuse dToF LiDARs by providing explicit per-pixel footprints and sensitivities in RGB space. This facilitates reliable cross-modal registration and fusion and is practically demonstrable with off-the-shelf hardware.

title: NEGATE: Constrained Semantic Guidance for Linguistic Negation in Text-to-Video Diffusion
summary: Negation is a fundamental linguistic operator, yet it remains inadequately modeled in diffusion-based generative systems. In this work, we present a formal treatment of linguistic negation in diffusion-based generative models by modeling it as a structured feasibility constraint on semantic guidance within diffusion dynamics. Rather than introducing heuristics or retraining model parameters, we reinterpret classifier-free guidance as defining a semantic update direction and enforce negation by projecting the update onto a convex constraint set derived from linguistic structure. This novel formulation provides a unified framework for handling diverse negation phenomena, including object absence, graded non-inversion semantics, multi-negation composition, and scope-sensitive disambiguation. Our approach is training-free, compatible with pretrained diffusion backbones, and naturally extends from image generation to temporally evolving video trajectories. In addition, we introduce a structured negation-centric benchmark suite that isolates distinct linguistic failure modes in generative systems, to further research in this area. Experiments demonstrate that our method achieves robust negation compliance while preserving visual fidelity and structural coherence, establishing the first unified formulation of linguistic negation in diffusion-based generative models beyond representation-level evaluation.

AI.tldr: Training-free projection of classifier-free guidance onto convex, linguistically derived constraint sets to enforce negation in diffusion models; unifies diverse negation cases for images and videos; new benchmark; improved negation compliance without sacrificing fidelity.
AI.motivation: Diffusion generators frequently fail to honor linguistic negation (absence, graded/non-inversion, multi-negation, scope). Existing fixes are heuristic or require retraining. A formal, unified, plug-and-play approach with principled evaluation is needed.
AI.method: Treat negation as a structured feasibility constraint on semantic guidance during diffusion. Reinterpret classifier-free guidance as a semantic update vector and enforce negation by projecting this update onto a convex constraint set induced by the negated linguistic structure. The formulation naturally composes multiple negations and handles scope, is training-free, backbone-agnostic, and extends from images to temporally evolving video trajectories.
AI.result: On a newly introduced negation-centric benchmark that isolates distinct linguistic failure modes, the approach yields robust compliance with negated prompts while preserving visual fidelity and structural/temporal coherence, working across pretrained diffusion backbones.
AI.conclusion: This is the first unified, formal treatment of linguistic negation for diffusion-based generation beyond representation-level evaluation, offering a practical, training-free control mechanism and a benchmark to spur further research.

title: SurgFormer: Scalable Learning of Organ Deformation with Resection Support and Real-Time Inference
summary: We introduce SurgFormer, a multiresolution gated transformer for data driven soft tissue simulation on volumetric meshes. High fidelity biomechanical solvers are often too costly for interactive use, so we train SurgFormer on solver generated data to predict nodewise displacement fields at near real time rates. SurgFormer builds a fixed mesh hierarchy and applies repeated multibranch blocks that combine local message passing, coarse global self attention, and pointwise feedforward updates, fused by learned per node, per channel gates to adaptively integrate local and long range information while remaining scalable on large meshes. For cut conditioned simulation, resection information is encoded as a learned cut embedding and provided as an additional input, enabling a unified model for both standard deformation prediction and topology altering cases. We also introduce two surgical simulation datasets generated under a unified protocol with XFEM based supervision: a cholecystectomy resection dataset and an appendectomy manipulation and resection dataset with cut and uncut cases. To our knowledge, this is the first learned volumetric surrogate setting to study XFEM supervised cut conditioned deformation within the same volumetric pipeline as standard deformation prediction. Across diverse baselines, SurgFormer achieves strong accuracy with favorable efficiency, making it a practical backbone for both tasks. {Code, data, and project page: \href{https://mint-vu.github.io/SurgFormer/}{available here}}

AI.tldr: SurgFormer is a multiresolution, gated transformer that learns to predict near–real-time soft-tissue deformations on volumetric meshes, including topology changes from cuts, using data supervised by XFEM solvers; it attains strong accuracy and efficiency across baselines and comes with two new surgical datasets.
AI.motivation: High-fidelity biomechanical solvers (e.g., XFEM/FEM) are too slow for interactive surgical simulation. There is a need for a fast, accurate, and scalable learned surrogate that can handle both standard deformations and cut-induced topology changes on volumetric meshes.
AI.method: Build a fixed multiresolution mesh hierarchy and stack multibranch blocks that mix: (1) local message passing, (2) coarse-level global self-attention, and (3) pointwise feedforward updates. Fuse these via learned per-node, per-channel gates to adaptively balance local vs. long-range information, enabling scalability. Train on solver-generated data to regress nodewise displacement fields. For cut-conditioned simulation, encode resection information as a learned cut embedding input, yielding a unified model for both uncut and cut cases.
AI.result: On two new XFEM-supervised datasets (cholecystectomy resections; appendectomy manipulation and resections with cut/uncut cases), SurgFormer achieves strong accuracy at near real-time rates with favorable efficiency versus diverse baselines, demonstrating practical scalability on large volumetric meshes.
AI.conclusion: A unified, scalable transformer-based surrogate that handles both standard and cut-conditioned volumetric soft-tissue deformation, delivering accurate, efficient predictions suitable as a practical backbone for surgical simulation. The authors release code, data, and a project page.

title: Modeling and Measuring Redundancy in Multisource Multimodal Data for Autonomous Driving
comment: This paper has been accepted by the Fourth IEEE International Conference on Mobility: Operations, Services, and Technologies (MOST) 2026
summary: Next-generation autonomous vehicles (AVs) rely on large volumes of multisource and multimodal ($M^2$) data to support real-time decision-making. In practice, data quality (DQ) varies across sources and modalities due to environmental conditions and sensor limitations, yet AV research has largely prioritized algorithm design over DQ analysis. This work focuses on redundancy as a fundamental but underexplored DQ issue in AV datasets. Using the nuScenes and Argoverse 2 (AV2) datasets, we model and measure redundancy in multisource camera data and multimodal image-LiDAR data, and evaluate how removing redundant labels affects the YOLOv8 object detection task. Experimental results show that selectively removing redundant multisource image object labels from cameras with shared fields of view improves detection. In nuScenes, mAP${50}$ gains from $0.66$ to $0.70$, $0.64$ to $0.67$, and from $0.53$ to $0.55$, on three representative overlap regions, while detection on other overlapping camera pairs remains at the baseline even under stronger pruning. In AV2, $4.1$-$8.6\%$ of labels are removed, and mAP${50}$ stays near the $0.64$ baseline. Multimodal analysis also reveals substantial redundancy between image and LiDAR data. These findings demonstrate that redundancy is a measurable and actionable DQ factor with direct implications for AV performance. This work highlights the role of redundancy as a data quality factor in AV perception and motivates a data-centric perspective for evaluating and improving AV datasets. Code, data, and implementation details are publicly available at: https://github.com/yhZHOU515/RedundancyAD

AI.tldr: They show that label redundancy across overlapping cameras and between images and LiDAR is a measurable data-quality issue in AV datasets, and that pruning redundant labels can improve or at least not harm YOLOv8 detection performance; code and data are released.
AI.motivation: AV perception depends on large multisource/multimodal datasets whose quality varies, but research has emphasized algorithm design over data-quality analysis. Redundancy—multiple labels for the same objects across overlapping sensors/modalities—may waste labeling effort and degrade training, yet is underexplored.
AI.method: On nuScenes and Argoverse 2, they model and quantify redundancy in (1) multisource camera streams with overlapping fields of view and (2) multimodal image–LiDAR pairs. They selectively remove (prune) redundant object labels and evaluate the impact on YOLOv8 object detection using mAP50, analyzing overlap regions and sensor pairs; they also assess redundancy between image and LiDAR modalities.
AI.result: In nuScenes, pruning redundant multisource image labels increased mAP50 from 0.66→0.70, 0.64→0.67, and 0.53→0.55 on three overlap regions; other overlapping camera pairs showed no degradation even with stronger pruning. In AV2, removing 4.1–8.6% of labels kept mAP50 near the 0.64 baseline. Multimodal analysis indicates substantial redundancy between images and LiDAR.
AI.conclusion: Redundancy is a concrete, quantifiable data-quality factor that can be exploited to improve or maintain AV object detection without extra modeling complexity. The work advocates a data-centric approach to AV dataset curation and provides public code/data to facilitate adoption.

title: EgoReasoner: Learning Egocentric 4D Reasoning via Task-Adaptive Structured Thinking
summary: Egocentric video understanding is inherently complex due to the dynamic 4D nature of the environment, where camera motion and object displacements necessitate a continuous re-evaluation of spatial relations. In this work, we target a suite of under-explored egocentric 4D reasoning tasks, including fixture interaction counting, viewpoint-relative fixture location, object movement itinerary tracking, and stationary object localization, that require fundamentally different cognitive operations: spatial anchoring, temporal tracking, and duration reasoning. We observe that these structural differences make task-agnostic approaches insufficient: generic Chain-of-Thought methods lack task-appropriate reasoning primitives, and uniform reinforcement learning actively destabilizes performance on spatial tasks. To address this, we propose EgoReasoner, a two-stage framework that aligns both the reasoning scaffold and the reward signal to each task's cognitive structure. In the first stage, Task-Adaptive Thinking Templates guide the synthesis of structured CoT traces that teach the model to reason adaptively across task types via supervised fine-tuning. In the second stage, task-aware reward functions verify entity grounding, temporal alignment, and task-adaptive logical consistency, selectively strengthening each reasoning pathway via reinforcement fine-tuning with GRPO. Our 3B-parameter model, trained on only 16K samples, achieves 37.5% average accuracy on the challenging HD-EPIC benchmark, surpassing Qwen2.5-VL-7B (25.7%) by over 10 points.

AI.tldr: EgoReasoner is a two-stage, task-aligned reasoning framework for egocentric 4D video tasks that uses task-adaptive Chain-of-Thought templates and task-aware RL rewards, yielding strong gains on HD-EPIC with a small 3B model trained on 16K samples.
AI.motivation: Egocentric video requires constant re-anchoring of spatial relations and temporal tracking due to camera motion and object dynamics. Generic, task-agnostic CoT and uniform RL struggle because they lack the right reasoning primitives and can destabilize spatial reasoning. The authors aim to close this gap with task-structured reasoning.
AI.method: Stage 1: Supervised fine-tuning with Task-Adaptive Thinking Templates to produce structured CoT traces aligned to each task’s cognitive demands (spatial anchoring, temporal tracking, duration reasoning). Stage 2: Reinforcement fine-tuning with GRPO using task-aware reward functions that verify entity grounding, temporal alignment, and task-specific logical consistency, selectively strengthening each reasoning pathway.
AI.result: A 3B-parameter model trained on 16K samples achieves 37.5% average accuracy on HD-EPIC, outperforming Qwen2.5-VL-7B (25.7%) by >10 points.
AI.conclusion: Aligning both reasoning scaffolds and reward signals to the cognitive structure of each task enables more reliable 4D egocentric reasoning than task-agnostic CoT or uniform RL, delivering substantial performance gains with modest model size and data.

title: Penguin-VL: Exploring the Efficiency Limits of VLM with LLM-based Vision Encoders
summary: Vision Language Model (VLM) development has largely relied on scaling model size, which hinders deployment on compute-constrained mobile and edge devices such as smartphones and robots. In this work, we explore the performance limits of compact (e.g., 2B and 8B) VLMs. We challenge the prevailing practice that state-of-the-art VLMs must rely on vision encoders initialized via massive contrastive pretraining (e.g., CLIP/SigLIP). We identify an objective mismatch: contrastive learning, optimized for discrimination, enforces coarse and category-level invariances that suppress fine-grained visual cues needed for dense captioning and complex VLM reasoning. To address this issue, we present Penguin-VL, whose vision encoder is initialized from a text-only LLM. Our experiments reveal that Penguin-Encoder serves as a superior alternative to traditional contrastive pretraining, unlocking a higher degree of visual fidelity and data efficiency for multimodal understanding. Across various image and video benchmarks, Penguin-VL achieves performance comparable to leading VLMs (e.g., Qwen3-VL) in mathematical reasoning and surpasses them in tasks such as document understanding, visual knowledge, and multi-perspective video understanding. Notably, these gains are achieved with a lightweight architecture, demonstrating that improved visual representation rather than model scaling is the primary driver of performance. Our ablations show that Penguin-Encoder consistently outperforms contrastive-pretrained encoders, preserving fine-grained spatial and temporal cues that are critical for dense perception and complex reasoning. This makes it a strong drop-in alternative for compute-efficient VLMs and enables high performance in resource-constrained settings. Code: https://github.com/tencent-ailab/Penguin-VL

AI.tldr: Penguin-VL replaces contrastive-pretrained vision encoders (e.g., CLIP/SigLIP) with a vision encoder initialized from a text-only LLM, yielding finer visual fidelity and better data-efficiency in compact (2B/8B) VLMs. It matches or beats larger SOTA VLMs on document, visual knowledge, and video understanding while remaining lightweight for edge settings.
AI.motivation: Scaling VLMs boosts accuracy but blocks deployment on mobile/edge hardware. Contrastive vision pretraining optimizes for coarse, category-level discrimination and can discard fine-grained spatial/temporal cues required for dense captioning and complex multimodal reasoning. The authors seek a representation that preserves fine detail without relying on massive models.
AI.method: Introduce Penguin-Encoder: a vision encoder initialized from a text-only LLM rather than from contrastive visual pretraining. Integrate it into a compact VLM (Penguin-VL) and train for multimodal understanding. Compare against CLIP/SigLIP-style encoders across image and video tasks; perform ablations to measure spatial/temporal fidelity and data efficiency; benchmark versus leading VLMs (e.g., Qwen3-VL).
AI.result: Penguin-VL attains comparable mathematical reasoning to top VLMs and surpasses them on document understanding, visual knowledge, and multi-perspective video tasks. Ablations show Penguin-Encoder consistently outperforms contrastive-pretrained encoders, preserving fine-grained cues and requiring less data to reach high performance. These gains hold for compact 2B/8B models.
AI.conclusion: Improved visual representation—via LLM-initialized vision encoders—rather than sheer scaling is the key driver of performance for compact VLMs. Penguin-Encoder serves as a drop-in alternative to contrastive pretraining, enabling state-of-the-art, compute-efficient VLMs suitable for resource-constrained devices.

title: SCOPE: Scene-Contextualized Incremental Few-Shot 3D Segmentation
summary: Incremental Few-Shot (IFS) segmentation aims to learn new categories over time from only a few annotations. Although widely studied in 2D, it remains underexplored for 3D point clouds. Existing methods suffer from catastrophic forgetting or fail to learn discriminative prototypes under sparse supervision, and often overlook a key cue: novel categories frequently appear as unlabelled background in base-training scenes. We introduce SCOPE (Scene-COntextualised Prototype Enrichment), a plug-and-play background-guided prototype enrichment framework that integrates with any prototype-based 3D segmentation method. After base training, a class-agnostic segmentation model extracts high-confidence pseudo-instances from background regions to build a prototype pool. When novel classes arrive with few labelled samples, relevant background prototypes are retrieved and fused with few-shot prototypes to form enriched representations without retraining the backbone or adding parameters. Experiments on ScanNet and S3DIS show that SCOPE achieves SOTA performance, improving novel-class IoU by up to 6.98% and 3.61%, and mean IoU by 2.25% and 1.70%, respectively, while maintaining low forgetting. Code is available https://github.com/Surrey-UP-Lab/SCOPE.

AI.tldr: SCOPE enriches few-shot prototypes for incremental 3D point-cloud segmentation by mining pseudo-instances from background regions during base training and fusing them with new-class prototypes at increment time, yielding state-of-the-art performance without retraining or extra parameters.
AI.motivation: Incremental few-shot learning in 3D is underexplored; existing approaches either forget base classes or build weak prototypes from sparse labels. Importantly, future novel classes often exist unlabeled in the background of base-training scenes, a signal that prior work underuses.
AI.method: After base training, a class-agnostic segmentation model extracts high-confidence pseudo-instances from background regions to form a prototype pool. When novel classes arrive with few annotations, SCOPE retrieves relevant background prototypes and fuses them with the few-shot prototypes to obtain enriched class representations. This plug-and-play procedure integrates with prototype-based 3D segmenters and requires no backbone retraining or extra parameters.
AI.result: On ScanNet and S3DIS, SCOPE sets new SOTA: novel-class IoU gains up to +6.98% (ScanNet) and +3.61% (S3DIS), and mean IoU gains of +2.25% and +1.70%, respectively, while keeping forgetting low.
AI.conclusion: Leveraging unlabeled background to build a reusable prototype pool mitigates data scarcity and forgetting in incremental 3D segmentation. The approach is practical (no retraining/params), broadly compatible with prototype-based methods, and empirically effective; code is publicly available.

title: Omni-Diffusion: Unified Multimodal Understanding and Generation with Masked Discrete Diffusion
summary: While recent multimodal large language models (MLLMs) have made impressive strides, they predominantly employ a conventional autoregressive architecture as their backbone, leaving significant room to explore effective and efficient alternatives in architectural design. Concurrently, recent studies have successfully applied discrete diffusion models to various domains, such as visual understanding and image generation, revealing their considerable potential as a promising backbone for multimodal systems. Drawing inspiration from these pioneering research, we introduce Omni-Diffusion, the first any-to-any multimodal language model built entirely on mask-based discrete diffusion models, which unifies understanding and generation across text, speech, and images. Omni-Diffusion employs a unified mask-based discrete diffusion model to directly capture the joint distribution over discrete multimodal tokens. This approach supports not only bimodal tasks but also more complex scenarios involving multiple modalities. On a diverse set of benchmarks, our method outperforms or performs on par with existing multimodal systems that process two or more modalities, highlighting the significant promise of diffusion models in powering the next generation of multimodal foundation models. Project webpage: https://omni-diffusion.github.io.

AI.tldr: Omni-Diffusion proposes an any-to-any multimodal model that replaces the typical autoregressive backbone with a unified mask-based discrete diffusion process to jointly model text, speech, and image tokens, achieving competitive or superior results across multimodal benchmarks.
AI.motivation: Most MLLMs rely on autoregressive architectures; the authors argue there is room for more effective/efficient alternatives. Recent success of discrete diffusion in vision and generation suggests it could serve as a strong backbone for multimodal understanding and generation.
AI.method: Use a single mask-based discrete diffusion model to learn the joint distribution over discrete tokens from multiple modalities (text, speech, images). This unified backbone enables both understanding and generation and supports tasks involving more than two modalities, enabling any-to-any conversions via masked token infilling/denoising within the joint token space.
AI.result: Across diverse multimodal benchmarks (bimodal and more complex multi-modality), Omni-Diffusion outperforms or matches existing systems handling two or more modalities.
AI.conclusion: Mask-based discrete diffusion is a promising alternative backbone for multimodal foundation models, enabling unified any-to-any understanding and generation across text, speech, and images, with strong empirical performance suggesting viability beyond autoregressive approaches.

title: Multimodal Large Language Models as Image Classifiers
summary: Multimodal Large Language Models (MLLM) classification performance depends critically on evaluation protocol and ground truth quality. Studies comparing MLLMs with supervised and vision-language models report conflicting conclusions, and we show these conflicts stem from protocols that either inflate or underestimate performance. Across the most common evaluation protocols, we identify and fix key issues: model outputs that fall outside the provided class list and are discarded, inflated results from weak multiple-choice distractors, and an open-world setting that underperforms only due to poor output mapping. We additionally quantify the impact of commonly overlooked design choices - batch size, image ordering, and text encoder selection - showing they substantially affect accuracy. Evaluating on ReGT, our multilabel reannotation of 625 ImageNet-1k classes, reveals that MLLMs benefit most from corrected labels (up to +10.8%), substantially narrowing the perceived gap with supervised models. Much of the reported MLLMs underperformance on classification is thus an artifact of noisy ground truth and flawed evaluation protocol rather than genuine model deficiency. Models less reliant on supervised training signals prove most sensitive to annotation quality. Finally, we show that MLLMs can assist human annotators: in a controlled case study, annotators confirmed or integrated MLLMs predictions in approximately 50% of difficult cases, demonstrating their potential for large-scale dataset curation.

AI.tldr: Conflicting claims about MLLM image classification stem largely from flawed evaluation protocols and noisy labels; when protocols are fixed and labels are reannotated, MLLMs perform substantially better and can aid dataset curation.
AI.motivation: Prior studies compare MLLMs to supervised and vision-language models but reach inconsistent conclusions. The authors suspect evaluation setups and ground-truth quality, not true model capability, drive much of the disagreement.
AI.method: Audit common evaluation protocols for image classification with MLLMs; identify and correct issues (out-of-list outputs being discarded, weak MCQ distractors inflating accuracy, poor open-world output mapping). Systematically study overlooked design choices (batch size, image order, text encoder). Create ReGT, a multilabel reannotation for 625 ImageNet-1k classes, and re-evaluate models. Run a controlled human-in-the-loop case study to test whether MLLMs assist annotation.
AI.result: Fixing protocol issues and using multilabel reannotations substantially boosts MLLM accuracy (up to +10.8%) and narrows the gap with supervised models. Models less dependent on supervised signals are most sensitive to improved annotation quality. Human annotators accepted or incorporated MLLM suggestions in ~50% of hard cases.
AI.conclusion: Much of MLLMs’ apparent underperformance on classification is an artifact of noisy labels and flawed evaluation. With corrected protocols and labels, MLLMs are more competitive and also useful as assistants for large-scale dataset curation.
